name: Seokguram Grotto and Bulguksa Temple
overview: Seokguram Grotto and Bulguksa Temple are a joint UNESCO World Heritage Site in Gyeongju. Established in the 8th century under the Silla Dynasty, they are masterpieces of Buddhist art in the Far East. Seokguram consists of a granite grotto enshrining a monumental Buddha statue looking at the sea, while Bulguksa is a temple complex featuring wooden buildings on raised stone terraces.
history_significance: Built during the reign of King Gyeongdeok of the Unified Silla Kingdom (751–774). Seokguram was constructed by Kim Dae-seong for his parents in his past life, while Bulguksa was for his parents in his present life. They represent the golden age of Buddhist culture in Korea.
highlights: The Main Buddha (Bonjonbul) in Seokguram, Dabotap and Seokgatap pagodas in Bulguksa, and the arched bridges (Cheongungyo and Baegungyo).

name: Haeinsa Temple Janggyeong Panjeon, the Depositories for the Tripitaka Koreana Woodblocks
overview: The Janggyeong Panjeon in the Temple of Haeinsa, on Mount Gaya, is home to the Tripitaka Koreana, the most complete collection of Buddhist texts, engraved on 80,000 woodblocks between 1237 and 1248.
history_significance: The depositories were built in the 15th century and designed specifically to ensure natural ventilation and temperature control to preserve the woodblocks, a remarkable feat of scientific conservation architecture.
highlights: The layout of the buildings (Sudara-jang and Beopbo-jeon), the scientific ventilation system involving window sizes and clay floors with charcoal and salt.

name: Jongmyo Shrine
overview: Jongmyo is the oldest and most authentic of the Confucian royal shrines to have been preserved. Dedicated to the forefathers of the Joseon dynasty, the shrine has existed in its present form since the 16th century and houses tablets bearing the teachings of members of the former royal family.
history_significance: Originally built in 1394 by King Taejo, it was destroyed by fire during the Japanese invasions (1592) and rebuilt in 1608. Ritual ceremonies linking music, song, and dance (Jongmyo Jerye) have taken place there for centuries.
highlights: Jeongjeon (Main Hall) which is the longest wooden building of traditional Korea, and the solemn atmosphere of the ancestral rites.

name: Changdeokgung Palace Complex
overview: Changdeokgung Palace was the second royal villa built following the construction of Gyeongbokgung Palace in 1405. It is an outstanding example of Far Eastern palace architecture and garden design, exceptional for the way in which the buildings are integrated into and harmonized with the natural setting.
history_significance: It served as the principal palace for many Joseon kings and is the best-preserved of the five remaining royal palaces of the Joseon Dynasty.
highlights: Biwon (Secret Garden), Injeongjeon Hall, and the layout that follows the topography of the land rather than imposing a grid.

name: Petroglyphs along the Bangucheon Stream
overview: Located along the Bangucheon Stream in Ulsan, this serial property comprises two exceptional prehistoric petroglyph sites that document aspects of prehistoric life, rituals, and symbolic expression from the Neolithic to the early Iron Age.
history_significance: The Bangudae Petroglyphs contain some of the world's earliest large-scale depictions of whales and marine hunting scenes, while the Cheonjeon-ri Petroglyphs exhibit multi-layered carvings spanning thousands of years. Together, they illustrate the cultural, subsistence, and spiritual practices of prehistoric peoples on the Korean Peninsula.
highlights: The whale hunting scenes of Bangudae, the superimposed carving layers at Cheonjeon-ri, and the natural rock shelter setting along the Bangucheon Stream.

name: Hwaseong Fortress
overview: Hwaseong is a piled-stone and brick fortress of the Joseon Dynasty that surrounds the center of Suwon City. It was built in the late 18th century by King Jeongjo for defensive purposes, to house a new political center, and to house the remains of his father, Crown Prince Sado.
history_significance: Constructed between 1794 and 1796 under the guidance of scholar Jeong Yak-yong, explicitly using the 'Hwaseong Seongyeok Uigwe' (Royal Protocols) which detailed the construction process. It incorporated scientific engineering concepts like the crane (Geojunggi).
highlights: Paldalmun Gate, Hwahongmun Gate, and the combination of military defense features with commercial functions.

name: Gochang, Hwasun and Ganghwa Dolmen Sites
overview: The prehistoric cemeteries at Gochang, Hwasun, and Ganghwa contain many hundreds of examples of dolmens — tombs from the 1st millennium BC constructed of large stone slabs. They represent the highest density of dolmens in the world.
history_significance: These sites provide key evidence of the megalithic culture in Korea and the social structure, technology, and spiritual values of the Bronze Age people.
highlights: The massive capstones, the variety of dolmen types (table type vs. paduk type), and the sheer number of monuments.

name: Gyeongju Historic Areas
overview: The Gyeongju Historic Areas contain a remarkable concentration of outstanding examples of Korean Buddhist art, in the form of sculptures, reliefs, pagodas, and the remains of temples and palaces from the Silla culture (7th to 10th century).
history_significance: Gyeongju was the capital of the Silla Kingdom for nearly a thousand years. The sites are divided into five belts representing different aspects of the culture: royal, buddhist, defensive, etc.
highlights: Cheomseongdae Observatory, Royal Tombs of Daereungwon, Anapji Pond (Donggung Palace and Wolji), and the ruins of Hwangnyongsa Temple.

name: Jeju Volcanic Island and Lava Tubes
overview: Jeju Volcanic Island and Lava Tubes is a natural World Heritage Site comprising three areas that make up 18,846 ha. It includes Geomunoreum, regarded as the finest lava tube system of caves anywhere, the dramatic fortress-like Seongsan Ilchulbong tuff cone, and Mount Halla, the highest mountain in South Korea.
history_significance: It bears testimony to the history of the planet, its features, and processes. The lava tubes are known for their spectacular speleothems and dark red carbonate formations.
highlights: Mount Halla (Hallasan), Seongsan Ilchulbong (Sunrise Peak), and the Manjanggul Lava Tube.

name: Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty
overview: The collection of 40 tombs of the Joseon Dynasty (1408-1966) scattered over 18 locations. Built to honor the memory of ancestors, show respect for their achievements, and assert royal authority, the tombs are meticulously landscaped according to geomantic principles (pungsu).
history_significance: They provide a unique insight into the tomb architecture, ritual practices, and ancestor worship of the Joseon Dynasty, maintained for over 500 years.
highlights: Donggureung Cluster, Seonjeongneung (in Gangnam), and Yeongneung (Tomb of King Sejong).

name: Historic Villages of Korea: Hahoe and Yangdong
overview: Hahoe and Yangdong are the two most representative historic clan villages in Korea. Founded in the 14th–15th centuries, they are located in settings that reflect the distinctive aristocratic Confucian culture of the early Joseon Dynasty.
history_significance: The villages have preserved their traditional layout, wooden houses, pavilions, and study halls, as well as their intangible cultural heritage including rituals and traditional games.
highlights: Traditional Hanok architecture, the location based on Pungsu (Feng Shui), and the preserved clan lineages (Ryu clan in Hahoe, Son and Lee clans in Yangdong).

name: Namhansanseong
overview: Namhansanseong was designed as an emergency capital for the Joseon Dynasty, in anticipation of an invasion. Built and defended by Buddhist monk-soldiers, it utilizes advanced fortification technology from China and Japan and embodies a synthesis of defensive military engineering concepts.
history_significance: It served as a temporary capital during the Second Manchu Invasion of 1636. It represents a turning point in fortress construction in the 17th century in East Asia.
highlights: The Fortress Walls (approx 12km), the Emergency Palace (Haenggung), and Sueojangdae Command Post.

name: Baekje Historic Areas
overview: Located in the mountainous mid-western region of the Republic of Korea, this property comprises eight archaeological sites dating from 475 to 660 CE, representing the later period of the Baekje Kingdom. It testifies to the exchange between the ancient East Asian kingdoms.
history_significance: The sites show the development of Baekje architecture, technology, and religion (Buddhism) and its transmission to Japan and other regions.
highlights: Gongsanseong Fortress, Royal Tombs in Songsan-ri, Busosanseong Fortress, Jeongnimsa Temple Site, and Mireuksaji Temple Site.

name: Sansa, Buddhist Mountain Monasteries in Korea
overview: Sansa, Buddhist Mountain Monasteries in Korea, are spatial arrangements of the seven temples that have been preserved as places of faith and daily religious practice since the 7th–9th centuries. They exhibit common characteristics of Korean Buddhist temples: the 'madang' (open courtyard) flanked by four buildings.
history_significance: These monasteries have survived as living centers of faith and daily practice for over a millennium, maintaining their original layout and architectural style.
highlights: Tongdosa (no Buddha statue, enshrines relics), Buseoksa (Muryangsujeon Hall), and Beopjusa (Palsangjeon Wooden Pagoda).

name: Seowon, Korean Neo-Confucian Academies
overview: This site comprises nine Seowon, representing a type of Neo-Confucian academy of the Joseon Dynasty. They were established to honor exemplary Neo-Confucian scholars and to educate youth. They illustrate an educational process where learning was not distinct from self-cultivation.
history_significance: Seowon served as the strongholds of Neo-Confucianism in local districts. Their architecture typically places the study area in the front and the ritual area in the rear (Jeonhak Hujye).
highlights: Sosu-seowon (the first Seowon), Dosan-seowon (associated with Yi Hwang), and the harmony of the buildings with the surrounding landscape.

name: Getbol, Korean Tidal Flats
overview: Getbol, Korean Tidal Flats, comprises four distinct sites on the south-western and southern coast of Korea. It demonstrates the link between geodiversity and biodiversity, serving as a vital stopover for migratory waterbirds.
history_significance: A globally important habitat for threatened species such as the Spoon-billed Sandpiper. It represents one of the world's most unique and complex macrotidal flat systems.
highlights: Seocheon Getbol, Gochang Getbol, Shinan Getbol (the largest area), and Boseong-Suncheon Getbol.

name: Gaya Tumuli
overview: The Gaya Tumuli consists of seven tomb clusters which display the architectural style and burial customs of the Gaya Confederacy (42-562 CE). These sites testify to the unique political system of Gaya, which maintained a confederacy of autonomous polities.
history_significance: It offers important evidence of the diversity of ancient East Asian civilizations and the exchange between the Gaya, Silla, and Japanese domains.
highlights: Daeseong-dong Tumuli (Gimhae), Marisan Tumuli (Haman), and Jisan-dong Tumuli (Goryeong).

name: Tchogha Zanbil
overview: The ruins of the holy city of the Kingdom of Elam, surrounded by three huge concentric walls. Founded c. 1250 BC, the city remained unfinished after it was invaded by Ashurbanipal.
history_significance: Contains the best preserved and the largest ziggurat outside of Mesopotamia. It testifies to the development of the Elamite civilization.
highlights: The massive Ziggurat dedicated to Inshushinak, the concentric walls, and the royal palaces.

name: Persepolis
overview: Founded by Darius I in 518 BC, Persepolis was the capital of the Achaemenid Empire. It was built on an immense half-artificial, half-natural terrace, where the king of kings created an impressive palace complex.
history_significance: The ceremonial capital of the first Persian Empire, showcasing the grandeur of Achaemenid architecture before its destruction by Alexander the Great.
highlights: Gate of All Nations, Apadana Palace, and the intricate bas-reliefs.

name: Meidan Emam, Esfahan
overview: Built by Shah Abbas I the Great at the beginning of the 17th century, this immense square is bordered by monumental buildings linked by a series of two-storeyed arcades.
history_significance: One of the largest public squares in the world and a masterpiece of Safavid architecture.
highlights: Royal Mosque (Imam Mosque), Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque, Ali Qapu Palace, and the Quesaysyeh Portico.

name: Takht-e Soleyman
overview: The archaeological site of Takht-e Soleyman, in north-western Iran, is situated in a valley set in a volcanic mountain region. It includes the principal Zoroastrian sanctuary partly rebuilt in the Ilkhanid (Mongol) period.
history_significance: The spiritual center of Zoroastrianism during the Sassanid era, featuring a fire temple built around a mineral lake.
highlights: The artesian lake, the Fire Temple of Azargoshnasp, and the Anahita Temple.

name: Bam and its Cultural Landscape
overview: Bam is situated in a desert environment on the southern edge of the Iranian high plateau. The origins of Bam can be traced back to the Achaemenid period (6th to 4th centuries BC). Its heyday was from the 7th to 11th centuries.
history_significance: Arg-e Bam is the largest adobe building in the world, representing a fortified medieval town built using mud layers (Chineh).
highlights: The Citadel (Arg-e Bam), the bazaar, and the ancient qanats.

name: Pasargadae
overview: Pasargadae was the first dynastic capital of the Achaemenid Empire, founded by Cyrus II the Great, in the 6th century BC.
history_significance: It is an exceptional testimony to the Achaemenid civilization and includes the mausoleum of Cyrus the Great.
highlights: Tomb of Cyrus the Great, Tall-e Takht, and the residential palace.

name: Soltaniyeh
overview: The mausoleum of Oljaytu was constructed in 1302–12 in the city of Soltaniyeh, the capital of the Ilkhanid dynasty. It is a key monument in the development of Islamic architecture.
history_significance: Features one of the oldest and largest double-shell brick domes in the world, influencing the Taj Mahal.
highlights: Dome of Soltaniyeh and its intricate turquoise tile work.

name: Bisotun
overview: Bisotun is located along the ancient trade route linking the Iranian high plateau with Mesopotamia and features remains from the prehistoric times to the Median, Achaemenid, Sassanid, and Ilkhanid periods.
history_significance: Famous for the bas-relief and cuneiform inscription ordered by Darius I the Great, which was crucial for deciphering cuneiform script.
highlights: The Darius I Inscription and the Statue of Hercules.

name: Armenian Monastic Ensembles of Iran
overview: Consists of three monastic ensembles of the Armenian Christian faith: St Thaddeus, St Stepanos, and the Chapel of Dzordzor.
history_significance: Testifies to the interchange between Armenian and Persian architectures and is a major center of Armenian culture in the region.
highlights: The Black Church (St. Thaddeus) and its stone reliefs.

name: Shushtar Historical Hydraulic System
overview: A masterpiece of creative genius, the hydraulic system involves the creation of two main diversion canals on the river Karun. It forms a spectacular cliff from which water cascades into a downstream basin.
history_significance: Dating back to Darius the Great and completed in the Sassanid era, it demonstrates advanced Roman-Persian engineering.
highlights: The Gargar Canal, watermills, and the Salasil Castle.

name: Sheikh Safi al-din Khānegāh and Shrine Ensemble in Ardabil
overview: Built between the beginning of the 16th century and the end of the 18th century, this place of spiritual retreat in the Sufi tradition uses Iranian traditional architectural forms to maximize the use of available space.
history_significance: Burial place of Sheikh Safi, the ancestor of the Safavid kings, and a center of Sufi mysticism.
highlights: The Allah-Allah Dome and the Chinikhaneh (House of Porcelain).

name: Tabriz Historic Bazaar Complex
overview: Tabriz has been a place of cultural exchange since antiquity. Its historic bazaar complex is one of the most important commercial centers on the Silk Road.
history_significance: The largest covered bazaar in the world, consisting of a series of interconnected, covered brick structures.
highlights: Mozaffariyeh (Carpet Bazaar) and the Amir Bazaar.

name: The Persian Garden
overview: The property includes nine gardens in as many provinces. They exemplify the diversity of Persian garden designs that evolved and adapted to different climate conditions.
history_significance: Represents the 'Eden' symbolism with four sectors (Chahar Bagh), water management, and pavilions.
highlights: Eram Garden, Fin Garden, and Shazdeh Garden.

name: Gonbad-e Qābus
overview: The 53m high tomb built in ad 1006 for Qābus Ibn Voshmgir, Ziyarid ruler and literati, near the ruins of the ancient city of Jorjan.
history_significance: A masterpiece of early Islamic brick architecture, it is the tallest pure brick tower in the world.
highlights: The star-shaped plan and the Kufic inscriptions.

name: Masjed-e Jāmé of Isfahan
overview: Located in the historic center of Isfahan, the Masjed-e Jāmé ('Friday mosque') can be seen as a stunning illustration of the evolution of mosque architecture over twelve centuries.
history_significance: The first Islamic building that adapted the four-iwan courtyard layout of Sassanid palaces to Islamic religious architecture.
highlights: The Nezam al-Molk Dome and the Taj al-Molk Dome.

name: Golestan Palace
overview: The lavish Golestan Palace is a masterpiece of the Qajar era, embodying the successful integration of earlier Persian crafts and architecture with Western influences.
history_significance: The seat of the Qajar government and a center of arts and architecture in the 19th century.
highlights: The Marble Throne (Takht-e Marmar), Shams-ol-Emareh, and the Mirror Hall.

name: Shahr-i Sokhta
overview: Shahr-i Sokhta, meaning 'Burnt City', is located at the junction of Bronze Age trade routes crossing the Iranian plateau. Founded around 3200 BC, it was populated during four main periods up to 1800 BC.
history_significance: Famous for advanced ancient findings including the world's oldest artificial eye, brain surgery evidence, and animation concepts.
highlights: The archaeological mounds and the cemetery.

name: Cultural Landscape of Maymand
overview: Maymand is a self-contained, semi-arid area at the end of a mountain valley. The villagers are semi-nomadic agro-pastoralists who live in troglodyte houses carved into the soft rock.
history_significance: An example of a system that appears to have been more widespread in the past and involves the movement of people rather than animals.
highlights: The hand-dug cave dwellings (Kicheh) occupied for thousands of years.

name: Susa
overview: Located in the lower Zagros Mountains, the property encompasses a group of archaeological mounds rising on the eastern side of the Shavur River, as well as Ardeshir's palace.
history_significance: One of the oldest known settlements in the world, serving as the capital of Elam and the Achaemenid winter capital.
highlights: The Apadana of Darius, the Tomb of Daniel, and the Acropolis.

name: Lut Desert
overview: The Lut Desert, or Dasht-e Lut, is located in the south-east of the country. Between June and October, this arid subtropical area is swept by strong winds, which transport sediment and cause erosion on a colossal scale.
history_significance: Known as the hottest place on Earth (recorded 70.7°C) and features spectacular Yardangs (sand ridges).
highlights: The Kaluts (Yardangs) of Shahdad and the massive sand dunes.

name: The Persian Qanat
overview: Throughout the arid regions of Iran, agricultural and permanent settlements are supported by the ancient qanat system of tapping alluvial aquifers at the heads of valleys and conducting the water along underground tunnels.
history_significance: An ancient engineering feat that allowed civilization to flourish in the desert, representing a tradition of communal management of water resources.
highlights: Ghasabeh Qanat (deepest), Zarch Qanat (longest), and the double-decked Moon Qanat.

name: Historic City of Yazd
overview: The City of Yazd is located in the middle of the Iranian plateau, close to the Spice and Silk Roads. It bears living testimony to the use of limited resources for survival in the desert.
history_significance: A center of Zoroastrianism and renowned for its adobe architecture, qanats, and windcatchers (badgirs).
highlights: Amir Chakhmaq Complex, Jame Mosque of Yazd, and the winding adobe lanes.

name: Sassanid Archaeological Landscape of Fars Region
overview: Eight archaeological sites situated in three geographical parts in the southeast of Fars Province: Firuzabad, Bishapur, and Sarvestan. These fortified structures, palaces, and city plans date back to the earliest and latest moments of the Sassanid Empire.
history_significance: Illustrates the formation of the Sassanid Empire (224-651 AD), its unique architecture (e.g., the use of domes on squinches), and the influence of Roman art.
highlights: Palace of Ardashir, the circular city of Ardashir Khurreh (Gur), and the Colossal Statue of Shapur I in Shapur Cave.

name: Hyrcanian Forests
overview: The Hyrcanian forests form a unique forested massif that stretches 850 km along the southern coast of the Caspian Sea. These broad-leaved forests date back 25 to 50 million years. Originally inscribed in 2019 for Iran, the site was extended in 2023 to include the Hirkan Forests in Azerbaijan.
history_significance: A biodiversity hotspot and a relic of the Tertiary period forests that survived the Ice Age, representing a remarkable floristic evolution. It is a transnational property sharing a rich ecosystem across the Iran-Azerbaijan border.
highlights: Golestan National Park, the Cloud Forest (Jangal-e Abr), ancient Yew trees, and the pristine Hirkan National Park areas in Azerbaijan.

name: Trans-Iranian Railway
overview: The Trans-Iranian Railway connects the Caspian Sea in the northeast with the Persian Gulf in the southwest crossing two mountain ranges as well as rivers, highlands, forests and plains, and four different climatic areas.
history_significance: Completed in 1938, it is a monumental engineering feat involving 174 large bridges and 224 tunnels.
highlights: Veresk Bridge and the spiral tunnels in the Zagros mountains.

name: Cultural Landscape of Hawraman/Uramanat
overview: The remote and mountainous landscape of Hawraman/Uramanat bears testimony to the traditional culture of the Hawrami people, an agropastoral Kurdish tribe that has inhabited the region since about 3000 BCE.
history_significance: Characterized by distinctive steep-slope architecture, dry-stone terracing, and seasonal migration (havar).
highlights: Uraman Takht village and the stone terraced gardens.

name: The Persian Caravanserai
overview: Caravanserais were roadside inns, sheltering travelers, merchants, and their livestock along ancient trade routes. This property comprises 54 selected caravanserais located across Iran.
history_significance: They display a wide range of architectural styles, adaptation to climatic conditions, and construction materials, representing the evolution of the caravanserai network over many centuries.
highlights: Deyr-e Gachin (Mother of Caravanserais), Zein-o-din (Circular), and Sa'd al-Saltaneh (Grand Bazaar style).

name: Hegmataneh
overview: Hegmataneh (Ecbatana) provides important evidence of the Medes civilization (7th and 6th centuries BCE) and later served as a summer capital for the Achaemenids, Seleucids, Parthians, and Sasanians.
history_significance: One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Iran, associated with the Median Kingdom.
highlights: The ancient mound of Hegmataneh and the extensive urban layout.

name: Prehistoric Sites of the Khorramabad Valley
overview: Located in the Zagros Mountains, this site includes a cluster of caves and rock shelters that provide key evidence of human habitation during the Paleolithic era.
history_significance: Testifies to the early presence of Homo sapiens and Neanderthals in the region and the transition to the Neolithic era.
highlights: Kaldar Cave, Yafteh Cave, and Gilvaran Cave.

name: Mesa Verde National Park
overview: A great concentration of ancestral Pueblo Indian dwellings built from the 6th to the 12th century. The site is famous for its cliff dwellings, notably Cliff Palace.
history_significance: Provides remarkable testimony to the ancient cultural traditions of Native American tribes in the prehistoric Southwest.
highlights: Cliff Palace, Balcony House, and the Spruce Tree House.

name: Yellowstone National Park
overview: The world's first national park, established in 1872. It contains half of all the world's known geothermal features and the world's largest concentration of geysers.
history_significance: A major site for the conservation of the grizzly bear, wolf, and bison, showcasing ongoing geological processes.
highlights: Old Faithful Geyser, Grand Prismatic Spring, and the Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone.

name: Grand Canyon National Park
overview: Carved out by the Colorado River, the Grand Canyon is nearly 1,500 m deep and is the most spectacular gorge in the world.
history_significance: It provides a unique view of geological history, exposing rocks that date back 2 billion years.
highlights: Mather Point, Yavapai Geology Museum, and the Bright Angel Trail.

name: Everglades National Park
overview: This site at the southern tip of Florida can be described as a river of grass flowing imperceptibly from the hinterland into the sea.
history_significance: The largest subtropical wilderness in North America, serving as a sanctuary for birds and reptiles like the manatee.
highlights: Shark Valley, Anhinga Trail, and the vast mangrove forests.

name: Independence Hall
overview: The Declaration of Independence (1776) and the Constitution of the United States (1787) were both signed in this building.
history_significance: These documents have had a profound impact on lawgivers and politicians around the world, influencing the principles of democracy.
highlights: The Assembly Room and the Liberty Bell Center across the street.

name: Kluane / Wrangell-St. Elias / Glacier Bay / Tatshenshini-Alsek
overview: These parks comprise an impressive complex of glaciers and high peaks on both sides of the border between Canada (Yukon Territory and British Columbia) and the United States (Alaska).
history_significance: Contains the largest non-polar icefield in the world and is home to grizzly bears, caribou, and Dall sheep.
highlights: Mount Logan, Hubbard Glacier, and the Alsek River.

locations.name: Wrangell-St. Elias National Park and Preserve (USA)
Glacier Bay National Park and Preserve (USA)

name: Redwood National and State Parks
overview: Located north of San Francisco, this park protects a coastal mountain region covered with the magnificent forest of coastal redwood trees, the tallest living things on earth.
history_significance: Preserves the remnants of a group of trees that have existed for 160 million years.
highlights: Lady Bird Johnson Grove, Fern Canyon, and the Tall Trees Grove.

name: Mammoth Cave National Park
overview: Mammoth Cave is the world's longest known cave system, with more than 400 miles explored. It forms a unique karst topography.
history_significance: Home to unique cave-dwelling flora and fauna, including eyeless fish.
highlights: The Rotunda, Frozen Niagara, and the Historic Tour route.

name: Olympic National Park
overview: Renowned for the diversity of its ecosystems, Olympic National Park features glacier-clad peaks, an extensive temperate rainforest, and a wild coastline.
history_significance: Contains the finest example of intact and protected temperate rainforest in the Pacific Northwest.
highlights: Hoh Rain Forest, Hurricane Ridge, and Ruby Beach.

name: Cahokia Mounds State Historic Site
overview: Cahokia Mounds is the largest pre-Columbian settlement north of Mexico. It was occupied primarily during the Mississippian period (800–1400 AD).
history_significance: Illustrates a complex chiefdom society with advanced urban planning and monumental agriculture.
highlights: Monks Mound (largest prehistoric earthen structure in the Americas) and Woodhenge.

name: Great Smoky Mountains National Park
overview: Stretching over more than 200,000 hectares, this park is home to more than 3,500 plant species. It is relatively untouched and gives an idea of the temperate flora before human influence.
history_significance: One of the most biologically diverse areas in the world's temperate zone.
highlights: Clingmans Dome, Cades Cove, and the mist-covered peaks.

name: La Fortaleza and San Juan National Historic Site in Puerto Rico
overview: A series of defensive structures built between the 15th and 19th centuries to protect the city and the Bay of San Juan. They represent a fine display of European military architecture adapted to harbor sites.
history_significance: Key strategic point in the Caribbean for the Spanish Empire.
highlights: Castillo San Felipe del Morro, Castillo San Cristóbal, and the City Walls.

name: Statue of Liberty
overview: Made in Paris by the French sculptor Bartholdi, in collaboration with Gustave Eiffel, this colossal statue was a gift from France to the United States.
history_significance: A universal symbol of freedom and democracy, welcoming millions of immigrants.
highlights: The pedestal museum and the view from the crown.

name: Yosemite National Park
overview: Yosemite is spectacular for its granite cliffs, waterfalls, clear streams, giant sequoia groves, and biological diversity.
history_significance: Shows the effects of glacial erosion on granitic bedrock, creating unique geological features.
highlights: El Capitan, Half Dome, Yosemite Falls, and Mariposa Grove.

name: Chaco Culture
overview: For over 2,000 years, Pueblo peoples inhabited a vast region of the south-western United States. Chaco Canyon was a major center of ancestral Puebloan culture between 850 and 1250.
history_significance: Noted for its monumental public and ceremonial buildings and distinctive architecture.
highlights: Pueblo Bonito, Casa Rinconada, and Chetro Ketl.

name: Hawaii Volcanoes National Park
overview: This park contains two of the most active volcanoes in the world, Mauna Loa and Kilauea. It provides a unique example of island building through volcanic processes.
history_significance: Offers insights into the birth of the Hawaiian Islands and the views of native people.
highlights: Kilauea Caldera, Thurston Lava Tube, and the Chain of Craters Road.

name: Monticello and the University of Virginia in Charlottesville
overview: Thomas Jefferson, author of the American Declaration of Independence and third president of the United States, designed Monticello (his plantation home) and the academical village of the University of Virginia.
history_significance: An outstanding example of Neoclassical architecture inspired by classical antiquity and the Age of Reason.
highlights: The Rotunda (UVA) and the Monticello house and gardens.

name: Taos Pueblo
overview: Situated in the valley of a small tributary of the Rio Grande, this adobe settlement represents the culture of the Pueblo Indians of Arizona and New Mexico.
history_significance: One of the oldest continuously inhabited communities in the United States.
highlights: The multi-storied adobe buildings and the San Geronimo Chapel.

name: Carlsbad Caverns National Park
overview: This park in New Mexico features more than 100 caves. The Carlsbad Cavern is one of the largest and most spectacular in the world.
history_significance: Notable for its huge chambers, fantastic speleothems, and the accessible Big Room.
highlights: The Big Room, Natural Entrance, and the Bat Flight Program.

name: Waterton-Glacier International Peace Park
overview: In 1932 Waterton Lakes National Park (Alberta, Canada) was combined with the Glacier National Park (Montana, United States) to form the world's first International Peace Park.
history_significance: Outstanding scenery and a rich variety of plant and mammal species as well as prairie, forest, alpine and glacial features.
highlights: Going-to-the-Sun Road (USA) and Upper Waterton Lake (Canada).

name: Papahānaumokuākea
overview: A vast cluster of small, low-lying islands and atolls in the Pacific Ocean. It is one of the largest marine protected areas in the world.
history_significance: Deep cosmological significance to Native Hawaiians and a habitat for threatened species like the Hawaiian monk seal.
highlights: Nihoa Island, Mokumanamana, and the pristine coral reefs.

name: Monumental Earthworks of Poverty Point
overview: Poverty Point owes its name to a 19th-century plantation close to the site. It comprises a massive complex of earthen ridges and mounds built by hunter-fisher-gatherers between 1700 and 1100 BC.
history_significance: A remarkable achievement in earthen construction in North America that was unsurpassed for at least 2,000 years.
highlights: Mound A (Bird Mound) and the concentric C-shaped ridges.

name: San Antonio Missions
overview: The site encompasses a group of five frontier mission complexes situated along a stretch of the San Antonio River basin, as well as a ranch located 37 km to the south.
history_significance: Illustrates the Spanish Crown's efforts to colonize, evangelize, and defend the northern frontier of New Spain.
highlights: The Alamo (Mission Valero) and Mission San José (Queen of the Missions).

locations.name: The Alamo (Mission San Antonio de Valero)

name: The 20th-Century Architecture of Frank Lloyd Wright
overview: This property consists of eight buildings in the United States designed by the architect Frank Lloyd Wright during the first half of the 20th century. They reflect the 'organic architecture' developed by Wright.
history_significance: Had a strong impact on the development of modern architecture in Europe and worldwide.
highlights: Fallingwater, Guggenheim Museum, and Unity Temple.

name: Hopewell Ceremonial Earthworks
overview: A series of eight monumental earthen enclosure complexes built between 2,000 and 1,600 years ago along the central tributaries of the Ohio River.
history_significance: The most representative surviving expressions of the Indigenous Hopewell culture, known for precise geometric earthworks and astronomical alignments.
highlights: Mound City Group, Great Circle Earthworks, and Octagon Earthworks.

name: L'Anse aux Meadows National Historic Site
overview: The remains of an 11th-century Viking settlement are evidence of the first European presence in North America.
history_significance: A unique milestone in the history of human migration and discovery, excavated remains of wood-framed peat-turf buildings are similar to those found in Norse Greenland and Iceland.
highlights: Reconstructed sod houses and the archaeological site.

name: Nahanni National Park Reserve
overview: Located along the South Nahanni River, this park is known for its deep canyons and huge waterfalls, as well as its unique limestone cave systems.
history_significance: Protects a portion of the Mackenzie Mountains Natural Region and features the spectacular Virginia Falls.
highlights: Virginia Falls (twice the height of Niagara) and the Cirque of the Unclimbables.

name: Dinosaur Provincial Park
overview: Located in the badlands of southeastern Alberta, this park contains some of the most important fossil discoveries ever made from the 'Age of Reptiles'.
history_significance: More than 35 species of dinosaurs dating back 75 million years have been discovered here.
highlights: The Badlands landscape and the Royal Tyrrell Museum field station.

name: Head-Smashed-In Buffalo Jump
overview: A buffalo jump where Indigenous peoples drove bison over a cliff for nearly 6,000 years. It is one of the oldest and best-preserved sites of its kind.
history_significance: Testifies to the sophisticated hunting techniques and communal life of the Plains people.
highlights: The sandstone cliff and the interpretive center built into the hill.

name: SGang Gwaay
overview: The village of Ninstints (Nansdins) is located on a small island off the west coast of the Queen Charlotte Islands (Haida Gwaii). It features remains of large cedar longhouses and totem poles.
history_significance: Commemorates the living culture of the Haida people and their relationship with the land and sea.
highlights: The weathering totem poles facing the ocean.

name: Wood Buffalo National Park
overview: Canada's largest national park, home to the largest population of wild bison in North America and the nesting ground of the whooping crane.
history_significance: Contains the world's largest inland delta (Peace-Athabasca Delta) and vast salt plains.
highlights: The Salt Plains and the bison herds.

name: Canadian Rocky Mountain Parks
overview: The contiguous national parks of Banff, Jasper, Kootenay, and Yoho, as well as three provincial parks, form a striking mountain landscape.
history_significance: Renowned for scenic splendor, including peaks, glaciers, lakes, waterfalls, canyons, and limestone caves (Burgess Shale fossils).
highlights: Lake Louise, Moraine Lake, and the Columbia Icefield.

name: Historic District of Old Québec
overview: Founded by the French explorer Champlain in the early 17th century, it is the only North American city to have preserved its ramparts.
history_significance: A remarkable example of a fortified colonial city and the cradle of French civilization in North America.
highlights: Château Frontenac, the Citadel, and the Petit-Champlain district.

name: Gros Morne National Park
overview: Located on the west coast of Newfoundland, the park provides a rare example of the process of continental drift, where deep ocean crust and the rocks of the earth's mantle lie exposed.
history_significance: The Tablelands offer a unique visible example of plate tectonics.
highlights: The Tablelands, Western Brook Pond fjord, and Green Gardens.

name: Old Town Lunenburg
overview: Lunenburg is the best surviving example of a planned British colonial settlement in North America. Established in 1753, it has retained its original layout.
history_significance: Famous for its colorful wooden architecture and shipbuilding tradition (home of the Bluenose II).
highlights: The waterfront, the red buildings, and the Fisheries Museum of the Atlantic.

name: Miguasha National Park
overview: Located on the southern coast of the Gaspé Peninsula, Miguasha is a paleontological site known for its abundance of fossils from the Devonian Period ('Age of Fishes').
history_significance: Contains the finest specimens of lobe-finned fish (Sarcopterygii), ancestors of the first four-legged land-living vertebrates.
highlights: The museum and fossil cliffs.

name: Rideau Canal
overview: A monumental early 19th-century construction covering 202 km of the Rideau and Cataraqui rivers from Ottawa south to Kingston Harbor on Lake Ontario.
history_significance: The best-preserved example of a slackwater canal in North America and remains operational with most of its original structures.
highlights: Ottawa Locks (near Parliament), Jones Falls, and the Kingston Mills.

name: Joggins Fossil Cliffs
overview: A paleontological site along the coast of Nova Scotia, known as the 'Coal Age Galápagos'. It contains the best fossil record of the Carboniferous period.
history_significance: Provides a complete record of life in the Carboniferous era, including the earliest reptiles (Hylonomus).
highlights: The fossil-rich cliffs and the Joggins Fossil Centre.

name: Landscape of Grand Pré
overview: A cultural landscape situated in the southern Minas Basin of Nova Scotia, the site is a memorial to the Acadian way of life and their deportation.
history_significance: Demonstrates the ingenuity of 17th-century Acadian settlers in adapting to the coastal environment using dykes (aboiteaux).
highlights: Grand-Pré National Historic Site and the dykelands.

name: Red Bay Basque Whaling Station
overview: An archaeological site on the coast of Labrador that was a major Basque whaling station in the 16th century.
history_significance: The largest and most complete example of early industrial-scale whaling in the world.
highlights: Saddle Island and the Interpretation Centre with the chalupa boat.

name: Mistaken Point
overview: Located at the southeastern tip of Newfoundland, this site contains the oldest known large, complex, multicellular life forms (Ediacaran biota).
history_significance: These fossils date back 565 million years and illustrate a watershed moment in the history of life on Earth.
highlights: The fossil-covered bedding planes along the coastline.

name: Pimachiowin Aki
overview: Pimachiowin Aki ('The Land That Gives Life') is a vast landscape of boreal forest, rivers, and lakes, inhabited by the Anishinaabe people.
history_significance: An exceptional example of the cultural tradition of Ji-ganawendamang Gidakiiminaan (Keeping the Land), harmonizing culture and nature.
highlights: The untouched boreal forest and traditional Anishinaabe territories.

name: Writing-on-Stone / Áísínai'pi
overview: Located in the Milk River Valley, this site features a landscape of hoodoos and the largest concentration of rock art on the Great Plains.
history_significance: A sacred place for the Blackfoot (Siksikáítapi) people, containing thousands of petroglyphs and pictographs.
highlights: The hoodoos, rock art panels, and the Milk River.

name: Anticosti
overview: Located in the Gulf of St. Lawrence, Anticosti Island contains the most complete fossil record of marine life covering 10 million years of Earth history (Ordovician-Silurian).
history_significance: It provides the best exposure of the first mass extinction event of animal life (Late Ordovician mass extinction).
highlights: Vast limestone cliffs rich in fossils and the Vauréal Falls.

name: Tr'ondëk-Klondike
overview: Located along the Yukon River, this serial property lies within the homeland of the Tr'ondëk Hwëch'in First Nation. It tells the story of the Klondike Gold Rush from an Indigenous perspective.
history_significance: Demonstrates the adaptation of Indigenous people to the unprecedented changes caused by the gold rush.
highlights: Fort Reliance, Dawson City, and Moosehide Village.

name: Historic Town of Ouro Preto
overview: Founded at the end of the 17th century, Ouro Preto (Black Gold) was the focal point of the gold rush and the golden age of Brazil in the 18th century.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Brazilian Baroque architecture and art, featuring the works of Aleijadinho.
highlights: Church of São Francisco de Assis and the steep cobbled streets.

name: Historic Centre of the Town of Olinda
overview: Founded in the 16th century by the Portuguese, the town's history is linked to the sugar cane industry. It was rebuilt after being looted by the Dutch.
history_significance: Known for its harmonious balance between buildings, gardens, 20 baroque churches, convents, and numerous small pasos (chapels).
highlights: Convent of São Francisco and the view from the Alto da Sé.

name: Jesuit Missions of the Guaranis
overview: The ruins of São Miguel das Missões in Brazil, and four other missions in Argentina (San Ignacio Miní, Santa Ana, Nuestra Señora de Loreto, and Santa María Mayor), are the remains of the Jesuit missions built in the land of the Guaranis.
history_significance: Testifies to the unique social and economic experiment of the Jesuit reductions (17th-18th centuries) and the cultural interaction between European missionaries and indigenous peoples.
highlights: Ruins of São Miguel Arcanjo and San Ignacio Miní.

locations.name: Ruins of São Miguel das Missões (Brazil)

name: Historic Centre of Salvador de Bahia
overview: As the first capital of Brazil from 1549 to 1763, Salvador de Bahia witnessed the blending of European, African, and Amerindian cultures.
history_significance: Renowned for its outstanding collection of 17th and 18th-century colonial architecture, particularly in the Pelourinho district.
highlights: Pelourinho, Church and Convent of São Francisco, and the Lacerda Elevator.

name: Sanctuary of Bom Jesus do Congonhas
overview: Built in the second half of the 18th century, this sanctuary consists of a church with a magnificent Rococo interior and an outdoor stairway decorated with statues of the prophets.
history_significance: Features the twelve sculptures of the prophets carved in soapstone by Aleijadinho, considered his masterpiece.
highlights: The Twelve Prophets and the six chapels of the Steps of the Passion.

name: Iguaçu National Park
overview: The park shares with Iguazú National Park in Argentina one of the world's largest and most impressive waterfalls, extending over some 2,700 m.
history_significance: Home to many rare and endangered species of flora and fauna, such as the giant otter and the giant anteater.
highlights: The Devil's Throat (Garganta do Diabo) and the subtropical rainforest.

name: Brasilia
overview: Brasilia, a capital created ex nihilo in the center of the country in 1956, was a landmark in the history of town planning.
history_significance: Designed by urban planner Lúcio Costa and architect Oscar Niemeyer, it is a masterpiece of Modernist architecture and urbanism.
highlights: National Congress, Cathedral of Brasília, and the Plaza of the Three Powers.

name: Serra da Capivara National Park
overview: This area contains the largest concentration of rock paintings in the Americas. Many of the numerous rock shelters in the park are decorated with cave paintings.
history_significance: Provides outstanding testimony to one of the oldest human communities in South America, dating back over 25,000 years.
highlights: Pedra Furada and the vivid rock art depicting rituals and hunting.

name: Historic Centre of São Luís
overview: Founded by the French and occupied by the Dutch before falling to Portuguese rule, São Luís has preserved the complete rectangular street plan of the 17th century.
history_significance: Famous for its extensive use of azulejos (tiled facades) and traditional Portuguese colonial architecture adapted to the tropical climate.
highlights: The tiled facades of colonial mansions and the Palace of the Lions.

name: Atlantic Forest South-East Reserves
overview: The site contains some of the best and most extensive examples of the Atlantic Forest, one of the most threatened biome in the world.
history_significance: Displays the biological evolution and history of the Atlantic Forest, with a high level of endemism.
highlights: Petar State Park (caves) and Ilha do Cardoso.

name: Discovery Coast Atlantic Forest Reserves
overview: Consisting of eight separate protected areas containing 112,000 ha of Atlantic forest and associated shrub (restingas).
history_significance: The world's richest area in terms of number of tree species per hectare (more than the Amazon).
highlights: Monte Pascoal National Park and Una Biological Reserve.

name: Historic Centre of the Town of Diamantina
overview: Diamantina, a colonial village set like a jewel in a necklace of rocky mountains, recalls the exploits of diamond prospectors in the 18th century.
history_significance: Shows the triumph of human cultural and artistic endeavor over a harsh environment.
highlights: The wooden colonial houses and the Church of Nossa Senhora do Carmo.

name: Central Amazon Conservation Complex
overview: The largest protected area in the Amazon Basin (over 6 million hectares) and one of the richest regions of the planet in terms of biodiversity.
history_significance: Home to key threatened species like the Amazon manatee, black caiman, and two species of river dolphins.
highlights: Jaú National Park and the flooded forests (igapó).

name: Pantanal Conservation Area
overview: The Pantanal consists of a cluster of four protected areas with a total area of 187,818 ha. It is part of one of the world's largest freshwater wetland ecosystems.
history_significance: Critical habitat for the jaguar, giant anteater, and thousands of bird species.
highlights: The seasonal flooding and wildlife watching opportunities.

name: Brazilian Atlantic Islands: Fernando de Noronha and Atol das Rocas Reserves
overview: The peaks of the Southern Atlantic submarine ridge form the Fernando de Noronha Archipelago and Rocas Atoll.
history_significance: Key site for the breeding of tuna, sharks, turtles, and marine mammals. Baía dos Golfinhos has an exceptional population of resident dolphins.
highlights: Baía do Sancho (often voted world's best beach) and the Spinner Dolphins.

name: Cerrado Protected Areas: Chapada dos Veadeiros and Emas National Parks
overview: These two parks characterize the Cerrado, one of the world's oldest and most diverse tropical ecosystems.
history_significance: Contains flora and fauna critical for maintaining the biodiversity of the Cerrado biome.
highlights: Moon Valley (Vale da Lua) in Chapada dos Veadeiros and termite mounds in Emas.

name: Historic Centre of the Town of Goiás
overview: Goiás testifies to the occupation and colonization of the lands of central Brazil in the 18th and 19th centuries.
history_significance: A fine example of an organic mining settlement adapted to the constraints of the local topography.
highlights: The Cora Coralina House and the simple vernacular architecture.

name: São Francisco Square in the Town of São Cristóvão
overview: São Francisco Square is a quadrilateral open space surrounded by monumental buildings such as the Church and Convent of São Francisco.
history_significance: A rare example in Brazil of Spanish architectural influence (during the Iberian Union) merged with Portuguese styles.
highlights: The Church and Convent of São Francisco and the monumental square layout.

name: Rio de Janeiro: Carioca Landscapes between the Mountain and the Sea
overview: The site consists of an exceptional urban setting encompassing the natural elements that have shaped the development of the city.
history_significance: Shows the artistic inspiration provided by the landscape to musicians, landscapers, and urbanists.
highlights: Christ the Redeemer (Corcovado), Sugarloaf Mountain, and the Botanical Garden.

name: Pampulha Modern Ensemble
overview: A garden city project created in 1940 at Belo Horizonte. Designed by Oscar Niemeyer, in collaboration with landscape architect Roberto Burle Marx.
history_significance: Represents the first large-scale expression of the modern architectural language in Brazil.
highlights: Church of Saint Francis of Assisi and the artificial lake.

name: Valongo Wharf Archaeological Site
overview: The Valongo Wharf is the site of the former harbour of Rio de Janeiro. It was the main landing point for enslaved Africans arriving in South America.
history_significance: The most significant physical trace of the arrival of African slaves on the American continent.
highlights: The archaeological remains of the stone wharf.

name: Paraty and Ilha Grande – Culture and Biodiversity
overview: Located between the Serra da Bocaina mountain range and the Atlantic Ocean, this mixed site includes the historic centre of Paraty and the Ilha Grande island.
history_significance: Combines the well-preserved colonial architecture of Paraty (a key gold route port) with high biodiversity.
highlights: Historic Centre of Paraty and the pristine beaches of Ilha Grande.

name: Sítio Roberto Burle Marx
overview: The site is a 'landscape laboratory' where landscape architect Roberto Burle Marx created over 3,500 species of plants.
history_significance: The first modern tropical garden, influencing garden design worldwide.
highlights: The diverse botanical collection and modernist landscape design.

name: Lençóis Maranhenses National Park
overview: Located on the eastern coast of Maranhão state, this park is known for its vast white sand dunes and seasonal rainwater lagoons.
history_significance: Offers exceptional scenic beauty and important geological processes of dune formation and movement.
highlights: The Blue Lagoon (Lagoa Azul) and Bonita Lagoon.

name: Cavernas do Peruaçu National Park
overview: Located in the São Francisco River valley, this park features spectacular karst canyons, monumental caves with immense entrance arches, and the perennial Peruaçu River.
history_significance: It houses some of the most impressive prehistoric rock art in the world, dating back over 11,000 years (São Francisco style), testifying to ancient human occupation in South America.
highlights: Gruta do Janelão (massive entrance), 'Perna da Bailarina' (world's largest stalactite), and the colorful rock paintings of Lapa do Rezar.

name: Historic Areas of Istanbul
overview: Strategically located on the Bosphorus peninsula between the Balkans and Anatolia, Istanbul was the capital of the Eastern Roman and Ottoman Empires. The site includes the Hippodrome, Hagia Sophia, and Topkapi Palace.
history_significance: A testament to the city's role as a political, religious, and artistic center for over 2,000 years, bridging Europe and Asia.
highlights: Hagia Sophia, Blue Mosque, Topkapi Palace, and the City Walls.

name: Göreme National Park and the Rock Sites of Cappadocia
overview: Located in a spectacular landscape entirely sculpted by erosion, the Göreme valley contains rock-hewn sanctuaries that provide unique evidence of Byzantine art.
history_significance: Illustrates a complex monastic settlement dating back to the 4th century, blending natural formations with human habitation.
highlights: Fairy Chimneys, Underground Cities (Derinkuyu/Kaymakli), and the Dark Church.

name: Great Mosque and Hospital of Divriği
overview: Built in the 13th century by Emir Ahmed Shah, this complex consists of a mosque and a hospital. It is renowned for its sophisticated technique of vault construction and creative stone carving.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Islamic architecture in Anatolia, notable for its unique three-dimensional ornamentation.
highlights: The elaborate stone portals and the ribbed vaults.

locations.name: Great Mosque and Hospital of Divriği

name: Hattusha: the Hittite Capital
overview: The archaeological site of Hattusha, former capital of the Hittite Empire, is notable for its urban organization, fortifications, and rock art.
history_significance: The center of the Hittite civilization (2nd millennium BC), which rivaled ancient Egypt.
highlights: The Lion Gate, Royal Gate, and the Yazılıkaya rock sanctuary.

name: Nemrut Dağ
overview: The mausoleum of Antiochus I (69–34 B.C.), who reigned over Commagene, is one of the most ambitious constructions of the Hellenistic period.
history_significance: Reflects the dual cultural origin (Greek and Persian) of the Commagene kingdom through its colossal statues.
highlights: The colossal stone heads of gods and the summit tumulus.

name: Hierapolis-Pamukkale
overview: Deriving from springs in a cliff almost 200 m high overlooking the plain, calcite-laden waters have created an unreal landscape of mineral forests and terraced basins.
history_significance: The thermal spa of Hierapolis was established at the end of the 2nd century B.C., serving as a major healing center.
highlights: The white travertines (Cotton Castle) and the Roman theatre of Hierapolis.

name: Xanthos-Letoon
overview: This site represents the blending of Lycian traditions and Hellenic influence. Xanthos was the capital of Lycia, while Letoon was its sacred cult center.
history_significance: Contains the longest and most important texts in the Lycian language, crucial for deciphering the ancient tongue.
highlights: The Nereid Monument (remains), Harpy Tomb, and the Letoon temples.

name: City of Safranbolu
overview: From the 13th century to the advent of the railway, Safranbolu was an important caravan station. Its architecture influenced urban development throughout the Ottoman Empire.
history_significance: A typical Ottoman city with preserved traditional houses, mosques, and baths.
highlights: The Ottoman timber-framed houses and the Cinci Han.

name: Archaeological Site of Troy
overview: Troy, with its 4,000 years of history, is one of the most famous archaeological sites in the world. Its rediscovery by Schliemann began modern archaeology.
history_significance: The setting for Homer's Iliad and the Trojan War, revealing 9 distinct layers of settlements.
highlights: The replica Trojan Horse and the defensive walls of Troy VI.

name: Selimiye Mosque and its Social Complex
overview: Built by the famous architect Mimar Sinan between 1569 and 1575, this square mosque with its single great dome and four slender minarets dominates the skyline.
history_significance: Considered by Sinan himself to be his masterpiece, representing the peak of Ottoman architecture.
highlights: The massive central dome and the intricate Iznik tile decorations.

name: Neolithic Site of Çatalhöyük
overview: Two hills form this site in Southern Anatolia. The taller eastern mound contains eighteen levels of Neolithic occupation between 7400 BC and 6200 BC.
history_significance: Provides unique evidence of the evolution from settled villages to urban agglomeration, with no streets (roof access).
highlights: The wall paintings, reliefs, and the reconstructed Neolithic house.

name: Bursa and Cumalıkızık: the Birth of the Ottoman Empire
overview: This property illustrates the creation of an urban and rural system establishing the Ottoman Empire in the early 14th century.
history_significance: The first capital of the Ottoman Empire, featuring the innovative 'kulliye' system of public buildings.
highlights: The Green Tomb (Yeşil Türbe), Grand Mosque (Ulu Cami), and Cumalıkızık village.

name: Pergamon and its Multi-Layered Cultural Landscape
overview: Pergamon rose to prominence in the Hellenistic period as the capital of the Attalid dynasty. The site includes the Acropolis, the Asclepion, and the Roman city.
history_significance: A major center of learning and medicine in the ancient world, home to the second largest library after Alexandria.
highlights: The Trajan Temple, the steep Theatre, and the Altar of Zeus (foundations).

name: Diyarbakır Fortress and Hevsel Gardens Cultural Landscape
overview: Located on the Upper Tigris River Basin, the fortified city of Diyarbakır and the surrounding landscape have been an important center since the Hellenistic period.
history_significance: The 5.8 km long city walls are among the longest and widest defensive walls in the world.
highlights: The City Walls (Surları) and the Hevsel Gardens.

name: Ephesus
overview: Located near the mouth of the Cayster River, Ephesus comprises successive Hellenistic and Roman settlements. It was one of the most important port cities of the ancient world.
history_significance: Home to the Temple of Artemis (one of the Seven Wonders) and the Library of Celsus.
highlights: Library of Celsus, Great Theatre, and the Temple of Artemis.

locations.name: Ayasuluk Hill (Castle & Basilica of St. John)

name: Archaeological Site of Ani
overview: This medieval city combines residential, religious, and military structures, built over centuries by Christian and Muslim dynasties. It was the capital of the Bagratid Armenian kingdom.
history_significance: A major stop on the Silk Road, known as the 'City of 1001 Churches'.
highlights: The Cathedral of Ani, Church of St. Gregory, and the Menüçer Mosque.

name: Aphrodisias
overview: Located in the upper valley of the Morsynus River, the site consists of two components: the archaeological site of Aphrodisias and the marble quarries northeast of the city.
history_significance: Famous for its sculpture school and the Temple of Aphrodite.
highlights: The Tetrapylon, the Stadium (one of the best preserved), and the Sebasteion.

name: Göbekli Tepe
overview: Located in the Germuş mountains, this property presents monumental round-oval and rectangular megalithic structures erected by hunter-gatherers between 9600 and 8200 BC.
history_significance: Regarded as the world's oldest known temple, changing our understanding of the dawn of civilization.
highlights: The T-shaped pillars with animal reliefs.

name: Arslantepe Mound
overview: Arslantepe is a 30-meter-tall archaeological mound located in the Malatya plain. It testifies to the earliest forms of state formation and bureaucracy.
history_significance: Known for the oldest known swords in the world and its mudbrick palace complex.
highlights: The Palace complex and the Royal Tomb.

name: Gordion
overview: Gordion was the political and cultural capital of Phrygia. The site includes the Citadel Mound and over 100 tumuli (burial mounds) in the surrounding landscape.
history_significance: Famous for the legend of the Gordian Knot and King Midas. It has a continuous settlement history of 4,500 years.
highlights: The Midas Tumulus (Great Tumulus) and the Early Phrygian Gate.

name: Wooden Hypostyle Mosques of Medieval Anatolia
overview: A serial property comprising five mosques built between the late 13th and mid-14th centuries. They feature a unique wooden hypostyle system with intricate woodcarving.
history_significance: Represents a distinct architectural typology of the Seljuk and early Ottoman periods.
highlights: Eşrefoğlu Mosque (largest), Afyon Grand Mosque, and their painted woodwork.

name: Ancient City of Sardis and the Lydian Tumuli of Bin Tepe
overview: Sardis was the capital of the Lydian Kingdom, an Iron Age civilization known for inventing metallic coinage. The site includes the ancient city and the massive Bin Tepe necropolis (royal mounds).
history_significance: A melting pot of cultures and a major political hub. It houses one of the largest synagogues of the ancient world and the monumental Temple of Artemis.
highlights: Temple of Artemis, the Gymnasium-Bath Complex, the Synagogue, and the Bin Tepe Tumuli.

name: Great Barrier Reef
overview: The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, composed of over 2,900 individual reefs and 900 islands stretching for over 2,300 kilometers.
history_significance: A globally outstanding example of an ecosystem that has evolved over millennia. It contains the world's largest collection of coral reefs, with 400 types of coral and 1,500 species of fish.
highlights: Heart Reef, Whitehaven Beach, and the rich marine biodiversity.

name: Kakadu National Park
overview: Kakadu is a unique archaeological and ethnological reserve in the Northern Territory. It has been inhabited continuously for more than 40,000 years.
history_significance: Features cave paintings, rock carvings, and archaeological sites that record the skills and way of life of the region's inhabitants, from the hunter-gatherers of prehistoric times to the Aboriginal people still living there.
highlights: Ubirr Rock Art, Jim Jim Falls, and Yellow Water Billabong.

name: Willandra Lakes Region
overview: The fossil remains of a series of lakes and sand formations that date from the Pleistocene period, together with archaeological evidence of human occupation dating back 45-60,000 years.
history_significance: Site of the discovery of Mungo Man and Mungo Lady, the oldest human remains found in Australia.
highlights: Walls of China (lunettes) and Lake Mungo.

name: Lord Howe Island Group
overview: An outstanding example of an island group formed from submarine volcanic activity. It has a spectacular landscape and unique endemic species like the Lord Howe Woodhen.
history_significance: Home to the world's southernmost coral reef and provides a rare example of the transition between tropical and temperate marine environments.
highlights: Ball's Pyramid (sea stack) and Mount Gower.

name: Tasmanian Wilderness
overview: Covering over 1 million hectares, this site constitutes one of the last expanses of temperate rainforest in the world.
history_significance: Contains limestone caves with evidence of human occupation dating back 20,000 years and is a stronghold for Gondwanan flora.
highlights: Cradle Mountain, Lake St Clair, and the Franklin River.

name: Gondwana Rainforests of Australia
overview: This serial property comprises 41 reserves along the Great Escarpment of eastern Australia. It contains the world's most extensive subtropical rainforest areas and the largest remaining areas of warm temperate rainforest and Antarctic beech cool temperate rainforest.
history_significance: An outstanding record of the major stages of the earth's evolutionary history. The site protects ancient lineages of flowering plants that trace their origins back to the supercontinent of Gondwana (over 100 million years ago).
highlights: Lamington National Park, Springbrook's waterfalls, the Tweed Volcano caldera (Wollumbin), and the Antarctic Beech forests of Barrington Tops.

name: Uluru-Kata Tjuta National Park
overview: This park features the spectacular geological formations of Uluru (Ayers Rock) and Kata Tjuta (The Olgas). It is the spiritual heart of the Anangu people.
history_significance: A powerful cultural landscape reflecting the Anangu's relationship with the land and the 'Tjukurpa' (Dreamtime) laws.
highlights: Uluru base walk, Kata Tjuta (Valley of the Winds), and the Cultural Centre.

name: Wet Tropics of Queensland
overview: Stretching along the northeast coast of Australia, this area provides the only habitat for numerous rare and endangered species of plants and animals.
history_significance: Contains the highest concentration of primitive flowering plant families in the world, a living record of the evolutionary history of plants.
highlights: Daintree Rainforest and the Cassowary population.

name: Shark Bay, Western Australia
overview: Shark Bay contains the largest and richest sea-grass beds in the world and is famous for its stromatolites (living fossils).
history_significance: The stromatolites in Hamelin Pool represent the oldest form of life on Earth (3.5 billion years old).
highlights: Hamelin Pool Stromatolites, Shell Beach, and the Monkey Mia dolphins.

name: K'gari (Fraser Island)
overview: K'gari is the world's largest sand island, stretching over 120 km. It features majestic rainforests growing on sand dunes and perched dune lakes.
history_significance: An outstanding example of ongoing biological and geological processes, including the formation of extensive dune systems.
highlights: Lake McKenzie, Maheno Shipwreck, and 75 Mile Beach.

name: Australian Fossil Mammal Sites (Riversleigh / Naracoorte)
overview: These two sites, located 2,000 km apart, provide an outstanding record of the evolution of the unique Australian mammal fauna.
history_significance: Riversleigh (Oligocene/Miocene) and Naracoorte (Pleistocene) reveal the history of marsupials, including the Marsupial Lion.
highlights: Riversleigh D Site and Naracoorte Caves (Victoria Fossil Cave).

name: Heard Island and McDonald Islands
overview: Located in the Southern Ocean, these islands are the only volcanically active sub-Antarctic islands. They are practically undisturbed by humans.
history_significance: Home to Big Ben (Mawson Peak), an active volcano, and massive populations of penguins and seals.
highlights: Mawson Peak and the pristine sub-Antarctic ecosystem.

name: Macquarie Island
overview: A sub-Antarctic island located about halfway between Australia and Antarctica. It is the only place on earth where rocks from the earth's mantle are actively being exposed above sea level.
history_significance: A site of major geoconservation significance and a breeding ground for the Royal Penguin.
highlights: The exposed ophiolite geology and the Royal Penguin colonies.

name: Greater Blue Mountains Area
overview: A sandstone plateau deeply dissected by gorges and covered by eucalypt forest. It consists of eight protected areas.
history_significance: Represents the evolutionary adaptation and diversification of the eucalypts in post-Gondwana isolation.
highlights: The Three Sisters, Wentworth Falls, and the Wollemi Pine discovery site.

name: Purnululu National Park
overview: Famous for the Bungle Bungle Range, a spectacular landscape of sculpted rocks with orange and black stripes.
history_significance: The world's finest example of a cone karst landscape in sandstone.
highlights: The Bungle Bungles, Cathedral Gorge, and Echidna Chasm.

name: Royal Exhibition Building and Carlton Gardens
overview: Built for the Great International Exhibition of 1880, this building and its surrounding gardens are a surviving manifestation of the international exhibition movement.
history_significance: One of the few remaining major 19th-century exhibition buildings in the world.
highlights: The Great Hall dome and the Victorian-era gardens.

name: Sydney Opera House
overview: Inaugurated in 1973, the Sydney Opera House is a masterpiece of 20th-century architecture designed by Jørn Utzon.
history_significance: Its innovative design comprises three groups of interlocking vaulted 'shells' and represents a daring visionary experiment.
highlights: The white ceramic tiled shells and the Concert Hall.

name: Australian Convict Sites
overview: A selection of 11 penal sites that represent the forced migration of convicts to penal colonies in the 18th and 19th centuries. These sites illustrate the diverse aspects of the convict experience in Australia.
history_significance: Testifies to the large-scale convict transportation and the colonial expansion of European powers through the presence and labour of convicts.
highlights: Port Arthur, Fremantle Prison, Hyde Park Barracks, and the Brickendon-Woolmers Estates.

name: Ningaloo Coast
overview: The Ningaloo Coast features one of the longest near-shore reefs in the world. It is also famous for its terrestrial karst system and subterranean fauna.
history_significance: Provides a rare opportunity to swim with Whale Sharks and boasts a high diversity of marine life.
highlights: Ningaloo Reef (Whale Sharks) and Cape Range National Park.

name: Budj Bim Cultural Landscape
overview: Located in the traditional Country of the Gunditjmara people, this site features the world's oldest aquaculture system, dating back 6,600 years.
history_significance: Demonstrates the sophisticated engineering skills of the Gunditjmara in manipulating water flows to trap eels (kooyang).
highlights: Lake Condah and the ancient stone eel traps.

name: Murujuga Cultural Landscape
overview: Located on the Burrup Peninsula (Murujuga) and surrounding Dampier Archipelago in Western Australia, this cultural landscape contains one of the world's largest known concentrations of petroglyphs, created over tens of thousands of years.
history_significance: The rock art of Murujuga records spiritual beliefs, fauna (including now-extinct species), and changing environments over up to 50,000 years, representing an outstanding testimony to the living cultural traditions of the Ngarda-Ngarli peoples.
highlights: Dense fields of petroglyphs depicting animals and ancestral beings, the dramatic coastal rock formations of the Burrup Peninsula, and views over the Dampier Archipelago.

name: Temple of Apollo Epicurius at Bassae
overview: This famous temple to the god of healing and the sun was built towards the middle of the 5th century B.C. in the lonely heights of the Arcadian mountains.
history_significance: A masterpiece of ancient Greek architecture, combining the Archaic style with the Doric, Ionic, and Corinthian orders.
highlights: The oldest Corinthian capital ever found and the unique frieze.

name: Archaeological Site of Mystras
overview: Known as the 'Wonder of the Morea', this fortress city was built in 1249. It served as the capital of the Byzantine Despotate of the Morea and was a center of Byzantine power and culture.
history_significance: The last center of Byzantine scholarship and art before the fall of the empire, influencing the Italian Renaissance.
highlights: The Palace of the Despots, Pantanassa Monastery, and the Peribleptos Monastery frescoes.

name: Acropolis, Athens
overview: The Acropolis of Athens and its monuments are universal symbols of the classical spirit and civilization and form the greatest architectural and artistic complex bequeathed by Greek Antiquity to the world.
history_significance: Illustrates the civilizations, myths, and religions that flourished in Greece over a period of more than 1,000 years.
highlights: The Parthenon, the Erechtheion, and the Propylaea.

name: Archaeological Site of Delphi
overview: The pan-Hellenic sanctuary of Delphi, where the oracle of Apollo spoke, was the site of the omphalos, the 'navel of the world'.
history_significance: The religious center of the ancient Greek world and a symbol of unity, influencing colonization and wars.
highlights: The Temple of Apollo, the Ancient Theatre, and the Tholos of Delphi.

name: Sanctuary of Asklepios at Epidaurus
overview: In a small valley in the Peloponnesus, the shrine of Asklepios, the god of medicine, developed out of a much earlier cult of Apollo.
history_significance: Contains the finest and best-preserved ancient theater in Greece, renowned for its exceptional acoustics.
highlights: The Theatre of Epidaurus, the Tholos, and the Abaton.

name: Medieval City of Rhodes
overview: The Order of St John of Jerusalem occupied Rhodes from 1309 to 1523 and set about transforming the city into a stronghold. It subsequently came under Turkish and Italian rule.
history_significance: One of the most beautiful urban ensembles of the Gothic period, with the Palace of the Grand Masters and the Street of the Knights.
highlights: Palace of the Grand Master and the Street of the Knights.

name: Meteora
overview: In a region of almost inaccessible sandstone peaks, monks settled on these 'columns of the sky' from the 11th century onwards.
history_significance: A unique blend of natural beauty and Byzantine monastic architecture, preserving 16th-century frescoes.
highlights: Monastery of Great Meteoron, Varlaam Monastery, and the towering rock pillars.

name: Mount Athos
overview: An Orthodox spiritual center since 1054, Mount Athos has enjoyed an autonomous statute since Byzantine times. The 'Holy Mountain' is forbidden to women and children.
history_significance: A conservatory of vernacular architecture and agricultural traditions, influencing Orthodox art worldwide.
highlights: Simonopetra Monastery and the 20 major monasteries.

name: Paleochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessalonika
overview: Founded in 315 B.C., Thessalonika was one of the first bases for the spread of Christianity. The site includes churches and city walls.
history_significance: Contains fine examples of mosaics and wall paintings from the early Christian and Byzantine periods.
highlights: Rotunda of Galerius, Church of St. Demetrios, and the White Tower.

name: Archaeological Site of Olympia
overview: The site of Olympia, in a valley in the Peloponnesus, has been inhabited since prehistoric times. In the 10th century B.C., Olympia became a center for the worship of Zeus.
history_significance: The birthplace of the Olympic Games (776 B.C.), featuring the massive Temple of Zeus which housed one of the Seven Wonders.
highlights: The Ancient Stadium, Temple of Zeus, and the Palaestra.

name: Delos
overview: According to Greek mythology, Apollo was born on this tiny island in the Cyclades archipelago. Delos was a major religious center and port.
history_significance: A unique archaeological site that conveys the image of a great cosmopolitan Mediterranean port.
highlights: Terrace of the Lions, House of Dionysus, and the Temple of Isis.

name: Monasteries of Daphni, Hosios Loukas and Nea Moni of Chios
overview: Although geographically distant from each other, these three monasteries belong to the same typological series and share the same aesthetic characteristics.
history_significance: Famous for their 11th and 12th-century gold mosaics, representing the 'second golden age' of Byzantine art.
highlights: The intricate gold mosaics of Daphni and Hosios Loukas.

name: Pythagoreion and Heraion of Samos
overview: Many civilisations have inhabited this small Aegean island. It was the home of Pythagoras and Epicurus.
history_significance: Features the Eupalinos Tunnel (an ancient aqueduct) and the Heraion, a massive temple dedicated to Hera.
highlights: Tunnel of Eupalinos and the single standing column of the Heraion.

name: Archaeological Site of Aigai (Vergina)
overview: The city of Aigai, the ancient first capital of the Kingdom of Macedonia, was discovered in the 19th century.
history_significance: Site of the royal tombs, including the tomb of Philip II, father of Alexander the Great, with exceptional gold finds.
highlights: The Museum of the Royal Tombs of Aigai and the Palace ruins.

name: Archaeological Sites of Mycenae and Tiryns
overview: These two cities flourished between the 15th and 12th centuries B.C. and gave their name to the Mycenaean civilization.
history_significance: Home to the Lion Gate and the Treasury of Atreus, linked to the Homeric epics (Agamemnon).
highlights: Lion Gate, Treasury of Atreus (Tomb of Agamemnon), and Cyclopean Walls of Tiryns.

name: The Historic Centre (Chorá) with the Monastery of Saint-John the Theologian and the Cave of the Apocalypse
overview: The small island of Pátmos in the Dodecanese is where St John the Theologian wrote both his Gospel and the Apocalypse.
history_significance: A major pilgrimage site featuring a fortress-like monastery dominating the island.
highlights: Cave of the Apocalypse and the fortified Monastery of Saint John.

name: Old Town of Corfu
overview: The Old Town of Corfu, on the Island of Corfu off the western coasts of Albania and Greece, is located in a strategic position at the entrance of the Adriatic Sea.
history_significance: A fortified Mediterranean port city with a distinct Venetian influence, defended by three forts.
highlights: The Old Fortress, the New Fortress, and the Spianada square.

name: Archaeological Site of Philippi
overview: Founded in 356 BC by King Philip II of Macedon, the city developed as a 'small Rome' with the establishment of the Roman Empire.
history_significance: The site where the Apostle Paul established the first Christian church in Europe (49-50 AD).
highlights: The Forum, the Theatre, and the Basilicas.

name: Zagori Cultural Landscape
overview: Located in the Pindus mountains in Epirus, Zagori is a cluster of stone villages (Zagorochoria) nestled in a spectacular natural landscape.
history_significance: Renowned for its traditional stone architecture, arched stone bridges, and the Vikos Gorge.
highlights: Vikos Gorge, Stone Bridges (e.g., Kokkori), and Papigo village.

name: Minoan Palatial Centres
overview: A serial property comprising the major administrative, religious, and economic centers of the Minoan civilization (Bronze Age). These palatial centers are architectural masterpieces known for their complex structures, water supply systems, and exquisite art.
history_significance: Represents the first literate civilization in Europe (Minoan), dating back to the 2nd millennium BC. It testifies to a sophisticated social structure and maritime power.
highlights: Palace of Knossos (Labyrinth legend), Palace of Phaistos (Phaistos Disc), and Malia.

name: Central Zone of the Town of Angra do Heroísmo in the Azores
