overview: Located at the foot of Mount Song, this cluster of monuments includes the Shaolin Monastery and the Gaocheng Astronomical Observatory.
history_significance: Reflects the ancient concept of the 'Centre of Heaven and Earth' and the power of the emperor.
highlights: Shaolin Temple (Pagoda Forest) and Songyue Pagoda.

name: China Danxia
overview: China Danxia is the name given to landscapes developed on continental red terrigenous sedimentary beds influenced by endogenous forces and weathering.
history_significance: Characterized by spectacular red cliffs and erosional landforms.
highlights: Mount Danxia, Taining, and Langshan.

name: West Lake Cultural Landscape of Hangzhou
overview: West Lake has inspired famous poets, scholars, and artists since the 9th century. It comprises numerous temples, pagodas, pavilions, gardens, and ornamental trees.
history_significance: An ideal model of landscape design in China, influencing garden design in the rest of East Asia.
highlights: Su Causeway, Leifeng Pagoda, and the Three Pools Mirroring the Moon.

name: Site of Xanadu
overview: The site of Xanadu was the first capital of Kublai Khan's Yuan dynasty. It represents the fusion of Mongolian nomadic and Han Chinese agrarian cultures.
history_significance: The legendary city described by Marco Polo.
highlights: Remains of the Imperial City and the Palace City.

name: Chengjiang Fossil Site
overview: A fossil site located in the Yunnan province, presenting one of the most complete records of an early Cambrian marine community.
history_significance: Provides evidence of the 'Cambrian Explosion' of life 530 million years ago.
highlights: Maotianshan Shales Museum.

name: Xinjiang Tianshan
overview: The site comprises four components (Tomur, Kalajun-Kuerdening, Bayinbukuke, and Bogda) within the Tianshan mountain range.
history_significance: Features spectacular snow-capped peaks, glacier-capped peaks, forests, meadows, and red bed canyons.
highlights: Tomur Peak and the Bayinbuluke Grassland.

name: Cultural Landscape of Honghe Hani Rice Terraces
overview: The Hani people have cultivated rice terraces on the steep slopes of the Ailao Mountains for over 1,300 years.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a resilient land management system that optimizes water resources.
highlights: Yuanyang Rice Terraces.

name: The Grand Canal
overview: The Grand Canal is the world's longest artificial waterway, linking Beijing in the north to Hangzhou in the south.
history_significance: A masterpiece of hydraulic engineering that facilitated the transport of grain and strategic raw materials for nearly 2,000 years.
highlights: Tongzhou Canal (Beijing), Slender West Lake (Yangzhou), and Gongchen Bridge (Hangzhou).

name: Silk Roads: the Routes Network of Chang'an-Tianshan Corridor
overview: This 5,000 km section of the Silk Road network stretches from Chang'an/Luoyang to the Zhetysu region of Central Asia. China has 22 sites.
history_significance: A major route for trade, religious exchange, and cultural interaction.
highlights: Big Wild Goose Pagoda (Xi'an), Maijishan Grottoes, and Bingling Temple.

name: Tusi Sites
overview: The Tusi sites are the remains of tribal domains whose leaders were appointed by the central government as 'Tusi' (hereditary rulers) from the 13th to the early 20th century.
history_significance: Testifies to the unique system of administration of ethnic minorities in southwest China.
highlights: Laosicheng, Tangya, and Hailongtun.

name: Zuojiang Huashan Rock Art Cultural Landscape
overview: A vast array of rock art painted on limestone cliffs along the Zuojiang River, dating from the 5th century BC to the 2nd century AD.
history_significance: Illustrates the life and rituals of the Luoyue people.
highlights: The giant red frog-like figures on the cliff faces.

name: Hubei Shennongjia
overview: Located in the Shennongjia Forestry District, the site protects the largest primary forests remaining in Central China.
history_significance: A major center of biodiversity and home to the Golden Snub-nosed Monkey.
highlights: Shennong Ding (Roof of Central China).

name: Qinghai Hoh Xil
overview: Located on the Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau, it is the largest and highest plateau in the world. It is an extensive area of alpine mountains and steppe systems.
history_significance: Crucial for the migration of the Tibetan antelope.
highlights: Vast untouched wilderness and wildlife.

name: Kulangsu, a Historic International Settlement
overview: Kulangsu (Gulangyu) is a tiny island off the coast of Xiamen. Following the First Opium War, it became an international settlement in 1903.
history_significance: An exceptional example of cultural fusion in architecture (Amoy Deco style) and a center for music.
highlights: Sunlight Rock, Shuzhuang Garden, and the Organ Museum.

name: Fanjingshan
overview: Fanjingshan is the highest peak of the Wuling Mountains. It is an island of metamorphic rock in a sea of karst.
history_significance: Home to many endemic species like the Fanjingshan Fir and the Guizhou Snub-nosed Monkey.
highlights: The Mushroom Stone and the Red Clouds Golden Summit.

name: Archaeological Ruins of Liangzhu City
overview: The ruins of the ancient city of Liangzhu (c. 3300-2300 BCE) provide a profound understanding of a sophisticated early state with complex social structures and a massive hydraulic system.
history_significance: Testifies to the development of the Yangtze River Basin civilization, representing a key stage of early China.
highlights: The large artificial earth mounds, internal river network, and jade artifacts.

name: Migratory Bird Sanctuaries along the Coast of Yellow Sea-Bohai Gulf of China (Phase I)
overview: Features the world's largest intertidal mudflat system. It is a vital hub for the East Asian-Australasian Flyway.
history_significance: Critical habitat for endangered species like the Spoon-billed Sandpiper.
highlights: Yancheng Nature Reserve.

name: Quanzhou: Emporium of the World in Song-Yuan China
overview: Quanzhou was one of the largest ports in the world along the maritime Silk Road, particularly between the 10th and 14th centuries.
history_significance: Illustrates the vibrancy of the city as a maritime emporium and the interchange of cultures and religions.
highlights: Kaiyuan Temple, Qingjing Mosque, and Luoyang Bridge.

name: Cultural Landscape of Old Tea Forests of the Jingmai Mountain in Pu'er
overview: A cultural landscape developed over a thousand years by the Blang and Dai peoples, featuring ancient tea groves, traditional villages, and protective forests.
history_significance: Testifies to the traditional under-storey tea cultivation method.
highlights: Jingmai Ancient Tea Plantation and traditional villages like Wengji.

name: Beijing Central Axis: A Building Ensemble Exhibiting the Ideal Order of the Chinese Capital
overview: The Beijing Central Axis is an ensemble of palaces, gardens, sacrificial structures, and ceremonial buildings running north-south through the heart of Beijing.
history_significance: Represents the ideal paradigm of Chinese capital planning and the ritual order of the state.
highlights: Drum and Bell Towers, Jingshan Hill, and the Tian'anmen Square complex.

locations.name: Altar of the God of Land and Grain (Zhongshan Park)

name: Badain Jaran Desert - Towers of Sand and Lakes
overview: Located in the Alxa Plateau, this desert features the world's tallest stabilized sand dunes (mega-dunes) and numerous inter-dunal lakes.
history_significance: Displays outstanding geological features and ongoing aeolian processes.
highlights: Bilutu Peak (highest sand dune) and Badain Lake.

name: Xixia Imperial Tombs
overview: The Xixia Imperial Tombs are the royal burial grounds of the Western Xia dynasty (1038–1227), located at the eastern foot of the Helan Mountains near Yinchuan in Ningxia.
history_significance: The complex comprises the mausoleums of Western Xia emperors and over 200 subordinate tombs, illustrating the political power, funerary traditions, and cultural identity of a once powerful empire on the Silk Road.
highlights: The main imperial tumuli, rammed-earth pyramid-like mounds, and the remains of palace and sacrificial structures set against the Helan Mountains.

name: Aachen Cathedral
overview: Aachen Cathedral is one of the oldest cathedrals in Europe. It was the place where 30 German kings were crowned over a period of 600 years.
history_significance: The palace chapel of Charlemagne, completed around 800 A.D., symbolizing the Holy Roman Empire.
highlights: The Palatine Chapel and the Shrine of Charlemagne.

name: Speyer Cathedral
overview: Speyer Cathedral is the largest Romanesque church in the world. It was the burial place of the German Emperors for almost 300 years.
history_significance: A major monument of Romanesque art and architecture in Europe.
highlights: The vast nave, the four towers, and the crypt.

name: Würzburg Residence with the Court Gardens and Residence Square
overview: A magnificent Baroque palace built between 1720 and 1744. It is one of the most homogenous Baroque palaces in Germany.
history_significance: A masterpiece of German Baroque, known for its lavish interior decoration.
highlights: The Imperial Hall, the Grand Staircase, and the largest fresco ever painted (by Tiepolo).

name: Pilgrimage Church of Wies
overview: Located in an alpine valley, this oval church was designed by Dominikus Zimmermann. It is a perfect example of Rococo art.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Bavarian Rococo, attracting millions of pilgrims.
highlights: The elaborate frescoes and the high altar.

name: Castles of Augustusburg and Falkenlust at Brühl
overview: These two palaces were built for the Archbishop-Elector of Cologne in the 18th century. They are masterpieces of Rococo architecture.
history_significance: Outstanding examples of the Rococo style in Germany, with gardens designed by Dominique Girard.
highlights: The Grand Staircase of Augustusburg Palace and Falkenlust hunting lodge.

name: St Mary's Cathedral and St Michael's Church at Hildesheim
overview: St Mary's Cathedral (11th–14th centuries) and St Michael's Church (11th century) are masterpieces of Romanesque art and architecture.
history_significance: They preserve magnificent examples of Ottonian art, including the bronze doors and the Bernward Column.
highlights: The Bernward Doors, the Rose Bush, and the wooden ceiling of St Michael's.

name: Roman Monuments, Cathedral of St Peter and Church of Our Lady in Trier
overview: Trier was the 'Second Rome' and capital of the Western Roman Empire. The city contains exceptional Roman monuments, including the Porta Nigra and the Imperial Baths.
history_significance: A remarkable collection of Roman and early Christian monuments, reflecting its role as a powerful Roman city.
highlights: Porta Nigra, Imperial Baths (Kaiserthermen), and the Amphitheater.

name: Hanseatic City of Lübeck
overview: Founded in the 12th century, Lübeck was the capital of the Hanseatic League. It is famous for its Gothic brick architecture (Backsteingotik).
history_significance: The leading centre for the Hanseatic League, symbolizing the spread of trade and cultural influence in the Baltic region.
highlights: Holstentor Gate, St Mary's Church, and the historic brick architecture.

name: Palaces and Parks of Potsdam and Berlin
overview: With 500 ha of parks and 150 buildings constructed between 1730 and 1916, this complex forms an artistic whole, whose eclectic nature reinforces its sense of uniqueness.
history_significance: A masterpiece of landscape design and architecture, reflecting the power and taste of the Prussian kings.
highlights: Sanssouci Palace, the New Palace, and Cecilienhof.

name: Abbey and Altenmünster of Lorsch
overview: The Carolingian Gate Hall is one of the few surviving architectural pieces of the Carolingian period.
history_significance: A major center of the Carolingian Empire and a cultural landmark in the 8th and 9th centuries.
highlights: The King's Hall (Torhall) and the monastery ruins.

name: Mines of Rammelsberg, Historic Town of Goslar and Upper Harz Water Management System
overview: Rammelsberg is the only mine in the world that was continuously in operation for over 1,000 years (968 to 1988). The historic town of Goslar is closely associated with the mine.
history_significance: Illustrates the history of mining and metallurgy in Europe. The water system is a complex hydrological network dating back to the 16th century.
highlights: Rammelsberg Mine Museum, Goslar Imperial Palace, and the water ditches.

name: Town of Bamberg
overview: Bamberg is an outstanding example of an early medieval town in Central Europe. It preserves its medieval street plan and more than 1,000 historic buildings.
history_significance: A major link with the Slav world, reaching its zenith in the 11th century under Bishop Henry II.
highlights: Bamberg Cathedral, Old Town Hall (Altes Rathaus), and 'Little Venice' (Klein Venedig).

name: Maulbronn Monastery Complex
overview: The best-preserved medieval Cistercian monastery complex north of the Alps. It houses every major architectural style from Romanesque to late Gothic.
history_significance: An outstanding example of Cistercian architecture and economic management.
highlights: The Paradise (vestibule), the monastery church, and the surrounding walls.

name: Collegiate Church, Castle and Old Town of Quedlinburg
overview: Quedlinburg was an important imperial center in the Middle Ages. It features a unique collection of half-timbered houses (Fachwerk) and a massive castle complex.
history_significance: The city became a focal point of the Ottonian dynasty in the 10th century.
highlights: The Renaissance-style City Hall, the half-timbered houses, and the Collegiate Church of St Servatius.

name: Völklingen Ironworks
overview: The Völklingen Ironworks is the only fully preserved ironworks dating from the peak of the industrial era (19th and early 20th centuries).
history_significance: A key monument of European industrial history, now a museum.
highlights: The massive blast furnace group and the sintering plant.

name: Messel Pit Fossil Site
overview: Messel Pit is the richest site in the world for understanding the living environment of the Eocene, between 57 million and 36 million years ago.
history_significance: Provides unique information about the early stages of the evolution of mammals.
highlights: Fossils of early horses, bats, and other mammals.

name: Cologne Cathedral
overview: Construction began in 1248, but was not completed until 1880. It is a masterpiece of Gothic architecture, featuring two massive spires.
history_significance: A powerful testament to the strength and persistence of European Christian belief.
highlights: The Shrine of the Three Kings and the stained glass windows.

name: Bauhaus and its Sites in Weimar, Dessau and Bernau
overview: The Bauhaus school (1919–1933) revolutionized architectural and aesthetic concepts. The site includes buildings in Weimar, Dessau, and Bernau.
history_significance: Represents the core of the Modern Movement, integrating art, craft, and technology.
highlights: Bauhaus Building (Dessau) and the Masters' Houses (Dessau).

name: Luther Memorials in Eisleben and Wittenberg
overview: These sites are associated with the life of Martin Luther and his companion Melanchthon. They commemorate the beginning of the Protestant Reformation.
history_significance: A fundamental influence on the religious and political history of Europe and the world.
highlights: Luther's Birth and Death Houses (Eisleben) and Wittenberg Castle Church (95 Theses).

name: Classical Weimar
overview: The city of Weimar was the cultural hub of the Enlightenment in the 18th and early 19th centuries. It is associated with Goethe and Schiller.
history_significance: Represents the intellectual and artistic life that flourished during the Weimar Classicism period.
highlights: Goethe's Residence, Schiller's Residence, and the Duchess Anna Amalia Library.

locations.name: City Church of St Peter and Paul (Herder Church)

name: Wartburg Castle
overview: Wartburg Castle, perched on a hill, is a key fortress from the feudal period. It was here that Martin Luther translated the New Testament into German.
history_significance: A symbol of the history of Germany and the Reformation.
highlights: The Palas (main hall) and Luther's Room.

name: Museumsinsel (Museum Island), Berlin
overview: Museum Island is a unique ensemble of five museums built between 1824 and 1930 on an island in the River Spree.
history_significance: A prime example of the 19th-century vision of the 'museum as a public cultural institution'.
highlights: Pergamon Museum (Ishtar Gate), Neues Museum (Nefertiti Bust), and the Old National Gallery.

name: Garden Kingdom of Dessau-Wörlitz
overview: A vast cultural landscape created in the 18th century under the guidance of Prince Leopold III. It is one of the earliest and largest English-style parks in Germany.
history_significance: An early expression of the Enlightenment principles in landscape design, rejecting the strict Baroque style.
highlights: Wörlitz Palace, the Gothic House, and the numerous bridges.

name: Monastic Island of Reichenau
overview: Located on Lake Constance, this island was the site of a Benedictine monastery founded in 724 A.D. It preserves three magnificent Romanesque churches.
history_significance: A spiritual and cultural center of the Holy Roman Empire, famous for its wall paintings.
highlights: St George's Church (Ototnian frescoes), St Peter and St Paul, and St Mary and Marcus.

name: Zollverein Coal Mine Industrial Complex in Essen
overview: The Zollverein complex is an outstanding example of the development of heavy industry in Europe. It operated from 1851 until 1986.
history_significance: Its two sections (Mine XI and Coking Plant) feature the Cubist design of the Bauhaus school.
highlights: Shaft 12 (most beautiful mine in the world) and the Coking Plant.

name: Upper Middle Rhine Valley
overview: The 65 km stretch of the Rhine River, from Bingen to Koblenz, is a cultural landscape that has inspired writers, painters, and composers for centuries.
history_significance: The valley features some 40 castles and fortresses, illustrating the region's long history as a key European trade route.
highlights: Lorelei Rock, Pfalzgrafenstein Castle, and Burg Katz.

name: Historic Centres of Stralsund and Wismar
overview: These two cities are classic examples of the Hanseatic League's brick architecture in the southern Baltic region.
history_significance: They were leading trading and defense centres, particularly in the 14th century.
highlights: St Nicholas' Church (Stralsund), the Market Square (Wismar), and the brick Gothic architecture.

name: Muskauer Park / Park Mużakowski
overview: A transboundary landscape park created by Prince Hermann von Pückler-Muskau between 1815 and 1844. Straddling the Neisse River, it is the largest and one of the most famous English-style landscape gardens in Central Europe.
history_significance: A masterpiece of nature-painting that influenced the development of landscape architecture in Europe and America. It integrates the town, castle, and bridges into a unified utopian landscape.
highlights: The New Castle (Neues Schloss), the Double Bridge connecting both countries, and the Arboretum on the Polish side.

name: Town Hall and Roland on the Marketplace of Bremen
overview: The Town Hall and the statue of Roland on the marketplace of Bremen are outstanding representations of civic autonomy and sovereignty, as these developed in the Holy Roman Empire in Europe.
history_significance: The Town Hall represents the medieval Brick Gothic style, while the Roland statue (1404) symbolizes trading rights and freedom.
highlights: The Brick Gothic Town Hall façade and the 5.5m tall Roland statue.

name: The Palaces of King Ludwig II of Bavaria: Neuschwanstein, Linderhof, Schachen and Herrenchiemsee
overview: A serial property of four palace complexes in the Bavarian Alps, built under King Ludwig II between 1868 and 1886. They combine romantic historicist architecture with advanced 19th-century engineering.
history_significance: Reflects the artistic vision and personal world of King Ludwig II of Bavaria, drawing on medieval castles, Versailles, German legends, and Wagnerian opera.
highlights: Neuschwanstein Castle, Linderhof Palace, King's House on Schachen, and Herrenchiemsee New Palace.

name: Old town of Regensburg with Stadtamhof
overview: Located on the Danube River, the Old Town of Regensburg with Stadtamhof is an exceptional example of a central-European medieval trading centre, which illustrates an interchange of cultural and architectural influences.
history_significance: The only major German medieval city to escape destruction during World War II, preserving its Romanesque and Gothic buildings.
highlights: St Peter's Cathedral, the Old Stone Bridge, and the Porta Praetoria.

name: Berlin Modernism Housing Estates
overview: The six housing estates testify to the innovative housing policies from 1910 to 1933, involving architects like Bruno Taut and Walter Gropius.
history_significance: Outstanding examples of the building reform movement that improved housing conditions for low-income people.
highlights: Horseshoe Estate (Hufeisensiedlung) and Siemensstadt.

name: Wadden Sea
overview: The Wadden Sea is the largest unbroken system of intertidal sand and mud flats in the world. It is a transboundary property spanning the Netherlands, Germany, and Denmark.
history_significance: An ecosystem of global importance for biodiversity, particularly as a critical stopover for millions of migratory birds on the East Atlantic Flyway.
highlights: Mudflat hiking (Wattwandern), seal watching, and the dynamic barrier islands.

locations.name: Lower Saxon Wadden Sea National Park (Germany)

name: Fagus Factory in Alfeld
overview: A shoe last factory designed by Walter Gropius and Adolf Meyer in 1911. It is a landmark in the development of modern architecture.
history_significance: One of the first buildings to use the modern glass curtain wall.
highlights: The main building with its fully glazed corners.

name: Ancient and Primeval Beech Forests of the Carpathians and Other Regions of Europe
overview: This transboundary property stretches over 18 countries. Since the end of the last Ice Age, European Beech spread from a few isolated refuge areas in the Alps, Carpathians, Dinarides, Mediterranean and Pyrenees over a short period of a few thousand years in a process that is still ongoing.
history_significance: A successful biological expansion and a complex ecosystem, providing a complete record of the history and evolution of the Fagus genus and its dominant role in the forests of the Northern Hemisphere.
highlights: Jasmund Chalk Cliffs (Germany), Poloniny (Slovakia), and the ancient forests of the Carpathians (Ukraine/Romania).

name: Prehistoric Pile Dwellings around the Alps
overview: This serial property of 111 small individual sites encompasses the remains of prehistoric pile-dwelling (or stilt house) settlements in and around the Alps built from around 5000 to 500 BC.
history_significance: Provides unique insights into life in early agrarian communities in the Alpine region, including agriculture, animal husbandry, and metallurgy.
highlights: Pfahlbaumuseum Unteruhldingen (reconstruction), Lake Ledro (Italy), and Lake Neuchâtel (Switzerland).

name: Margravial Opera House Bayreuth
overview: A masterpiece of Baroque theatre architecture, built between 1745 and 1750. It is one of the few surviving theatres of that period.
history_significance: The only entirely preserved example of court opera architecture where the culture of Baroque court opera can be experienced.
highlights: The lavish wooden interior and the auditorium.

name: Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe
overview: A Baroque park landscape descending a long hill, dominated by a giant statue of Hercules. It features a spectacular system of hydro-pneumatic waterworks.
history_significance: An outstanding example of the era of European absolutism and landscape gardening.
highlights: The Hercules Monument, the Cascades, and the Teufelsbrücke.

name: Carolingian Westwork and Civitas Corvey
overview: Corvey was a major Benedictine abbey in the Carolingian Empire. The Westwork is the only standing structure that dates back to the Carolingian era.
history_significance: Illustrates the role of imperial monasteries in securing territorial control and cultural diffusion.
highlights: The Carolingian Westwork facade.

name: Speicherstadt and Kontorhaus District with Chilehaus
overview: The Speicherstadt is the largest warehouse district in the world built on timber piles. The adjacent Kontorhaus district features large office buildings.
history_significance: An outstanding example of the rapid growth of international trade in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
highlights: Speicherstadt warehouses and the Chilehaus.

name: Caves and Ice Age Art in the Swabian Jura
overview: Modern humans first arrived in Europe 43,000 years ago during the last Ice Age. One of the areas where they took up residence was the Swabian Jura.
history_significance: The caves have revealed the oldest figurative art and musical instruments ever found.
highlights: Hohle Fels, Vogelherd Cave, and the Lion Man statuette.

name: Naumburg Cathedral
overview: The Cathedral of Naumburg, whose construction began in 1028, is an outstanding testimony to medieval art and architecture.
history_significance: Famous for the work of the 'Naumburg Master', particularly the twelve donor statues.
highlights: The statues of Uta and Ekkehard in the west choir.

name: Archaeological Border complex of Hedeby and the Danevirke
overview: Hedeby was a major trading settlement in the Viking Age. The Danevirke is a line of fortification crossing the Schleswig isthmus.
history_significance: A key site for understanding the economic, social, and historical developments in Europe during the Viking Age.
highlights: Viking Museum Haithabu and the Danevirke earthen walls.

name: Erzgebirge/Krušnohoří Mining Region
overview: A transboundary region rich in metals, mined from the Middle Ages onwards. The region became the most important source of silver ore in Europe from 1460 to 1560 and was the source of the 'Thaler' (Dollar).
history_significance: Illustrates the technological and administrative interchange in mining and the development of state-controlled mining systems that influenced the rest of the world.
highlights: St. Anne's Church (Annaberg-Buchholz), Freiberg Mining Academy, and the Red Tower of Death (Jáchymov).

name: Water Management System of Augsburg
overview: The water management system of Augsburg has evolved in successive phases from the 14th century to the present day.
history_significance: Includes canals, water towers, and monumental fountains, demonstrating exemplary water engineering.
highlights: The Water Towers at the Red Gate and the Augustus Fountain.

name: Mathildenhöhe Darmstadt
overview: A colony for artists established in 1897 by Grand Duke Ernst Ludwig. It was a center for emerging reform movements in architecture, arts, and crafts.
history_significance: A pioneering example of the early Modern Movement.
highlights: The Wedding Tower (Hochzeitsturm) and the Exhibition Hall.

name: Frontiers of the Roman Empire
overview: This transnational property represents the border line of the Roman Empire at its greatest extent in the 2nd century AD. It includes Hadrian's Wall and the Antonine Wall in the UK, and the Upper German-Raetian Limes in Germany.
history_significance: Illustrates the ambitious military engineering and defensive strategies of the Roman Empire, marking the boundary between the Roman world and the 'barbarian' territories.
highlights: Hadrian's Wall, Housesteads Roman Fort, Saalburg Roman Fort, and the Antonine Wall.

name: Frontiers of the Roman Empire – The Danube Limes (Western Segment)
overview: This transnational serial property covers almost 600 km of the Danube frontier of the Roman Empire. It includes components in Germany, Austria, and Slovakia, featuring legionary fortresses, auxiliary forts, watchtowers, and civilian settlements.
history_significance: Illustrates the specific defensive strategies of the Roman Empire along the river border, adapting to the dynamic landscape of the Danube to protect the northern frontier.
highlights: Porta Praetoria in Regensburg (Germany), Archaeological Park Carnuntum (Austria), and the Roman Camp of Gerulata (Slovakia).

name: Frontiers of the Roman Empire – The Lower German Limes
overview: The Lower German Limes marked the border of the Roman Empire along the Rhine River for approximately 400 km, from the Rhenish Massif in Germany to the North Sea coast in the Netherlands. It defended the province of Germania Inferior.
history_significance: A masterpiece of ancient military engineering, featuring 102 component parts including legionary fortresses, forts, watchtowers, and harbours that facilitated trade and cultural exchange.
highlights: Archaeological Park Xanten, Praetorium in Cologne, and the Roman fortifications in Nijmegen and Utrecht.

name: ShUM Sites of Speyer, Worms and Mainz
overview: The ShUM sites are the Jewish community centers of Speyer, Worms, and Mainz, which formed a unique association in the Middle Ages.
history_significance: Outstanding testimony to Jewish communal life and religious architecture in medieval Europe.
highlights: Worms Synagogue, the Jewish Cemetery 'Holy Sands', and the Speyer Jewry-Court.

name: Jewish-Medieval Heritage of Erfurt
overview: Located in the medieval historic centre of Erfurt, the property comprises the Old Synagogue, the Mikveh, and the Stone House.
history_significance: Provides exceptional testimony to the Jewish community in Central Europe during the Middle Ages and their coexistence with the Christian majority.
highlights: The Old Synagogue (one of the oldest in Europe) and the medieval Mikveh.

name: Schwerin Residence Ensemble
overview: The ensemble reflects the last flourishing of court culture in the 19th century. The centerpiece is the Schwerin Palace, located on an island in Lake Schwerin.
history_significance: A prime example of historicist architecture (Neo-Renaissance) and landscape design.
highlights: Schwerin Castle, the Court Garden, and the State Theatre.

name: Mont-Saint-Michel and its Bay
overview: Perched on a rocky islet in the midst of vast sandbanks exposed to powerful tides between Normandy and Brittany stand the 'Wonder of the West', a Gothic-style Benedictine abbey dedicated to the Archangel St Michael, and the village that grew up in the shadow of its great walls.
history_significance: Built between the 11th and 16th centuries, the abbey is a technical and artistic tour de force, having had to adapt to the problems posed by this unique natural site.
highlights: The Abbey Church, the Merveille buildings, and the ramparts.

name: Pont du Gard (Roman Aqueduct)
overview: The Pont du Gard is an ancient Roman aqueduct bridge built in the first century AD to carry water over 50 km (31 mi) to the Roman colony of Nemausus (Nîmes). It crosses the river Gardon near the town of Vers-Pont-du-Gard in southern France.
history_significance: It is the highest of all elevated Roman aqueducts and one of the best preserved. It bears witness to the human genius of Roman engineering.
highlights: The three-tiered arch structure, the canal on the top tier, and the Museum of the Pont du Gard.

name: Megaliths of Carnac and of the shores of Morbihan
overview: Located in Brittany, this serial property features the world's largest concentration of megalithic monuments. Erected during the Neolithic period (c. 5000–2300 BCE), it includes thousands of standing stones (menhirs) arranged in alignments, as well as dolmens and tumuli.
history_significance: Testifies to the technical sophistication and complex social organization of Neolithic communities. The alignments are unique in their scale and structured arrangement, reflecting a sophisticated understanding of the landscape.
highlights: The Ménec Alignments (11 rows of menhirs), Kermario Alignments, and the Tumulus of Saint-Michel.

name: Chartres Cathedral
overview: Partly built starting in 1145, and then reconstructed over a 26-year period after the fire of 1194, Chartres Cathedral marks the high point of French Gothic art.
history_significance: The vast nave, in pure ogival style, the porches adorned with fine sculptures from the mid-12th century, and the magnificent 12th- and 13th-century stained-glass windows, all in remarkable condition, combine to make it a masterpiece.
highlights: The Blue Virgin window, the Royal Portal, and the Labyrinth.

name: Palace and Park of Versailles
overview: The Palace of Versailles was the principal residence of the French kings from the time of Louis XIV to Louis XVI. Embellished by several generations of architects, sculptors, decorators and landscape architects, it provided Europe with a model of the ideal royal residence for over a century.
history_significance: The absolute monarchy of the Ancien Régime is associated with this site. It was also the place where the Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919.
highlights: The Hall of Mirrors, the King's Grand Apartments, the Gardens by Le Nôtre, and the Trianon palaces.

name: Vézelay, Church and Hill
overview: Shortly after its founding in the 9th century, the Benedictine abbey of Vézelay acquired the relics of St Mary Magdalene and became a major pilgrimage site.
history_significance: St Bernard preached the Second Crusade there in 1146. The basilica is a masterpiece of Burgundian Romanesque art and architecture.
highlights: The Basilica of St Mary Magdalene, the sculpted capitals, and the tympanum of the narthex.

name: Prehistoric Sites and Decorated Caves of the Vézère Valley
overview: The Vézère valley contains 147 prehistoric sites dating from the Palaeolithic and 25 decorated caves. It is of exceptional interest from the ethnological and anthropological, as well as the aesthetic point of view.
history_significance: The discovery of Lascaux Cave in 1940 was of great importance for the history of prehistoric art. The hunting scenes show some 100 animal figures.
highlights: Lascaux Cave (Lascaux IV), Font-de-Gaume, and Cap Blanc.

locations.name: Lascaux IV (Centre International de l'Art Pariétal)

name: Palace and Park of Fontainebleau
overview: Used by the kings of France from the 12th century, the medieval royal hunting lodge of Fontainebleau was transformed, enlarged and embellished in the 16th century by François I, who wanted to make a 'New Rome' of it.
history_significance: It introduced the Italian Mannerist style to France, which influenced French art (School of Fontainebleau). Napoleon I called it the 'true home of kings'.
highlights: The Gallery of François I, the Ballroom, and the Horseshoe Staircase.

name: Amiens Cathedral
overview: The Cathedral of Notre-Dame of Amiens is one of the largest classic Gothic churches of the 13th century. It is notable for the coherence of its plan, the beauty of its three-tier interior elevation and the particularly fine display of sculptures.
history_significance: Built between 1220 and 1288, it represents the apogee of Gothic architecture in France.
highlights: The West Front sculptures, the soaring nave (42.3 meters high), and the Weeping Angel.

name: Arles, Roman and Romanesque Monuments
overview: Arles offers an interesting example of the adaptation of an ancient city to medieval European civilization. It features impressive Roman monuments and Romanesque churches.
history_significance: Includes the Arena, the Roman Theatre, the Cryptoporticus, the Baths of Constantine, and the Church of St Trophime (a major work of Provençal Romanesque architecture).
highlights: The Amphitheatre (Arena), the Roman Theatre, and the portal of St Trophime.

name: Cistercian Abbey of Fontenay
overview: This Burgundian monastery was founded by St Bernard in 1119. With its church, cloister, refectory, sleeping quarters, bakery and ironworks, it is an excellent illustration of the ideal of self-sufficiency practiced by the earliest Cistercian communities.
history_significance: One of the oldest and most complete Cistercian abbeys in Europe.
highlights: The Abbey Church, the Cloister, and the Forge.

name: Roman Theatre and its Surroundings and the 'Triumphal Arch' of Orange
overview: Situated in the Rhone valley, the ancient theatre of Orange, with its 103-m-long facade, is one of the best preserved of all the great Roman theatres. The Triumphal Arch is one of the most beautiful examples of a provincial triumphal arch.
history_significance: Testifies to the glory of Imperial Rome and the prosperity of the province of Narbonensis.
highlights: The Stage Wall of the theatre and the reliefs on the Triumphal Arch.

name: From the Great Saltworks of Salins-les-Bains to the Royal Saltworks of Arc-et-Senans
overview: The Royal Saltworks of Arc-et-Senans was built by Claude-Nicolas Ledoux to process brine from Salins. It is a masterpiece of Enlightenment industrial architecture.
history_significance: It represents the first major achievement of industrial architecture reflecting the ideal of progress of the Age of Enlightenment.
highlights: The Director's House, the semi-circular layout, and the salt evaporation buildings.

name: Gulf of Porto: Calanche of Piana, Girolata, Scandola Reserve
overview: The nature reserve, which is part of the Regional Natural Park of Corsica, occupies the Scandola peninsula, an impressive mass of porphyritic rock. The site includes the Calanche of Piana and the Gulf of Girolata.
history_significance: An outstanding example of volcanic scrubland and maquis vegetation with dramatic coastal cliffs and diverse marine life.
highlights: The red granite cliffs of the Calanques de Piana and the Scandola Nature Reserve.

name: Abbey Church of Saint-Savin sur Gartempe
overview: Known as the 'Romanesque Sistine Chapel', the Abbey Church of Saint-Savin contains many beautiful 11th- and 12th-century murals which are still in a remarkable state of preservation.
history_significance: It houses the largest and best-preserved collection of Romanesque frescoes in France.
highlights: The nave vault frescoes depicting scenes from the Old Testament.

name: Place Stanislas, Place de la Carrière and Place d'Alliance in Nancy
overview: Nancy, the temporary residence of a king without a kingdom, Stanislas Leszczynski, is the oldest example of a capital city developed in the modern era where an enlightened monarch proved to be sensitive to the needs of the public.
history_significance: A masterpiece of 18th-century urban planning, linking the medieval town with the new town.
highlights: Place Stanislas with its gilded wrought-iron gates and the Hôtel de Ville.

name: Strasbourg, Grande-Île and Neustadt
overview: The Grande-Île is the historic centre of Strasbourg. The Neustadt is a new town designed and built under the German administration (1871–1918).
history_significance: Offers a unique urban landscape characteristic of Rhineland Europe, blending French and Germanic influences.
highlights: Strasbourg Cathedral (Notre-Dame), Petite France district, and the Palais du Rhin.

name: Cathedral of Notre-Dame, Former Abbey of Saint-Remi and Palace of Tau, Reims
overview: The Cathedral of Notre-Dame in Reims is a masterpiece of Gothic art. The Palace of Tau and the Abbey of Saint-Remi are closely linked to the history of the French monarchy.
history_significance: The cathedral was the site of the coronation of the kings of France. It is adorned with fine statuary and 13th-century stained glass.
highlights: The Smiling Angel statue, the Gallery of Kings, and the Saint-Remi Basilica.

name: Paris, Banks of the Seine
overview: From the Louvre to the Eiffel Tower, from the Place de la Concorde to the Grand and Petit Palais, the evolution of Paris and its history can be seen from the River Seine.
history_significance: Includes the Cathedral of Notre-Dame, the Sainte Chapelle, the Louvre, the Palais de l'Institut, the Invalides, the Place de la Concorde, the École Militaire, the Monnaie, the Grand Palais, and the Eiffel Tower.
highlights: Eiffel Tower, Notre-Dame Cathedral, Louvre Museum, and Musée d'Orsay.

name: Bourges Cathedral
overview: The Cathedral of St Etienne of Bourges, built between the late 12th and late 13th centuries, is one of the great masterpieces of Gothic art and is admired for its proportions and the unity of its design.
history_significance: Notable for its tympanum, sculptures, and stained-glass windows, and for having no transept, creating a unified interior space.
highlights: The Last Judgment tympanum and the 13th-century stained glass.

name: Historic Centre of Avignon: Papal Palace, Episcopal Ensemble and Avignon Bridge
overview: In the 14th century, this city in the South of France was the seat of the Papacy. The Palais des Papes is an austere fortress decorated with sumptuous frescoes.
history_significance: It was the center of the Christian world during the 14th century. The ensemble includes the Petit Palais, the Romanesque Cathedral, and the famous Pont d'Avignon.
highlights: Palais des Papes, Pont Saint-Bénézet (Avignon Bridge), and the City Walls.

name: Canal du Midi
overview: This 360-km network of navigable waterways linking the Mediterranean and the Atlantic through 328 structures (locks, aqueducts, bridges, tunnels) is one of the most remarkable feats of civil engineering in modern times.
history_significance: Built between 1667 and 1694 by Pierre-Paul Riquet, it paved the way for the Industrial Revolution.
highlights: The Fonserannes Locks and the Malpas Tunnel.

name: Historic Fortified City of Carcassonne
overview: Carcassonne is an excellent example of a medieval fortified town, with its massive defences encircling the castle and the surrounding buildings, its streets and its fine Gothic cathedral.
history_significance: Restored by Viollet-le-Duc in the 19th century, it had a profound influence on the conservation and restoration of monuments.
highlights: The double line of ramparts, the Château Comtal, and the Basilica of Saints Nazarius and Celsus.

name: Pyrénées - Mont Perdu
overview: This outstanding mountain landscape spans the border between France and Spain. It centres on the peak of Mont Perdu, a calcareous massif that rises to 3,352 m.
history_significance: Offers a classic example of a transhumance agricultural system that has persisted for centuries, blending spectacular geological formations with traditional pastoral life.
highlights: Cirque de Gavarnie (France), Ordesa Valley (Spain), and the Mont Perdu peak.

locations.name: Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park (Spain)

name: Routes of Santiago de Compostela in France
overview: Santiago de Compostela was the supreme goal for countless thousands of pious pilgrims who converged there from all over Europe throughout the Middle Ages. This site includes 71 monuments and 7 sections of trail in France.
history_significance: Testifies to the power of the Christian faith and the exchange of ideas and arts across Europe.
highlights: Basilica of Saint-Sernin (Toulouse), Abbey of Conques, and the Cathedral of Le Puy-en-Velay.

name: Historic Site of Lyon
overview: Lyon was founded by the Romans in the 1st century B.C. as the capital of the Three Gauls. It has played a continuous major role in Europe's political, cultural and economic development.
history_significance: Illustrates the urban evolution over two millennia, from the Roman district to the Renaissance Vieux Lyon and the 19th-century Presqu'île.
highlights: The Roman Theatres, Vieux Lyon with its traboules, and the Basilica of Notre-Dame de Fourvière.

name: Jurisdiction of Saint-Émilion
overview: Viticulture was introduced to this fertile region of Aquitaine by the Romans. The jurisdiction of Saint-Émilion is an outstanding example of a historic vineyard landscape that has survived intact.
history_significance: Illustrates the intensive cultivation of grapes for wine in a clearly delimited region for almost 2,000 years.
highlights: The Monolithic Church, the King's Keep, and the surrounding vineyards.

name: Belfries of Belgium and France
overview: Twenty-three belfries in the north of France and the belfry of Gembloux in Belgium were inscribed as an extension to the 32 Belgian belfries inscribed in 1999.
history_significance: They represent the birth of municipal power in the Middle Ages, independent of the church and the feudal lord.
highlights: Belfry of Arras, Belfry of Lille, and Belfry of Bergues.

name: The Loire Valley between Sully-sur-Loire and Chalonnes
city: Centre-Val de Loire & Pays de la Loire
overview: The Loire Valley is an outstanding cultural landscape of great beauty, containing historic towns and villages, great architectural monuments (the châteaux), and cultivated lands.
history_significance: Illustrates the ideals of the Renaissance and the Age of the Enlightenment on western European thought and design.
highlights: Château de Chambord, Château de Chenonceau, and the Château d'Amboise.

name: Provins, Town of Medieval Fairs
overview: The fortified medieval town of Provins is situated in the former territory of the Counts of Champagne. It bears witness to early developments in the organization of international trading fairs.
history_significance: One of the best-preserved examples of a medieval market town.
highlights: The Tour César, the Grange aux Dîmes (Tithe Barn), and the well-preserved ramparts.

name: Le Havre, the City Rebuilt by Auguste Perret
overview: The city of Le Havre was heavily bombed during World War II. The rebuilt area (1945–1964) is an outstanding example of post-war urban planning and architecture.
history_significance: Designed by Auguste Perret, it exploits the possibilities of concrete and is a landmark in the integration of urban planning traditions and innovative technology.
highlights: St Joseph's Church, the Town Hall, and the 'Appartement Témoin' (Show Flat).

name: Bordeaux, Port of the Moon
overview: The historic center of Bordeaux is an outstanding urban and architectural ensemble, created in the Age of Enlightenment, whose values continued up to the first half of the 20th century.
history_significance: With more protected buildings than any other French city except Paris, it represents a major exchange of cultural values over more than 2,000 years.
highlights: Place de la Bourse, Grand Théâtre, and the Water Mirror.

name: Fortifications of Vauban
overview: This property consists of 12 groups of fortified buildings and sites along the western, northern and eastern borders of France. They represent the finest examples of the work of Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban.
history_significance: Vauban's designs played a major role in the history of military architecture in Europe and beyond.
highlights: Citadel of Besançon, Neuf-Brisach, and Villefranche-de-Conflent.

name: Lagoons of New Caledonia: Reef Diversity and Associated Ecosystems
overview: This site consists of six marine clusters that represent the main diversity of coral reefs and associated ecosystems in the French Pacific archipelago of New Caledonia.
history_significance: Home to the world's second-largest barrier reef and extensive seagrass beds.
highlights: The Great Barrier Reef of New Caledonia and the Zone Côtière Ouest.

name: Episcopal City of Albi
overview: The Episcopal City of Albi, situated in the southwest of France, is a coherent and homogeneous ensemble of monuments and quarters centering on the Saint-Cecile Cathedral.
history_significance: The cathedral is a masterpiece of Southern Gothic style, built in brick as a fortress against the Cathar heresy.
highlights: Sainte-Cécile Cathedral, Palais de la Berbie, and the Pont Vieux.

name: Pitons, cirques and remparts of Reunion Island
overview: The site covers more than 40% of Reunion Island. It features a rugged terrain of two volcanoes, the Piton des Neiges and the Piton de la Fournaise, along with three massive amphitheaters (cirques).
history_significance: Offers a spectacular landscape and serves as a global center of plant diversity.
highlights: Piton de la Fournaise (active volcano), Cirque de Mafate, and Cirque de Cilaos.

name: The Causses and the Cévennes, Mediterranean agro-pastoral Cultural Landscape
overview: A mountain landscape situated in the south of the Massif Central. It is a landscape of deep valleys and high plateaus where the relationship between agropastoral systems and the biophysical environment is evident.
history_significance: Illustrates the evolution of pastoralism and transhumance over three millennia.
highlights: Mont Lozère, Gorges du Tarn, and the traditional stone architecture.

name: Nord-Pas de Calais Mining Basin
overview: This area in northern France bears testimony to the quest for coal from the 18th to the 20th century. It features pits, slag heaps, railway stations, and workers' villages.
history_significance: Illustrates a significant period in the history of industrial Europe and the development of a model workers' city.
highlights: The twin slag heaps of Loos-en-Gohelle and the mining center of Lewarde.

name: Decorated Cave of Pont d'Arc, known as Grotte Chauvet-Pont d'Arc
overview: Located in a limestone plateau of the Ardèche River, the cave contains the earliest-known and best-preserved figurative drawings in the world, dating back to the Aurignacian period (30,000–32,000 BP).
history_significance: An exceptional testimony to prehistoric art, featuring over 1,000 images of animals and humans.
highlights: The Panel of the Lions and the original cave entrance (closed to public, replica nearby).

name: Champagne Hills, Houses and Cellars
overview: The site encompasses the places where the method of producing sparkling wines was developed. It includes vineyards, production sites, and underground cellars.
history_significance: Illustrates the specialized artisan activity that has become a global agro-industrial enterprise.
highlights: Avenue de Champagne in Épernay, Saint-Nicaise Hill in Reims, and the historic vineyards.

name: The Climats, terroirs of Burgundy
overview: The 'Climats' are precisely delimited vineyard parcels on the slopes of the Côte de Nuits and Côte de Beaune. They have been cultivated for wine production for centuries.
history_significance: An outstanding example of grape cultivation and wine production developed since the High Middle Ages.
highlights: The vineyards of Côte de Nuits and Côte de Beaune, and the historic center of Dijon.

name: The Architectural Work of Le Corbusier, an Outstanding Contribution to the Modern Movement
overview: Chosen from the work of Le Corbusier, the 17 sites comprising this transnational serial property are spread over seven countries. They testify to the invention of a new architectural language that made a break with the past.
history_significance: The sites reflect the solutions that the Modern Movement sought to apply during the 20th century to the challenges of inventing new architectural techniques to respond to the needs of society. It represents a global dissemination of these ideals.
highlights: Villa Savoye (France), Capitol Complex (India), National Museum of Western Art (Japan), and Unité d'habitation (France).

locations.name: Maisons La Roche et Jeanneret (Paris, France)
Petite villa au bord du lac Léman (Corseaux, Switzerland)
Villa Savoye and Gardener's Lodge (Poissy, France)
National Museum of Western Art (Tokyo, Japan)

name: Taputapuatea
overview: Taputapuatea on Ra'iatea Island is a sacred landscape and marae complex. It was the center of the Polynesian alliance and a key site for traditional navigation.
history_significance: An outstanding example of 1,000 years of Mā'ohi civilization and a symbol of the Polynesian expansion across the Pacific.
highlights: The Marae Taputapuatea complex and the sacred mountain.

name: Chaîne des Puys - Limagne fault
overview: This site in the Massif Central illustrates the processes of continental rifting. It features the long Limagne Fault, the alignment of the Chaîne des Puys volcanoes, and the inverted relief of Montagne de la Serre.
history_significance: An exceptional illustration of the break-up of continents and associated volcanism.
highlights: Puy de Dôme and the alignment of 80 volcanoes.

name: French Austral Lands and Seas
overview: The site comprises the Crozet Archipelago, the Kerguelen Islands, Saint-Paul and Amsterdam Islands, and 60 small sub-Antarctic islands.
history_significance: Supports one of the highest concentrations of birds and marine mammals in the world, including the largest population of King Penguins.
highlights: The vast colonies of King Penguins and the pristine marine ecosystem.

name: The Great Spa Towns of Europe
overview: A transnational serial property comprising 11 spa towns in 7 European countries: Baden bei Wien (Austria), Spa (Belgium), Františkovy Lázně, Karlovy Vary and Mariánské Lázně (Czechia), Vichy (France), Bad Ems, Baden-Baden and Bad Kissingen (Germany), Montecatini Terme (Italy), and City of Bath (UK).
history_significance: Testifies to the international European spa culture that developed from the early 18th to the 1930s. These towns influenced urban typology around medicinal springs, creating dedicated landscapes for health and leisure.
highlights: The Kurhaus and trinkhallen (pump rooms), extensive thermal parks, grand casinos, and the distinct 19th-century spa architecture across eleven towns.

name: Cordouan Lighthouse
overview: Located on a rocky plateau in the Atlantic Ocean at the mouth of the Gironde estuary, the lighthouse was designed by Louis de Foix in the late 16th century.
history_significance: A masterpiece of maritime signaling, often called the 'Versailles of the Sea' due to its ornate architecture.
highlights: The lighthouse tower and the King's Apartment inside.

name: Nice, Winter Resort Town of the Riviera
overview: Nice reflects the development of the city as a winter resort for the European aristocracy and upper class from the mid-18th century onwards.
history_significance: Showcases the fusion of international cultural influences in its urban planning and architecture.
highlights: Promenade des Anglais, the Negresco Hotel, and the Castle Hill.

name: Volcanoes and Forests of Mount Pelée and the Pitons of Northern Martinique
overview: This site in the Lesser Antilles is a hotspot of biodiversity and home to the Mount Pelée volcano. It covers a significant portion of the island of Martinique.
history_significance: Mount Pelée's eruption in 1902 was a landmark event in volcanology. The forests are home to globally threatened species.
highlights: Mount Pelée volcano and the Pitons du Carbet.

name: The Maison Carrée of Nîmes
overview: The Maison Carrée is an ancient Roman temple in Nîmes, southern France. It is one of the best-preserved Roman temples to survive in the territory of the former Roman Empire.
history_significance: Built in the 1st century AD dedicated to the grandsons of Augustus, it is an exceptional example of Augustan religious architecture and the imperial cult.
highlights: The Corinthian columns and the intact portico.

name: Funerary and memory sites of the First World War (Western Front)
overview: This transnational serial property comprises 139 sites along the Western Front of the First World War. It includes military cemeteries, battlefield burials, and memorial monuments located between the north of Belgium and the east of France.
history_significance: Witnesses to the emergence of a new cult of the dead in the 20th century, honoring individual victims of mass warfare regardless of rank or social status.
highlights: Tyne Cot Cemetery, Menin Gate, Douaumont Ossuary, and Thiepval Memorial.

locations.name: Thiepval Memorial to the Missing (Somme, France)
Notre-Dame de Lorette & Ring of Remembrance (France)

name: Te Henua Enata – The Marquesas Islands
overview: Located in the South Pacific Ocean, this mixed site testifies to the exceptional territorial occupation of the Marquesas archipelago by a human civilization that arrived by sea around the year 1000 CE.
history_significance: It is a hotspot of biodiversity and endemism, and a center of traditional Marquesan culture with impressive lithic architecture and tiki statues.
highlights: The rugged landscapes, the tiki statues, and the marine biodiversity.

name: Alhambra, Generalife and Albayzín, Granada
overview: Rising above the modern lower town, the Alhambra and the Albayzín, situated on two adjacent hills, form the medieval part of Granada. To the east of the Alhambra fortress and residence are the magnificent gardens of the Generalife.
history_significance: A unique testimony to the Moorish culture in Spain and the end of the Nasrid dynasty. It preserves exceptional examples of Islamic architecture.
highlights: Nasrid Palaces, Court of the Lions, Generalife Gardens, and the winding streets of Albayzín.

name: Burgos Cathedral
overview: Our Lady of Burgos was begun in the 13th century at the same time as the great cathedrals of the Ile-de-France and was completed in the 15th and 16th centuries.
history_significance: A comprehensive history of Gothic art, housing the tomb of El Cid and magnificent stained glass windows.
highlights: The spires of the facade, the Condestable Chapel, and the Golden Staircase.

name: Historic Centre of Cordoba
overview: Cordoba's period of greatest glory began in the 8th century after the Moorish conquest, when some 300 mosques and innumerable palaces and public buildings were built.
history_significance: Once the capital of the Umayyad Caliphate, it rivaled Constantinople and Baghdad in power and culture.
highlights: The Great Mosque (Mezquita-Catedral), the Roman Bridge, and the Alcázar de los Reyes Cristianos.

name: Monastery and Site of the Escurial, Madrid
overview: Built at the end of the 16th century by Philip II, the Escurial is a monastery, royal palace, and tomb of the Kings of Spain, designed in a severe and monumental style.
history_significance: The greatest architectural monument of the Spanish Golden Age, symbolizing the power of the Spanish monarchy.
highlights: The Basilica, the Royal Library, and the Pantheon of the Kings.

name: Works of Antoni Gaudí
overview: Seven properties built by the architect Antoni Gaudí (1852–1926) testify to the exceptional creative contribution of this architect to the development of architecture and building technology in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
history_significance: Represents the Eclectic and Personal Modernista style which anticipated and influenced many forms of modern construction.
highlights: Sagrada Família, Park Güell, and Casa Milà.

locations.name: Nativity Façade and Crypt of La Sagrada Família

name: Cave of Altamira and Paleolithic Cave Art of Northern Spain
overview: Seventeen decorated caves of the Paleolithic age were added as an extension to the Altamira Cave. The property provides a masterpiece of creative genius and humanity's earliest art.
history_significance: Altamira is famous for its Polychrome Ceiling (c. 14,000 years old), often called the 'Sistine Chapel of Quaternary Art'.
highlights: Cave of Altamira (Museum/Neocave), Cave of El Castillo, and Cave of Tito Bustillo.

name: Monuments of Oviedo and the Kingdom of the Asturias
overview: In the 9th century, the flame of Christianity was kept alive in the small Kingdom of the Asturias. An innovative Pre-Romanesque architectural style was created here.
history_significance: These monuments played a decisive role in the development of religious architecture in the Iberian Peninsula.
highlights: Santa María del Naranco, San Miguel de Lillo, and La Foncalada (fountain).

name: Old Town of Ávila with its Extra-Muros Churches
overview: Founded in the 11th century to protect the Spanish territories from the Moors, this 'City of Saints and Stones' has maintained its medieval austerity.
history_significance: Famous for its intact medieval city walls (2.5 km long, 87 towers) and as the birthplace of Saint Teresa.
highlights: The City Walls, Ávila Cathedral, and the Basilica of San Vicente.

name: Old Town of Segovia and its Aqueduct
overview: The Roman aqueduct of Segovia, built around AD 50, is remarkably well preserved. This impressive construction, with its two tiers of arches, forms part of the setting of the magnificent historic city.
history_significance: A symbol of the complex history of Spain, featuring the Alcázar (inspiration for Disney castles) and the Gothic Cathedral.
highlights: Roman Aqueduct, The Alcázar of Segovia, and the Cathedral.

name: Santiago de Compostela (Old Town)
overview: This famous pilgrimage site in north-west Spain became a symbol in the Spanish Christians' struggle against Islam. Destroyed by the Muslims at the end of the 10th century, it was completely rebuilt in the following century.
history_significance: The final destination of the Way of St. James (Camino de Santiago), holding the tomb of the Apostle James.
highlights: The Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela, Plaza del Obradoiro, and the Hostal de los Reyes Católicos.

name: Garajonay National Park
overview: Located in the centre of the island of La Gomera, this park covers some 10% of the island. It contains a remarkably well-preserved laurel forest (laurisilva).
history_significance: A relic of the Tertiary period forests that once covered southern Europe, thriving here due to the misty climate.
highlights: The ancient Laurisilva forest and the Roque de Agando.

name: Historic City of Toledo
overview: Successively a Roman municipium, the capital of the Visigothic Kingdom, a fortress of the Emirate of Cordoba, and the capital of the Spanish Empire, Toledo is a repository of more than 2,000 years of history.
history_significance: Known as the 'City of Three Cultures' (Christian, Islamic, and Jewish) where they coexisted during the Middle Ages.
highlights: Toledo Cathedral, Alcázar of Toledo, and the Synagogue of El Tránsito.

name: Mudejar Architecture of Aragon
overview: The Mudejar art of Aragon symbolizes the peaceful co-existence of Christian and Muslim cultures. It is characterized by the refined use of brick and glazed tiles.
history_significance: Represents a unique aesthetic phenomenon in Spain, blending Islamic tradition with Western styles (Gothic/Renaissance).
highlights: Towers of San Martín and San Salvador (Teruel), and the Aljafería Palace (Zaragoza).

locations.name: Cathedral of Santa María de Mediavilla (Teruel)

name: Old Town of Cáceres
overview: The city's history of battles between Moors and Christians is reflected in its architecture, which is a blend of Roman, Islamic, Northern Gothic, and Italian Renaissance styles.
history_significance: One of the most complete medieval and renaissance urban ensembles in the world.
highlights: Bujaco Tower, the Co-cathedral of Santa María, and the medieval walls.

name: Cathedral, Alcázar and Archivo de Indias in Seville
overview: These three buildings form a remarkable monumental complex in the heart of Seville. The Cathedral is the largest Gothic building in Europe.
history_significance: Testifies to the civilization of the Almohads and Christian Andalusia, and Seville's role as a trading hub with the New World.
highlights: The Giralda tower, the Tomb of Columbus, and the Real Alcázar gardens.

name: Old City of Salamanca
overview: Salamanca, the oldest university town in Spain, was conquered by the Carthaginians in the 3rd century B.C. Its historic center is full of important Romanesque, Gothic, Moorish, Renaissance, and Baroque monuments.
history_significance: Home to one of the oldest universities in Europe (founded 1218) and the Churrigueresque style of Plaza Mayor.
highlights: Plaza Mayor, the Old and New Cathedrals, and the University facade.

name: Poblet Monastery
overview: This Cistercian abbey in Catalonia is one of the largest and most complete in the world. It includes a massive church, a fortified royal residence, and the pantheon of the kings of Aragon.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Cistercian art and a unique fortified royal residence.
highlights: The Royal Tombs and the Alabaster Altarpiece.

name: Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida
overview: The colony of Augusta Emerita was founded in 25 B.C. as the capital of Lusitania. Its well-preserved remains include a bridge, an amphitheatre, a theatre, a vast circus, and water-supply systems.
history_significance: One of the best-preserved Roman cities in Spain, serving as a model for provincial Roman capitals.
highlights: The Roman Theatre, Amphitheatre, and the Temple of Diana.

name: Routes of Santiago de Compostela: Camino Francés and Routes of Northern Spain
overview: A network of four Christian pilgrimage routes in northern Spain that converge at Santiago de Compostela. It includes the Camino Francés, Coastal Route, Interior Route, and Liébana Route.
history_significance: A major axis of cultural exchange in Europe during the Middle Ages.
highlights: The diverse landscapes, bridges, and thousands of historic buildings along the route.

name: Royal Monastery of Santa María de Guadalupe
overview: The monastery is an outstanding repository of four centuries of Spanish religious architecture. It symbolizes two significant events in world history: the reconquest of the peninsula and the arrival of Columbus in the Americas.
history_significance: Famous for its statue of the Virgin of Guadalupe, a powerful symbol of the Christianization of the New World.
highlights: The Mudejar Cloister, the Sacristy, and the facade of the church.

name: Doñana National Park
overview: Doñana occupies the right bank of the Guadalquivir river at its estuary on the Atlantic Ocean. It is notable for the great diversity of its biotopes, especially lagoons and marshlands.
history_significance: The most important wetland in Spain and a major site for migrating birds between Africa and Europe.
highlights: The shifting sand dunes and the Iberian Lynx habitat.

name: Historic Walled Town of Cuenca
overview: Built by the Moors in a defensive position at the heart of the Caliphate of Cordoba, Cuenca is an unusually well-preserved medieval fortified city.
history_significance: Famous for its 'Hanging Houses' (Casas Colgadas) suspended from sheer cliffs overlooking the Huécar river.
highlights: Casas Colgadas, Cuenca Cathedral, and the San Pablo Bridge.

name: La Lonja de la Seda de Valencia
overview: Built between 1482 and 1533, this group of buildings was originally used for trading in silk. It is a masterpiece of late Gothic architecture.
history_significance: Symbolizes the power and wealth of the mercantile city of Valencia in the 15th and 16th centuries.
highlights: The Column Hall (Sala de Contratación) with its twisted columns.

name: Las Médulas
overview: In the 1st century A.D., the Roman Imperial authorities began to exploit the gold deposits of this region using a technique based on hydraulic power (ruina montium).
history_significance: The largest open-pit gold mine in the entire Roman Empire, leaving a spectacular cultural landscape of red peaks.
highlights: The distinctive red clay mountains and ancient tunnels.

name: Palau de la Música Catalana and Hospital de Sant Pau, Barcelona
overview: These are two of the finest contributions to Barcelona's architecture by the Catalan art nouveau architect Lluís Domènech i Montaner.
history_significance: Masterpieces of the imaginative and exuberant Art Nouveau that flowered in early 20th-century Barcelona.
highlights: The stained glass skylight of Palau de la Música and the garden city layout of the Hospital.

name: San Millán Yuso and Suso Monasteries
overview: The monastic community founded by St Millán in the mid-6th century became a place of pilgrimage. Suso (upper) is the older, while Yuso (lower) was built later.
history_significance: Considered the birthplace of the modern written Spanish language (Castilian).
highlights: The Mozarabic architecture of Suso and the library of Yuso.

locations.name: Monastery of San Millán de Suso
Monastery of San Millán de Yuso

name: Prehistoric Rock Art Sites in the Côa Valley and Siega Verde
city: Vila Nova de Foz Côa (Portugal) & Salamanca (Spain)
overview: This transboundary property contains thousands of rock engravings from the Upper Palaeolithic era (22,000 to 10,000 BC), depicting animals like horses and aurochs. It includes sites in the Côa Valley (Portugal) and Siega Verde (Spain).
history_significance: The most outstanding open-air ensemble of Palaeolithic art in the Peninsula, providing a unique view of the early human artistic capabilities.
highlights: Canada do Inferno, Penascosa, and Siega Verde rock engravings.

locations.name: Canada do Inferno Rock Art Site (Portugal)

name: Rock Art of the Mediterranean Basin on the Iberian Peninsula
overview: This serial property includes 758 sites distributed across the eastern part of Spain. It features the largest group of rock-art sites in Europe.
history_significance: Provides an exceptional picture of human life in a critical phase of human development (Levantine Art).
highlights: Caves of El Cogul (Dancers) and Valltorta Gorge.

name: University and Historic Precinct of Alcalá de Henares
overview: Founded by Cardinal Jiménez de Cisneros in the early 16th century, Alcalá de Henares was the world's first planned university city.
history_significance: The birthplace of Cervantes and a model for universities in Europe and the Americas. It embodies the Civitas Dei (City of God) concept.
highlights: The University Façade, Paraninfo, and Cervantes' Birthplace.

name: Ibiza, Biodiversity and Culture
overview: Ibiza provides an excellent example of the interaction between the marine and coastal ecosystems. The dense prairies of oceanic Posidonia (seagrass) support diverse marine life.
history_significance: The Upper Town (Dalt Vila) is an outstanding example of Renaissance military architecture.
highlights: Dalt Vila (Old Town), Posidonia seagrass meadows, and the Phoenician settlement of Sa Caleta.

name: San Cristóbal de La Laguna
overview: San Cristóbal de La Laguna consists of two distinct parts: the unplanned Upper Town and the Lower Town, the first ideal 'city-territory' laid out according to philosophical principles.
history_significance: Its grid layout served as a model for colonial cities in the Americas (e.g., Havana, Lima).
highlights: The Cathedral, the grid street plan, and colonial mansions.

name: Archaeological Ensemble of Tarraco
overview: Tarraco was a major administrative and mercantile city in Roman Spain and the center of the Imperial cult for all the Iberian provinces.
history_significance: Features impressive Roman remains including an amphitheatre by the sea, a circus, and walls.
highlights: The Roman Amphitheatre, the Circ, and the Les Ferreres Aqueduct (Devil's Bridge).

name: Archaeological Site of Atapuerca
overview: The caves of the Sierra de Atapuerca contain a rich fossil record of the earliest human beings in Europe, from nearly one million years ago.
history_significance: Key site for understanding human evolution, yielding fossils of Homo antecessor and Homo heidelbergensis.
highlights: The Sima de los Huesos (Pit of Bones) and the Gran Dolina.

name: Catalan Romanesque Churches of the Vall de Boí
overview: The narrow Vall de Boí features a remarkable cluster of Romanesque churches from the 11th and 12th centuries, known for their tall bell towers.
history_significance: An exceptionally pure example of Romanesque art in a rural setting, influenced by Lombardy.
highlights: Sant Climent de Taüll (famous frescoes) and Santa Maria de Taüll.

name: Palmeral of Elche
overview: The Palmeral of Elche is a landscape of groves of date palms, formally laid out with elaborate irrigation systems.
history_significance: A unique example of Arab agricultural practices in Europe, dating back to the 8th century.
highlights: The Imperial Palm and the Huerto del Cura garden.

name: Roman Walls of Lugo
overview: The walls of Lugo were built in the later part of the 3rd century to defend the Roman town of Lucus. The entire circuit survives intact.
history_significance: The finest example of late Roman fortifications in western Europe.
highlights: The complete 2 km circuit of walls and its 85 towers.

name: Aranjuez Cultural Landscape
overview: The Aranjuez cultural landscape is an entity of complex relationships: between nature and human activity, between sinuous watercourses and geometric landscape design.
history_significance: A Royal Summer Residence for centuries, showcasing the evolution of concepts of the landscape and the absolute monarchy.
highlights: The Royal Palace, the Island Garden, and the Prince's Garden.

name: Renaissance Monumental Ensembles of Úbeda and Baeza
overview: The urban morphology of these two small cities in southern Spain dates back to the Moorish 9th century and the Reconquista. They flourished in the 16th century.
history_significance: Outstanding examples of the Italian Renaissance style imported to Spain, influencing architecture in Latin America.
highlights: Vázquez de Molina Square (Úbeda) and Baeza Cathedral.

name: Vizcaya Bridge
overview: The Vizcaya Bridge straddles the mouth of the Ibaizabal estuary. Designed by Alberto de Palacio, it was completed in 1893.
history_significance: The world's first transporter bridge and a masterpiece of the Industrial Revolution iron architecture.
highlights: The gondola transporting cars and passengers, and the pedestrian walkway.

name: Teide National Park
overview: Situated on the island of Tenerife, the park features the Teide-Pico Viejo stratovolcano. At 3,718 m, it is the highest peak in Spain.
history_significance: Provides critical evidence of the geological processes that underpin the evolution of oceanic islands.
highlights: Mount Teide, the Roques de García, and the vast caldera of Las Cañadas.

name: Tower of Hercules
overview: The Tower of Hercules has served as a lighthouse and landmark at the entrance of La Coruña harbour since the late 1st century A.D.
history_significance: The only Roman lighthouse in the world that remains in use today.
highlights: The Roman core of the lighthouse and the surrounding sculpture park.

name: Cultural Landscape of the Serra de Tramuntana
overview: Located on the sheer-sided north-west coast of Mallorca, the Serra de Tramuntana shows how agriculture has adapted to a difficult landscape.
history_significance: Notable for its network of water management devices, terraces, and dry stone walls.
highlights: The dry stone terraces, olive groves, and the villages of Deià and Valldemossa.

name: Heritage of Mercury. Almadén and Idrija
overview: The property includes the mining sites of Almadén (Spain), where mercury has been extracted since antiquity, and Idrija (Slovenia), where mercury was first found in 1490. Together, they represent the two largest mercury mines in the world.
history_significance: These two sites illustrate the intercontinental trade in mercury, which was vital for the extraction of silver from ores in the Americas using the amalgamation process. They bear witness to the evolution of industrial and technical systems for mercury extraction.
highlights: The Almadén Mining Park and Hexagonal Bullring in Spain; Anthony's Shaft and Gewerkenegg Castle in Slovenia.

locations.name: San Rafael Royal Miners Hospital (Almadén, Spain)
Anthony's Main Road (Antonijev rov, Idrija, Slovenia)

name: Antequera Dolmens Site
overview: Located in Andalusia, the site comprises three megalithic monuments (Menga, Viera, and Tholos of El Romeral) and two natural monuments (Peña de los Enamorados and El Torcal).
history_significance: One of the most remarkable examples of European megalithism, unique for its orientation towards natural landmarks rather than the sun.
highlights: The Menga Dolmen and the surreal rock formations of El Torcal.

name: Caliphate City of Medina Azahara
overview: Medina Azahara is an archaeological site of a city built in the mid-10th century as the seat of the Caliphate of Cordoba. It was destroyed shortly after and forgotten for 1,000 years.
history_significance: Provides complete knowledge of the material culture of the Umayyad civilization of Al-Andalus at its peak.
highlights: The House of Ja'far, the Upper Basilical Hall, and the vast reception halls.

name: Risco Caido and the Sacred Mountains of Gran Canaria Cultural Landscape
overview: Located in the mountainous center of Gran Canaria, this site contains settlements of the pre-Hispanic Canarians (Guanches), who occupied the island in isolation for 1,500 years.
history_significance: Features troglodyte settlements and sacred sites (almogarenes) with astronomical alignments.
highlights: Risco Caido Cave 6 (astronomical calendar) and Roque Bentayga.

name: Paseo del Prado and Buen Retiro, a landscape of Arts and Sciences
overview: A cultural landscape in the heart of Madrid, evolving since the 16th century. It includes the Paseo del Prado avenue, the Buen Retiro Park, and major cultural institutions.
history_significance: The first urban tree-lined promenade in Europe, designed to be enjoyed by all citizens, illustrating the enlightened absolutism.
highlights: Prado Museum, Retiro Park, Cibeles Fountain, and the Royal Botanical Garden.

name: Prehistoric Sites of Talayotic Menorca
overview: This site bears testimony to the occupation of the island of Menorca by a prehistoric population known for its Cyclopean architecture (Talayotic culture).
history_significance: Features a high density of prehistoric monuments like navetas (burial tombs), taulas (sanctuaries), and talayots (towers).
highlights: Naveta d'Es Tudons, Torre d'en Galmés, and Torralba d'en Salort.

name: Castles and Town Walls of King Edward in Gwynedd
overview: The castles of Beaumaris and Harlech and the fortified complexes of Caernarfon and Conwy are located in the former principality of Gwynedd in North Wales. They are extremely fine examples of late 13th-century and early 14th-century military architecture in Europe.
history_significance: Built by King Edward I after his conquest of Wales, these fortifications demonstrate the sophistication of English military architecture of the time, influenced by Savoyard engineers.
highlights: Caernarfon Castle, Conwy Castle, Harlech Castle, and Beaumaris Castle.

name: Durham Castle and Cathedral
overview: Durham Cathedral was built in the late 11th and early 12th centuries to house the relics of St Cuthbert (evangelist of Northumbria) and the Venerable Bede. It attests to the importance of the early Benedictine monastic community and is the largest and finest example of Norman architecture in England.
history_significance: The cathedral's ribbed vaulting was a precursor to Gothic architecture. The castle was the stronghold of the Prince-Bishops of Durham.
highlights: The Ribbed Vault of the Cathedral, the Galilee Chapel, and the Norman Castle.

name: Giant's Causeway and Causeway Coast
overview: The Giant's Causeway lies at the foot of the basalt cliffs along the sea coast on the edge of the Antrim plateau. It is made up of some 40,000 massive black basalt columns sticking out of the sea.
history_significance: A spectacular geological formation resulting from volcanic activity during the Tertiary, some 50–60 million years ago. It has inspired legends of giants crossing the sea to Scotland.
highlights: The hexagonal basalt columns, the Wishing Chair, and the coastal hiking trails.

name: Ironbridge Gorge
