overview: Ironbridge is known throughout the world as the symbol of the Industrial Revolution. It contains all the elements of progress that contributed to the rapid development of this industrial region in the 18th century.
history_significance: Site of the world's first cast-iron bridge (built in 1779) and the furnace where Abraham Darby I perfected the technique of smelting iron with coke.
highlights: The Iron Bridge, Blists Hill Victorian Town, and the Coalbrookdale Museum of Iron.

name: St Kilda
overview: This volcanic archipelago, with its spectacular cliffs and sea stacks, bears the traces of human occupation in extreme conditions for over 2,000 years until the evacuation in 1930.
history_significance: A unique example of a subsistence economy based on bird-catching and farming in isolation. It also hosts some of the largest colonies of seabirds in Europe.
highlights: Village Bay on Hirta, the Soay sheep, and the massive seabird colonies.

name: Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites
overview: Stonehenge and Avebury are the most famous groups of megaliths in the world. The two sanctuaries consist of circles of menhirs arranged in a pattern whose astronomical significance is still being explored.
history_significance: These holy places and the nearby Neolithic sites provide an incomparable testimony to prehistoric times.
highlights: The Stone Circle of Stonehenge, the Avebury Henge (largest in the world), and Silbury Hill.

name: Studley Royal Park including the Ruins of Fountains Abbey
overview: A striking landscape created in the 18th century, integrating the magnificent ruins of the Cistercian Fountains Abbey (founded 1132) and Fountains Hall into a water garden.
history_significance: A masterpiece of 18th-century landscape design and one of the few great Cistercian houses to survive the Dissolution of the Monasteries relatively intact.
highlights: Fountains Abbey ruins, the Water Garden, and St Mary's Church.

name: Blenheim Palace
overview: Blenheim Palace was built as a gift to John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough, in gratitude for his victory over the French and Bavarian troops at the Battle of Blenheim in 1704.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Baroque architecture by Sir John Vanbrugh and Nicholas Hawksmoor. It is also the birthplace of Sir Winston Churchill.
highlights: The Great Hall, the Long Library, and the park designed by 'Capability' Brown.

name: City of Bath
overview: Founded by the Romans as a thermal spa, Bath became an important centre of the wool industry in the Middle Ages. In the 18th century, under George III, it developed into an elegant town with Neoclassical Palladian buildings.
history_significance: Famous for its Roman remains and 18th-century architecture, particularly the Royal Crescent and the Circus.
highlights: Roman Baths, Bath Abbey, Royal Crescent, and Pulteney Bridge.

name: Palace of Westminster and Westminster Abbey including Saint Margaret's Church
overview: Westminster Palace, rebuilt in the 19th century in the Gothic Revival style, is the seat of the British Parliament. Westminster Abbey is the coronation church of British monarchs.
history_significance: A site of immense historic and symbolic significance, representing the history of the British monarchy and parliamentary democracy.
highlights: Big Ben (Elizabeth Tower), Westminster Hall, and the Coronation Chair in the Abbey.

name: Canterbury Cathedral, St Augustine's Abbey, and St Martin's Church
overview: Canterbury is the spiritual head of the Church of England. St Martin's is the oldest church in England. St Augustine's Abbey was founded by St Augustine in 597 AD.
history_significance: The cradle of Christianity in England and the site of Thomas Becket's martyrdom in 1170.
highlights: The Cathedral Nave, the site of Becket's martyrdom, and the ruins of St Augustine's Abbey.

name: Henderson Island
overview: Henderson Island is one of the few atolls in the world whose ecology has been practically untouched by a human presence. It is an elevated coral atoll in the South Pacific.
history_significance: Notable for its endemic species (10 plants and 4 land birds) and its intact phosphate reserves.
highlights: The pristine coral reef and endemic bird species like the Henderson Crake.

name: Tower of London
overview: The massive White Tower is a typical example of Norman military architecture. Built by William the Conqueror in 1066 to protect London and assert his power.
history_significance: Served as a royal palace, fortress, prison, and place of execution. It houses the Crown Jewels.
highlights: The White Tower, the Crown Jewels, and the Ravens.

name: Gough and Inaccessible Islands
overview: These islands in the South Atlantic are one of the least disrupted island and marine ecosystems in the Cool Temperate Zone.
history_significance: Home to spectacular cliffs and massive seabird colonies, including the endemic Tristan Albatross and Gough Bunting.
highlights: The steep cliffs and the untouched ecosystem.

name: Old and New Towns of Edinburgh
overview: Edinburgh consists of two distinct districts: the Old Town, dominated by a medieval fortress, and the neoclassical New Town, which had a far-reaching influence on European urban planning.
history_significance: The harmonization of the medieval and Georgian periods makes it one of the most beautiful cities in Europe.
highlights: Edinburgh Castle, Royal Mile, and Charlotte Square.

name: Maritime Greenwich
overview: The ensemble of buildings at Greenwich, an outlying district of London, and the park in which they are set, symbolize English artistic and scientific endeavour in the 17th and 18th centuries.
history_significance: Home to the Royal Observatory, which defines the Prime Meridian (Longitude 0°) and Greenwich Mean Time.
highlights: Royal Observatory, Old Royal Naval College, and the Queen's House.

name: Heart of Neolithic Orkney
overview: The group of Neolithic monuments on the Orkney Islands consists of a large chambered tomb (Maeshowe), two ceremonial stone circles (Stenness and Brodgar), and a settlement (Skara Brae).
history_significance: Provides a graphic depiction of life in this remote archipelago 5,000 years ago.
highlights: Skara Brae (preserved village), Ring of Brodgar, and Maeshowe.

name: Blaenavon Industrial Landscape
overview: The area around Blaenavon is evidence of the pre-eminence of South Wales as the world's major producer of iron and coal in the 19th century.
history_significance: Contains all the elements of an industrial landscape: coal and ore mines, quarries, a primitive railway system, furnaces, and workers' homes.
highlights: Big Pit National Coal Museum and the Blaenavon Ironworks.

name: Historic Town of St George and Related Fortifications, Bermuda
overview: The Town of St George, founded in 1612, is an outstanding example of the earliest English urban settlement in the New World.
history_significance: Its associated fortifications graphically illustrate the development of English military engineering from the 17th to the 20th century.
highlights: King's Square, St Peter's Church, and Fort St Catherine.

name: Derwent Valley Mills
overview: The Derwent Valley in central England contains a series of 18th- and 19th-century cotton mills and an industrial landscape of high historical and technological interest.
history_significance: The birthplace of the factory system where Richard Arkwright first applied water power to textile production.
highlights: Cromford Mill, Belper North Mill, and the worker housing clusters.

name: Dorset and East Devon Coast (Jurassic Coast)
overview: The cliff exposures along the Dorset and East Devon coast provide an almost continuous sequence of rock formations spanning the Mesozoic Era, or some 185 million years of the earth's history.
history_significance: Renowned for its fossil discoveries, including the first complete ichthyosaur found by Mary Anning.
highlights: Durdle Door, Lulworth Cove, and the fossil-rich beaches of Lyme Regis.

name: New Lanark
overview: New Lanark is a small 18th-century village set in a sublime Scottish landscape where the philanthropist and utopian idealist Robert Owen modeled a model industrial community.
history_significance: A milestone in social history, Owen introduced progressive reforms like education for workers' children and decent housing.
highlights: The cotton mills, Robert Owen's House, and the School for Children.

name: Saltaire
overview: Saltaire is an exceptionally complete and well-preserved industrial village of the second half of the 19th century. It was built by Titus Salt for the workers of his textile mill.
history_significance: A prime example of 19th-century philanthropic paternalism, influencing the garden city movement.
highlights: Salts Mill (now an art gallery) and the United Reformed Church.

name: Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew
overview: This historic landscape garden features elements that illustrate significant periods of the art of gardens from the 18th to the 20th centuries. It houses botanic collections (conserved plants, living plants, and documents) that have been enriched through centuries.
history_significance: A leading center for botanical research and a masterpiece of landscape design.
highlights: The Palm House, the Temperate House, and the Pagoda.

name: Cornwall and West Devon Mining Landscape
overview: Much of the landscape of Cornwall and West Devon was transformed in the 18th and early 19th centuries as a result of the rapid growth of copper and tin mining.
history_significance: The region made a vital contribution to the Industrial Revolution and exported mining technology and culture globally.
highlights: Botallack Mine (Crowns Engine Houses), St Agnes, and the Tavistock Canal.

name: Pontcysyllte Aqueduct and Canal
overview: Situated in north-eastern Wales, the 18-km long Pontcysyllte Aqueduct and Canal is a feat of civil engineering of the Industrial Revolution, completed in the early years of the 19th century.
history_significance: Designed by Thomas Telford, the aqueduct is a pioneering masterpiece of cast iron and masonry construction.
highlights: The Pontcysyllte Aqueduct (Stream in the Sky) and the Chirk Aqueduct.

name: The Forth Bridge
overview: This railway bridge spanning the estuary of the Forth River in Scotland is the world's longest multi-span cantilever bridge. It opened in 1890.
history_significance: A milestone in bridge design and construction during the period when railways came to dominate long-distance land travel.
highlights: The massive red steel cantilever structures.

name: Gorham's Cave Complex
overview: The steep limestone cliffs on the eastern side of the Rock of Gibraltar contain four caves with archaeological deposits that provide evidence of Neanderthal occupation.
history_significance: Provides a unique testimony to the traditions and lifestyle of the Neanderthals and their adaptation to their environment.
highlights: Gorham's Cave and Vanguard Cave.

name: The English Lake District
overview: Located in northwest England, the Lake District is a mountainous area whose valleys have been modelled by glaciers in the Ice Age and subsequently shaped by an agro-pastoral land-use system.
history_significance: Celebrated by the Romantic poets (like Wordsworth) and artists, it inspired the concept of landscape appreciation and conservation.
highlights: Lake Windermere, Scafell Pike, and Hill Top (Beatrix Potter's house).

name: Jodrell Bank Observatory
overview: Jodrell Bank is one of the world's leading radio astronomy observatories. It is located in a rural area of northwest England, free from radio interference.
history_significance: Its Lovell Telescope (1957) was the world's largest steerable radio telescope and played a key role in the Space Age (e.g., tracking Sputnik).
highlights: The Lovell Telescope and the Discovery Centre.

name: The Slate Landscape of Northwest Wales
overview: The landscape illustrates the transformation that industrial slate quarrying and mining brought about in the traditional rural environment of the mountains and valleys of the Snowdon massif.
history_significance: In the late 19th century, the area produced about a third of the world's output of roofing slate. It influenced slate quarrying worldwide.
highlights: Penrhyn Quarry, Dinorwig Quarry, and the Talyllyn Railway.

locations.name: Gorseddau and Prince of Wales Slate Quarries, Railways and Mill
Ffestiniog Slate Mines and Railway to Porthmadog
Bryneglwys Slate Quarry and Abergynolwyn / Talyllyn Railway

name: Moravian Church Settlements
overview: Gracehill is the UK component of the transnational Moravian Church Settlements. Founded in 1759, it reflects the social and religious ideals of the Moravian Church.
history_significance: Demonstrates unique urban planning centered on community living, equality, and education.
highlights: The Square, the Church, and the distinct brothers' and sisters' houses.

name: The Flow Country
overview: Located in the far north of Scotland, the Flow Country is widely considered to be the largest area of blanket bog in the world.
history_significance: An outstanding example of an accumulating blanket bog landscape, critical for carbon storage and biodiversity.
highlights: The vast peatland landscape and its pools (dubh lochans).

name: Historic Centre of Saint Petersburg and Related Groups of Monuments
overview: The 'Venice of the North', with its numerous canals and more than 400 bridges, is the result of a vast urban project begun in 1703 under Peter the Great. It is renowned for its architectural heritage, fusing Baroque and Neoclassical styles.
history_significance: Capital of the Russian Empire for two centuries, closely associated with the October Revolution.
highlights: The Hermitage Museum (Winter Palace), Peter and Paul Fortress, and the palaces of Peterhof and Tsarskoye Selo.

name: Kizhi Pogost
overview: The Kizhi Pogost is located on one of the many islands in Lake Onega. It features two 18th-century wooden churches and an octagonal bell tower built of wood without a single nail.
history_significance: A unique example of Russian carpentry and wooden architecture, representing the pinnacle of this tradition.
highlights: Church of the Transfiguration (22 domes) and the Church of the Intercession.

name: Kremlin and Red Square, Moscow
overview: Inextricably linked to all the most important historical and political events in Russia since the 13th century, the Kremlin is a fortified complex containing palaces and cathedrals. Red Square lies adjacent to its eastern wall.
history_significance: The seat of Russian power (Grand Prince, Tsar, Soviet, and Federation) and a masterpiece of Russian Orthodox architecture.
highlights: The Kremlin Walls, Saint Basil's Cathedral, the Lenin Mausoleum, and the Cathedral of the Dormition.

name: Historic Monuments of Novgorod and Surroundings
overview: Veliky Novgorod was one of the most ancient cities of Russia and a major trade center on the Varangian route. It preserves remarkable monuments of medieval architecture.
history_significance: The birthplace of Russian statehood and a center of Orthodox spirituality and art (Theophanes the Greek).
highlights: Novgorod Kremlin (Detinets), Cathedral of St Sophia, and Yuriev Monastery.

locations.name: Church of the Transfiguration on Ilyina Street

name: Cultural and Historic Ensemble of the Solovetsky Islands
overview: The Solovetsky archipelago in the White Sea comprises six islands. They have been inhabited since the 5th millennium BC, but are most famous for the fortified Solovetsky Monastery founded in the 15th century.
history_significance: A resilient monastic community in a harsh northern environment, which later served as a notorious Soviet prison camp (Gulag).
highlights: Solovetsky Monastery complex and the network of canals and lakes.

name: White Monuments of Vladimir and Suzdal
overview: These two artistic centers in central Russia hold a prominent place in the country's architectural history. They feature a distinct style of white limestone architecture from the 12th and 13th centuries.
history_significance: Represents the rise of the Principality of Vladimir-Suzdal, a precursor to the Muscovite state.
highlights: Cathedral of the Assumption (Vladimir), Golden Gate (Vladimir), and the Kremlin of Suzdal.

name: Architectural Ensemble of the Trinity Sergius Lavra in Sergiev Posad
overview: This is a fine example of a working Orthodox monastery, with military features that are typical of the 15th to the 18th century. It is the spiritual center of the Russian Orthodox Church.
history_significance: Founded by St. Sergius of Radonezh in 1337, it contains the tomb of Boris Godunov and Andrei Rublev's 'Trinity' icon (originally).
highlights: The Cathedral of the Assumption with its blue and gold domes.

name: Church of the Ascension, Kolomenskoye
overview: Built in 1532 on the imperial estate of Kolomenskoye to celebrate the birth of the future Ivan the Terrible, this church is a masterpiece of Russian hipped-roof architecture.
history_significance: It represents a break from Byzantine tradition and had a profound influence on Russian ecclesiastical architecture.
highlights: The white stone tent-roof structure and the view over the Moskva River.

name: Virgin Komi Forests
overview: Covering 3.28 million ha of tundra and mountain tundra in the Urals, this is one of the most extensive areas of virgin boreal forest remaining in Europe.
history_significance: A vast, pristine ecosystem home to elk, wolves, bears, and extensive peatlands.
highlights: Manpupuner rock formations and the Yugyd Va National Park.

name: Lake Baikal
overview: Lake Baikal is the oldest (25 million years) and deepest (1,700 m) lake in the world. It contains 20% of the world's total unfrozen freshwater reserve.
history_significance: Known as the 'Galapagos of Russia' due to its exceptional biodiversity and endemic species like the Baikal seal (nerpa).
highlights: Olkhon Island, the Circum-Baikal Railway, and the Nerpa seals.

name: Volcanoes of Kamchatka
overview: This is one of the most outstanding volcanic regions in the world, with a high density of active volcanoes, a variety of types, and a wide range of related features.
history_significance: Contains 6 distinct sites showing the interaction of active volcanism and glaciers.
highlights: Valley of Geysers, Klyuchevskaya Sopka (highest active volcano in Eurasia), and the brown bears.

name: Golden Mountains of Altai
overview: The Altai mountains in southern Siberia represent the major mountain range in the western Siberia biogeographic region and provide the source of its greatest rivers (Ob and Irtysh).
history_significance: Habitat for endangered species such as the Snow Leopard and the Altai Argali.
highlights: Mount Belukha, Lake Teletskoye, and the Ukok Plateau.

name: Western Caucasus
overview: The Western Caucasus extends over 275,000 ha of the extreme western end of the Caucasus mountains. It is one of the few large mountain areas of Europe that has not experienced significant human impact.
history_significance: The only large mountain area in Europe with a natural, untouched forest ecosystem, featuring European Bison reintroduction.
highlights: Caucasus Nature Reserve and the diverse mountain landscapes.

name: Curonian Spit
overview: This transboundary property is an elongated sand dune peninsula, 98 km long, separating the Curonian Lagoon from the Baltic Sea. It stretches from the Sambian Peninsula in the Kaliningrad Oblast of Russia to Klaipėda in Lithuania.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a landscape of sand dunes that is under constant threat from natural forces and human stabilization efforts. It represents a vital cultural landscape shaped by human interaction with sand and wind over centuries.
highlights: Parnidis Dune (Nida), the Hill of Witches (Juodkrantė), the Efa Dune (Russia), and the mysterious Dancing Forest.

name: Ensemble of the Ferapontov Monastery
overview: The Ferapontov Monastery, in the Vologda region of northern Russia, is an exceptionally well-preserved and complete example of a Russian Orthodox monastic complex of the 15th–17th centuries.
history_significance: Famous for the frescoes painted by Dionisy, the greatest Russian artist of the end of the 15th century.
highlights: The Cathedral of the Nativity of the Virgin and Dionisy's frescoes.

name: Historic and Architectural Complex of the Kazan Kremlin
overview: Built on an ancient site, the Kazan Kremlin dates from the Muslim period of the Golden Horde and the Khanate of Kazan. It was conquered by Ivan the Terrible in 1552.
history_significance: The only surviving Tatar fortress in Russia and a symbol of the synthesis of Tatar and Russian architectural styles.
highlights: Kul Sharif Mosque, Annunciation Cathedral, and the Soyembika Tower.

name: Central Sikhote-Alin
overview: The Sikhote-Alin mountain range contains one of the richest and most unusual temperate forests of the world. It is a mixing zone between taiga and subtropics.
history_significance: The primary habitat of the endangered Amur Tiger (Siberian Tiger).
highlights: Sikhote-Alin Nature Reserve and the habitat of the Amur Tiger.

name: Citadel, Ancient City and Fortress Buildings of Derbent
overview: The Citadel, Ancient City and Fortress Buildings of Derbent were part of the northern lines of the Sassanian Persian Empire, which extended east and west of the Caspian Sea.
history_significance: One of the oldest cities in Russia and a strategic gateway between Europe and Asia (Caspian Gates).
highlights: Naryn-Kala Citadel and the ancient city walls.

name: Uvs Nuur Basin
overview: The Uvs Nuur Basin is a vast, enclosed drainage basin in Central Asia, surrounded by mountains and containing diverse ecosystems from desert to tundra. It is one of the best-preserved natural landscapes in the world.
history_significance: The property encompasses multiple protected areas across Mongolia and Russia, forming an outstanding example of ongoing ecological and biological processes in the steppes, cold deserts, and alpine regions of Central Asia.
highlights: Uvs Lake, Turgen Mountains, Tsagaan Shuvuut Mountain, snow leopards, and untouched steppe–desert–tundra transitions.

name: Natural System of Wrangel Island Reserve
overview: Located well above the Arctic Circle, this site includes the mountainous Wrangel Island and Herald Island. It has the highest level of biodiversity in the high Arctic.
history_significance: The world's largest concentration of Pacific walrus and the ancestral denning ground of the Polar Bear. It was also the last refuge of the Woolly Mammoth.
highlights: Polar Bears, Walruses, and the high Arctic tundra.

name: Ensemble of the Novodevichy Convent
overview: The Novodevichy Convent, in south-western Moscow, built in the 16th and 17th centuries in the so-called Moscow Baroque style, was part of a chain of monastic ensembles that were integrated into the defence system of the city.
history_significance: Closely linked to the political, cultural and religious history of Russia, and used by women of the Tsar's family.
highlights: Smolensk Cathedral and the adjacent Novodevichy Cemetery.

name: Historical Centre of the City of Yaroslavl
overview: Situated at the confluence of the Volga and Kotorosl Rivers, the historic center of Yaroslavl is an outstanding example of the urban planning reform ordered by Empress Catherine the Great in 1763.
history_significance: Famous for its 17th-century churches and the radial urban plan.
highlights: Church of Elijah the Prophet and the Spaso-Preobrazhensky Monastery.

name: Putorana Plateau
overview: This site coincides with the area of the Putoransky State Nature Reserve, located in the central part of the Putorana Plateau in northern Siberia. It features a pristine basalt trap landscape.
history_significance: A major stopover for bird migration and the habitat of the Putorana Bighorn Sheep.
highlights: The countless waterfalls, deep canyons, and trap rock formations.

name: Lena Pillars Nature Park
overview: The Lena Pillars Nature Park is marked by spectacular rock pillars that reach a height of approximately 100 m along the banks of the Lena River.
history_significance: Contains significant Cambrian fossil finds that document the early diversification of life (Cambrian Explosion).
highlights: The majestic stone pillars along the river and the Sinskye Stolby.

name: Bolgar Historical and Archaeological Complex
overview: This property lies on the shores of the Volga River. It contains evidence of the medieval city of Bolgar, an early settlement of the civilization of Volga-Bolgars.
history_significance: The place where the Volga Bolgars officially adopted Islam in 922 AD, and a sacred pilgrimage site for Tatars.
highlights: The Great Minaret, the Northern Mausoleum, and the White Mosque.

name: Landscapes of Dauria
overview: This transboundary property spanning Mongolia and Russia is an outstanding example of the Daurian Steppe ecosystem. It includes the cyclical wet and dry changes of the Torey Lakes.
history_significance: A critical habitat for millions of migratory birds on the East Asian-Australasian Flyway and the migration path of the Mongolian Gazelle.
highlights: Mongol Daguur Strictly Protected Area, Daursky Nature Reserve, and the Torey Lakes.

name: Assumption Cathedral and Monastery of the town-island of Sviyazhsk
overview: The Assumption Cathedral is located on the island-town of Sviyazhsk. It was founded by Ivan the Terrible in 1551 as an outpost for the conquest of the Kazan Khanate.
history_significance: Features a unique cycle of 16th-century mural paintings, including the rare depiction of St. Christopher with a horse's head.
highlights: The Assumption Cathedral frescoes and the island-town layout.

name: Churches of the Pskov School of Architecture
overview: This serial property comprises ten monuments in the historic city of Pskov: churches, cathedrals, monasteries, and administrative buildings built between the 12th and 17th centuries.
history_significance: Represent the Pskov School of Architecture, characterized by cubic volumes, domes, porches, and belfries, which influenced Russian architecture.
highlights: Cathedral of St. John the Baptist and the Mirozhsky Monastery.

locations.name: Cathedral of St. John the Baptist

name: Petroglyphs of Lake Onega and the White Sea
overview: The property contains 4,500 petroglyphs carved in the Neolithic period, located in two clusters: one on the shores of Lake Onega and the other on the White Sea.
history_significance: One of the largest sites of Neolithic rock art in Europe, documenting the culture of forest hunters and fishers.
highlights: Besov Nos (Lake Onega) and Zalavruga (White Sea).

name: Astronomical Observatories of Kazan Federal University
overview: This site comprises two component parts: the Kazan City Astronomical Observatory (1837) and the Engelhardt Astronomical Observatory (1901).
history_significance: Illustrates the transition from classical optical astronomy to astrophysics and the development of scientific instruments.
highlights: The historical telescope domes and the Meridian Circle.

name: Cultural Landscape of Kenozero Lake
overview: Located in Kenozero National Park, this landscape preserves the cultural traditions of the Russian North, with wooden churches, chapels, and holy groves harmoniously integrated into the lake environment.
history_significance: A living cultural landscape reflecting the synthesis of indigenous Finno-Ugric and Slavic traditions over centuries.
highlights: The wooden Chapel of St. Nicholas in Vershinino and the holy groves.

name: Rock Paintings of Shulgan-Tash Cave
overview: Located in the Southern Ural Mountains, the Shulgan-Tash Cave (also known as Kapova Cave) contains a remarkable collection of Upper Palaeolithic rock art dating back to 14,000–17,000 years ago.
history_significance: Contains some of the only examples of Palaeolithic cave painting in Eastern Europe, featuring red ochre depictions of mammoths, woolly rhinoceroses, horses, and geometric signs.
highlights: The Hall of Drawings (Chaos Hall), figures of mammoths and horses, and the Triple Grotto.

name: Flemish Béguinages
overview: The Béguinages were architectural ensembles composed of houses, churches, ancillary buildings, and green spaces, often enclosed by walls or moats. They were built by Beguines, women who dedicated their lives to God without retiring from the world.
history_significance: A unique testimony to the Beguine movement in North-West Europe during the Middle Ages, offering a specific architectural solution to a religious community.
highlights: Béguinage of Leuven (Great Beguinage), Béguinage of Bruges, and Béguinage of Mechelen.

name: La Grand-Place, Brussels
overview: La Grand-Place in Brussels is a remarkably homogeneous body of public and private buildings, dating mainly from the late 17th century. It is considered one of the most beautiful squares in the world.
history_significance: Illustrates the vitality of a commercial and political center in Northern Europe at the height of its prosperity.
highlights: The Town Hall (Hôtel de Ville), the King's House (Maison du Roi), and the opulent Guildhalls.

name: The Four Lifts on the Canal du Centre and their Environs, La Louvière and Le Roeulx
overview: The four hydraulic boat lifts on this short stretch of the historic Canal du Centre are industrial monuments of the highest quality. They were designed to overcome a gradient of 66 meters.
history_significance: The only remaining example of 19th-century hydraulic boat lifts still in their original working condition.
highlights: Lift No. 1 (Houdeng-Goegnies) and the surrounding industrial landscape.

name: Historic Centre of Brugge
overview: Bruges is an outstanding example of a medieval historic settlement, which has maintained its historic fabric as this has evolved over the centuries.
history_significance: Once a world commercial metropolis (Hanseatic League), it is known as the birthplace of the school of the Flemish Primitives in painting.
highlights: The canals, the Markt square, Church of Our Lady, and the Groeningemuseum.

name: Major Town Houses of the Architect Victor Horta (Brussels)
overview: The four major town houses – Hôtel Tassel, Hôtel Solvay, Hôtel van Eetvelde, and Maison & Atelier Horta – designed by Victor Horta are pioneering works of Art Nouveau.
history_significance: Horta radically revolutionized the 19th-century stylistic traditions by opening up the floor plan and using new materials like steel and glass.
highlights: Horta Museum (Maison & Atelier), the whiplash lines, and light-filled interiors.

name: Neolithic Flint Mines at Spiennes
overview: The Neolithic flint mines at Spiennes cover more than 100 hectares and are the largest and earliest concentration of ancient mines in Europe.
history_significance: Demonstrates the technological ingenuity of prehistoric man in extracting raw materials for tool-making.
highlights: The ancient mine shafts and the interpretive center.

name: Notre-Dame Cathedral in Tournai
overview: The Cathedral of Notre-Dame in Tournai was built in the first half of the 12th century. It is distinguished by a Romanesque nave of extraordinary dimensions and a Gothic choir.
history_significance: A masterpiece of the West, it blends the Romanesque style of the Scheldt valley with the early Gothic style of the Île-de-France.
highlights: The five massive towers and the reliquary shrine of Our Lady.

name: Plantin-Moretus House-Workshops-Museum Complex
overview: The Plantin-Moretus Museum is a printing plant and publishing house dating from the Renaissance and Baroque periods. It contains the two oldest surviving printing presses in the world.
history_significance: A unique testimony to the history of printing and the role of Antwerp as a major center of 16th-century humanism.
highlights: The original printing presses, the library, and the Rubens portraits.

name: Stoclet House
overview: Commissioned by the banker Adolphe Stoclet, this house was designed by the Austrian architect Josef Hoffmann in 1905. It marks a turning point from Art Nouveau to Art Deco.
history_significance: A masterpiece of the Vienna Secession (Wiener Werkstätte) realized in Brussels, integrating architecture and interior design.
highlights: The geometric exterior and the Klimt friezes inside (not open to public).

name: Major Mining Sites of Wallonia
overview: The four mining sites span the 170 km long coal basin. They feature the best-preserved 19th- and 20th-century coal mining architecture in Belgium.
history_significance: Illustrates the industrial revolution in continental Europe and the social utopian architecture of the 'cités ouvrières'.
highlights: Grand-Hornu (neoclassical complex), Bois-du-Luc, and Bois du Cazier.

name: Colonies of Benevolence
overview: A transnational serial property comprising four settlements: Frederiksoord, Wilhelminaoord, and Veenhuizen in the Netherlands, and Wortel in Belgium. They were established in the 19th century as a social experiment to alleviate poverty through domestic agricultural colonies.
history_significance: Witness to a unique utopian social experiment in poverty alleviation, based on the Enlightenment ideas of the 19th century.
highlights: Museum De Proefkolonie (Frederiksoord), National Prison Museum (Veenhuizen), and the cultural landscape of Wortel-Kolonie.

name: Historic Centre of Prague
overview: Built between the 11th and 18th centuries, the Old Town, the Lesser Town and the New Town speak of the great architectural and cultural influence enjoyed by this city since the Middle Ages.
history_significance: One of the most beautiful cities in Europe, illustrating the process of continuous urban growth from the Middle Ages to the present day.
highlights: Prague Castle, Charles Bridge, and the Old Town Square.

name: Historic Centre of Český Krumlov
overview: Situated on the banks of the Vltava river, the town was built around a 13th-century castle with Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque elements.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a small central European medieval town whose architectural heritage has remained intact for more than five centuries.
highlights: Český Krumlov Castle and the meandering Vltava River.

name: Historic Centre of Telč
overview: The houses in Telč, which stand on a hilltop, were originally built of wood. After a fire in the late 14th century, they were rebuilt in stone, surrounded by walls and strengthened by a network of artificial ponds.
history_significance: A masterpiece of town planning, featuring a triangular marketplace surrounded by Renaissance and Baroque houses with high gables and arcades.
highlights: Zachariáš of Hradec Square and Telč Château.

name: Pilgrimage Church of St John of Nepomuk at Zelená Hora
overview: This pilgrimage church, built in honour of St John of Nepomuk, is the final work of Jan Blazej Santini, a great architect of the Baroque period.
history_significance: A unique example of the 'Baroque Gothic' style, constructed on a star-shaped plan.
highlights: The Star-shaped Church layout.

locations.name: Pilgrimage Church of St John of Nepomuk

name: Kutná Hora: Historical Town Centre with the Church of St Barbara and the Cathedral of Our Lady at Sedlec
overview: Kutná Hora developed as a result of the exploitation of the silver mines. In the 14th century it became a royal city endowed with monuments that symbolized its prosperity.
history_significance: The Church of St Barbara and the Cathedral of Our Lady at Sedlec are masterpieces of Central European architecture, influencing buildings across the region.
highlights: St Barbara's Church and the Sedlec Ossuary (Bone Church).

locations.name: Cathedral of Our Lady at Sedlec

name: Lednice-Valtice Cultural Landscape
overview: Between the 17th and 20th centuries, the ruling Dukes of Liechtenstein transformed their domains in southern Moravia into a striking landscape.
history_significance: One of the largest artificial landscapes in Europe, blending Baroque, Classical, and Neo-Gothic architecture with English romantic gardens.
highlights: Lednice Palace, Valtice Palace, and the Minaret.

name: Gardens and Castle at Kroměříž
overview: Kroměříž stands on the site of an earlier ford across the River Morava. The gardens and castle are an exceptionally complete and well-preserved example of a European Baroque princely residence.
history_significance: The Flower Garden represents the transition from Italian to French garden styles.
highlights: Archbishop's Chateau and the Flower Garden (Květná zahrada).

name: Holašovice Historic Village
overview: Holašovice is an exceptionally complete and well-preserved example of a traditional central European village.
history_significance: Contains a number of high-quality vernacular buildings from the 18th and 19th centuries in the 'South Bohemian Folk Baroque' style.
highlights: The village green and the gable-fronted farmsteads.

name: Litomyšl Castle
overview: Litomyšl Castle is an arcade castle built in the Renaissance style, which was developed in Italy and then adopted and modified in the Czech lands.
history_significance: An outstanding example of an aristocratic residence from the High Renaissance, featuring sgraffito decorations.
highlights: The sgraffito facades and the castle theatre.

name: Holy Trinity Column in Olomouc
overview: This memorial column, erected in the early years of the 18th century, is the most outstanding example of a type of monument specific to central Europe.
history_significance: A unique example of Central European Baroque art, symbolizing gratitude for the end of a plague epidemic.
highlights: The Holy Trinity Column on the Upper Square.

name: Tugendhat Villa in Brno
overview: The Tugendhat Villa in Brno, designed by the architect Mies van der Rohe, is an outstanding example of the international style in the modern movement in architecture.
history_significance: A pioneering work of modern architecture using a steel frame, glass walls, and an open plan.
highlights: The Onyx Wall and the main living area.

name: Jewish Quarter and St Procopius' Basilica in Třebíč
overview: The ensemble of the Jewish Quarter, the old Jewish cemetery, and the Basilica of St Procopius are reminders of the co-existence of Jewish and Christian cultures from the Middle Ages to the 20th century.
history_significance: One of the best-preserved Jewish ghettos in Europe and a symbol of interfaith tolerance.
highlights: Rear Synagogue, Jewish Cemetery, and St Procopius' Basilica.

name: Landscape for Breeding and Training of Ceremonial Carriage Horses at Kladruby nad Labem
overview: This site is a specialized landscape on the Elbe plain, designed for the breeding and training of Kladruber horses, a breed used for ceremonial purposes by the Habsburg imperial court.
history_significance: One of the oldest stud farms in the world, operating continuously since 1579.
highlights: National Stud Farm and the cultural landscape.

locations.name: National Stud Farm Kladruby nad Labem

name: Žatec and the Landscape of Saaz Hops
overview: This cultural landscape has been shaped by the cultivation and processing of the world-renowned Saaz hop variety for over 700 years.
history_significance: Illustrates the complete process of hop cultivation, processing, and trading, featuring unique hop-drying kilns and the historic town center.
highlights: Historic Centre of Žatec, Hop Museum, and Trnovany Hop Drying Kilns.

name: Historic Centre of Kraków
overview: The historic centre of Kraków, the former capital of Poland, is situated at the foot of the Royal Wawel Castle. It contains Europe's largest market square and numerous historical houses, palaces and churches.
history_significance: A masterpiece of medieval European urban planning and the spiritual heart of Poland.
highlights: Main Market Square (Rynek Główny), Wawel Castle, St. Mary's Basilica, and Kazimierz (Jewish Quarter).

name: Wieliczka and Bochnia Royal Salt Mines
overview: This serial property consists of the Wieliczka and Bochnia salt mines and the Wieliczka Saltworks Castle. They illustrate the historic stages of the development of mining techniques in Europe from the 13th to the 20th centuries.
history_significance: One of the world's oldest operating salt mines, featuring underground chapels, lakes, and statues carved from salt.
highlights: Chapel of St. Kinga (Wieliczka) and the underground tourist routes.

name: Auschwitz Birkenau
overview: The fortified walls, barbed wire, platforms, barracks, gallows, gas chambers and cremation ovens show the conditions within which the Nazi genocide took place in the former concentration and extermination camp of Auschwitz-Birkenau.
history_significance: A symbol of humanity's cruelty to its fellow human beings in the 20th century. The largest concentration camp of the Third Reich.
highlights: Auschwitz I (Stammlager), Auschwitz II-Birkenau, and the Memorial.

name: Białowieża Forest
overview: Situated on the watershed of the Baltic and Black Seas, this immense forest range consists of evergreens and broad-leaved trees. It is the last and largest remaining part of the immense primeval forest that once spread across the European Plain.
history_significance: Home to the largest free-roaming population of the European Bison, the continent's heaviest land animal. It is an irreplaceable area for biodiversity conservation.
highlights: The Strict Protection Area, European Bison Show Reserve, and the Royal Oaks Trail.

name: Historic Centre of Warsaw
overview: During the Warsaw Uprising in August 1944, more than 85% of Warsaw's historic centre was destroyed by Nazi troops. After the war, a five-year reconstruction campaign resulted in the meticulous restoration of the Old Town.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a near-total reconstruction of a span of history covering the 13th to the 20th century.
highlights: Royal Castle, Old Town Market Place, and the Barbican.

name: Old City of Zamość
overview: Zamość was founded in the 16th century by the chancellor Jan Zamoyski on the trade route linking western and northern Europe with the Black Sea.
history_significance: A perfect example of a late 16th-century Renaissance town, designed by the Italian architect Bernardo Morando (the 'Padua of the North').
highlights: Great Market Square, Town Hall, and the fortifications.

name: Castle of the Teutonic Order in Malbork
overview: This 13th-century fortified monastery belonging to the Teutonic Order was substantially enlarged and embellished after 1309, when the seat of the Grand Master moved from Venice to Malbork.
history_significance: The largest castle in the world measured by land area and a supreme example of a medieval brick castle.
highlights: The High Castle, Middle Castle, and the Grand Master's Palace.

name: Medieval Town of Toruń
overview: Toruń owes its origins to the Teutonic Order, which built a castle there in the mid-13th century as a base for the conquest and evangelization of Prussia.
history_significance: A major Hanseatic trading centre and the birthplace of Nicolaus Copernicus. It preserves a remarkably intact medieval street pattern.
highlights: Old Town Hall, Copernicus House, and the Leaning Tower.

name: Kalwaria Zebrzydowska: The Mannerist Architectural and Park Landscape Complex and Pilgrimage Park
overview: Kalwaria Zebrzydowska is a breathtaking cultural landscape of great spiritual significance. Its natural setting was used to lay out a series of symbolic places of worship relating to the Passion of Jesus Christ.
history_significance: A unique example of a Calvary shrine (Kalwaria) in the Mannerist style, which influenced other pilgrimages in Central Europe.
highlights: The Basilica of St. Mary and the chapels of the Way of the Cross.

name: Churches of Peace in Jawor and Świdnica
overview: The Churches of Peace in Jawor and Świdnica are the largest timber-framed religious buildings in Europe. They were built in the mid-17th century by the Lutherans of Silesia.
history_significance: A testimony to the quest for religious freedom and a rare expression of Lutheran ideology in an idiom generally associated with the Catholic Church.
highlights: The timber-framed structures and the baroque interiors.

name: Wooden Churches of Southern Małopolska
overview: The wooden churches of southern Little Poland (Małopolska) represent outstanding examples of the different aspects of medieval church-building traditions in Roman Catholic culture.
history_significance: Built using the horizontal log technique, these churches display high artistic quality and date from the Middle Ages.
highlights: St Michael the Archangel's Church in Binarowa and the church in Dębno.

name: Centennial Hall in Wrocław
overview: The Centennial Hall, a landmark in the history of reinforced concrete architecture, was erected in 1911–1913 by the architect Max Berg.
history_significance: A pioneering work of modern engineering and architecture, demonstrating the potential of reinforced concrete.
highlights: The massive concrete dome and the Four Domes Pavilion.

name: Wooden Tserkvas of the Carpathian Region in Poland and Ukraine
overview: Situated in the eastern fringe of the Carpathian Mountains, this transnational property numbers 16 tserkvas (churches). They were built of horizontal wooden logs between the 16th and 19th centuries.
history_significance: Outstanding examples of the once widespread Orthodox and Greek Catholic ecclesiastical timber building tradition.
highlights: St Michael the Archangel's Tserkva (Brunary Wyżne) and St Paraskeva's Tserkva (Radruż).

locations.name: St Michael the Archangel's Tserkva (Brunary Wyżne)
Birth of the Blessed Virgin Mary Tserkva (Chotyniec)

name: Tarnowskie Góry Lead-Silver-Zinc Mine and its Underground Water Management System
overview: Located in Upper Silesia, this site includes the entire underground mine with adits, shafts, galleries, and a water management system.
history_significance: A masterpiece of hydraulic engineering, featuring a gravity-fed water drainage system used for centuries.
highlights: Black Trout Adit and the Historic Silver Mine.

name: Krzemionki Prehistoric Striped Flint Mining Region
overview: Krzemionki is an ensemble of four mining sites, dating from the Neolithic to the Bronze Age (c. 3900–1600 BCE), dedicated to the extraction and processing of striped flint.
history_significance: One of the most comprehensive prehistoric underground flint extraction and processing systems in the world.
highlights: The underground mining galleries and the preserved Neolithic landscape.

name: Schokland and Surroundings
overview: Schokland was a peninsula that became an island by the 15th century. Occupied and then abandoned as the sea encroached, it was reclaimed from the sea in the 1940s.
history_significance: Symbolizes the heroic, age-old struggle of the people of the Netherlands against the encroachment of the waters.
highlights: The former island contours, the church ruins, and the Museum Schokland.

name: Dutch Water Defence Lines
overview: This site comprises the Defence Line of Amsterdam (Stelling van Amsterdam) and the New Dutch Waterline. It is a defense system based on the control of water (inundation) to protect the country.
history_significance: A unique example of hydraulic engineering used for defense purposes, extending over 200 km.
highlights: Fort Pampus, Muiden Castle, Fort Vechten, and the fortified town of Naarden.

name: Mill Network at Kinderdijk-Elshout
overview: The network of 19 windmills in Kinderdijk-Elshout demonstrates the outstanding contribution made by the people of the Netherlands to the technology of handling water.
history_significance: Constructed around 1740, this is the largest concentration of old windmills in the Netherlands.
highlights: The alignments of windmills along the canals and the pumping stations.

name: Historic Area of Willemstad, Inner City and Harbour, Curaçao
overview: The people of the Netherlands established a trading settlement at a fine natural harbour on the Caribbean island of Curaçao in 1634. The architecture blends Dutch and Spanish colonial styles.
history_significance: A remarkably preserved example of a Dutch colonial trading settlement in the Caribbean.
highlights: Handelskade waterfront, Queen Emma Pontoon Bridge, and Fort Amsterdam.

name: Ir.D.F. Woudagemaal (D.F. Wouda Steam Pumping Station)
overview: The Wouda Pumping Station is the largest steam-pumping station ever built and is still in operation. It was opened in 1920 to protect the citizens of Friesland from flooding.
history_significance: A masterpiece of the Amsterdam School architects and a high point in the application of steam power to hydraulic engineering.
highlights: The Machine Hall with its four steam engines and centrifugal pumps.

name: Droogmakerij de Beemster (Beemster Polder)
overview: The Beemster Polder is the oldest of the reclaimed lands of the Netherlands, dating from the early 17th century. It preserves its landscape of fields, roads, canals, dykes, and settlements.
history_significance: A creative masterpiece of landscape planning and hydraulic engineering using classical Renaissance principles.
highlights: The geometric grid pattern of roads and canals, and the historic farmhouses.

name: Rietveld Schröderhuis (Rietveld Schröder House)
overview: Built in 1924 by the architect Gerrit Rietveld, this house is the manifesto of the De Stijl group of artists and architects.
history_significance: An icon of the Modern Movement in architecture, featuring a flexible spatial arrangement and primary colors.
highlights: The open-plan upper floor with sliding walls and the distinctive exterior.

name: Seventeenth-Century Canal Ring Area of Amsterdam inside the Singelgracht
overview: The historic urban ensemble of the canal district of Amsterdam was a project for a new 'port city' built at the end of the 16th and beginning of the 17th centuries.
history_significance: A masterpiece of hydraulic engineering and town planning, facilitating the city's Golden Age trade and cultural expansion.
highlights: Herengracht, Keizersgracht, Prinsengracht, and the gabled canal houses.

name: Van Nellefabriek (Van Nelle Factory)
overview: Designed and built in the 1920s on the banks of a canal, the Van Nelle Factory is an icon of 20th-century industrial architecture.
history_significance: A prime example of the International Style and Constructivism, symbolizing the modernization of the Netherlands.
highlights: The glass and steel facades, and the internal transport bridges.

name: Eise Eisinga Planetarium
overview: Built between 1774 and 1781 by Eise Eisinga in his living room, this is the oldest continuously operating planetarium in the world.
history_significance: An outstanding example of an 18th-century orrery, demonstrating the scale of the solar system and the movement of planets with remarkable accuracy.
highlights: The moving ceiling model of the solar system and the clockwork mechanism.

name: Royal Domain of Drottningholm
overview: Located on an island in Lake Mälaren, the Royal Domain of Drottningholm features the palace, a theatre, and magnificent gardens. It is the finest example of an 18th-century northern European royal residence.
history_significance: Inspired by the Palace of Versailles, it serves as the private residence of the Swedish royal family.
highlights: Drottningholm Palace, The Chinese Pavilion, and the Drottningholm Palace Theatre.

name: Birka and Hovgården
overview: Located on two islands (Björkö and Adelsö) in Lake Mälaren, Birka and Hovgården bear witness to the trading network of the Vikings during the 9th and 10th centuries.
history_significance: Birka was a key trading center, and Hovgården was the royal residence governing it. They provide exceptional evidence of the Viking Age.
highlights: The Black Earth harbour area, the hillfort, and the Ansgar Cross.

name: Engelsberg Ironworks
overview: Engelsberg is the best-preserved example of a Swedish iron-working estate from the 17th to 19th centuries. It includes a manor house, smelting house, and forge.
history_significance: Testifies to Sweden's role as a major iron producer in the 17th and 18th centuries, utilizing high-quality ore and timber.
highlights: The smelting house and the blast furnace.

name: Rock Carvings in Tanum
overview: The rock carvings in Tanum, in the north of Bohuslän, are a unique artistic achievement. They depict varied scenes of Bronze Age life, warfare, and rituals.
history_significance: Provides exceptional evidence of the social life and beliefs of the Bronze Age people in Europe.
highlights: Vitlycke Rock Carvings (The Bridal Couple) and the museum.

name: Skogskyrkogården
overview: This cemetery was created between 1917 and 1920 by two young architects, Asplund and Lewerentz. It blends architecture and vegetation to create a landscape of great beauty.
history_significance: A masterpiece of modern architecture and landscape design, influencing cemetery design worldwide.
highlights: The Woodland Chapel, The Chapel of the Holy Cross, and the Granite Cross.

name: Hanseatic Town of Visby
overview: Visby is a former Viking site on the island of Gotland. From the 12th to the 14th century, it was the main centre of the Hanseatic League in the Baltic.
history_significance: The most well-preserved fortified commercial city in northern Europe, surrounded by a 3.5 km medieval ring wall.
highlights: The City Wall, St. Mary's Cathedral, and the medieval church ruins.

name: Church Town of Gammelstad, Luleå
overview: Gammelstad is the best-preserved example of a 'church village' found in northern Scandinavia. It consists of 424 wood-built cottages huddled around the early 15th-century stone church.
history_significance: Illustrates the tradition of church villages where parishioners from remote areas stayed overnight for church services.
highlights: Nederluleå Church and the surrounding red cottages.

name: Laponian Area
overview: This is the largest unmodified nature area in the world still cultured by an indigenous people, the Saami. It features majestic mountains, glaciers, and wetlands.
history_significance: A combined natural and cultural heritage site, preserving the ancestral way of life of the Saami people based on reindeer herding.
highlights: Sarek National Park, Rapadalen Delta, and Padjelanta.

name: Naval Port of Karlskrona
overview: Founded in 1680, Karlskrona is an outstanding example of a European planned naval city of the late 17th century.
history_significance: Reflects the ambition of Sweden as a major power and features exceptionally well-preserved naval architecture and fortifications.
highlights: Kungsholms Fort, Drottningskär Citadel, and the Naval Museum.

name: Agricultural Landscape of Southern Öland
overview: The southern part of the island of Öland consists of a vast limestone plateau (Stora Alvaret). It has been shaped by human settlement and agriculture for 5,000 years.
history_significance: A unique example of human adaptation to a physical constraint (thin soil on limestone), preserving medieval land-use patterns.
highlights: Stora Alvaret, traditional windmills, and linear villages.

name: Mining Area of the Great Copper Mountain in Falun
overview: The Great Copper Mountain was the most important copper producing area in the world in the 17th century. The site includes the mine, the town, and the surrounding industrial landscape.
history_significance: Influenced mining technology worldwide and funded Sweden's era as a great power. Famous for the 'Falu red' paint.
highlights: The Great Pit (Stora Stöten), the Mine Museum, and Old Falun.

name: High Coast / Kvarken Archipelago
overview: This transboundary site illustrates the rapid land uplift process following the last Ice Age. It comprises the High Coast (Sweden), with its steep rugged hills, and the Kvarken Archipelago (Finland), featuring low-lying moraine islands.
history_significance: A global reference area for understanding isostatic uplift (land rising after the weight of glaciers is removed), forming unique geological landscapes.
highlights: Skuleberget (Sweden), Slåttdalsskrevan (Sweden), and the De Geer moraines (Finland).

name: Struve Geodetic Arc
overview: The Struve Arc is a chain of survey triangulations stretching 2,820 km from Hammerfest in Norway to the Black Sea in Ukraine. It yielded the first accurate measurement of a long segment of a meridian.
history_significance: An extraordinary example of scientific collaboration among monarchs and scientists of different countries for a common scientific cause in the 19th century.
highlights: Meridian Column in Hammerfest, Tartu Observatory, and the Fuglenes Point.

name: Grimeton Radio Station, Varberg
overview: Built in 1922-24, Grimeton Radio Station is an exceptionally well-preserved monument to early wireless transatlantic communication.
history_significance: The only surviving example of a major transmitting station based on pre-electronic technology (Alexanderson alternator).
highlights: The six antenna towers and the Alexanderson transmitter.

name: Decorated Farmhouses of Hälsingland
overview: This serial property comprises seven timber farmhouses in eastern Sweden, representing the zenith of a regional timber building tradition.
history_significance: Famous for their elaborately decorated interiors (festoons) painted by traveling artists, reflecting the prosperity of independent farmers.
highlights: Erik-Anders, Jon-Lars, and Pallars farmhouses.

name: Fossil Hominid Sites of South Africa
overview: Known as the 'Cradle of Humankind', this site contains a vast number of hominid fossils dating back 3.5 million years. It provides crucial evidence for the evolution of humans.
history_significance: Contains the Sterkfontein, Swartkrans, and Kromdraai caves, which have yielded some of the world's most significant hominid fossils, including 'Mrs. Ples' and 'Little Foot'.
highlights: Sterkfontein Caves and the Maropeng Visitor Centre.

name: Robben Island
overview: Located off the coast of Cape Town, Robben Island was used as a prison, a hospital for socially unacceptable groups, and a military base between the 17th and 20th centuries.
history_significance: Famous as the place where Nelson Mandela and other political prisoners were incarcerated during the apartheid era, symbolizing the triumph of the human spirit.
highlights: Nelson Mandela's prison cell and the lime quarry.

name: iSimangaliso Wetland Park
overview: iSimangaliso means 'miracle and wonder'. It contains three major lake systems, eight interlinking ecosystems, most of South Africa's remaining swamp forests, and 526 bird species.
history_significance: Features ongoing fluvial, marine, and aeolian processes, including coral reefs, long sandy beaches, coastal dunes, and lake systems.
highlights: Lake St. Lucia, Cape Vidal, and the Sodwana Bay coral reefs.

name: Maloti-Drakensberg Park
overview: A transnational property spanning the border between South Africa and Lesotho. It features spectacular basalt buttresses, dramatic cutbacks, and golden sandstone ramparts.
history_significance: Contains the largest and most concentrated group of paintings in Africa south of the Sahara, made by the San people over 4,000 years.
highlights: The Amphitheatre, Giant's Castle, and the San rock art sites.

name: Mapungubwe Cultural Landscape
overview: Mapungubwe is set against the northern border of South Africa, joining Zimbabwe and Botswana. It was the largest kingdom in the subcontinent before it was abandoned in the 14th century.
history_significance: Evidence of a highly sophisticated civilization that traded gold and ivory with China and India. Famous for the golden rhino artifact.
highlights: Mapungubwe Hill and the Interpretive Centre.

name: Cape Floral Region Protected Areas
overview: A serial property consisting of 13 protected areas. It is one of the richest areas for plants in the world, containing nearly 20% of Africa's total flora.
history_significance: Represents outstanding ecological and biological processes associated with the Fynbos biome.
highlights: Table Mountain National Park, De Hoop Nature Reserve, and Kirstenbosch Botanical Garden.

name: Vredefort Dome
overview: The Vredefort Dome is the oldest, largest, and most deeply eroded meteorite impact site in the world. It dates back 2.023 billion years.
history_significance: Provides critical evidence of the Earth's geological history and the magnitude of energy released during a major asteroid impact.
highlights: The ring structure visible from space and geological outcrops.

name: Richtersveld Cultural and Botanical Landscape
overview: A mountainous desert landscape in the north-west of the country. It is owned and managed by the Nama people, who practice seasonal pastoralism.
history_significance: The only area where the Nama people still construct portable rush-mat houses (haru om) and maintain their traditional transhumance lifestyle.
highlights: The orange River valley and the seasonal wild flowers.

name: ǂKhomani Cultural Landscape
overview: Located at the border with Botswana and Namibia, this vast sand dune area contains evidence of human occupation from the Stone Age to the present.
history_significance: Associated with the ǂKhomani San people and their unique strategies for survival in the harsh desert environment.
highlights: Kgalagadi Transfrontier Park dunes and San heritage sites.

name: Barberton Makhonjwa Mountains
overview: This site comprises 40% of the Barberton Greenstone Belt, one of the world's oldest geological structures.
history_significance: Contains the best-preserved succession of volcanic and sedimentary rock dating back 3.6 to 3.25 billion years, offering a glimpse into the early Earth.
highlights: Geotrail and the ancient rock formations.

name: The Emergence of Modern Human Behaviour: The Pleistocene Occupation Sites of South Africa
overview: A serial property comprising three archaeological sites: Diepkloof Rock Shelter, Pinnacle Point Site Complex, and Sibhudu Cave.
history_significance: Provides the most complete record of the development of modern human behaviour, including the use of symbols and complex tools, dating back 162,000 years.
highlights: Evidence of early symbolic thought and systematic use of marine resources.

name: Human Rights, Liberation and Reconciliation: Nelson Mandela Legacy Sites
overview: A serial property of 14 components located across four provinces. It reflects key events in the struggle against apartheid and the building of a democratic South Africa.
history_significance: Commemorates the legacy of Nelson Mandela and the liberation struggle, emphasizing human rights and reconciliation.
highlights: Union Buildings, Sharpeville Memorial, and The Great Place at Mqhekezweni.

name: Los Glaciares National Park
overview: The Los Glaciares National Park is an area of exceptional natural beauty, with rugged, towering mountains and numerous glacial lakes, including Lake Argentino which is 160 km long.
history_significance: Contains some of the few glaciers in the world that are advancing rather than retreating, most notably the Perito Moreno Glacier.
highlights: Perito Moreno Glacier, Mount Fitz Roy, and Lake Argentino.

name: Iguazu National Park
overview: Located in Misiones Province, the park features the spectacular semicircular waterfall at the heart of this site, which is some 80 m high and 2,700 m in diameter.
history_significance: Together with its sister park in Brazil, it preserves the highly diverse Atlantic Forest biodiversity and one of the world's largest waterfalls.
highlights: Devil's Throat (Garganta del Diablo) and the surrounding subtropical rainforest.

name: Cueva de las Manos, Río Pinturas
overview: The Cueva de las Manos, Río Pinturas, contains an exceptional assemblage of cave art, executed between 13,000 and 9,500 years ago.
history_significance: Famous for the stenciled outlines of human hands, as well as depictions of animals like guanacos, hunting scenes, and geometric shapes.
highlights: The Hand Paintings (positive and negative) and the Río Pinturas canyon landscape.

name: Península Valdés
overview: A site of global significance for the conservation of marine mammals. It is located on the Atlantic coast and features diverse coastal geomforms.
history_significance: Important breeding ground for the southern right whale, southern elephant seals, and southern sea lions. Orcas here have developed a unique hunting strategy.
highlights: Whale watching (Puerto Pirámides), Punta Norte (Orcas), and Caleta Valdés.

name: Ischigualasto / Talampaya Natural Parks
overview: These two contiguous parks contain the most complete continental fossil record known from the Triassic Period (245-208 million years ago).
history_significance: Reveals the evolution of vertebrates and the nature of palaeo-environments in the Triassic period. Talampaya is known for its red sandstone cliffs.
highlights: Valley of the Moon (Ischigualasto) and the Talampaya Canyon.

locations.name: Ischigualasto Provincial Park (Valley of the Moon)

name: Jesuit Block and Estancias of Córdoba
overview: The core of the former Jesuit Province of Paraguay contains the University, the church, the residence of the Society of Jesus, and the college, along with five farming estates (estancias).
history_significance: Illustrates the unique religious, social, and economic experiment carried out by the Jesuits in South America for over 150 years.
highlights: The Jesuit Block (Manzana Jesuítica) in Córdoba city and the Estancia of Alta Gracia.

name: Quebrada de Humahuaca
overview: This narrow mountain valley traces the line of a major cultural route, the Camino Inca, along the spectacular valley of the Rio Grande.
history_significance: A strategic trade route for 10,000 years, showcasing the evolution of pre-Hispanic and colonial settlements.
highlights: The Hill of Seven Colors (Purmamarca), Pucará de Tilcara, and the Serranía de Hornocal.

name: Qhapaq Ñan, Andean Road System
overview: This extensive Inca communication, trade, and defence network of roads covers 30,000 km. Constructed by the Incas over several centuries and partly based on pre-Inca infrastructure, this extraordinary network reaches one of the world's most extreme geographical terrains.
history_significance: A masterpiece of engineering that integrated the immense Tawantinsuyu empire, linking snow-capped Andes to the coast and rainforests across six countries.
highlights: Ingapirca (Ecuador), Cusco (Peru), and Puente del Inca (Argentina).

name: Los Alerces National Park
overview: Located in the Andes of northern Patagonia, the park protects the last portions of the Patagonian Forest in an almost pristine state.
history_significance: Vital for the protection of the Alerce tree (Fitzroya cupressoides), one of the longest-living tree species in the world.
highlights: The millennial Alerce forests and Lake Menéndez.

name: ESMA Site Museum - Former Clandestine Centre of Detention, Torture and Extermination
overview: The former Officers' Quarters of the Navy School of Mechanics (ESMA) was the largest clandestine detention center during the military dictatorship (1976-1983).
history_significance: A testimony to the state terrorism and a symbol of the memory of the disappeared (Desaparecidos).
highlights: The Site Museum and the memory space.

name: City of Cuzco
overview: Developed under the Inca ruler Pachacutec into a complex urban centre with distinct religious and administrative functions. It was preserved by the Spanish conquerors who built Baroque churches on Inca foundations.
history_significance: The former capital of the Inca Empire and a masterpiece of the fusion of Inca and Hispanic architecture.
highlights: Plaza de Armas, Qurikancha (Temple of the Sun), and the fortress of Sacsayhuamán.

name: Historic Sanctuary of Machu Picchu
overview: Standing 2,430 m above sea level, in the middle of a tropical mountain forest, Machu Picchu was probably the most amazing urban creation of the Inca Empire at its height.
history_significance: An outstanding example of Inca landscape architecture and planning, remaining hidden from the outside world until 1911.
highlights: The Citadel, Huayna Picchu, and the Intihuatana stone.

name: Chavin (Archaeological Site)
overview: The archaeological site of Chavin gave its name to the culture that developed between 1500 and 300 B.C. in this high valley of the Peruvian Andes.
history_significance: A major ceremonial center and pilgrimage site, known for its complex underground galleries and stone carvings like the Lanzón.
highlights: The Old Temple, The New Temple, and the Lanzón Monolith.

name: Huascarán National Park
overview: Situated in the Cordillera Blanca, the world's highest tropical mountain range, Mount Huascarán rises to 6,768 m. The deep ravines watered by numerous torrents, glacial lakes, and varied vegetation form a spectacular landscape.
history_significance: Protects a high diversity of flora and fauna, including the Spectacled Bear and the Andean Condor.
highlights: Mount Huascarán, Llanganuco Lakes, and Pastoruri Glacier.

name: Chan Chan Archaeological Zone
overview: Chan Chan, the capital of the Chimú Kingdom, reached its zenith in the 15th century, shortly before falling to the Incas. It was the largest earthen architecture city in pre-Columbian America.
history_significance: A masterpiece of town planning with distinct citadels (palaces) marked by high earthen walls.
highlights: Tschudi Palace (Nik An), the friezes, and the ceremonial plazas.

name: Manú National Park
overview: This huge 1.5 million-hectare park has successive tiers of vegetation rising from 150 to 4,200 m above sea level. The tropical forest in the lower tiers is home to an unrivaled variety of animal and plant species.
history_significance: One of the most biodiverse areas on Earth, protecting uncontacted indigenous tribes.
highlights: Salvador Lake (Cocha Salvador), Manu River, and clay licks.

name: Historic Centre of Lima
overview: Founded by Francisco Pizarro in 1535, the 'City of Kings' was the capital of the Spanish Viceroyalty of Peru. Its historic centre preserves many colonial buildings with balconies.
history_significance: Illustrates the architectural fusion of Hispano-American Baroque and the Creole style.
highlights: Plaza Mayor, Monastery of San Francisco (Catacombs), and the Cathedral of Lima.

locations.name: Basilica and Convent of San Francisco

name: Río Abiseo National Park
overview: The park was created in 1983 to protect the fauna and flora of the rainforests characteristic of this region of the Andes. It is also home to over 30 pre-Hispanic archaeological sites.
history_significance: Contains the Gran Pajatén ruins, a masterpiece of the Chachapoyas culture.
highlights: Gran Pajatén archaeological site and the Yellow-tailed Woolly Monkey habitat.

name: Lines and Geoglyphs of Nasca and Palpa
overview: Located in the arid coastal plain of Peru, these huge geoglyphs dating from 500 B.C. to A.D. 500 depict living creatures, stylized plants, and geometric figures.
history_significance: One of the great enigmas of archaeology, believed to have had ritual astronomical functions.
highlights: The Hummingbird, The Monkey, The Spider, and the Observation Tower.

name: Historical Centre of the City of Arequipa
overview: Built in volcanic sillar rock, Arequipa represents an integration of European and native building techniques. The city is dominated by snow-capped volcanoes.
history_significance: A masterpiece of the creative integration of European and Native characteristics, crucial to the colonial expression.
highlights: Santa Catalina Monastery, Plaza de Armas, and the Cathedral of Arequipa.

name: Sacred City of Caral-Supe
overview: The 5000-year-old city of Caral is the oldest centre of civilization in the Americas. It features complex architecture with six large pyramidal structures.
history_significance: Testifies to the genesis of Andean civilization, flourishing simultaneously with Egypt and Mesopotamia.
highlights: The Greater Pyramid and the Circular Altar.

name: Chankillo Archaeoastronomical Complex
overview: This prehistoric site (250-200 BC) functions as a solar calendar. It includes the Fortified Temple and the Thirteen Towers running along a hill ridge.
history_significance: The oldest solar observatory in the Americas, demonstrating sophisticated astronomical knowledge.
highlights: The Thirteen Towers and the Fortified Temple.

name: Abbey of St Gall
overview: The Convent of St Gall, a perfect example of a great Carolingian monastery, was, from the 8th century to its secularization in 1805, one of the most important in Europe.
history_significance: Its library is one of the richest and oldest in the world, containing precious manuscripts. The architectural plan (Plan of St Gall) is the only surviving major architectural drawing from the early Middle Ages.
highlights: The Abbey Library (Stiftsbibliothek), the Baroque Cathedral, and the Abbey Plan.

name: Benedictine Convent of St John at Müstair
overview: The Convent of Müstair, which stands in a valley in the Grisons, is a good example of Christian monastic renovation during the Carolingian period.
history_significance: It preserves the greatest series of figurative murals in the world, painted c. AD 800, along with Romanesque frescoes and stuccoes.
highlights: The Carolingian frescoes and the Plantatower.

name: Old City of Berne
overview: Founded in the 12th century on a hill site surrounded by the Aare River, Berne developed over the centuries in line with a coherent planning concept.
history_significance: Retains its original medieval character with 15th-century arcades and 16th-century fountains. It was the home of Einstein when he developed the Theory of Relativity.
highlights: The Zytglogge (Clock Tower), the arcades (Lauben), and the Cathedral (Münster).

name: Three Castles, Defensive Wall and Ramparts of the Market-Town of Bellinzona
overview: The Bellinzona site consists of a group of fortifications grouped around the castle of Castelgrande, which stands on a rocky peak looking out over the entire Ticino valley.
history_significance: A key strategic point controlling the route to the Alpine passes, featuring the only visible example of medieval military architecture in the Alpine arc.
highlights: Castelgrande, Montebello Castle, and Sasso Corbaro Castle.

name: Swiss Alps Jungfrau-Aletsch
overview: This site is an outstanding example of the formation of the High Alps, including the most glaciated part of the mountain range and the Aletsch Glacier, the largest in Eurasia.
history_significance: Provides classic examples of glacial phenomena and is a world-famous center for mountaineering and literary inspiration.
highlights: Aletsch Glacier, Jungfraujoch, and the peaks of Eiger, Mönch, and Jungfrau.

name: Lavaux, Vineyard Terraces
overview: The Lavaux vineyard terraces, stretching for about 30 km along the south-facing northern shores of Lake Geneva, date back to the 11th century when Benedictine and Cistercian monasteries controlled the area.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a centuries-long interaction between people and their environment to optimize local resources for producing highly valued wine.
highlights: The terraced vineyards, Epesses, and Saint-Saphorin.

name: Swiss Tectonic Arena Sardona
overview: The site features the Glarus Overthrust, a dramatic geological phenomenon where older rock layers (250-300 million years old) were thrust over younger rocks (35-50 million years old).
history_significance: A key site for the science of geology, demonstrating the process of mountain building (orogeny) and plate tectonics clearly visible in the landscape.
highlights: The 'Magic Line' of the Glarus Overthrust and Martinsloch.

name: La Chaux-de-Fonds / Le Locle, Watchmaking Town Planning
overview: These twin towns in the Jura mountains illustrate a unique urban planning concept dedicated to the watchmaking industry. The streets are laid out in a grid to maximize daylight for the workshops.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a mono-industrial manufacturing town that transitioned from artisanal to factory production.
highlights: The International Watchmaking Museum and the grid street layout.

name: Historic Centre of the City of Salzburg
overview: Salzburg has managed to preserve an extraordinarily rich urban fabric, developed over the period from the Middle Ages to the 19th century when it was a city-state ruled by a prince-archbishop.
history_significance: Famous as the birthplace of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart and for its baroque architecture north of the Alps.
highlights: Hohensalzburg Fortress, Salzburg Cathedral, and Getreidegasse (Mozart's Birthplace).

name: Palace and Gardens of Schönbrunn
overview: From the 18th century to 1918, Schönbrunn was the residence of the Habsburg emperors. The palace and gardens illustrate the tastes, interests, and aspirations of successive Habsburg monarchs.
history_significance: A magnificent example of Baroque architecture and a symbol of the power of the Habsburg dynasty.
highlights: The Great Gallery, the Gloriette, and the Privy Garden.

name: Hallstatt-Dachstein / Salzkammergut Cultural Landscape
overview: Human activity in the magnificent natural landscape of the Salzkammergut began in prehistoric times, with the salt deposits being exploited as early as the 2nd millennium BC.
history_significance: The site of the Hallstatt culture (Early Iron Age) and the world's oldest salt mines.
highlights: Hallstatt Salt Mine, Lake Hallstatt, and the Dachstein Ice Caves.

name: Semmering Railway
overview: The Semmering Railway, built between 1848 and 1854, passes through 41 km of high mountains. It is one of the greatest feats of civil engineering from the pioneering phase of railway building.
history_significance: The world's first true mountain railway, demonstrating that railways could traverse major mountain ranges.
highlights: The Kalte Rinne Viaduct and the scenic mountain route.

name: City of Graz – Historic Centre and Schloss Eggenberg
overview: Graz exemplifies the harmonious integration of architectural styles from the Middle Ages and the Renaissance to the 18th century. Schloss Eggenberg was added in 2010.
history_significance: A central European urban complex influenced by the secular presence of the Habsburgs and the cultural role of the aristocracy.
highlights: The Clock Tower (Uhrturm), Schloss Eggenberg, and the Kunsthaus Graz.

name: Wachau Cultural Landscape
overview: The Wachau is a stretch of the Danube Valley between Melk and Krems, a landscape of high visual quality. It preserves many traces of its evolution since prehistoric times.
history_significance: Famous for its vineyards, fortified churches, and the magnificent Melk Abbey.
highlights: Melk Abbey, Göttweig Abbey, and the vineyards of Dürnstein.

name: Historic Centre of Vienna
overview: Vienna developed from early Celtic and Roman settlements into a Medieval and Baroque city, the capital of the Austro-Hungarian Empire. It played a major role as a leading European music centre.
history_significance: Showcases three key periods: Middle Ages, Baroque, and the Gründerzeit (Ringstrasse era).
highlights: St. Stephen's Cathedral, Hofburg Palace, and the Ringstrasse.

name: Fertö / Neusiedlersee Cultural Landscape
overview: This transboundary cultural landscape on the border of Austria and Hungary has been a meeting place of different cultures for eight millennia. It is centered on Lake Neusiedl (Fertö), the westernmost steppe lake in Eurasia.
