history_significance: A remarkable example of the evolutionary symbiosis between human activity and the physical environment, featuring 18th and 19th-century rural architecture and palaces.
highlights: Rust Old Town, Esterházy Palace (Fertőd), and the Fertőrákos Quarry.

name: Rock-Hewn Churches, Lalibela
overview: The 11 medieval monolithic cave churches of this 13th-century 'New Jerusalem' are situated in a mountainous region in the heart of Ethiopia near a traditional village with circular-shaped dwellings.
history_significance: Carved out of solid volcanic rock, these churches are a testament to the Ethiopian Orthodox Christian civilization and King Lalibela's vision to create a new holy land for pilgrims.
highlights: Biete Medhane Alem (largest monolithic church), Biete Giorgis (cross-shaped), and the system of trenches connecting them.

name: Simien National Park
overview: Massive erosion over millions of years has created one of the most spectacular landscapes in the world, with jagged mountain peaks, deep valleys, and sharp precipices dropping some 1,500 m.
history_significance: A refuge for extremely rare and endemic species such as the Gelada baboon, the Simien fox (Ethiopian wolf), and the Walia ibex.
highlights: Ras Dashen (highest peak in Ethiopia), Jinbar Waterfall, and the Gelada Baboon population.

name: Fasil Ghebbi, Gondar Region
overview: In the 16th and 17th centuries, the fortress-city of Fasil Ghebbi was the residence of the Ethiopian emperor Fasilides and his successors.
history_significance: Surrounded by a 900-m-long wall, the city contains palaces, churches, monasteries, and unique public and private buildings marked by Hindu and Arab influences.
highlights: Fasilides' Castle, the Palace of Iyasu I, and the Library of Tzadich Yohannes.

name: Aksum
overview: The ruins of the ancient city of Aksum are found close to Ethiopia's northern border. They mark the location of the heart of ancient Ethiopia, when the Kingdom of Aksum was the most powerful state between the Eastern Roman Empire and Persia.
history_significance: Features massive monolithic obelisks, giant stelae, royal tombs, and the ruins of ancient castles dating from the 1st to the 13th century AD.
highlights: Northern Stelae Field (Obelisk of Axum), Tombs of Kings Kaleb and Gebre Meskel, and the Chapel of the Tablet.

locations.name: Tombs of Kings Kaleb and Gebre Meskel
Chapel of the Tablet (Church of Our Lady Mary of Zion)

name: Lower Valley of the Awash
overview: The Awash valley contains one of the most important groupings of palaeontological sites on the African continent. The remains found at the site, the oldest of which date back at least 4 million years, provide evidence of human evolution.
history_significance: Famous for the discovery of the 3.2-million-year-old Australopithecus afarensis fossil known as 'Lucy' (Dinkinesh), which changed the understanding of human history.
highlights: Hadar archaeological site and the discovery place of 'Lucy'.

name: Lower Valley of the Omo
overview: A prehistoric site near Lake Turkana, the lower valley of the Omo is world-renowned. The discovery of many fossils there, especially Homo gracilis, has been of fundamental importance in the study of human evolution.
history_significance: Yielded the Omo Kibish fossils (early Homo sapiens) and stone tools dating back 2.4 million years from the Shungura Formation.
highlights: Shungura Formation, Usno Formation, and the Fejej paleontological site.

name: Tiya
overview: Tiya is among the most important of the roughly 160 archaeological sites discovered in the Soddo region, south of Addis Ababa.
history_significance: Contains 36 monuments, including 32 carved stelae covered with symbols, representing an ancient Ethiopian culture whose age has not yet been precisely determined.
highlights: The Stelae Field with engraved swords and symbols.

name: Harar Jugol, the Fortified Historic Town
overview: Located on a plateau surrounded by deep gorges and savanna, Harar is a fortified historic town and a center of Islamic culture in Ethiopia.
history_significance: Considered the fourth holiest city of Islam, it has 82 mosques (three from the 10th century) and 102 shrines enclosed by the Jugol wall.
highlights: The Jugol Wall with its five gates, the traditional Harari houses, and the Hyena Feeding sites.

name: Konso Cultural Landscape
overview: A 55km2 arid property of stone walled terraces and fortified settlements (paletas) in the Konso highlands. It constitutes a spectacular example of a living cultural tradition stretching back more than 400 years.
history_significance: Renowned for its dry stone terraces, waka (wooden funerary statues), and complex social organization.
highlights: Walled town of Gamole, Dokatu, and the waka statues.

name: The Gedeo Cultural Landscape
overview: Located along the eastern edge of the Main Ethiopian Rift, this area features an agroforestry system utilizing multilayer cultivation with large trees sheltering enset (false banana) and coffee.
history_significance: The landscape is dotted with megalithic monuments (stelae) and sacred forests, testifying to the sustainable relationship between the Gedeo people and their environment.
highlights: Tuto Fela Stelae Field, Chelba Tutiti Stelae Field, and Sede Mercato.

name: Bale Mountains National Park
overview: This park protects a landscape mosaic of extraordinary beauty, shaped by ancient lava outpourings and glaciation. It includes the largest area of Afro-alpine habitat on the African continent.
history_significance: A critical biodiversity hotspot, home to the endemic and endangered Ethiopian Wolf and Mountain Nyala.
highlights: Sanetti Plateau, Harenna Forest, and the Gaysay Grasslands.

name: Melka Kunture and Balchit
overview: A cluster of prehistoric sites located in the Upper Awash Valley, preserving a continuous record of human occupation from 2 million years ago.
history_significance: Provides key evidence of the Oldowan and Acheulean lithic industries and fossils of Homo erectus and Homo heidelbergensis.
highlights: Gombore excavation site, Garba site, and the Balchit obsidian outcrops.

name: Jelling Mounds, Runic Stones and Church
overview: The Jelling monuments, created by Kings Gorm the Old and Harald Bluetooth in the 10th century, are considered the 'birth certificate' of Denmark.
history_significance: Includes the two massive burial mounds and the two Runic Stones, one of which is the earliest mention of 'Denmark'.
highlights: The Great Jelling Stone, the burial mounds, and the whitewashed church.

name: Roskilde Cathedral
overview: The first Gothic cathedral built of brick in Scandinavia, and the main burial site of Danish monarchs.
history_significance: Influenced the spread of the Brick Gothic style across Northern Europe.
highlights: The Royal Tombs, red brick facade, and distinctive spires.

name: Kronborg Castle
overview: A major Renaissance castle overlooking the Øresund Strait, known as Elsinore in Shakespeare's Hamlet.
history_significance: Controlled the Sound Dues and symbolized Danish maritime power.
highlights: The Knight's Hall, the Casemates, and the Hamlet connection.

name: Ilulissat Icefjord
overview: The sea mouth of Sermeq Kujalleq, one of the fastest-moving glaciers in the world.
history_significance: A key site for studying glaciology and climate change.
highlights: Sermeq Kujalleq glacier and the massive calved icebergs.

name: Stevns Klint
overview: A 15 km-long fossil-rich cliff preserving the impact layer from the meteorite that caused the mass extinction 66 million years ago.
history_significance: Contains the clearest K/Pg boundary layer (Fish Clay).
highlights: The Fish Clay layer, Old Højerup Church, and the fossil-rich coast.

name: The Par force hunting landscape in North Zealand
overview: Baroque hunting grounds created for par force hunting by King Christian V in the 17th century.
history_significance: A demonstration of absolute monarchy expressed through landscape design.
highlights: Hermitage Hunting Lodge, star-shaped intersections, and deer populations.

name: Kujataa Greenland: Norse and Inuit Farming at the Edge of the Ice Cap
overview: A sub-arctic farming landscape showing Norse and Inuit agricultural traditions.
history_significance: Earliest example of farming introduced into the Arctic.
highlights: Ruins of Brattahlíð, Gardar, and Hvalsey Church.

name: Aasivissuit – Nipisat. Inuit Hunting Ground between Ice and Sea
overview: A vast Arctic cultural landscape reflecting 4,200 years of Inuit hunting traditions.
history_significance: Preserves migration routes and seasonal settlements between ice cap and sea.
highlights: Communal winter houses, caribou drive systems, and coastal sites.

name: Viking-Age Ring Fortresses
overview: Five ring-shaped Viking fortresses built during Harald Bluetooth's reign in the 10th century.
history_significance: Unified military architecture showing centralized royal power.
highlights: Circular ramparts and reconstructed Viking buildings.

name: Møns Klint
overview: A spectacular 6 km stretch of chalk cliffs rising 128 meters above the sea on Møn island.
history_significance: Shows dramatic Ice Age glacial deformation of chalk layers.
highlights: White chalk cliffs, fossil beaches, and GeoCenter Møns Klint.

name: Boyana Church
overview: Located on the outskirts of Sofia, the Boyana Church consists of three buildings. The frescoes in the second church, painted in 1259, are one of the most important collections of medieval paintings.
history_significance: A masterpiece of the Second Bulgarian Empire, exhibiting the high achievements of Bulgarian medieval culture and the synthesis of Eastern and Western artistic traditions.
highlights: The 13th-century frescoes (portraits of Sebastocrator Kaloyan and Desislava).

name: Madara Rider
overview: The Madara Rider is a unique relief carved into a 100m-high cliff. It depicts a knight triumphing over a lion and dates back to the early 8th century.
history_significance: The only rock relief of its kind in Europe from the Early Middle Ages, symbolizing the grandeur of the First Bulgarian Empire.
highlights: The rock relief of the Horseman and the surrounding archaeological reserve.

name: Rock-Hewn Churches of Ivanovo
overview: In the valley of the Rusenski Lom River, a complex of rock-hewn churches, chapels, monasteries, and cells were carved. The frescoes date from the 13th and 14th centuries.
history_significance: Famous for its well-preserved medieval frescoes which are exceptional examples of the Tarnovo Artistic School.
highlights: The Church of the Holy Mother of God (Main frescoes) and the panoramic valley view.

name: Thracian Tomb of Kazanlak
overview: Discovered in 1944, this tomb dates from the Hellenistic period (end of the 4th century BC). It is located near the ancient Thracian capital of Seuthopolis.
history_significance: Renowned for its unique murals showing a Thracian funeral feast, representing the best-preserved artistic masterpieces of the Hellenistic period in Bulgaria.
highlights: The Tomb of Kazanlak (Original & Replica) and the Rose Valley.

name: Ancient City of Nessebar
overview: Situated on a rocky peninsula on the Black Sea, the more than 3,000-year-old site of Nessebar was originally a Thracian settlement (Menebria).
history_significance: A rich open-air museum featuring acropolis remains, a temple of Apollo, and numerous medieval churches blending Byzantine and Bulgarian styles.
highlights: Church of Saint Sophia (Old Metropolis), Church of Christ Pantocrator, and the Old Town fortifications.

name: Pirin National Park
overview: Spread over an area of over 27,000 ha, at an altitude between 1,008 and 2,914 m in the Pirin Mountains, the park features a limestone Balkan landscape with lakes, waterfalls, and caves.
history_significance: Protects an exceptional example of mountain ecosystem evolution and contains the Baikushev's pine, one of the oldest trees in the world.
highlights: Vihren Peak, Baikushev's Pine, and the glacial lakes.

name: Rila Monastery
overview: Founded in the 10th century by St John of Rila, this monastery is the largest and most famous Eastern Orthodox monastery in Bulgaria. It is situated in the Rila Mountains.
history_significance: A symbol of Bulgarian cultural identity during centuries of Ottoman occupation and a masterpiece of the Bulgarian National Revival.
highlights: The Main Church of Nativity of the Virgin, Hrelyo's Tower, and the monks' cells.

name: Srebarna Nature Reserve
overview: The Srebarna Nature Reserve is a freshwater lake adjacent to the Danube River. It is the breeding ground of almost 100 species of birds, many of which are rare or endangered.
history_significance: The only major nesting site of the Dalmatian Pelican in Bulgaria and a key stopover on the Via Pontica migration route.
highlights: Lake Srebarna and the Pelican colony.

name: Thracian Tomb of Sveshtari
overview: Discovered in 1982 near the village of Sveshtari, this 3rd-century BC tomb reflects the fundamental structural principles of Thracian cult buildings.
history_significance: Unique for its architectural decor featuring 10 caryatids (half-human, half-plant female figures) carved in high relief.
highlights: The Central Burial Chamber with Caryatids.

name: Danube Delta
overview: The waters of the Danube, which flow into the Black Sea, form the largest and best preserved of Europe's deltas. The Danube delta hosts over 300 species of birds and 45 freshwater fish species in its numerous lakes and marshes.
history_significance: The largest continuous marshland in Europe and a critical biosphere reserve for migratory birds.
highlights: The labyrinth of channels and lakes, pelican colonies, and the Letea Forest.

name: Villages with Fortified Churches in Transylvania
overview: These seven villages provide a vivid picture of the cultural landscape of southern Transylvania. They are characterized by a specific land-use system, settlement pattern, and organization of the family farmstead that have been preserved since the late Middle Ages.
history_significance: Dominated by their fortified churches, these settlements illustrate the defensive architecture of the Transylvanian Saxons from the 13th to the 16th centuries.
highlights: Biertan Fortified Church, Viscri (White Church), and Prejmer.

name: Monastery of Horezu
overview: Founded in 1690 by Prince Constantine Brancovan, the monastery of Horezu is a masterpiece of the 'Brancovan' style. It is known for its architectural purity and balance, the richness of its sculptural detail, and its religious compositions.
history_significance: The finest example of the Brâncovenesc style, a synthesis of Byzantine, Ottoman, and Renaissance influences.
highlights: The Katholikon (main church) frescoes and the princely residence.

name: Churches of Moldavia
overview: These eight churches of northern Moldavia, built from the late 15th century to the late 16th century, are unique in Europe. Their external walls are covered in fresco paintings inspired by Byzantine art.
history_significance: Famous for their external frescoes which are masterpieces of mural painting, serving as a 'Bible for the poor'.
highlights: Voroneț Monastery ('Voroneț Blue'), Sucevița Monastery, and Moldovița Monastery.

name: Historic Centre of Sighișoara
overview: Founded by German craftsmen and merchants known as the Transylvanian Saxons, Sighișoara is a fine example of a small, fortified medieval town which played an important strategic and commercial role on the fringes of Central Europe.
history_significance: One of the best-preserved inhabited medieval citadels in Europe and the birthplace of Vlad the Impaler (Dracula).
highlights: The Clock Tower, the Covered Staircase, and the Church on the Hill.

name: Wooden Churches of Maramureș
overview: These eight churches are outstanding examples of a range of architectural solutions from different periods and areas. They show the variety of designs and craftsmanship adopted in these narrow, high timber constructions with their characteristic tall, slim clock towers.
history_significance: Represents the expression of the vernacular religious architecture of the Maramureș landscape.
highlights: Bârsana Monastery, Șurdești Church (one of the tallest wooden structures), and Ieud Hill Church.

name: Dacian Fortresses of the Orăștie Mountains
overview: Built in the 1st centuries BC and AD under the Dacian rule, these fortresses show an unusual fusion of military and religious architectural techniques and concepts from the classical world and the late European Iron Age.
history_significance: The heart of the Dacian Kingdom before the Roman conquest, featuring the capital Sarmizegetusa Regia.
highlights: Sarmizegetusa Regia, Costești-Cetățuie, and the Sacred Area.

name: Roșia Montană Mining Landscape
overview: Roșia Montană features the most significant, extensive and technically diverse underground Roman gold mining complex known at the time of inscription.
history_significance: Illustrates the Roman extraction of gold (Alburnus Maior) using hydraulic wheels, fire-setting, and intricate gallery systems.
highlights: Roman mining galleries (Cătălina-Monulești), the mining museum, and the artificial lakes.

name: Brâncuși Monumental Ensemble of Târgu Jiu
overview: This monumental ensemble was created in 1937–1938 by the Romanian sculptor Constantin Brâncuși to commemorate those who died defending the city during the First World War.
history_significance: A masterpiece of modern public art, integrating sculpture, landscape, and urban planning by one of the 20th century's greatest artists.
highlights: The Endless Column, The Gate of the Kiss, and The Table of Silence.

name: Frontiers of the Roman Empire – Dacia
overview: This property represents the eastern frontier of the Roman Empire in the province of Dacia. The remains include fortresses, watchtowers, roads, and defensive structures that formed a complex border system along the Carpathians and the Transylvanian plateau.
history_significance: The Dacian limes, created between the 1st and 3rd centuries AD, illustrates the Roman Empire's military engineering, frontier organization, and administrative control. It demonstrates how Rome integrated and defended newly conquered territories.
highlights: The well-preserved legionary fortress at Potaissa, the ramparts and towers of the Limes Alutanus, and the fortified structures of the Limes Transalutanus.

name: Old Havana and its Fortification System
overview: Havana was founded in 1519 by the Spanish. By the 17th century, it had become one of the Caribbean's main shipbuilding centers. Its old centre retains an interesting mix of Baroque and neoclassical monuments.
history_significance: A strategic stopover for the Spanish treasure fleets, defended by a vast network of fortifications that are among the oldest and largest in the Americas.
highlights: Plaza de la Catedral, Castillo del Morro, and La Cabaña Fortress.

name: Trinidad and the Valley de los Ingenios
overview: Founded in the early 16th century, Trinidad was a bridgehead for the conquest of the American continent. Its 18th- and 19th-century buildings owe their prosperity to the sugar trade.
history_significance: One of the best-preserved colonial cities in the Caribbean, illustrating the peak of the sugar industry.
highlights: Plaza Mayor, Manaca Iznaga Tower, and the colonial mansions.

locations.name: Manaca Iznaga Tower (Valley of the Sugar Mills)

name: San Pedro de la Roca Castle, Santiago de Cuba
overview: This intricate complex of forts, magazines, bastions, and batteries is the most complete, best-preserved example of Spanish-American military architecture, based on Italian and Renaissance design principles.
history_significance: Built to protect the important port of Santiago from commercial and political rivals (pirates and other European powers) in the Caribbean.
highlights: The main citadel and the views of the Caribbean Sea.

name: Desembarco del Granma National Park
overview: The uplifted marine terraces of this park and the associated ongoing development of karst topography and features represent a globally significant example of geomorphological and physiographic features.
history_significance: Contains the most significant and extensive system of marine terraces (submerged and emerged) on calcareous rocks in the world.
highlights: The Marine Terraces of Cabo Cruz and the Hoyo de Morlotte.

name: Viñales Valley
overview: The Viñales Valley is encircled by mountains and its landscape is interspersed with dramatic rocky outcrops (mogotes). Traditional agriculture, particularly tobacco growing, has survived unchanged for several centuries.
history_significance: An outstanding karst landscape where traditional methods of agriculture (tobacco) and vernacular architecture present a high quality of cultural landscape.
highlights: The Mogotes (limestone hills), Mural de la Prehistoria, and tobacco plantations.

name: Archaeological Landscape of the First Coffee Plantations in the South-East of Cuba
overview: The remains of the 19th-century coffee plantations in the foothills of the Sierra Maestra are unique evidence of a pioneer form of agriculture in a difficult terrain.
history_significance: Illustrates the technological, economic and social history of the Caribbean and the influence of French settlers fleeing Haiti.
highlights: La Isabelica Coffee Plantation museum and the drying floors (secaderos).

name: Alejandro de Humboldt National Park
overview: Complex geology and varied topography have generated a diversity of ecosystems and species unmatched in the insular Caribbean. It is one of the most biologically diverse tropical island sites on earth.
history_significance: A global center for plant diversity and a refuge for endemic fauna, such as the Almiquí (Cuban Solenodon).
highlights: The diverse rainforest ecosystems and endemic species.

name: Urban Historic Centre of Cienfuegos
overview: The colonial town of Cienfuegos was founded in 1819 in the Spanish territory but was initially settled by immigrants of French origin. It became a trading place for sugar cane, tobacco and coffee.
history_significance: An outstanding example of an architectural ensemble representing the new ideas of modernity, hygiene and order in urban planning (Enlightenment) in Latin America.
highlights: Parque José Martí, Palacio de Valle, and the Tomas Terry Theatre.

name: Historic Centre of Camagüey
overview: One of the first seven villages founded by the Spaniards in Cuba. The historic centre represents a unique urban architectural type, with an irregular system of squares and winding streets.
history_significance: Unlike other Spanish colonial towns with grid layouts, Camagüey has a medieval European layout adapted to the traditional clay pot (tinajón) water storage culture.
highlights: Plaza San Juan de Dios, Iglesia de Nuestra Señora de la Soledad, and the winding streets.

name: Borobudur Temple Compounds
overview: This famous Buddhist temple, dating from the 8th and 9th centuries, is located in central Java. It is built in three tiers: a pyramidal base with five concentric square terraces, the trunk of a cone with three circular platforms and, at the top, a monumental stupa.
history_significance: One of the greatest Buddhist monuments in the world, representing the Buddhist conception of the universe (Mandala).
highlights: The Main Stupa, the narrative bas-reliefs, and the sunrise view from the top tier.

name: Ujung Kulon National Park
overview: Located at the south-western tip of Java, this park includes the Ujung Kulon peninsula and several offshore islands, including the nature reserve of Krakatoa.
history_significance: The last remaining habitat of the critically endangered Javan Rhinoceros and a prime example of ongoing geological processes (Krakatoa).
highlights: Krakatoa Volcano (Anak Krakatau) and the tropical rainforest.

name: Komodo National Park
overview: These volcanic islands are inhabited by a population of around 5,700 giant lizards, whose appearance and aggressive behavior have led to them being called 'Komodo dragons'.
history_significance: The only place in the world where the Komodo dragon (Varanus komodoensis) exists in the wild.
highlights: Komodo Dragon trekking, Pink Beach, and Padar Island viewpoint.

name: Prambanan Temple Compounds
overview: Built in the 10th century, this is the largest temple complex dedicated to Shiva in Indonesia. Rising above the center of the last of these concentric squares are three temples decorated with reliefs illustrating the epic of the Ramayana.
history_significance: An outstanding example of Hindu art and architecture in Southeast Asia, coexisting with nearby Buddhist sites.
highlights: Shiva Temple (Loro Jonggrang), Sewu Temple, and the Ramayana Ballet performance.

name: Sangiran Early Man Site
overview: Excavations here from 1936 to 1941 led to the discovery of the first hominid fossil at this site. Later, 50 fossils of Meganthropus palaeo and Pithecanthropus erectus/Homo erectus were found.
history_significance: One of the key sites for understanding human evolution, yielding half of all known hominid fossils in the world.
highlights: The Sangiran Museum of Ancient Man (Klaster Krikilan) and the excavation layers.

name: Lorentz National Park
overview: Lorentz National Park is the largest protected area in Southeast Asia (2.35 million ha). It is the only protected area in the world to incorporate a continuous, intact transect from snow-capped mountain to tropical marine environment.
history_significance: Contains Puncak Jaya, the highest peak in Oceania, and demonstrates complex geological processes and high biodiversity.
highlights: Puncak Jaya (Carstensz Pyramid) and Lake Habbema.

name: Tropical Rainforest Heritage of Sumatra
overview: This site comprises three national parks: Gunung Leuser, Kerinci Seblat, and Bukit Barisan Selatan. It holds the greatest potential for long-term conservation of the distinctive biota of Sumatra.
history_significance: Home to many endangered species, including the Sumatran Orangutan, Sumatran Tiger, and Sumatran Rhino.
highlights: Gunung Leuser (Orangutans), Mount Kerinci (Volcano), and Bukit Barisan forests.

name: Cultural Landscape of Bali Province: the Subak System as a Manifestation of the Tri Hita Karana Philosophy
overview: The cultural landscape of Bali consists of five rice terraces and their water temples that cover 19,500 ha. The temples are the focus of a cooperative water management system of canals and weirs, known as subak.
history_significance: Reflects the Tri Hita Karana philosophy, which brings together the realms of the spirit, the human world, and nature.
highlights: Jatiluwih Rice Terraces, Pura Ulun Danu Batur, and Taman Ayun Temple.

locations.name: Pura Ulun Danu Batur (Supreme Water Temple)

name: Ombilin Coal Mining Heritage of Sawahlunto
overview: Built for the extraction, processing, and transport of high-quality coal in an inaccessible region of Sumatra, this industrial site was developed by the Netherlands East Indies government in the late 19th century.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a fully integrated colonial coal mining system, incorporating mining, railway, and port facilities.
highlights: Mbah Soero Tunnel, Goedang Ransoem Museum, and the Sawahlunto Train Station.

name: The Cosmological Axis of Yogyakarta and its Historic Landmarks
overview: The central axis of Yogyakarta was established in the 18th century by Sultan Mangkubumi. It links Mount Merapi in the north to the Indian Ocean in the south, with the Kraton (Palace) at its centre.
history_significance: A physical representation of Javanese cosmology and philosophy, symbolizing the cycle of life and the connection between God, humans, and nature.
highlights: The Kraton (Sultan's Palace), Tugu Yogyakarta, and Panggung Krapyak.

name: Historical Complex of Split with the Palace of Diocletian
overview: The ruins of Diocletian's Palace, built between the late 3rd and the early 4th centuries A.D., can be found throughout the city. The cathedral was built in the Middle Ages, reusing materials from the ancient mausoleum.
history_significance: One of the most famous and integral architectural and cultural features on the Croatian Adriatic coast, preserving a continuous urban life for over 1700 years.
highlights: Diocletian's Palace, Cathedral of Saint Domnius, and the Peristyle.

name: Old City of Dubrovnik
overview: The 'Pearl of the Adriatic', situated on the Dalmatian coast, became an important Mediterranean sea power from the 13th century onwards. The planned city is enclosed by medieval walls.
history_significance: A masterpiece of urban planning and preservation, representing the wealth and power of the Republic of Ragusa.
highlights: The City Walls, Stradun (main street), and the Rector's Palace.

name: Plitvice Lakes National Park
overview: The waters flowing over the limestone and chalk have, over thousands of years, deposited travertine barriers, creating natural dams which in turn have created a series of beautiful lakes, caves, and waterfalls.
history_significance: An outstanding example of ongoing geological processes and tufa formation, hosting a diverse range of flora and fauna including brown bears.
highlights: The 16 terraced lakes, Veliki Slap (Great Waterfall), and the travertine barriers.

name: Episcopal Complex of the Euphrasian Basilica in the Historic Centre of Poreč
overview: The group of religious monuments in Porec, where Christianity was established as early as the 4th century, constitutes the most complete surviving complex of its type.
history_significance: An outstanding example of early Byzantine architecture and art in the Mediterranean region, featuring spectacular 6th-century mosaics.
highlights: The Euphrasian Basilica, the Atrium, the Baptistery, and the Episcopal Palace.

name: Historic City of Trogir
overview: Trogir is a remarkable example of urban continuity. The orthogonal street plan of this island settlement dates back to the Hellenistic period and was embellished by successive rulers with many fine public and domestic buildings and fortifications.
history_significance: A medieval town built on the layout of a Hellenistic and Roman city, showcasing a rich blend of Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque architecture.
highlights: The Cathedral of St. Lawrence, Kamerlengo Fortress, and the Radovan Portal.

name: The Cathedral of St James in Šibenik
overview: The Cathedral of St James in Šibenik (1431-1535) bears witness to the considerable exchanges in the field of monumental arts between Northern Italy, Dalmatia, and Tuscany in the 15th and 16th centuries.
history_significance: A masterpiece of the transition from Gothic to Renaissance art, unique for being built entirely from stone using a unique interlocking technique.
highlights: The frieze of 71 sculpted heads on the exterior and the stone dome.

name: Stari Grad Plain
overview: The Stari Grad Plain on the Adriatic island of Hvar is a cultural landscape that has remained practically intact since it was first colonized by Ionian Greeks from Paros in the 4th century BC.
history_significance: The ancient 'chora' (land division) system is still visible today, marked by dry stone walls and trims, reflecting the ancient Greek system of agriculture.
highlights: The ancient geometric land division and the olive groves and vineyards.

name: Stećci Medieval Tombstone Graveyards
overview: This serial property combines 28 sites located in Bosnia and Herzegovina, western Serbia, western Montenegro, and central and southern Croatia. It features regionally distinctive medieval tombstones called 'stećci', dating from the 12th to 16th centuries.
history_significance: These monolithic stone monuments bear testimony to a unique cultural tradition in Southeast Europe. They feature a wide range of decorative motifs and inscriptions that represent iconographic continuities within medieval Europe as well as locally distinctive traditions.
highlights: Radimlja (BiH), Velika and Mala Crljivica (Croatia), Grčko groblje (Montenegro), and Mramorje (Serbia).

locations.name: Velika and Mala Crljivica (Cista Velika, Croatia)

name: Port, Fortresses and Group of Monuments, Cartagena
overview: Situated on the northern coast of Colombia, Cartagena de Indias has the most extensive fortifications in South America. The city was a major port for the Spanish empire.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Spanish military engineering in the New World, protecting the city from pirate attacks (like Francis Drake) and rival powers.
highlights: Castillo San Felipe de Barajas, the Walled City, and the Palace of the Inquisition.

name: Los Katíos National Park
overview: Located in north-western Colombia, extending into Panama, the park consists of low hills, forests, and wet plains. It is part of the Darién Gap.
history_significance: An area of exceptional biological diversity and a filter for the exchange of species between North and South America.
highlights: The Darién rainforest and endemic species like the Darién small-eared shrew.

name: Historic Centre of Santa Cruz de Mompox
overview: Founded in 1540 on the banks of the River Magdalena, Mompox played a key role in the Spanish colonization of northern South America. It has preserved the harmony and unity of its urban landscape.
history_significance: Known for its colonial architecture adapted to the climate and its tradition of fine gold filigree work.
highlights: Church of Santa Bárbara, the Albarrada (riverfront), and the cemetery.

name: National Archeological Park of Tierradentro
overview: This park contains several monumental statues of human figures and many hypogea (underground tombs) dating from the 6th to the 10th century.
history_significance: The huge underground tombs (some up to 12 m wide) with polychrome wall paintings are unique in the Americas.
highlights: Segovia site (painted tombs), El Duende, and Alto de San Andrés.

name: San Agustín Archaeological Park
overview: The largest group of religious monuments and megalithic sculptures in South America stands in a wild, spectacular landscape. These works of art display the creativity of a northern Andean culture.
history_significance: Features massive stone statues (some 7m high) depicting gods and mythical animals, dating from the 1st-8th century AD.
highlights: Fuente de Lavapatas (ceremonial pool), Bosque de las Estatuas, and Alto de los Ídolos.

name: Malpelo Fauna and Flora Sanctuary
overview: Located some 500 km off the coast of Colombia, Malpelo Island is a shear rock formation rising from the deep ocean. It includes a vast marine protected area.
history_significance: A top diving destination and a reservoir for sharks, including massive schools of hammerhead and silky sharks.
highlights: Diving with Hammerhead Sharks and the steep oceanic cliffs.

name: Coffee Cultural Landscape of Colombia
city: Caldas, Quindío, Risaralda & Valle del Cauca
overview: An exceptional example of a sustainable and productive cultural landscape that is unique and representative of a tradition that is a strong symbol for coffee growing areas worldwide.
history_significance: Illustrates the 'Eje Cafetero' lifestyle, architecture (bahareque), and the adaptation of coffee growing to challenging mountain conditions.
highlights: Valle de Cocora (Wax Palms), Salento, and the steep hillside coffee plantations.

name: Chiribiquete National Park – “The Maloca of the Jaguar”
overview: Chiribiquete is the largest protected area in Colombia. It features tepuis (table-top mountains) rising out of the Amazon forest and over 75,000 rock paintings.
history_significance: A sacred site for indigenous peoples for millennia, depicting scenes of hunting, battles, and the jaguar cult.
highlights: The Tepuis formations and the ancient rock art panels.

name: Masada
overview: Masada is a rugged natural fortress, of majestic beauty, in the Judaean Desert overlooking the Dead Sea. It is a symbol of the ancient kingdom of Israel, its violent destruction and the last stand of Jewish patriots in the face of the Roman army.
history_significance: Built by Herod the Great, it was the site of the siege of Masada (73–74 CE). The camps, fortifications, and assault ramp constitute the most complete Roman siege works surviving today.
highlights: The Northern Palace, the Snake Path, and the Roman Ramp.

name: Old City of Acre
overview: Acre is a historic walled port city with continuous settlement from the Phoenician period. The present city is characteristic of a fortified Ottoman town dating from the 18th and 19th centuries, with citadel, mosques, khans, and baths.
history_significance: Preserves the substantial remains of the medieval Crusader city (1104–1291) almost intact beneath the Ottoman level.
highlights: The Knights' Halls (Crusader City), Al-Jazzar Mosque, and the Templars' Tunnel.

name: White City of Tel-Aviv – The Modern Movement
overview: Tel Aviv was founded in 1909 and developed as a metropolitan city under the British Mandate in Palestine. It includes over 4,000 buildings constructed in the Bauhaus or International Style.
history_significance: A synthesis of outstanding significance of the various trends of the Modern Movement in architecture and town planning in the early 20th century.
highlights: Rothschild Boulevard, Dizengoff Square, and the Bauhaus Center.

name: Biblical Tels - Megiddo, Hazor, Beer Sheba
overview: Tels (prehistoric settlement mounds), are characteristic of the flatter lands of the eastern Mediterranean. These three Tels contain substantial remains of cities with biblical connections.
history_significance: They are the best examples in the Levant of elaborate Iron Age underground water collecting systems and city planning.
highlights: The Water Systems of Megiddo and Hazor, and the Solomonic Gates.

name: Incense Route - Desert Cities in the Negev
overview: The four Nabatean towns of Haluza, Mamshit, Avdat and Shivta, along with associated fortresses and agricultural landscapes in the Negev Desert, are spread along routes linking them to the Mediterranean end of the Incense and Spice route.
history_significance: Testifies to the hugely profitable trade in frankincense and myrrh from South Arabia to the Mediterranean.
highlights: Avdat National Park, Shivta ruins, and the desert irrigation systems.

name: Bahá'i Holy Places in Haifa and the Western Galilee
overview: These sites are inscribed for their profound spiritual meaning and the testimony they bear to the strong tradition of pilgrimage in the Bahá'i faith.
history_significance: Contains the two holiest places in the Bahá'i religion: the Shrine of Bahá'u'lláh in Acre and the Shrine of the Báb in Haifa.
highlights: The Terraced Gardens in Haifa (Shrine of the Báb) and the Shrine of Bahá'u'lláh.

name: Sites of Human Evolution at Mount Carmel: The Nahal Me'arot / Wadi el-Mughara Caves
overview: Located on the western slopes of Mount Carmel, this site includes the caves of Tabun, Jamal, el-Wad, and Skhul. It preserves 500,000 years of human evolution.
history_significance: A key site demonstrating the transition from a hunter-gathering lifestyle to agriculture and animal husbandry. Evidence of Neanderthals and early Anatomy Modern Humans coexisting.
highlights: The Cave of el-Wad and the fossilized reefs.

name: Caves of Maresha and Bet-Guvrin in the Judean Lowlands as a Microcosm of the Land of the Caves
overview: This archaeological site contains some 3,500 underground chambers carved into the soft chalk of the Judean Lowlands.
history_significance: Used for 2,000 years as cisterns, oil presses, baths, columbaria (dovecotes), stables, religious worship, and burial.
highlights: The Bell Caves, the Columbarium Cave, and the Sidonian Burial Caves.

name: Necropolis of Bet She'arim: A Landmark of Jewish Renewal
overview: Consisting of a series of catacombs, the necropolis developed from the 2nd century AD as the primary burial place for Jews outside Jerusalem following the failed second Jewish revolt against Rome.
history_significance: The most important ancient Jewish cemetery, associated with the compilation of the Mishna by Rabbi Judah the Patriarch.
highlights: The Cave of the Coffins and the Menorah engravings.

name: Old City of Jerusalem and its Walls
overview: A holy city for Judaism, Christianity and Islam. The current walls were built by Suleiman the Magnificent in the 16th century.
history_significance: Contains some of the holiest sites in the world, including the Temple Mount, Western Wall, Church of the Holy Sepulchre, and Dome of the Rock.
highlights: Western Wall, Dome of the Rock, Church of the Holy Sepulchre, and Via Dolorosa.

name: Medina of Fez
overview: Founded in the 9th century and home to the oldest university in the world, Fez reached its height in the 13th–14th centuries under the Marinids.
history_significance: The cultural and spiritual centre of Morocco, known for its vast pedestrianized medieval city (Fes el-Bali).
highlights: University of Al Quaraouiyine, Chouara Tannery, and Bou Inania Madrasa.

name: Medina of Marrakesh
overview: Founded in 1070–72 by the Almoravids, Marrakesh remained a political, economic and cultural centre for a long period. Its influence was felt throughout the western Muslim world.
history_significance: A masterpiece of city planning featuring impressive monuments dating from that period.
highlights: Jemaa el-Fnaa square, Koutoubia Mosque, and the Saadian Tombs.

name: Ksar of Ait-Ben-Haddou
overview: The Ksar of Ait-Ben-Haddou is a striking example of southern Moroccan architecture. The ksar is a mainly collective grouping of dwellings with defensive walls reinforced by angle towers.
history_significance: A traditional pre-Saharan habitat and a former strategic stop on the caravan route between the Sahara and Marrakesh.
highlights: The earthen clay architecture and the views from the granary at the top.

name: Historic City of Meknes
overview: Founded in the 11th century as a military settlement, Meknes became a capital under Sultan Moulay Ismail (1672–1727).
history_significance: Designed in the Spanish-Moorish style, surrounded by high walls with great doors, creating an impressive imperial city.
highlights: Bab Mansour gate, Mausoleum of Moulay Ismail, and the vast granaries (Heri es-Souani).

name: Archaeological Site of Volubilis
overview: The Mauritanian capital, founded in the 3rd century B.C., became an important outpost of the Roman Empire and was graced with many fine buildings.
history_significance: The best-preserved Roman ruins in Morocco, famous for their extensive and well-preserved mosaics.
highlights: The Basilica, the Triumphal Arch of Caracalla, and the mosaic floors.

name: Medina of Tétouan (formerly known as Titawin)
overview: Tétouan was of particular importance in the Islamic period, from the 8th century onwards, as the main point of contact between Morocco and Andalusia.
history_significance: Rebuilt by Andalusian refugees expelled by the Spanish, the city reveals a strong Andalusian influence in its architecture and art.
highlights: The distinct white-walled architecture and the Royal Palace.

name: Medina of Essaouira (formerly Mogador)
overview: Essaouira is an exceptional example of a late 18th-century fortified town, built according to the principles of contemporary European military architecture in a North African context.
history_significance: A major international trading seaport, linking Morocco and its Saharan hinterland with Europe and the rest of the world.
highlights: The Skala de la Ville (ramparts), the fishing harbor, and the medina streets.

name: Portuguese City of Mazagan (El Jadida)
overview: The Portuguese fortification of Mazagan, now part of the city of El Jadida, was built as a fortified colony on the Atlantic coast in the early 16th century.
history_significance: An outstanding example of the interchange of influences between European and Moroccan cultures, well reflected in architecture like the cistern.
highlights: The Portuguese Cistern and the Manueline Church of the Assumption.

name: Rabat, Modern Capital and Historic City: a Shared Heritage
overview: Rabat provides an outstanding example of modern urbanism (built under the French Protectorate from 1912 to the 1930s) alongside the older parts of the city dating back to the 12th century.
history_significance: Combines the Almohad walls and gates with early 20th-century architecture creating a unique synthesis.
highlights: Hassan Tower, Kasbah of the Udayas, and the Chellah Necropolis.

name: Amphitheatre of El Jem
overview: This impressive ruin of the largest colosseum in North Africa, a huge amphitheatre which could hold up to 35,000 spectators, is found in the small village of El Jem.
history_significance: Illustrates the grandeur and extent of Imperial Rome in North Africa. It is one of the best preserved Roman amphitheatres in the world.
highlights: The arena floor, the underground passages (hypogeum), and the three tiers of arcades.

name: Archaeological Site of Carthage
overview: Founded in the 9th century B.C. on the Gulf of Tunis, Carthage developed into a great trading empire covering the Mediterranean. It was destroyed by Rome in 146 B.C. and later rebuilt as Roman Carthage.
history_significance: The capital of the Punic civilization and later the capital of the Roman province of Africa.
highlights: Byrsa Hill, Antonine Baths, and the Tophet of Salammbô.

name: Medina of Tunis
overview: Under the Almohads and the Hafsids, from the 12th to the 16th century, Tunis was considered one of the greatest and wealthiest cities in the Islamic world.
history_significance: Contains some 700 monuments, including palaces, mosques, mausoleums, madrasas, and fountains, testifying to its remarkable past.
highlights: Al-Zaytuna Mosque, Dar Lasram, and the Souks.

name: Ichkeul National Park
overview: The Ichkeul Lake and marshland are a major stopover for hundreds of thousands of migrating birds, such as ducks, geese, storks, and pink flamingoes.
history_significance: The last remaining lake in a chain that once extended across North Africa.
highlights: Lake Ichkeul and the birdwatching opportunities.

name: Punic Town of Kerkuane and its Necropolis
overview: This Phoenician city was probably abandoned during the First Punic War (c. 250 B.C.) and as a result was not rebuilt by the Romans.
history_significance: The only surviving example of a Phoenician-Punic city, providing a unique insight into Punic urban planning.
highlights: The residential houses with baths (hip-baths) and the Necropolis of Arg el Ghazouani.

name: Kairouan
overview: Founded in 670, Kairouan flourished under the Aghlabid dynasty in the 9th century. Despite the transfer of the political capital to Tunis in the 12th century, Kairouan remained the Maghreb's principal holy city.
history_significance: Home to the Great Mosque, the oldest Muslim place of worship in North Africa, and a masterpiece of Islamic architecture.
highlights: The Great Mosque of Kairouan, the Mosque of the Three Doors, and the Basins of the Aghlabids.

name: Medina of Sousse
overview: Sousse was an important commercial and military port during the Aghlabid period (800–909). The Medina is a typical example of a town dating from the first centuries of Islam.
history_significance: Features the Ribat, a fortress-monastery which is a unique monument of Islamic military architecture.
highlights: The Ribat, the Great Mosque of Sousse, and the Kasbah.

name: Dougga / Thugga
overview: Dougga is the best-preserved Roman small town in North Africa. It was originally a Numidian fortified town before becoming a Roman provincial capital.
history_significance: Illustrates the synthesis of Numidian, Punic, Hellenistic, and Roman cultures.
highlights: The Capitol, the Theatre, and the Libyco-Punic Mausoleum.

name: Djerba: Testimony to a Settlement Pattern in an Island Territory
overview: This serial property is a testimony to a settlement pattern that developed on the island of Djerba around the 9th century. It features a unique land-use system and diverse religious architecture.
history_significance: Reflects the Ibadite religious minority's adaptation to a water-scarce environment and their coexistence with Jewish and other communities.
highlights: Houmt Souk, the El Ghriba Synagogue, and the underground mosques.

name: Complex of Hué Monuments
overview: Established as the capital of unified Viet Nam in 1802, Hué was not only the political but also the cultural and religious centre under the Nguyen dynasty until 1945.
history_significance: A remarkable example of late feudal urban planning in East Asia, featuring the Imperial City and royal tombs aligned cosmologically.
highlights: The Imperial City (Citadel), Thien Mu Pagoda, and the Tomb of Khai Dinh.

name: Ha Long Bay - Cat Ba Archipelago
overview: Ha Long Bay features thousands of limestone karsts and isles in various shapes and sizes. In 2023, the site was extended to include the Cat Ba Archipelago.
history_significance: A spectacular seascape of pillars and islands, representing the most extensive and best-preserved example of marine-invaded tower karst in the world.
highlights: Sung Sot Cave, Ti Top Island, and Cat Ba National Park.

name: My Son Sanctuary
overview: My Son is a cluster of abandoned and partially ruined Hindu temples constructed between the 4th and the 14th century by the Kings of Champa.
history_significance: The spiritual and political capital of the Champa Kingdom, dedicated to the worship of the god Shiva.
highlights: The brick tower temples (Kalan) and the surrounding jungle landscape.

name: Hoi An Ancient Town
overview: Hoi An is an exceptionally well-preserved example of a South-East Asian trading port dating from the 15th to the 19th century. Its buildings reflect a fusion of indigenous and foreign influences.
history_significance: A major international port known as Faifo, featuring a unique blend of Vietnamese, Chinese, Japanese, and European architectural styles.
highlights: The Japanese Covered Bridge, the Assembly Hall of the Fujian Chinese Congregation, and the Old Houses.

name: Phong Nha-Ke Bang National Park
overview: The park protects one of the world's two largest karst regions with 300 caves and grottoes. It also protects the ecosystem of limestone forests of the Annamite Range.
history_significance: Home to Son Doong, the world's largest cave, and shows significant geomorphic features like underground rivers.
highlights: Son Doong Cave, Paradise Cave, and the underground river of Phong Nha Cave.

name: Central Sector of the Imperial Citadel of Thang Long - Hanoi
overview: The Thang Long Imperial Citadel was built in the 11th century by the Ly Viet Dynasty on the remains of a Chinese fortress. It was the center of regional political power for almost 13 centuries.
history_significance: Reflects a unique South-East Asian culture specific to the lower Red River Valley, at the intersection of influences from China and Champa.
highlights: The Hanoi Flag Tower, the Doan Mon Gate, and the archaeological site at 18 Hoang Dieu.

name: Citadel of the Ho Dynasty
overview: The Citadel of the Ho Dynasty was built in 1397 according to feng shui principles. It testifies to the flowering of Neo-Confucianism in late 14th-century Vietnam.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a new style of imperial city construction using massive stone blocks.
highlights: The massive stone walls and the four arched gates.

name: Trang An Landscape Complex
overview: Trang An is a spectacular landscape of limestone karst peaks permeated with valleys, many of them partly submerged and surrounded by steep, almost vertical cliffs.
history_significance: Exploration of caves has revealed archaeological traces of human activity over a continuous period of more than 30,000 years.
highlights: Tam Coc (boat tour), Bich Dong Pagoda, and the Hoa Lu Ancient Capital.

name: Yen Tu-Vinh Nghiem-Con Son, Kiep Bac Complex of Monuments and Landscapes
city: Quang Ninh, Bac Giang, Hai Duong
overview: This serial property spans three provinces and is centered on the Yen Tu Mountain Range. It is the birthplace of Truc Lam Zen Buddhism, a uniquely Vietnamese Buddhist tradition founded by King-Monk Tran Nhan Tong in the 13th century.
history_significance: Represents a vast system of spiritual and cultural relics associated with the Tran Dynasty and the development of Vietnamese Zen Buddhism, harmoniously integrated with spectacular natural landscapes.
highlights: Dong Pagoda (Yen Tu peak), Vinh Nghiem Pagoda (woodblocks), and Con Son - Kiep Bac complex.

name: Lake Turkana National Parks
overview: Lake Turkana is the most saline of Africa's large lakes and an outstanding laboratory for the study of plant and animal communities. The parks serve as a stopover for migrant waterfowl and are major breeding grounds for the Nile crocodile, hippopotamus and a variety of venomous snakes.
history_significance: The Koobi Fora deposits, rich in mammalian, molluscan and other fossil remains, have contributed more to the understanding of paleo-environments than any other site on the continent.
highlights: Sibiloi National Park, Central Island, and South Island.

name: Mount Kenya National Park/Natural Forest
overview: Mount Kenya (5,199 m) is the second highest peak in Africa. It is an ancient extinct volcano, which during its period of activity (3.1-2.6 million years ago) is thought to have risen to 6,500 m.
history_significance: With its rugged glacier-clad peaks and forested middle slopes, Mount Kenya is one of the most impressive landscapes in East Africa.
highlights: Batian Peak, Nelion Peak, and the unique Afro-alpine flora.

name: Lamu Old Town
overview: Lamu Old Town is the oldest and best-preserved Swahili settlement in East Africa, retaining its traditional functions. Built in coral stone and mangrove timber, the town is characterized by the simplicity of structural forms enriched by such features as inner courtyards, verandas, and elaborately carved wooden doors.
history_significance: Hosted major Muslim religious festivals since the 19th century and has become a significant centre for the study of Islamic and Swahili cultures.
highlights: Lamu Fort, Swahili House Museum, and the donkey sanctuary.

name: Sacred Mijikenda Kaya Forests
overview: The Mijikenda Kaya Forests consist of 11 separate forest sites spread over some 200 km along the coast containing the remains of numerous fortified villages, known as Kayas, of the Mijikenda people.
history_significance: The Kayas, created as from the 16th century but abandoned by the 1940s, are now regarded as the abodes of ancestors and are revered as sacred sites.
highlights: Kaya Giriama, Kaya Rabai, and Kaya Kinondo.

name: Fort Jesus, Mombasa
overview: Built by the Portuguese at the end of the 16th century (1593-1596) to protect the town of Mombasa, the Fort is one of the most outstanding and well preserved examples of 16th Portuguese military fortification.
history_significance: The layout and form of the Fort reflected the Renaissance ideal that perfect proportions and geometric harmony are to be found in the human body.
highlights: The Oman House, the Portuguese canons, and the museum collection.

name: Kenya Lake System in the Great Rift Valley
overview: The Kenya Lake System in the Great Rift Valley, a natural property of outstanding beauty, comprises three inter-linked relatively shallow lakes (Lake Bogoria, Lake Nakuru and Lake Elementaita).
history_significance: Home to 13 globally threatened bird species and some of the highest bird diversities in the world. It is the single most important foraging site for the lesser flamingo anywhere.
highlights: Lake Nakuru (Flamingos and Rhinos), Lake Bogoria (Geysers), and Lake Elementaita.

name: Thimlich Ohinga Archaeological Site
overview: Thimlich Ohinga is a dry-stone walled settlement that was probably built in the 16th century CE. The Ohinga (i.e. settlement) seems to have served as a fort for communities and livestock.
history_significance: It is an exceptional example of the tradition of massive dry-stone walled enclosures, typical of the first pastoral communities in the Lake Victoria Basin.
highlights: The massive dry-stone walls (up to 4m high) and the traditional Luo homesteads.

name: The Historic Town and Archaeological Site of Gede
overview: Gedi is one of the most important Swahili cities on the East African coast, occupied from the 10th to 17th centuries. It features a walled town with mosques, a palace, and houses made of coral stone.
history_significance: A major trade center in the Indian Ocean network, linking African coastal centers with Persia and other areas. The site was abandoned in the 17th century.
highlights: The Great Mosque, the Palace, and the Pillar Tombs.

name: Budapest, including the Banks of the Danube, the Buda Castle Quarter and Andrássy Avenue
overview: This site has the remains of monuments such as the Roman city of Aquincum and the Gothic castle of Buda. It represents one of the world's outstanding urban landscapes.
history_significance: Illustrates the periods of history of the Hungarian capital, including the glory of the Austro-Hungarian Empire.
highlights: Hungarian Parliament Building, Buda Castle, Chain Bridge, and Heroes' Square.

name: Old Village of Hollókő and its Surroundings
overview: Hollókő is an outstanding example of a deliberately preserved traditional settlement. This village, which developed mainly during the 17th and 18th centuries, is a living example of rural life before the agricultural revolution.
history_significance: Preserves the Palóc ethnic group's traditional architecture and lifestyle.
highlights: The traditional houses with wooden porches and the Hollókő Castle ruins.

name: Caves of Aggtelek Karst and Slovak Karst
overview: The variety of formations and the fact that they are concentrated in a restricted area makes the 712 caves currently identified make up a typical temperate-zone karstic system.
history_significance: Displays an extremely rare combination of tropical and glacial climatic effects on karst formation.
highlights: Baradla Cave (Hungary's portion of the Domica-Baradla cave system) and its stalactites.

name: Millenary Benedictine Abbey of Pannonhalma and its Natural Environment
overview: The first Benedictine monks settled here in 996. They went on to convert the Hungarians, to found the country's first school and, in 1055, to write the first document in Hungarian.
history_significance: A symbol of Christianity in Hungary and an outstanding example of the continuous evolution of a monastic community.
highlights: The Basilica, the Library (with 360,000 volumes), and the Arboretum.

name: Hortobágy National Park - the Puszta
overview: The Hortobágy National Park is the vastest continuous natural grassland in Europe. It represents a cultural landscape which preserved intact and visible traces of its traditional land-use forms.
history_significance: Shows the harmonious interaction between man and nature (pastoralism) over two millennia.
highlights: The Nine-Arched Bridge, traditional sweep wells, and the Przewalski's horses.

name: Early Christian Necropolis of Pécs (Sopianae)
overview: In the 4th century A.D., a remarkable series of decorated tombs were constructed in the cemetery of the Roman provincial town of Sopianae (modern Pécs).
history_significance: These are important both structurally and architecturally as early Christian monuments, with murals of outstanding quality.
highlights: The Peter and Paul Burial Chamber and the Cella Septichora.

name: Tokaj Wine Region Historic Cultural Landscape
overview: The Tokaj Wine Region Historic Cultural Landscape demonstrates the long tradition of wine production in this region of low hills and river valleys.
history_significance: The birthplace of Tokaji Aszú wine, the world's oldest botrytized wine. The intricate network of cellars illustrates every facet of wine production.
highlights: The historic wine cellars of Tokaj and the vineyards of Mád.

name: Al-Hijr Archaeological Site (Madâin Sâlih)
overview: Formerly known as Hegra, this is the largest conserved site of the civilization of the Nabataeans south of Petra in Jordan. It features 111 monumental tombs with elaborately ornamented facades.
history_significance: A major site of the Nabataean civilization (1st century BC to 1st century AD), bearing witness to the caravan trade routes.
highlights: Qasr al-Farid (The Lonely Castle) and the Diwan (rock-cut dining room).

name: At-Turaif District in ad-Dir'iyah
overview: Located in the heart of the Arabian Peninsula, north-west of Riyadh, this property was the first capital of the Saudi Dynasty. It preserves distinctive Najdi mud-brick architecture.
history_significance: The citadel of temporal power of the House of Saud and the spread of the Salafi reform in the 18th century.
highlights: Salwa Palace, the mud-brick fortifications, and the Imam Mohammad bin Saud Mosque.

name: Historic Jeddah, the Gate to Makkah
overview: Historic Jeddah (Al-Balad) is an outstanding example of a Red Sea architectural tradition. It was established as a major port for Indian Ocean trade routes and the gateway for Muslim pilgrims to Mecca.
history_significance: Characterized by tower houses built in the late 19th century with large wooden Roshan casements.
highlights: Nasseef House, Al-Shafi'i Mosque, and the intricate Roshan wooden balconies.

name: Rock Art in the Hail Region of Saudi Arabia
overview: This property includes two components: Jabel Umm Sinman at Jubbah and the Jabal al-Manjor and Raat at Shuwaymis. It contains numerous petroglyphs and inscriptions.
history_significance: Traces the history of human populations and the environmental changes from the Holocene period to the present, depicting animals like aurochs and cheetahs.
highlights: Petroglyphs of Jubbah and Shuwaymis.

name: Al-Ahsa Oasis, an Evolving Cultural Landscape
overview: In the eastern Arabian Peninsula, the Al-Ahsa Oasis is a serial property comprising gardens, canals, springs, wells, and a drainage lake, as well as historical buildings.
history_significance: The largest oasis in the world with 2.5 million date palms, representing traces of continued human settlement since the Neolithic period.
highlights: Jawatha Mosque, Ibrahim Palace, and the extensive palm groves.

name: Ḥimā Cultural Area
overview: Located in an arid, mountainous area of southwest Saudi Arabia, on one of the Arabian Peninsula's ancient caravan routes. It features a substantial collection of rock art images.
history_significance: Contains inscriptions in various scripts (Musnad, Aramaic-Nabataean, South-Arabian, Thamudic, Greek, and Arabic), documenting 7,000 years of history.
highlights: Bir Hima wells and the extensive rock art panels.

name: 'Uruq Bani Ma'arid
overview: This site occupies the western part of the Empty Quarter (Rub' al-Khali), the greatest expanse of blown sand on Earth. It features a spectacular desert landscape.
history_significance: The only major sand desert in tropical Asia and the only place where the Arabian Oryx has been successfully reintroduced into the wild in its native habitat.
highlights: The massive linear dunes and the Arabian Oryx population.

name: The Cultural Landscape of Al-Faw Archaeological Area
overview: Located at a strategic point on the ancient trade routes of the Arabian Peninsula, Al-Faw was the capital of the first Kindah Kingdom. It includes the remains of the city, market, temples, and tombs.
history_significance: Testifies to the coexistence of nomadic and settled communities and the management of scarce water resources in a desert environment.
highlights: The ancient market (Souq), the residential area, and the frescoes found in the palace.

name: Urnes Stave Church
overview: The wooden church of Urnes (the stavkirke) stands in the natural setting of Sogn og Fjordane. It was built in the 12th and 13th centuries and brings together traces of Celtic art, Viking traditions, and Romanesque spatial structures.
history_significance: The oldest and most decorated of the 28 remaining stave churches in Norway, representing traditional Scandinavian wooden architecture.
highlights: The intricate wood carvings of the north portal.

name: Bryggen
overview: Bryggen is the historic harbour district in Bergen, one of North Europe's oldest port cities. It was established as a centre for trade by the 12th century.
history_significance: A reminder of the town's importance as part of the Hanseatic League's trading empire from the 14th to the mid-16th century.
highlights: The colorful wooden commercial buildings and the Hanseatic Museum.

name: Røros Mining Town and the Circumference
overview: Røros Mining Town is linked to the copper mines established in the 17th century. The site illustrates the establishment and flourishing of a culture based on the semi-agricultural mining of copper.
history_significance: A unique wooden mining town that has preserved much of its original character, including the smelter and the winter transport route.
highlights: Røros Church, the Smelthytta museum, and the wooden houses.

name: Rock Art of Alta
overview: This group of petroglyphs in the Alta Fjord, near the Arctic Circle, bears the traces of a settlement dating from c. 4200 to 500 B.C.
history_significance: The most significant collection of rock carvings in Northern Europe, depicting hunter-gatherer culture and their rituals.
highlights: Hjemmeluft (Jiepmaluokta) site and the Alta Museum.

name: Vegaøyan - The Vega Archipelago
overview: A cluster of dozens of islands centered on Vega, just south of the Arctic Circle. The landscape reflects a frugal way of life based on fishing and the harvesting of the down of eider ducks.
history_significance: A unique cultural landscape testifying to a sustainable way of life maintained by women (tending eider ducks) for over 1,500 years.
highlights: The eider duck nesting houses and the fishing villages.

name: West Norwegian Fjords – Geirangerfjord and Nærøyfjord
overview: Situated in south-western Norway, Geirangerfjord and Nærøyfjord are among the world's longest and deepest fjords. They are archetypal fjord landscapes.
history_significance: Provides a classic display of glacial processes and geomorphology, with sheer crystalline rock walls rising up to 1,400 m from the sea.
highlights: Geirangerfjord, Nærøyfjord, and the Seven Sisters Waterfall.

name: Rjukan-Notodden Industrial Heritage Site
overview: Located in a dramatic landscape of mountains, waterfalls and river valleys, the site comprises hydroelectric power plants, transmission lines, factories, transport systems and towns.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a new global industry (electrochemical fertilizer production) in the early 20th century, utilizing hydroelectric power.
highlights: Vemork Hydroelectric Power Plant and the industrial towns of Rjukan and Notodden.

name: Historic Town of Banská Štiavnica and the Technical Monuments in its Vicinity
overview: Over the centuries, the town of Banská Štiavnica was visited by many outstanding engineers and scientists who contributed to its fame as a center of mining technology.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a medieval mining center that grew into a town with Renaissance and Baroque palaces, churches, and elegant squares.
highlights: The Old Castle (Starý zámok), the New Castle, and the intricate system of water reservoirs (Tajchy).

name: Levoča, Spišský Hrad and the Associated Cultural Monuments
overview: Spis Castle is one of the largest castle sites in Central Europe. The associated town of Levoča preserves the integrity of its medieval center and fortifications.
history_significance: Illustrates the power of the Saxon settlements in the Spis region and features the work of Master Paul of Levoča.
highlights: Spiš Castle, St. James Church in Levoča (tallest wooden altar in the world), and Spišská Kapitula.

name: Vlkolínec
overview: Vlkolínec, situated in the centre of Slovakia, is a remarkably intact settlement of 45 buildings with the traditional features of a central European village.
history_significance: The most complete group of these kinds of traditional log houses, often found in mountainous areas.
highlights: The traditional log houses and the wooden bell tower.

name: Bardejov Town Conservation Reserve
overview: Bardejov is a small but exceptionally complete and well-preserved example of a fortified medieval town, which typifies the urbanization in this region.
history_significance: A major trade center on the route to Poland, known for its Jewish suburb and 15th-century fortifications.
highlights: Town Hall Square, Church of St. Egidius, and the Jewish Suburb.

name: Wooden Churches of the Slovak part of the Carpathian Mountain Area
overview: The property consists of two Roman Catholic, three Protestant and three Greek Orthodox churches built between the 16th and 18th centuries.
history_significance: Good examples of a rich local tradition of religious architecture, marked by the meeting of Latin and Byzantine cultures.
highlights: Hervartov (oldest), Kežmarok (Articular church), and Ladomirová.

name: Sacred City of Anuradhapura
overview: This sacred city was established around a cutting from the 'tree of enlightenment', the Buddha's fig tree, brought there in the 3rd century B.C. by Sanghamitta.
history_significance: The political and religious capital of Ceylon for 1,300 years, known for its colossal dagobas (stupas).
highlights: Sri Maha Bodhi tree, Ruwanwelisaya Stupa, and Jetavanaramaya.

name: Ancient City of Polonnaruwa
overview: Polonnaruwa was the second capital of Sri Lanka after the destruction of Anuradhapura in 993. It comprises, besides the Brahmanic monuments built by the Cholas, the monumental ruins of the fabulous garden-city created by Parakramabahu I.
history_significance: A masterpiece of medieval landscape gardening and irrigation, featuring the Gal Vihara rock sculptures.
highlights: Gal Vihara (Rock temple), Royal Palace, and the Quadrangle (Dalada Maluwa).

name: Ancient City of Sigiriya
overview: The ruins of the capital built by the parricidal King Kassapa I (477–95) lie on the steep slopes and at the summit of a granite peak standing some 180m high (the 'Lion's Rock').
history_significance: A unique example of ancient urban planning, integrating the natural rock into a palace complex with famous frescoes.
highlights: The Lion's Paws entrance, the Mirror Wall, and the frescoes of the 'Maidens of the Clouds'.

name: Sinharaja Forest Reserve
overview: Located in south-west Sri Lanka, Sinharaja is the country's last viable area of primary tropical rainforest. More than 60% of the trees are endemic and many of them are considered rare.
history_significance: A biodiversity hotspot with a high degree of endemism in trees, birds, and insects.
highlights: Rainforest trekking and birdwatching (Blue Magpie).

name: Sacred City of Kandy
overview: This sacred Buddhist site, popularly known as the city of Senkadagalapura, was the last capital of the Sinhala kings whose patronage enabled the Dinahala culture to flourish for more than 2,500 years.
history_significance: Home to the Temple of the Tooth Relic (the sacred tooth of the Buddha), a famous pilgrimage site.
highlights: Temple of the Sacred Tooth Relic and Kandy Lake.

name: Old Town of Galle and its Fortifications
overview: Founded in the 16th century by the Portuguese, Galle reached the height of its development in the 18th century, before the arrival of the British. It is the best example of a fortified city built by Europeans in South and South-East Asia.
history_significance: Illustrates the interaction between European architectural styles and South Asian traditions.
highlights: Galle Fort ramparts, the Lighthouse, and the Dutch Reformed Church.

name: Golden Temple of Dambulla
overview: A sacred pilgrimage site for 22 centuries, this cave monastery, with its five sanctuaries, is the largest, best-preserved cave-temple complex in Sri Lanka.
history_significance: Contains outstanding Buddhist mural paintings covering 2,100 m² and 157 statues.
highlights: The five cave shrines and the Golden Buddha statue.

name: Central Highlands of Sri Lanka
overview: This site comprises the Peak Wilderness Protected Area, the Horton Plains National Park and the Knuckles Conservation Forest. These montane forests rise to 2,500 m above sea-level.
history_significance: A super-biodiversity hotspot, home to the Sri Lankan leopard and the western purple-faced langur.
highlights: World's End (Horton Plains), Adam's Peak (Sri Pada), and Baker's Falls.

name: Historic City of Ayutthaya
overview: Founded in 1350, Ayutthaya became the second Siamese capital after Sukhothai. It was destroyed by the Burmese in the 18th century. Its remains, characterized by the prang (reliquary towers) and gigantic monasteries, give an idea of its past splendour.
history_significance: Once one of the world's largest and most cosmopolitan cities, a center of global diplomacy and commerce.
highlights: Wat Mahathat (Buddha head in tree), Wat Phra Si Sanphet, and Wat Chaiwatthanaram.

name: Historic Town of Sukhothai and Associated Historic Towns
overview: Sukhothai was the capital of the first Kingdom of Siam in the 13th and 14th centuries. It illustrates the beginnings of Thai architecture.
history_significance: The cradle of Thai civilization, where the Thai alphabet and the 'Sukhothai style' of art were developed.
highlights: Wat Mahathat (Sukhothai), Wat Chang Lom (Si Satchanalai), and Wat Phra Kaeo (Kamphaeng Phet).

name: Thungyai-Huai Kha Khaeng Wildlife Sanctuaries
overview: Stretching over more than 600,000 ha along the Myanmar border, the sanctuaries contain examples of almost all the forest types of continental South-East Asia.
history_significance: The largest conservation area in Mainland Southeast Asia and a stronghold for tigers, elephants, and leopards.
highlights: The untouched biodiversity and the pristine river systems.

name: Ban Chiang Archaeological Site
overview: Ban Chiang is considered the most important prehistoric settlement so far discovered in South-East Asia. It marks an important stage in human cultural, social and technological evolution.
history_significance: Evidence of early wet-rice agriculture and bronze metallurgy dating back to 1500 BC, challenging previous theories about Asian development.
highlights: The distinctive red painted pottery and the on-site museum.

name: Dong Phayayen-Khao Yai Forest Complex
overview: The complex spans 230 km between Ta Phraya National Park on the Cambodian border and Khao Yai National Park. It is home to more than 800 species of fauna.
history_significance: A critical habitat for the survival of endangered species like the pileated gibbon and the Siamese crocodile.
highlights: Haew Narok Waterfall and the wildlife watching in Khao Yai.

name: Kaeng Krachan Forest Complex
overview: Located along the Tenasserim range, this site is a major biodiversity hotspot. It contains semi-evergreen/dry evergreen and moist evergreen forest.
history_significance: A convergence zone for four zoogeographical regions, hosting endemic and endangered species like the Asian elephant and tiger.
highlights: Kaeng Krachan Lake and the abundance of butterfly species.

name: The Ancient Town of Si Thep and its Associated Dvaravati Monuments
overview: Si Thep was a major urban center of the Dvaravati culture (6th–10th centuries) and later the Khmer Empire. It features a twin-town layout with an inner and outer city.
history_significance: Demonstrates the coexistence of Hinduism and Theravada and Mahayana Buddhism, with unique Dvaravati sculpture and architecture.
highlights: Khao Klang Nok (massive stupa), Khao Klang Nai, and the Prang Si Thep.

name: Phu Phrabat, a testimony to the Sīma stone tradition of the Dvaravati period
overview: Located within the Phu Phra Bat Historical Park, this site features a collection of large sandstone Sīma stones (boundary markers) from the Dvaravati period (7th–11th centuries).
history_significance: Represents the unique adaptation of the Buddhist Sīma stone tradition in the region, marking sacred areas within natural rock shelters.
highlights: Hor Nang Usa (rock formation) and the intricate Sīma stone carvings.

name: Al Qal'a of Beni Hammad
overview: In a mountainous site of extraordinary beauty, the ruins of the first capital of the Hammadid emirs, founded in 1007 and demolished in 1152, provide an authentic picture of a fortified Muslim city.
history_significance: A key site of Islamic civilization, influencing the development of Arab architecture in North Africa and Andalusia.
highlights: The Great Mosque with its 25-meter minaret and the Palace of the Emirs.

name: Djémila
overview: Djémila, or Cuicul, with its forum, temples, basilicas, triumphal arches and houses, is an interesting example of Roman town planning adapted to a mountain location.
history_significance: One of the most beautiful Roman ruins in the world, illustrating the grandeur of Roman architecture in North Africa.
highlights: The Arch of Caracalla, the Severan Temple, and the Great Baths.

name: M'Zab Valley
overview: A traditional human habitat created in the 10th century by the Ibadites around five ksour (fortified cities). It has preserved its way of life and building techniques adapted to the desert environment.
history_significance: A masterpiece of desert urban planning and architecture, influencing modern architects like Le Corbusier.
highlights: The five ksour: Ghardaïa, Beni Isguen, Melika, Bou Noura, and El Atteuf.

name: Tassili n'Ajjer
overview: Located in a strange lunar landscape of great geological interest, this site has one of the most important groupings of prehistoric cave art in the world.
history_significance: More than 15,000 drawings and engravings record the climatic changes, the animal migrations and the evolution of human life on the edge of the Sahara from 6000 BC to the first centuries of the present era.
highlights: The 'forest of rock' formations and the prehistoric rock art.

name: Timgad
overview: Timgad lies on the northern slopes of the Aurès mountains and was created ex nihilo as a military colony by the Emperor Trajan in AD 100.
history_significance: The archetypal example of a Roman city built on the grid system (Hippodamian plan).
highlights: The Trajan Arch, the Library, and the Forum.

name: Tipasa
overview: On the shores of the Mediterranean, Tipasa was an ancient Punic trading-post conquered by Rome and turned into a strategic base for the conquest of the kingdoms of Mauretania.
history_significance: A unique group of Phoenician, Roman, palaeo-Christian and Byzantine ruins alongside indigenous monuments.
highlights: The Royal Mausoleum of Mauretania, the Great Basilica, and the Amphitheatre.

name: Kasbah of Algiers
overview: The Kasbah is a unique kind of medina, or Islamic city. It stands in one of the finest coastal sites on the Mediterranean, overlooking the islands where a Carthaginian trading-post was established.
history_significance: Contains the remains of the citadel, old mosques and Ottoman-style palaces, associated with a deep sense of community.
highlights: Ketchaoua Mosque, the Citadel, and the labyrinthine streets.

name: City of Potosí
overview: In the 16th century, this area was regarded as the world's largest industrial complex. The extraction of silver ore relied on a series of hydraulic mills.
history_significance: The 'Imperial City' was the source of the silver that fueled the Spanish Empire and the global economy. Cerro Rico is the mountain made of silver ore.
