highlights: Cerro Rico (Sumaj Orck'o), the Casa de la Moneda (Royal Mint), and the colonial churches.

name: Jesuit Missions of the Chiquitos
overview: Between 1696 and 1760, six settlements of Christianized Indians were founded by the Jesuits, inspired by the 'ideal cities' of the 16th-century philosophers.
history_significance: Unique for blending Catholic architecture with local traditions, these wooden churches are still active centers of the community.
highlights: San Javier, Concepción, and San José de Chiquitos.

name: Historic City of Sucre
overview: Sucre, the first capital of Bolivia, was founded by the Spanish in the first half of the 16th century. Its many well-preserved 16th-century religious buildings illustrate the blending of local architectural traditions with styles imported from Europe.
history_significance: Known as the 'White City' of the Americas, it was the judicial and religious center of the region (Charcas).
highlights: Casa de la Libertad, San Francisco Church, and the Recoleta Monastery.

name: Fuerte de Samaipata
overview: The archaeological site of Samaipata consists of two parts: the hill with its many carvings, believed to have been the ceremonial centre of an ancient town (14th–16th centuries), and the area to the south which formed the administrative and residential district.
history_significance: Featuring a massive sculpted rock (the largest in the world), it served as a ceremonial center for the Mojocoyas and later the Incas.
highlights: The sculpted rock (El Cascabel) and the Inca ruins.

name: Tiwanaku: Spiritual and Political Centre of the Tiwanaku Culture
overview: The city of Tiwanaku was the capital of a powerful pre-Hispanic empire that dominated a large area of the southern Andes and beyond, reaching its apogee between 500 and 900 AD.
history_significance: Notable for its monumental stone architecture, including the Akapana Pyramid and the Gate of the Sun.
highlights: The Gate of the Sun, Kalasasaya Temple, and the Puma Punku complex.

name: Noel Kempff Mercado National Park
overview: The National Park is one of the largest and most intact parks in the Amazon Basin. It contains an evolutionary history dating back over a billion years to the Precambrian period.
history_significance: A pristine wilderness with diverse habitats ranging from Cerrado savannahs to Amazonian forests and impressive waterfalls.
highlights: Huanchaca Plateau and Arcoiris Waterfall.

name: Kyiv: Saint-Sophia Cathedral and Related Monastic Buildings, Kyiv-Pechersk Lavra
overview: Designed to rival Hagia Sophia in Constantinople, Saint-Sophia Cathedral symbolizes the 'New Constantinople', capital of the Christian principality of Kyiv, which was created in the 11th century.
history_significance: The Lavra has been a spiritual and intellectual center for Orthodox Christians since its foundation in 1051. It preserves unique subterranean caves and baroque architecture.
highlights: Saint-Sophia Cathedral (Mosaics), Kyiv-Pechersk Lavra (Caves), and the Great Lavra Bell Tower.

locations.name: Church of the Saviour at Berestove

name: L'viv – the Ensemble of the Historic Centre
overview: The city of Lviv, founded in the late Middle Ages, flourished as an administrative, religious and commercial center for several centuries. Its medieval urban topography has been preserved virtually intact.
history_significance: A fusion of architectural and artistic traditions of Eastern Europe with those of Italy and Germany.
highlights: Market Square (Rynok), High Castle Park, and St. George's Cathedral.

name: The Residence of Bukovinian and Dalmatian Metropolitans
overview: Built between 1864 and 1882 by the Czech architect Josef Hlávka, this complex represents a synergy of architectural styles. It now houses the Chernivtsi National University.
history_significance: An outstanding example of 19th-century historicist architecture, reflecting the cultural identity of the Orthodox Church within the Austro-Hungarian Empire.
highlights: The Seminary Church, the Marble Hall, and the Red Hall.

name: Ancient City of Tauric Chersonese and its Chora
overview: Founded by Dorian Greeks in the 5th century BC on the northern shores of the Black Sea, the city and its 'chora' (agricultural hinterland) were a major center of wine production and trade.
history_significance: Provides an outstanding example of an ancient Greek polis with its agricultural hinterland: the polis and extended chora illustrate ancient land-division, urban planning, viticulture and continuous habitation over two millennia.
highlights: The Bell of Chersonesos, the 1935 Basilica ruins, and the ancient vineyards.

locations.name: Chora plot on isthmus of Mayachny Peninsula – part I
Chora plot on isthmus of Mayachny Peninsula – part II

name: The Historic Centre of Odesa
overview: Odesa, a free city, a world port, and a cultural center, developed rapidly in the late 18th and 19th centuries. Its architecture reflects the diversity of its multi-ethnic population.
history_significance: Known as the 'Pearl of the Black Sea', it is an outstanding example of a 19th-century European city planned by engineers and architects from various countries.
highlights: The Potemkin Stairs, Odesa Opera and Ballet Theater, and Primorsky Boulevard.

name: Abu Mena
overview: The church, baptistry, basilicas and public buildings, streets, monasteries, houses and workshops in this early Christian holy city were built over the tomb of the martyr Menas of Alexandria.
history_significance: A major pilgrimage center in Late Antiquity, reflecting the spread of Christianity in Egypt.
highlights: The Great Basilica, the Baptistery, and the Pilgrim's Court.

name: Ancient Thebes with its Necropolis
overview: Thebes, the city of the god Amon, was the capital of Egypt during the period of the Middle and New Kingdoms. With the temples and palaces at Karnak and Luxor, and the necropolises of the Valley of the Kings and the Valley of the Queens.
history_significance: One of the world's greatest open-air museums, testifying to the civilization of ancient Egypt at its height.
highlights: Karnak Temple Complex, Luxor Temple, Valley of the Kings (Tutankhamun's Tomb), and Hatshepsut's Temple.

name: Historic Cairo
overview: Tucked away amid the modern urban area of Cairo lies one of the world's oldest Islamic cities, with its famous mosques, madrasas, hammams and fountains.
history_significance: Founded in the 10th century, it became the new centre of the Islamic world, reaching its golden age in the 14th century.
highlights: Al-Azhar Mosque, Citadel of Saladin, and Khan el-Khalili market.

name: Memphis and its Necropolis – the Pyramid Fields from Giza to Dahshur
overview: The capital of the Old Kingdom of Egypt has some of the most extraordinary funerary monuments in the world, including the Pyramids of Giza, Saqqara, Dahshur, Abu Rawash, and Abu Sir.
history_significance: Contains the only surviving Wonder of the Ancient World (Great Pyramid) and illustrates the evolution of pyramid construction.
highlights: Great Pyramid of Giza, The Great Sphinx, Step Pyramid of Djoser, Red Pyramid, and Pyramids of Abu Sir.

name: Nubian Monuments from Abu Simbel to Philae
overview: This outstanding archaeological area contains magnificent monuments saved from the rising waters of the Nile (Lake Nasser), including Abu Simbel, Philae, and several relocated temples.
history_significance: A masterpiece of ancient Egyptian architecture and a landmark example of international cooperation to save heritage.
highlights: Great Temple of Abu Simbel, Temple of Philae, Temple of Amada, and Wadi es-Sebua.

name: Saint Catherine Area
overview: The Orthodox Monastery of St Catherine stands at the foot of Mount Horeb where, the Old Testament records, Moses received the Tablets of the Law. The area is sacred to three world religions.
history_significance: Founded in the 6th century, it is the oldest Christian monastery still in use for its initial function.
highlights: St. Catherine's Monastery, Mount Sinai, and the Burning Bush.

name: Wadi Al-Hitan (Whale Valley)
overview: Wadi Al-Hitan contains invaluable fossil remains of the earliest, and now extinct, suborder of whales, Archaeoceti. These fossils represent one of the major stories of evolution: the emergence of the whale as an ocean-going mammal from a land-based life.
history_significance: The most important site in the world to demonstrate the evolution of whales.
highlights: Fossilized whale skeletons and the desert landscape.

name: Rapa Nui National Park
overview: Rapa Nui (Easter Island) bears witness to a unique cultural phenomenon. A society of Polynesian origin established a unique, imaginative and original tradition of monumental sculpture and architecture.
history_significance: Famous for the Moai statues and ceremonial shrines (ahus) created between the 10th and 16th centuries.
highlights: Ahu Tongariki, Rano Raraku quarry, and Orongo ceremonial village.

name: Churches of Chiloé
overview: The Churches of Chiloé represent a unique example of timber architecture in Latin America. They represent a tradition initiated by the Jesuit Peripatetic Mission in the 17th and 18th centuries.
history_significance: Outstanding examples of the fusion of European and indigenous building traditions. The site includes 16 wooden churches.
highlights: Church of Castro, Church of Achao, and Church of Quinchao.

name: Historic Quarter of the Seaport City of Valparaíso
overview: The historic quarter of Valparaíso presents an excellent example of late 19th-century urban and architectural development in Latin America. In its natural amphitheater-like setting, the city is characterized by a vernacular urban fabric.
history_significance: A key commercial port on the sea routes of the Pacific coast of South America.
highlights: The funiculars (elevators), Cerro Concepción, and Cerro Alegre.

name: Humberstone and Santa Laura Saltpeter Works
overview: These works contain over 200 former saltpeter works where workers from Chile, Peru and Bolivia lived in company towns and forged a distinct communal Pampinos culture.
history_significance: Represents the industrial heritage of the saltpeter era (1880–1930) which transformed the global agricultural landscape.
highlights: The industrial plant ruins and the workers' theatre.

name: Sewell Mining Town
overview: Situated at 2,000 m in the Andes, Sewell was built by the Braden Copper Company in 1905 to house workers at what was to become the world's largest underground copper mine, El Teniente.
history_significance: An outstanding example of the company towns that were born in remote parts of the world from the fusion of local labour and resources with an industrialized nation's capital and technology.
highlights: The steep staircases (no streets for cars) and timber buildings.

name: Settlement and Artificial Mummification of the Chinchorro Culture in the Arica and Parinacota Region
overview: The site consists of three components: Faldeo Norte del Morro de Arica, Colón 10, and the mouth of the Camarones River. It testifies to a marine hunter-gatherer culture that resided in the arid northern coast of the Atacama Desert.
history_significance: Contains the oldest archaeological evidence of artificial mummification of bodies in the world (dating back 7,000 years).
highlights: The Chinchorro Mummies and the Morro de Arica.

locations.name: Faldeo Norte del Morro de Arica

name: Petra
overview: Inhabited since prehistoric times, this Nabataean caravan-city, situated between the Red Sea and the Dead Sea, was an important crossroads between Arabia, Egypt and Syria-Phoenicia.
history_significance: Famous for its rock-cut architecture and water conduit system, it is one of the world's most famous archaeological sites.
highlights: The Treasury (Al-Khazneh), The Monastery (Ad Deir), and the Siq.

name: Quseir Amra
overview: Built in the early 8th century, this desert castle is an exceptionally well-preserved example of Umayyad architecture. It was both a fortress with a garrison and a residence of the Umayyad caliphs.
history_significance: Notable for its extensive frescoes depicting hunting scenes, fruit, wine, and naked women, which are unique in Islamic art.
highlights: The Reception Hall frescoes and the Bathhouse.

name: Um er-Rasas (Kastrom Mefa'a)
overview: Most of this archaeological site has not been excavated. It contains remains from the Roman, Byzantine, and Early Muslim periods (end of 3rd to 9th centuries AD).
history_significance: Features a fortified Roman military camp and 16 churches, some with well-preserved mosaic floors.
highlights: The Church of St. Stephen and its mosaic floor depicting ancient cities.

name: Wadi Rum Protected Area
overview: The 74,000-hectare property bears witness to the evolution of pastoral, agricultural, and urban activity in the region. The varied desert landscape includes narrow gorges, natural arches, and towering cliffs.
history_significance: Contains 25,000 rock carvings and 20,000 inscriptions tracing the evolution of human thought and the early development of the alphabet.
highlights: Khazali Canyon petroglyphs and the Seven Pillars of Wisdom rock formation.

name: Baptism Site "Bethany Beyond the Jordan" (Al-Maghtas)
overview: Situated on the eastern bank of the River Jordan, nine kilometres north of the Dead Sea, the archaeological site consists of two distinct areas: Tell Al-Kharrar (Elijah's Hill) and the area of the churches of Saint John the Baptist.
history_significance: Believed to be the location where Jesus of Nazareth was baptized by John the Baptist.
highlights: Elijah's Hill, the baptismal pools, and the modern churches.

locations.name: Area of the Churches of St. John the Baptist

name: As-Salt - The Place of Tolerance and Urban Hospitality
overview: Built on three closely spaced hills in the Balqa highland, As-Salt was an important trading link between the eastern desert and the west. It flourished during the late Ottoman period.
history_significance: Distinctive yellow limestone architecture and a tradition of hospitality and tolerance between Muslim and Christian communities.
highlights: The Historic Old Salt Museum (Abu Jaber House) and the Hammam Street.

name: Umm Al-Jimāl
overview: A rural settlement in northern Jordan that developed organically on the site of an earlier Roman settlement. It features structures built from black basalt rock.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a Hauran-style settlement, illustrating the transition from Late Antiquity to the Umayyad period in a rural context.
highlights: The Barracks, the numerous churches, and the sophisticated water collection system.

name: Fortress of Suomenlinna
overview: Built in the second half of the 18th century by Sweden on a group of islands located at the entrance of Helsinki's harbour, this fortress is an especially interesting example of European military architecture.
history_significance: Served as a defense against Russian expansion and has been under Swedish, Russian, and Finnish rule.
highlights: King's Gate, the Great Courtyard, and the dry dock.

name: Old Rauma
overview: Situated on the Gulf of Bothnia, Rauma is one of the oldest harbours in Finland. Built around a Franciscan monastery, the mid-15th-century Holy Cross Church is still standing.
history_significance: An outstanding example of an old Nordic wooden town constructed in the vernacular tradition.
highlights: The Church of the Holy Cross and the historic wooden houses.

name: Petäjävesi Old Church
overview: The Petäjävesi Old Church was built of logs between 1763 and 1765. It is a typical example of an architectural tradition that is unique to eastern Scandinavia.
history_significance: Combines the Renaissance conception of a centrally planned church with older forms derived from Gothic groin vaults.
highlights: The wooden bell tower and the unpainted interior.

name: Verla Groundwood and Board Mill
overview: The Verla Groundwood and Board Mill and its associated residential area is an outstanding, remarkably well-preserved example of the small-scale rural industrial settlements associated with pulp, paper and board production.
history_significance: A living museum of the early forest industry in Northern Europe during the 19th and early 20th centuries.
highlights: The mill buildings, the workers' cottages, and the Verlankoski rapids.

name: Bronze Age Burial Site of Sammallahdenmäki
overview: This Bronze Age burial site features more than 30 granite burial cairns, providing a unique insight into the funerary practices and social and religious structures of northern Europe more than three millennia ago.
history_significance: The most extensive and representative Bronze Age cairn site in all of Scandinavia.
highlights: Kirkonlaattia (Church Floor) and the Huilu cairn.

name: Ngorongoro Conservation Area
overview: The Ngorongoro Conservation Area covers vast highland plains, savannah, woodlands and volcanic mountains. Its centerpiece is the Ngorongoro Crater, the world's largest unbroken caldera. The area also protects archaeological sites, including Olduvai Gorge, offering a record of human evolution spanning nearly 4 million years.
history_significance: It is globally significant for wildlife diversity and seasonal migrations as well as its evidence of early human evolution. The region contains famous paleoanthropological discoveries such as those from Olduvai Gorge and Laetoli hominid footprints.
highlights: Ngorongoro Crater, Olduvai Gorge, Laetoli hominid footprints, vast herds of wildlife including the Big Five, dramatic volcanic landscape.

name: Ruins of Kilwa Kisiwani and Ruins of Songo Mnara
overview: Located on two islands close to each other just off the Tanzanian coast about 300km south of Dar es Salaam. These were major Swahili trading cities that controlled the Indian Ocean trade with Arabia, India, and China.
history_significance: Kilwa Kisiwani was described by Ibn Battuta in the 14th century as one of the most beautiful cities in the world. It features the Great Mosque and the Palace of Husuni Kubwa.
highlights: The Great Mosque of Kilwa, Husuni Kubwa Palace, and the Gereza (Fort).

name: Serengeti National Park
overview: The Serengeti contains 1.5 million ha of savanna. It is famous for its annual migration of huge herds of herbivores (wildebeest, gazelles, and zebras), followed by their predators.
history_significance: One of the most impressive natural events in the world, preserving an intact ecosystem of the Pleistocene era.
highlights: The Great Migration, Seronera Valley, and the kopjes (rock formations).

name: Selous Game Reserve
overview: Large numbers of elephants, black rhinoceroses, cheetahs, giraffes, hippopotamuses and crocodiles live in this immense sanctuary, which measures 50,000 km² and is relatively undisturbed by human impact.
history_significance: One of the largest protected areas in Africa, featuring a high diversity of vegetation zones and the Rufiji River system.
highlights: Rufiji River, Stiegler's Gorge, and the walking safaris.

name: Kilimanjaro National Park
overview: At 5,895 m, Kilimanjaro is the highest point in Africa. This volcanic massif stands in splendid isolation above the surrounding plains, with its snowy peak looming over the savanna.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a super-volcano structure with five distinct vegetation zones from the base to the summit.
highlights: Uhuru Peak (Kibo), Mawenzi Peak, and the Shira Plateau.

name: Stone Town of Zanzibar
overview: The Stone Town of Zanzibar is a fine example of the Swahili coastal trading towns of East Africa. It retains its urban fabric and townscape virtually intact.
history_significance: A cultural fusion of African, Arab, Indian, and European influences, and a major center of the spice trade and the slave trade abolition movement.
highlights: House of Wonders, the Old Fort, and the Anglican Cathedral (former slave market).

name: Kondoa Rock-Art Sites
overview: On the eastern slopes of the Masai escarpment bordering the Great Rift Valley are natural rock shelters, overhanging slabs of sedimentary rocks fragmented by rift faults, whose vertical planes have been used for rock paintings for at least two millennia.
history_significance: Provides a unique testimony to the changing socio-economic base of the region from hunter-gatherer to agro-pastoralist societies.
highlights: Mongomi wa Kolo rock shelter and the stylized paintings.

name: Island of Gorée
overview: The island of Gorée lies off the coast of Senegal, opposite Dakar. From the 15th to the 19th century, it was the largest slave-trading centre on the African coast.
history_significance: A memorial to the African diaspora and a symbol of human exploitation and reconciliation.
highlights: House of Slaves (Maison des Esclaves), Fort d'Estrées, and the colonial architecture.

name: Niokolo-Koba National Park
overview: Located in a well-watered area along the banks of the Gambia river, the gallery forests and savannas of Niokolo-Koba National Park have a very rich fauna.
history_significance: Contains the largest population of Derby eland (giant eland), as well as chimpanzees, lions, and elephants.
highlights: The Gambia River and the wildlife viewing.

name: Djoudj National Bird Sanctuary
overview: Situated in the Senegal river delta, the Djoudj Sanctuary is a wetland of 16,000 ha. It serves as a major wintering site for migratory birds.
history_significance: One of the most important bird sanctuaries in the world, specifically for the white pelican and the purple heron.
highlights: The Great White Pelican colony and the Senegal River delta.

name: Island of Saint-Louis
overview: Founded as a French colonial settlement in the 17th century, Saint-Louis was urbanised in the mid-19th century. It was the capital of Senegal from 1872 to 1957.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a colonial city, characterized by its grid plan, quay system, and the Faidherbe Bridge.
highlights: Faidherbe Bridge and the colonial Governor's Palace.

name: Stone Circles of Senegambia
overview: The site consists of four large groups of stone circles that represent an extraordinary concentration of over 1,000 monuments in a band 100 km wide along some 350 km of the River Gambia.
history_significance: The largest and best-preserved complex of megalithic stone circles in the world, dating from the 3rd century BC to the 16th century AD.
highlights: Sine Ngayène (largest site) and Wanar (Senegal).

name: Saloum Delta
overview: Fishing and shellfish gathering have sustained human life in the 5,000 km² property formed by the arms of three rivers. The site comprises brackish channels encompassing over 200 islands.
history_significance: Remarkable for its 218 shellfish mounds (amas coquilliers), some of which are used as burial sites, testifying to human occupation over 2,000 years.
highlights: The shell mounds (tumuli) and the mangrove ecosystem.

name: Bassari Country: Bassari, Fula and Bedik Cultural Landscapes
overview: Located in the south-east of Senegal, the property includes three distinct geographical areas: the Bassari–Salémata area, the Bedik–Bandafassi area and the Fula–Dindéfello area.
history_significance: A well-preserved multicultural landscape where indigenous peoples adapted their agricultural and settlement practices to the physical environment.
highlights: Iwol (Bedik village), Salémata, and the Dindéfello Falls.

name: Hatra
overview: A large fortified city under the influence of the Parthian Empire and capital of the first Arab Kingdom. Hatra withstood invasions by the Romans in AD 116 and 198 thanks to its high, thick walls reinforced by towers.
history_significance: An excellent example of the blending of Hellenistic and Roman architecture with Eastern decorative features.
highlights: The Great Temple complex, the proliferation of statues, and the massive defensive walls.

name: Ashur (Qal'at Sherqat)
overview: The ancient city of Ashur is located on the Tigris River. It dates back to the 3rd millennium BC. From the 14th to the 9th centuries BC it was the first capital of the Assyrian Empire.
history_significance: The religious capital of the Assyrians and the site of the temple of the national god Ashur.
highlights: The Ziggurat of Ashur and the remains of the Royal Palaces.

name: Samarra Archaeological City
overview: Samarra was the capital of the Abbasid Caliphate from 836 to 892. It is the site of one of the largest mosques in the Islamic world, known for its unique spiral minaret.
history_significance: A powerful Islamic capital that controlled a vast empire, influencing art and architecture (such as the 'Samarra style' of stucco).
highlights: The Great Mosque of Samarra (Malwiya Minaret), Abu Dulaf Mosque, and the Caliphal Palace.

name: Erbil Citadel
overview: A fortified settlement on top of an imposing ovoid-shaped tell. The citadel has been continuously inhabited for millennia, with layers of settlements dating back to the 6th millennium BC.
history_significance: One of the longest continuously inhabited sites in the world, featuring a distinct fan-like urban pattern.
highlights: The Grand Gate, the Textile Museum, and the traditional courtyard houses.

name: The Ahwar of Southern Iraq: Refuge of Biodiversity and the Relict Landscape of the Mesopotamian Cities
overview: This site comprises three archaeological sites (Ur, Uruk, and Eridu) and four wetland marsh areas. It represents the 'cradle of civilization' and a unique aquatic ecosystem in an arid environment.
history_significance: The Sumerian cities developed alongside the marshes between the 4th and 3rd millennia BCE. The marshes are home to the Ma'dan culture.
highlights: Ziggurat of Ur, Uruk archaeological site, and the Huwaizah Marshes.

name: Babylon
overview: Situated 85 km south of Baghdad, the ruins of Babylon represent the capital of the Neo-Babylonian Empire. It includes the remains of temples, palaces, and the city walls.
history_significance: One of the most influential cities of the ancient world, associated with Hammurabi, Nebuchadnezzar II, the Hanging Gardens, and the Tower of Babel.
highlights: The Ishtar Gate foundations, the Lion of Babylon, and the Processional Way.

name: Itchan Kala
overview: Itchan Kala is the inner fortified city of the ancient oasis of Khiva. It preserved a coherent and well-maintained urban environment with monuments primarily from the 18th and 19th centuries.
history_significance: A major stop on the Silk Road and the last resting place of caravans before crossing the desert to Iran.
highlights: Kalta Minor Minaret, Juma Mosque, and the Tash-Hauli Palace.

name: Historic Centre of Bukhara
overview: Bukhara is the most complete example of a medieval city in Central Asia, with an urban fabric that has remained largely intact.
history_significance: A major cultural center of the Islamic world, home to the Samanid Mausoleum (10th century), a masterpiece of Islamic architecture.
highlights: Kalyan Minaret, Samanid Mausoleum, and the trading domes.

name: Historic Centre of Shakhrisyabz
overview: Shakhrisyabz contains exceptional monuments from the Timurid period. It was the birthplace of Amir Timur (Tamerlane).
history_significance: Features the remains of the Ak-Saray Palace, built to be the most magnificent of all Timur's constructions.
highlights: Ak-Saray Palace ruins, Dorut Tilavat Complex, and the Kok Gumbaz Mosque.

name: Samarkand – Crossroad of Cultures
overview: The historic town of Samarkand is a crossroad and melting pot of the world's cultures. It was the capital of the Timurid Empire.
history_significance: One of the most important cities on the Silk Road, showcasing the height of Islamic architecture in the 14th and 15th centuries.
highlights: Registan Square, Gur-e-Amir Mausoleum, Bibi-Khanym Mosque, and Shah-i-Zinda.

name: Western Tien-Shan
overview: A transboundary property comprising 13 sites across Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Uzbekistan. It features diverse landscapes ranging from dark conifer forests to alpine meadows and glaciers, illustrating the evolution of the Tien-Shan mountain system.
history_significance: A global center of origin for many cultivated fruit crops (wild apples, apricots, pistachios, walnuts) and a critical habitat for endangered species like the Snow Leopard and the Menzbier's Marmot.
highlights: Aksu-Jabagly Nature Reserve (Kazakhstan), Sary-Chelek Biosphere Reserve (Kyrgyzstan), and Chatkal Biosphere Reserve (Uzbekistan).

name: Cold Winter Deserts of Turan
overview: This transnational property represents the cold winter deserts of the temperate zone. It spans the arid lands of Central Asia, characterized by extreme temperature fluctuations—very hot summers and freezing winters.
history_significance: The site represents a unique ecosystem with significant ongoing ecological and biological processes. It is critical for the survival of ungulates like the Goitered Gazelle, Saiga Antelope, and Urial, as well as specialized desert flora like Saxaul forests.
highlights: The Singing Dunes of Altyn-Emel, the salt marshes of Barsa-Kelmes, the vast sand seas of the Karakum, and the dramatic cliffs of the Ustyurt Plateau.

name: Silk Roads: Zarafshan-Karakum Corridor
overview: This transboundary corridor covers a 866 km stretch of the Silk Roads, linking the rugged mountains of Tajikistan, the fertile Zarafshan River valley in Uzbekistan, and the Karakum Desert in Turkmenistan.
history_significance: A vital artery for East-West exchange from the 2nd century BCE to the 16th century CE, this corridor illustrates the interaction between sedentary and nomadic cultures and features diverse architecture like caravanserais, forts, and water management systems.
highlights: Ancient Penjikent (Tajikistan), Rabati Malik Caravanserai (Uzbekistan), and the Amul Settlement (Turkmenistan).

name: Anjar
overview: The city of Anjar was founded by Caliph Walid I at the beginning of the 8th century. The ruins reveal a very regular layout, reminiscent of the palace-cities of ancient times, and are a unique testimony to city planning under the Umayyads.
history_significance: It is the only Umayyad trading city in Lebanon, never completed and destroyed quickly, preserving a snapshot of 8th-century Islamic architecture.
highlights: The Grand Palace, the Tetrapylon, and the Umayyad Baths.

name: Baalbek
overview: Baalbek, also known as Heliopolis, counts among the wonders of the ancient world. It contains some of the largest and best-preserved Roman temples ever built.
history_significance: A Phoenician city that became a major Roman religious center. The Temple of Jupiter is built on a massive podium with some of the largest stone blocks in the world.
highlights: Temple of Bacchus (exceptionally preserved), Temple of Jupiter, and the Six Columns.

name: Byblos
overview: Byblos is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. It bears witness to the beginnings of the Phoenician civilization.
history_significance: Directly associated with the history and diffusion of the Phoenician alphabet. The ruins range from Neolithic huts to a Crusader castle.
highlights: The Crusader Castle, the Phoenician Necropolis, and the Temple of the Obelisks.

name: Tyre
overview: According to legend, Tyre was the birthplace of Europa and Dido. It was a great Phoenician city that ruled the seas and founded colonies such as Carthage and Cadiz.
history_significance: Once famous for the discovery of purple dye. It features impressive Roman ruins, including a hippodrome and a triumphal arch.
highlights: The Roman Hippodrome (one of the largest), the Al-Bass archaeological site, and the Triumphal Arch.

name: Ouadi Qadisha (the Holy Valley) and the Forest of the Cedars of God (Horsh Arz el-Rab)
overview: The Qadisha Valley is one of the most important early Christian monastic settlements in the world. Nearby stands the Forest of the Cedars of God, survivors of the immense forests that were prized in antiquity.
history_significance: A refuge for Maronite monks for centuries, containing rock-cut monasteries. The cedars were cited 103 times in the Bible.
highlights: Monastery of Qozhaya, Monastery of Qannoubine, and the ancient Cedar trees.

name: Rachid Karami International Fair-Tripoli
overview: Located in Tripoli, this fair complex was designed by the Brazilian architect Oscar Niemeyer in the 1960s. It is one of the major representative works of 20th-century modern architecture in the Arab Near East.
history_significance: An outstanding example of world modernism, symbolizing the modernization policy of Lebanon. It was never fully completed due to the civil war.
highlights: The Grand Canopy (boomerang shape), the Experimental Theatre (dome), and the Space Museum/Helipad.

name: Mausoleum of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi
overview: The Mausoleum was built at the time of Timur (Tamerlane), from 1389 to 1405. It is dedicated to the famous Sufi master Khoja Ahmed Yasawi.
history_significance: One of the largest and best-preserved constructions of the Timurid period. Its unfinished state provides evidence of construction techniques of the time.
highlights: The massive main dome (largest brick dome in Central Asia) and the bronze cauldron (Taykazan).

name: Petroglyphs within the Archaeological Landscape of Tamgaly
overview: Set around the lush Tamgaly Gorge, amidst the vast, arid Chu-Ili mountains, is a remarkable concentration of some 5,000 petroglyphs (rock carvings) dating from the second half of the 2nd millennium BC to the beginning of the 20th century.
history_significance: A testimony to the husbandry, social organization, and rituals of pastoral peoples of the Central Asian steppes.
highlights: The 'Sun-headed' deities petroglyphs and the ancient altars.

name: Saryarka – Steppe and Lakes of Northern Kazakhstan
overview: Saryarka comprises two protected areas: Naurzum State Nature Reserve and Korgalzhyn State Nature Reserve. It features vast wetlands that serve as key stopovers for migratory birds.
history_significance: Crucial for the Siberian White Crane, Dalmatian Pelican, and Pallas's Fish Eagle. It also protects the Saiga antelope.
highlights: Lake Tengiz (flamingos) and the vast steppe landscapes.

name: Kinabalu Park
overview: Kinabalu Park, in the State of Sabah on the northern end of the island of Borneo, is dominated by Mount Kinabalu (4,095 m), the highest mountain between the Himalayas and New Guinea.
history_significance: A biodiversity hotspot with a wide range of habitats, from lowland rainforest to high-altitude scrub.
highlights: Mount Kinabalu Summit, Poring Hot Springs, and the Rafflesia plants.

name: Gunung Mulu National Park
overview: Gunung Mulu National Park is the most studied tropical karst area in the world, home to enormous caves and limestone formations.
history_significance: Contains the Sarawak Chamber, the largest known cave chamber in the world.
highlights: The Pinnacles, Deer Cave (bat exodus), and Sarawak Chamber.

name: Melaka and George Town, Historic Cities of the Straits of Malacca
overview: Melaka and George Town developed over 500 years of trading and cultural exchange between East and West in the Straits of Malacca.
history_significance: Illustrates multicultural trading settlements shaped by Malay, Portuguese, Dutch, and British influences.
highlights: The Stadthuys (Melaka), A Famosa (Melaka), and the Clan Jetties (George Town).

name: Archaeological Heritage of the Lenggong Valley
overview: The Lenggong Valley contains four archaeological sites spanning nearly 2 million years of human history.
history_significance: Home to the 'Perak Man', the oldest known human skeleton in Peninsular Malaysia.
highlights: Gua Gunung Runtuh, Bukit Bunuh meteorite impact site, and the stone tool assemblages.

name: The Archaeological Heritage of Niah National Park's Caves Complex
overview: Niah Caves hold the longest continuous record of human rainforest occupation, dating back at least 50,000 years.
history_significance: Discovery site of the 'Deep Skull', the oldest modern human remains in Southeast Asia.
highlights: Niah Great Cave and the Painted Cave.

name: Forest Research Institute Malaysia (FRIM)
overview: FRIM is the largest and oldest man-made tropical forest in the world.
history_significance: A successful reforestation model of degraded mining land.
highlights: Canopy Walkway, Arboretum, and colonial research buildings.

name: Orkhon Valley Cultural Landscape
overview: A cultural landscape with archaeological remains from the 6th century onward.
history_significance: Includes Karakorum, capital of the Mongol Empire.
highlights: Karakorum ruins, Erdene Zuu Monastery, and Orkhon Waterfall.

name: Petroglyphic Complexes of the Mongolian Altai
overview: Three petroglyph complexes illustrating 12,000 years of nomadic culture.
history_significance: Documents the evolution from hunting to herding societies.
highlights: Tsagaan Salaa-Baga Oigor petroglyphs and burial mounds.

name: Great Burkhan Khaldun Mountain and its surrounding sacred landscape
overview: Sacred mountain landscape associated with Mongolian spiritual practices.
history_significance: Traditionally linked to the life of Genghis Khan.
highlights: Burkhan Khaldun peak and sacred oboos.

name: Deer Stone Monuments and Related Bronze Age Sites
overview: Deer stones and Bronze Age khirigsuurs across central Mongolia.
history_significance: Represents artistic and ritual life of Bronze Age nomads.
highlights: Khoid Tamir, Jargalantyn Am, and Uushigiin Uver sites.

name: Archaeological Ruins at Moenjodaro
overview: The ruins of the huge city of Moenjodaro are located in the valley of the Indus River. Built entirely of unbaked brick in the 3rd millennium B.C., it presents the earliest urban preservation in South Asia.
history_significance: A metropolis of the Indus Valley Civilization, exhibiting sophisticated urban planning, drainage systems, and water management.
highlights: The Great Bath, the Buddhist Stupa, and the Citadel area.

name: Taxila
overview: From the ancient Neolithic tumulus of Saraikala to the ramparts of Sirkap and the city of Sirsukh, Taxila illustrates the different stages in the development of a city on the Indus that was alternately influenced by Persia, Greece, and Central Asia.
history_significance: A major center of learning and Buddhism (Gandhara art) from the 5th century BC to the 2nd century AD.
highlights: Dharmarajika Stupa, Jaulian Monastery, and the Sirkap city ruins.

name: Buddhist Ruins of Takht-i-Bahi and Neighbouring City Remains at Sahr-i-Bahlol
overview: The Buddhist monastic complex of Takht-i-Bahi ('Throne of Origins') was founded in the early 1st century A.D. Nearby are the ruins of Sahr-i-Bahlol, a small fortified city of the same period.
history_significance: Exceptionally well-preserved examples of Gandharan Buddhist architecture and monastic life.
highlights: The main stupa court, the assembly hall, and the votive stupas.

name: Historical Monuments at Makli, Thatta
overview: The capital of three successive dynasties and later ruled by the Mughal emperors of Delhi, Thatta was constantly embellished from the 14th to the 18th century. The remains of the city and its necropolis provide a unique view of civilization in Sindh.
history_significance: One of the largest necropolises in the world, with approximately half a million tombs, blending Muslim, Hindu, and Persian architectural styles.
highlights: Tomb of Jam Nizamuddin II and the intricate stone carvings.

name: Fort and Shalamar Gardens in Lahore
overview: These two masterpieces from the time of the brilliant Mughal civilization are located in Lahore. The Fort contains marble palaces and mosques decorated with mosaics and gilt. The Shalamar Gardens are a masterpiece of the Mughal garden tradition.
history_significance: Represents the apogee of Mughal artistic expression under Emperors Shah Jahan and Aurangzeb.
highlights: Sheesh Mahal (Palace of Mirrors), Alamgiri Gate, and the three terraces of Shalamar Gardens.

name: Rohtas Fort
overview: Built by Sher Shah Suri in 1541 to control the Gakhars and the route to Kabul, Rohtas Fort is an exceptional example of early Muslim military architecture in Central and South Asia.
history_significance: A masterpiece of fortification that influenced the development of Mughal architectural style.
highlights: The massive defensive walls (4 km circumference) and the monumental Sohail Gate.

name: Fortifications on the Caribbean Side of Panama: Portobelo-San Lorenzo
overview: These Panamanian forts on the Caribbean coast form part of the defence system built by the Spanish Crown to protect transatlantic trade.
history_significance: Magnificent examples of 17th- and 18th-century military architecture, built to defend the 'Royal Road' and the silver fleet.
highlights: Fort San Lorenzo (mouth of Chagres River) and the batteries of Portobelo.

name: Darien National Park
overview: Forming a bridge between the two continents of the Americas, Darien National Park contains an exceptional variety of habitats - sandy beaches, rocky coasts, mangroves, swamps, and lowland and upland tropical forests.
history_significance: A biodiversity hotspot and home to indigenous tribes (Emberá, Wounaan, Kuna).
highlights: The untouched rainforests and indigenous cultural landscapes.

name: Archaeological Site of Panamá Viejo and Historic District of Panamá
overview: Founded in 1519, Panamá Viejo was the first European settlement on the Pacific coast of the Americas. It was abandoned in the mid-17th century and replaced by the 'Historic District' (Casco Antiguo), which preserves its original street plan.
history_significance: Illustrates the evolution of a Spanish colonial city and its strategic role in global trade.
highlights: Cathedral Tower (Panamá Viejo), Plaza de Francia (Casco Antiguo), and the Golden Altar (San José).

name: Coiba National Park and its Special Zone of Marine Protection
overview: Coiba National Park protects Coiba Island and 38 smaller islands. Buffered from the cold winds and El Niño effects, the marine environment sustains exceptional biodiversity.
history_significance: A natural laboratory for evolution (like the Galapagos) and a vital refuge for threatened marine species like sharks and whales.
highlights: Granito de Oro islet, diving with sharks, and the primary forests.

name: Talamanca Range-La Amistad Reserves / La Amistad National Park
overview: This transboundary property covers large tracts of the highest non-volcanic mountain range in Central America. It includes the Talamanca Range-La Amistad Reserves in Costa Rica and La Amistad National Park in Panama.
history_significance: A unique area where Quaternary glaciers left their mark, allowing the flora and fauna of North and South America to interbreed (Great American Interchange).
highlights: Cloud forests, the jaguar habitat, and the high biodiversity of the Talamanca mountains.

name: The Colonial Transisthmian Route of Panamá
overview: This serial property encompasses the principal components of the colonial transisthmian route across Panama, which connected the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans during the Spanish colonial period. It includes urban centers and military fortifications that illustrate the strategic and economic significance of the isthmus.
history_significance: The route played a critical role in global maritime trade between the 16th and 18th centuries, serving as the backbone of the Spanish Empire's transoceanic exchange. Its components reflect colonial urbanism, military architecture, and the logistical network that moved goods, people, and wealth across the isthmus.
highlights: The ruins of Panamá Viejo, the colonial architecture of the Historic District of Panamá, and the Caribbean fortifications of Portobelo and San Lorenzo.

name: Ancient City of Damascus
overview: Founded in the 3rd millennium B.C., Damascus is one of the oldest cities in the Middle East. It was the capital of the Umayyads and features the Great Mosque, a masterpiece of Islamic architecture.
history_significance: A testament to the civilizations which created it – Hellenistic, Roman, Byzantine, and Islamic. It is considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
highlights: The Great Umayyad Mosque, Al-Azm Palace, and the Souq Al-Hamidiyah.

name: Ancient City of Bosra
overview: Once the capital of the Roman province of Arabia, Bosra was an important stopover on the ancient caravan route to Mecca. A magnificent 2nd-century Roman theatre is preserved within its walls.
history_significance: Contains early Christian ruins and several mosques, including the Al-Omari Mosque, one of the oldest surviving mosques in Islamic history.
highlights: The Roman Theatre (one of the largest and best preserved), the Citadel, and the Al-Omari Mosque.

name: Site of Palmyra
overview: An oasis in the Syrian desert, north-east of Damascus, Palmyra contains the monumental ruins of a great city that was one of the most important cultural centers of the ancient world.
history_significance: Known as the 'Bride of the Desert', it showcases the fusion of Graeco-Roman techniques with local traditions and Persian influences.
highlights: The Temple of Bel, the Colonnaded Street, and the Valley of the Tombs.

name: Ancient City of Aleppo
overview: Located at the crossroads of trade routes from the 2nd millennium B.C., Aleppo was ruled successively by the Hittites, Assyrians, Arabs, Mongols, Mamelukes, and Ottomans.
history_significance: Famous for its Citadel, the 12th-century Great Mosque, and its covered souqs, representing diverse architectural styles.
highlights: The Citadel of Aleppo, the Great Mosque of Aleppo, and the Al-Madina Souq.

name: Crac des Chevaliers and Qal'at Salah El-Din
overview: These two castles represent the most significant examples illustrating the exchange of influences and documenting the evolution of fortified architecture in the Near East during the time of the Crusades.
history_significance: Crac des Chevaliers is considered the best-preserved Crusader castle in the world. Qal'at Salah El-Din shows the Ayyubid fortification features.
highlights: The massive concentric walls of Crac des Chevaliers and the Byzantine citadel of Qal'at Salah El-Din.

name: Ancient Villages of Northern Syria
overview: Also known as the 'Dead Cities', this site comprises some 40 villages grouped in eight parks. They provide remarkable testimony to rural life in late Antiquity and the Byzantine period.
history_significance: Exceptionally well-preserved landscapes featuring pagan temples, churches, cisterns, and bathhouses abandoned in the 8th–10th centuries.
highlights: Church of Saint Simeon Stylites, Serjilla, and Al-Bara.

name: Old Bridge Area of the Old City of Mostar
overview: The historic town of Mostar, spanning a deep valley of the Neretva River, developed in the 15th and 16th centuries as an Ottoman frontier town and during the Austro-Hungarian period.
history_significance: Famous for its Old Bridge (Stari Most), designed by the renowned architect Sinan, which was destroyed in the 1990s conflict and meticulously reconstructed.
highlights: Stari Most (Old Bridge), Koski Mehmed Pasha Mosque, and the old bazaar (Kujundžiluk).

name: Mehmed Paša Sokolović Bridge in Višegrad
overview: Built across the Drina River in the late 16th century by the court architect Mimar Sinan, this bridge is a masterpiece of Ottoman monumental architecture and civil engineering.
history_significance: A symbol of the apogee of the Ottoman Empire, combining beauty and functionality. It gained worldwide fame through Ivo Andrić's novel 'The Bridge on the Drina'.
highlights: The 11 masonry arches and the sofa (central meeting point).

name: Vjetrenica Cave, Ravno
overview: Located in the Dinaric Alps, Vjetrenica ('Wind Cave') is a biodiversity hotspot for cave-dwelling fauna. It contains the world's highest diversity of subterranean aquatic species.
history_significance: A globally significant site for biospeleology, hosting ancient relict species and the unique 'human fish' (Olm).
highlights: The extensive cave system, the Golden Hall, and the subterranean lakes.

name: Baroque Churches of the Philippines
overview: These four churches, the first of which was built by the Spanish in the late 16th century, are located in Manila, Santa Maria, Paoay and Miagao. Their unique architectural style is a reinterpretation of European Baroque by Chinese and Philippine craftsmen.
history_significance: Represents the fusion of European church design and construction using local materials and decorative motifs to withstand the country's physical conditions (Earthquake Baroque).
highlights: San Agustin Church (Manila), Paoay Church (massive buttresses), and Miagao Church (relief facade).

locations.name: Church of La Nuestra Señora de la Asuncion (Santa Maria)
Church of Santo Tomas de Villanueva (Miagao)

name: Tubbataha Reefs Natural Park
overview: The Tubbataha Reefs Natural Park is a protected area of the Philippines located in the middle of the Sulu Sea. It covers 97,030 hectares, including the North and South Atolls and the Jessie Beazley Reef.
history_significance: A pristine coral reef example with a spectacular 100-m perpendicular wall, extensive lagoons, and two coral islands. It is a key habitat for marine megafauna.
highlights: North Atoll, South Atoll, and the lighthouse.

name: Rice Terraces of the Philippine Cordilleras
overview: For 2,000 years, the high rice fields of the Ifugao have followed the contours of the mountains. The fruit of knowledge handed down from one generation to the next, and the expression of sacred traditions and a delicate social balance, they have helped to create a landscape of great beauty.
history_significance: An outstanding example of an evolved, living cultural landscape that can be traced as far back as two millennia.
highlights: Batad Rice Terraces (amphitheater shape) and Bangaan Rice Terraces.

name: Historic City of Vigan
overview: Established in the 16th century, Vigan is the best-preserved example of a planned Spanish colonial town in Asia. Its architecture reflects the coming together of cultural elements from elsewhere in the Philippines, from China and from Europe.
history_significance: Famous for its cobblestone streets and 'Bahay na Bato' (stone houses) which fuse Spanish and Oriental building styles.
highlights: Calle Crisologo, Plaza Salcedo, and the Syquia Mansion.

name: Puerto-Princesa Subterranean River National Park
overview: This park features a spectacular limestone karst landscape with an underground river. One of the river's distinguishing features is that it emerges directly into the sea, and its lower portion is subject to tidal influences.
history_significance: Contains a full 'mountain-to-sea' ecosystem and some of the most important forests in Asia.
highlights: The Underground River cave tour and the karst mountain landscape.

name: Mount Hamiguitan Range Wildlife Sanctuary
overview: Forming a mountain ridge running north-south along the Pujada Peninsula in the south-eastern part of the Eastern Mindanao Biodiversity Corridor, the sanctuary provides critical habitat for a range of plant and animal species.
history_significance: Famous for its unique 'pygmy forest' of century-old trees growing in ultramafic soil.
highlights: The Pygmy Forest and the habitat of the Philippine Eagle.

name: Walled City of Baku with the Shirvanshah's Palace and Maiden Tower
overview: Built on a site inhabited since the Palaeolithic period, the Walled City of Baku reveals evidence of Zoroastrian, Sasanian, Arabic, Persian, Shirvani, Ottoman, and Russian presence in cultural continuity.
history_significance: The Inner City (Icherisheher) preserves much of its 12th-century defensive walls. The Maiden Tower is built over earlier structures dating from the 7th to 6th centuries BC.
highlights: Maiden Tower (Qiz Qalasi), Palace of the Shirvanshahs, and the walled old city streets.

name: Gobustan Rock Art Cultural Landscape
overview: Gobustan covers three areas of a plateau of rocky boulders rising out of the semi-desert of central Azerbaijan, with an outstanding collection of more than 6,000 rock engravings bearing testimony to 40,000 years of rock art.
history_significance: Features images of prehistoric life, including hunting, dancing, and reed boats, as well as the Gaval Dash (musical stone).
highlights: Petroglyphs Museum and the mud volcanoes nearby.

name: Historic Centre of Sheki with the Khan's Palace
overview: The historic city of Sheki is located at the foot of the Greater Caucasus Mountains and divided in two by the Gurjana River. The northern part contains the historic centre and the Khan's Palace.
history_significance: An important city along the Silk Roads, famous for its silk production and the exquisite Sheki Khan's Palace with its 'shebeke' (stained glass) windows.
highlights: Palace of Shaki Khans, Karavansaray, and the cobblestone streets.

name: Cultural Landscape of Khinalig People and “Köç Yolu” Transhumance Route
overview: This property is comprised of the high-mountain village of Khinalig and the Köç Yolu transhumance route. It bears witness to the semi-nomadic lifestyle of the Khinalig people.
history_significance: Illustrates the ancient practice of vertical transhumance, moving livestock between summer pastures in the Caucasus and winter pastures in the lowlands.
highlights: Khinalig Village (highest in Azerbaijan) and the historic migration route.

name: Angkor
overview: Angkor is one of the most important archaeological sites in South-East Asia. Stretching over some 400 km2, including forested area, it contains the magnificent remains of the different capitals of the Khmer Empire.
history_significance: The center of the Khmer Kingdom from the 9th to the 15th century, featuring masterpieces of Khmer art and hydraulic engineering.
highlights: Angkor Wat, Bayon Temple (Angkor Thom), and Ta Prohm (Tomb Raider temple).

name: Temple of Preah Vihear
overview: Situated on the edge of a plateau that dominates the plain of Cambodia, the Temple of Preah Vihear is dedicated to Shiva. It is composed of a series of sanctuaries linked by a system of pavements and staircases.
history_significance: An outstanding masterpiece of Khmer architecture, unique for its linear axis layout along a 800m cliff edge.
highlights: The cliffside location, the Gopuras (entrance pavilions), and the precipice view.

name: Temple Zone of Sambor Prei Kuk, Archaeological Site of Ancient Ishanapura
overview: Identified as Ishanapura, the capital of the Chenla Empire that flourished in the late 6th and early 7th centuries AD. The site comprises more than a hundred temples, ten of which are octagonal.
history_significance: Pre-dates Angkor and features the unique 'Sambor Prei Kuk Style' of art and octagonal temple architecture.
highlights: Prasat Tao (Lion Temple), Prasat Sambor, and the Flying Palaces reliefs.

name: Koh Ker: Archaeological Site of Ancient Lingapura or Chok Gargyar
overview: Koh Ker was the capital of the Khmer Empire under King Jayavarman IV (928–944 AD). It is famous for its unique seven-tiered stepped pyramid, Prasat Prang.
history_significance: Represents a unique period in Khmer history with a distinctive architectural style and massive sculptures.
highlights: Prasat Prang (The Pyramid), Prasat Thom, and the White Elephant temple.

name: Cambodian Memorial Sites: From centres of repression to places of peace and reflection
overview: This serial property comprises three sites that bear witness to the atrocities committed by the Khmer Rouge regime (1975–1979). It includes the former M-13 prison, the Tuol Sleng Genocide Museum (S-21), and the Choeung Ek Genocidal Center.
history_significance: A testament to the genocide and crimes against humanity under the Khmer Rouge. These sites have been transformed from places of suffering into places of memory, peace, and reflection.
highlights: The archives and cells of Tuol Sleng (S-21), the Memorial Stupa at Choeung Ek filled with skulls, and the archaeological remains of the M-13 prison.

name: Historic Centre of Riga
overview: Riga was a major centre of the Hanseatic League, deriving its prosperity from trade with Central and Eastern Europe. Its medieval centre reflects this prosperity, though most of the earliest buildings were destroyed by fire or war.
history_significance: Noted for its Art Nouveau/Jugendstil architecture, which is unparalleled in quality and quantity anywhere in the world, and its 19th-century wooden architecture.
highlights: House of the Blackheads, Riga Cathedral (Dome), and the Art Nouveau district (Albert Street).

name: Old Town of Kuldīga
overview: Located in the western part of Latvia, the Old Town of Kuldīga is an exceptionally well-preserved example of a traditional urban settlement, which developed from a small medieval hamlet into an important administrative centre of the Duchy of Courland and Semigallia.
history_significance: Retains its 18th and 19th-century urban fabric with traditional log architecture and foreign influences illustrating the rich exchange between local and travelling craftsmen.
highlights: The Venta Rapid (widest waterfall in Europe), the Old Brick Bridge, and the historic wooden architecture.

name: Royal Palaces of Abomey
overview: From 1625 to 1900, 12 kings succeeded one another at the head of the powerful Kingdom of Abomey. With the exception of King Akaba, who had his own separate enclosure, they all had their palaces built within the same cob-wall area.
history_significance: A unique testimony to the Kingdom of Dahomey, a powerful military and commercial empire in West Africa. The palace compounds contain bas-reliefs recounting the history of the kingdom.
highlights: The Palaces of Kings Ghézo and Glélé, the Historical Museum of Abomey, and the bas-reliefs.

name: Koutammakou, the Land of the Batammariba
overview: The Koutammakou landscape, straddling the border between north-eastern Togo and north-western Benin, is home to the Batammariba people. It is famous for its takienta tower-houses and living cultural traditions.
history_significance: A cultural landscape where the Batammariba people preserve traditional architecture, land-use patterns, rituals, and a strong spiritual relationship with the natural environment. The property was extended in 2023 to include the Benin side.
highlights: Takienta tower-houses, sacred groves, and the traditional villages such as Nadoba and Boukombé.

name: Taï National Park
overview: This park is one of the last major remnants of the primary tropical forest of West Africa. Its rich natural flora and threatened mammal species such as the pygmy hippopotamus make it a site of great scientific interest.
history_significance: A biodiversity hotspot noted for its population of chimpanzees that have been observed using tools.
highlights: Chimpanzee tracking and the ancient primary rainforest.

name: Comoé National Park
overview: One of the largest protected areas in West Africa, this park is characterized by its great diversity of vegetation. The Comoé river running through the park explains the presence of riparian habitats.
history_significance: An outstanding example of transitional habitats from forest to savannah.
highlights: The Comoé River, gallery forests, and savannah wildlife.

name: Historic Town of Grand-Bassam
overview: The first capital of Côte d'Ivoire, Grand-Bassam is an example of a late 19th- and early 20th-century colonial town planned with quarters specializing in commerce, administration, and housing.
history_significance: Illustrates the complex social relations between Europeans and Africans during the colonial era and the independence movement.
highlights: The Governor's Palace, the Costume Museum, and the N'zima fishing village.

name: Sudanese style mosques in northern Côte d'Ivoire
overview: The eight small adobe mosques, at Tengréla, Kouto, Sorobango, Samatiguila, M'Bengué, Kong and Kaouara, are characterized by protruding timbers, vertical buttresses topped by pottery or ostrich eggs, and tapering minarets.
history_significance: They present a specific architectural style that originated in Djenné (Mali) and spread south with the trans-Saharan trade.
highlights: The Grand Mosque of Kong and the Mosque of Kaouara.

name: Mount Nimba Strict Nature Reserve
overview: Located on the borders of Guinea, Liberia and Côte d'Ivoire, Mount Nimba rises above the surrounding savannah. Its slopes are covered by dense forest at the foot of grassy mountain pastures.
history_significance: A refuge for endemic species such as the viviparous toad and chimpanzees that use stones as tools.
highlights: The high-altitude grasslands and endemic wildlife.

locations.name: Mount Nimba Strict Nature Reserve (Guinean Sector)

name: Virunga National Park
overview: Virunga National Park covers an area of 790,000 ha that comprises an outstanding diversity of habitats, ranging from the swamps of the Rift Valley to the snowfields of Rwenzori.
history_significance: Africa's oldest national park and a critical refuge for the endangered Mountain Gorilla. It also features active volcanoes.
highlights: Mountain Gorillas, Mount Nyiragongo (Lava Lake), and the Rwenzori Mountains.

name: Garamba National Park
overview: Located in the north-eastern part of the country, the park includes immense savannas, grasslands, and woodlands, interspersed with gallery forests along the river banks.
history_significance: Historically the last stronghold of the Northern White Rhinoceros (now likely extinct in the wild) and home to large elephant herds.
highlights: Elephant domestication station (historic) and the vast savanna landscape.

name: Kahuzi-Biega National Park
overview: A vast area of primary tropical forest dominated by two spectacular extinct volcanoes, Kahuzi and Biega. It has a rich and diverse fauna.
history_significance: One of the last refuges of the Eastern Lowland Gorilla (Grauer's gorilla), a subspecies endemic to the DRC.
highlights: Mount Kahuzi, Mount Biega, and Gorilla trekking.

name: Salonga National Park
overview: Salonga National Park is Africa's largest tropical rainforest reserve. Situated at the heart of the central basin of the Congo river, it is very isolated and accessible only by water.
history_significance: The most important habitat for the Bonobo (pygmy chimpanzee), our closest living relative, and the Congo Peafowl.
highlights: The untouched rainforest and the Bonobo habitat.

name: Okapi Wildlife Reserve
overview: The Okapi Wildlife Reserve occupies about one-fifth of the Ituri Forest in the north-east of the country. The reserve contains threatened species of primates and birds.
history_significance: Created to protect the Okapi, a forest giraffe endemic to the DRC. It is also the traditional home of the Mbuti and Efe pygmies.
highlights: Epulu Station and the Ituri Forest ecosystem.

name: Galápagos Islands
overview: Situated in the Pacific Ocean some 1,000 km from the South American continent, these 19 islands and the surrounding marine reserve have been called a unique 'living museum and showcase of evolution'.
history_significance: The site that inspired Charles Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection. Home to unique species like giant tortoises and marine iguanas.
highlights: Charles Darwin Research Station, Tortuga Bay, and Bartolomé Island.

name: City of Quito
overview: Quito, the capital of Ecuador, was founded in the 16th century on the ruins of an Inca city and stands at an altitude of 2,850 m. It has the best-preserved, least altered historic centre in Latin America.
history_significance: A masterpiece of the 'Baroque school of Quito', fusing Spanish, Italian, Moorish, and indigenous art.
highlights: Church of San Francisco, Plaza de la Independencia, and La Compañía de Jesús.

name: Sangay National Park
overview: With its outstanding natural beauty and two active volcanoes, the park illustrates the entire spectrum of ecosystems, ranging from tropical rainforests to glaciers.
history_significance: Contains the Tungurahua and Sangay volcanoes and El Altar (an extinct volcano with a caldera lake).
highlights: Tungurahua Volcano, El Altar (Capac Urcu), and the paramo landscapes.

name: Historic Centre of Santa Ana de los Ríos de Cuenca
overview: Cuenca is a typical inland colonial town of the Andes. Its layout and townscape illustrate the successful implementation of the Renaissance urban planning principles in the Americas.
history_significance: Notable for its fusion of different societies and cultures and its numerous historical buildings made of local marble and brick.
highlights: New Cathedral of Cuenca, Old Cathedral, and the flower market.

name: Archaeological Site of Leptis Magna
overview: Enlarged and embellished by Septimius Severus, who was born there, Leptis Magna was one of the most beautiful cities of the Roman Empire, with its imposing public monuments, harbour, market-place, storehouses, shops and residential districts.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Roman urban planning and architecture in North Africa, showcasing the grandeur of the Severan dynasty.
highlights: The Severan Arch, the Hadrianic Baths, and the massive Amphitheatre.

name: Archaeological Site of Sabratha
overview: A Phoenician trading-post that served as an outlet for the products of the African hinterland, Sabratha was part of the short-lived Numidian Kingdom of Massinissa before being Romanized and rebuilt in the 2nd and 3rd centuries A.D.
history_significance: Famous for its magnificent theatre, one of the best preserved in the Roman world, which retains its three-storey architectural backdrop.
highlights: The Roman Theatre, the Temple of Liber Pater, and the seaside ruins.

name: Archaeological Site of Cyrene
overview: A colony of the Greeks of Thera, Cyrene was one of the principal cities of the Hellenic world. It was Romanized and remained a great capital until the earthquake of 365. A thousand years of history is written into its ruins.
history_significance: One of the most impressive complexes of Greek and Roman ruins in the world, once a center of philosophy (Cyrenaics).
highlights: The Temple of Apollo, the Sanctuary of Demeter and Persephone, and the Great Baths.

name: Rock-Art Sites of Tadrart Acacus
overview: Bordering Tassili N'Ajjer in Algeria, this rocky massif features thousands of cave paintings in very different styles, dating from 12,000 B.C. to A.D. 100.
history_significance: Reflects deep changes in the flora and fauna, and the different ways of life of the populations that succeeded one another in this region of the Sahara.
highlights: Prehistoric rock paintings depicting elephants, giraffes, and camels.

name: Old Town of Ghadamès
overview: Built in an oasis, Ghadamès, the 'pearl of the desert', is one of the oldest pre-Saharan cities and an outstanding example of a traditional settlement.
history_significance: Characterized by a unique vertical division of functions: the ground floor for storage, the first floor for families, and open-air terraces for women.
highlights: The covered streets, traditional mud-brick houses, and the interconnected terraces.

name: Vilnius Historic Centre
overview: Vilnius was the political centre of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania from the 13th to the end of the 18th century. It has had a profound influence on the cultural and architectural development of much of eastern Europe.
history_significance: Preserves an impressive complex of Gothic, Renaissance, Baroque and classical buildings and a medieval layout.
highlights: Gediminas' Tower, Vilnius Cathedral, and St. Anne's Church.

name: Kernavė Archaeological Site (Cultural Reserve of Kernavė)
overview: The Kernavė Archaeological Site represents an exceptional testimony to some 10 millennia of human settlements in this region. It includes the town of Kernavė, forts, unfortified settlements, burial sites and other archaeological monuments.
history_significance: The site of the first capital of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania (13th century) and a complex ensemble of five hillforts.
highlights: The five hillforts (Mounds) and the open-air archaeological museum.

name: Modernist Kaunas: Architecture of Optimism, 1919-1939
overview: Between the two World Wars, Kaunas served as the temporary capital of Lithuania. The city experienced a rapid transformation, resulting in a dense concentration of modernist architecture.
history_significance: An outstanding example of the Modern Movement's adaptation to a specific political, social, and cultural context (national romanticism and functionalism).
highlights: Church of the Resurrection, the Central Post Office, and the Bank of Lithuania building.

name: Bahla Fort
overview: The oasis of Bahla owed its prosperity to the Banu Nebhan tribe who dominated the central Omani region from the 12th to the end of the 15th century. The immense fort with its walls and towers of unbaked brick is a remarkable example of this type of fortification.
history_significance: A masterpiece of mud-brick military architecture in the Omani desert.
highlights: The massive mud-brick walls, the citadel, and the Friday Mosque.

name: Archaeological Sites of Bat, Al-Khutm and Al-Ayn
overview: The protohistoric site of Bat lies near a palm grove in the interior of the Sultanate of Oman. Together with the neighbouring sites, it forms the most complete collection of settlements and necropolises from the 3rd millennium B.C. in the world.
history_significance: Unique evidence of the Bronze Age funeral practices in the Arabian Peninsula.
highlights: The beehive tombs of Al-Ayn and the monumental tower at Bat.

name: Land of Frankincense
overview: The frankincense trees of Wadi Dawkah and the remains of the caravan oases of Shisr/Wubar and the affiliated ports of Khor Rori and Al-Baleed illustrate the trade in frankincense that flourished in this region for many centuries.
history_significance: Testifies to the prominence of the frankincense trade which was one of the most important trading activities in the ancient and medieval world.
highlights: The Frankincense Park (Wadi Dawkah) and the archaeological site of Al-Baleed.

name: Aflaj Irrigation Systems of Oman
overview: The property includes five 'aflaj' irrigation systems and is representative of some 3,000 such systems still in use in Oman. The origins of this method of irrigation may date back to AD 500.
history_significance: An exceptional example of a water management system used in arid regions for over 1,500 years, relying on gravity and communal management.
highlights: Falaj Daris (largest) and the watchtowers protecting the water sources.

name: Ancient City of Qalhat
overview: The site represents the remains of the city of Qalhat, a major port on the east coast of the Arabian Peninsula that developed between the 11th and 15th centuries CE.
history_significance: A key hub in the Indian Ocean trade network, visited and described by Marco Polo and Ibn Battuta. Its decline began after a devastating earthquake and Portuguese attacks.
highlights: The Mausoleum of Bibi Maryam and the remains of the city walls.

name: Birthplace of Jesus: Church of the Nativity and the Pilgrimage Route, Bethlehem
overview: The site identifies the place where Jesus was born. It includes the Church of the Nativity and the pilgrimage route used for centuries. The church was originally built in 339 AD.
history_significance: One of the oldest continuously operating churches in the world and a site of immense religious significance for Christians.
highlights: The Grotto of the Nativity, the silver star marking the birthplace, and the Door of Humility.

name: Palestine: Land of Olives and Vines – Cultural Landscape of Southern Jerusalem, Battir
overview: The landscape of Battir, south-west of Jerusalem, comprises a series of farmed valleys with stone terraces. It illustrates a unique water distribution system used for centuries.
history_significance: An outstanding example of traditional land-use and ancient agricultural practices (dry-stone terracing) supporting olive and vine cultivation.
highlights: The ancient irrigation canals (qanats) and the agricultural terraces.

name: Hebron/Al-Khalil Old Town
overview: The use of a local limestone shaped the construction of the old town of Hebron during the Mamluk period. The centre of interest is the Al-Ibrahimi Mosque/The Tomb of the Patriarchs.
history_significance: A sacred site for Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, housing the tombs of Abraham and his family.
highlights: The Tomb of the Patriarchs (Al-Ibrahimi Mosque) and the Mamluk-style architecture.

name: Ancient Jericho/Tell es-Sultan
overview: Located in the Jordan Valley, Tell es-Sultan is an oval-shaped mound that contains archaeological deposits of human activity dating back to about 10,500 BC.
history_significance: The oldest fortified city in the world, providing a unique timeline of urbanization and the transition to a sedentary lifestyle.
highlights: The Neolithic Tower (one of the oldest stone structures) and the city walls.

name: Saint Hilarion Monastery/ Tell Umm Amer
overview: Situated on the coastal dunes of Nuseirat, Tell Umm Amer contains the ruins of the Monastery of Saint Hilarion. It was founded in the 4th century by Hilarion, the father of Palestinian monasticism.
history_significance: One of the oldest, largest, and most complex monasteries in the Middle East, illustrating the spread of monasticism in the Holy Land.
highlights: The large church complex, the crypt, and the extensive mosaic floors.

name: Stari Ras and Sopoćani
overview: On the outskirts of Stari Ras, the first capital of Serbia, there is an impressive group of medieval monuments consisting of fortresses, churches and monasteries.
history_significance: The Monastery of Sopoćani contains frescoes that are considered masterpieces of Serbian medieval art and precursors to the Renaissance.
highlights: Sopoćani Monastery frescoes, Church of St Peter and Paul, and the fortress of Ras.

locations.name: Church of the Holy Apostles Peter and Paul
Archaeological Site of the Medieval Town of Ras

name: Studenica Monastery
overview: The Studenica Monastery was established in the late 12th century by Stefan Nemanja, founder of the medieval Serb state. It is the largest and richest of Serbia's Orthodox monasteries.
history_significance: Its two principal monuments, the Church of the Virgin and the Church of the King, enshrine priceless collections of 13th- and 14th-century Byzantine painting.
highlights: The white marble Church of the Virgin and the fresco 'The Crucifixion'.

name: Medieval Monuments in Kosovo
overview: The four components of the site reflect the high points of the Byzantine-Romanesque ecclesiastical culture that developed in the Balkans between the 13th and 17th centuries.
history_significance: Features unique wall paintings that developed a distinct style of Balkan art. The site includes Dečani Monastery, Patriarchate of Peć, and others.
highlights: Dečani Monastery, Gračanica Monastery, and the Church of the Holy Virgin of Ljeviš.

locations.name: Church of the Holy Virgin of Ljeviš

name: Gamzigrad-Romuliana, Palace of Galerius
overview: The Late Roman fortified palace compound and memorial complex of Gamzigrad-Romuliana was commissioned by Emperor Caius Valerius Galerius Maximianus in the late 3rd and early 4th centuries.
history_significance: A unique testimony to the Roman building tradition of the Second Tetrarchy period, featuring palaces, temples, and mosaics.
highlights: The Felix Romuliana palace ruins and the fine floor mosaics.

name: Škocjan Caves
overview: This system of limestone caves comprises collapsed dolines, some 6 km of underground passages with a total depth of more than 200 m, and one of the largest known underground canyons in the world.
history_significance: An exceptional example of karst hydrography and speleology, featuring the vast Martel's Chamber.
highlights: The underground canyon of the Reka River and the Silent Cave.

name: The works of Jože Plečnik in Ljubljana – Human Centred Urban Design
overview: The work of architect Jože Plečnik in Ljubljana between the two World Wars presents an example of a human-centred urban design that changed the identity of the city.
history_significance: A unique example of modern urban planning based on classical and regional elements, creating a series of public spaces and institutions.
highlights: Triple Bridge (Tromostovje), National and University Library, and Žale Cemetery.

locations.name: National and University Library & Vegova Street (Green Promenade)
Church of St. Francis of Assisi (Šiška)
Žale Cemetery – Garden of All Saints

name: Proto-urban Site of Sarazm
overview: Sarazm means 'where the land begins', is an archaeological site bearing witness to the development of human settlements in Central Asia, from the 4th millennium BCE to the end of the 3rd millennium BCE.
history_significance: One of the oldest settlements in Central Asia, demonstrating early urban development, agriculture, and extensive trade networks.
highlights: The excavated settlement ruins and artifacts showing trade links.

name: Tajik National Park (Mountains of the Pamirs)
overview: Covering more than 2.5 million hectares in the east of the country, the park is at the center of the 'Pamir Knot', a meeting point of the highest mountain ranges on the Eurasian continent.
history_significance: Contains some of the longest glaciers outside the Polar regions (Fedchenko Glacier) and is a habitat for the Marco Polo Argali sheep and Snow Leopard.
highlights: Fedchenko Glacier, Lake Sarez, and the high-altitude desert landscapes.

name: Tugay forests of the Tigrovaya Balka Nature Reserve
overview: This reserve is located in the Vakhsh River valley and contains extensive tugay forests (riparian forests) unique to Central Asia.
history_significance: One of the last remaining large tugay forest ecosystems in the world, vital for regional biodiversity and migratory birds.
highlights: The tugay forests along the Vakhsh River.

name: Cultural Heritage Sites of Ancient Khuttal
overview: This serial property encompasses 11 archaeological and architectural sites representing the medieval kingdom of Khuttal, which flourished between the 7th and 16th centuries. Located between the Vakhsh and Panj rivers, it was a vital hub on the Silk Roads.
history_significance: Illustrates the region's role in the exchange of commercial goods (gold, silver, horses) and cultural values, blending Buddhist and Islamic traditions. Its capital, Hulbuk, featured advanced urban infrastructure and artistic achievements.
highlights: Hulbuk Palace (citadel), Ajina-Teppa Buddhist Monastery (reclining Buddha), and the Zoli Zard Fortress.

name: State Historical and Cultural Park "Ancient Merv"
overview: Merv is the oldest and best-preserved of the oasis-cities along the Silk Route in Central Asia. It consists of several walled cities built adjacent to each other over 2,500 years.
history_significance: Once one of the largest cities in the world (12th century) and a major center of Islamic learning before being destroyed by the Mongols.
highlights: Mausoleum of Sultan Sanjar, Greater Kyz Kala, and the Erk Kala citadel.

name: Kunya-Urgench
overview: Kunya-Urgench is situated in north-western Turkmenistan, on the left bank of the Amu Daria River. Urgench was the capital of the Khorezm region, part of the Achaemenid Empire.
history_significance: Contains monuments mainly from the 11th to 16th centuries, including the tallest minaret in Central Asia at the time of its construction.
highlights: Kutlug-Timur Minaret, Mausoleum of Turabek-Khanum, and Mausoleum of Sultan Tekesh.

name: Parthian Fortresses of Nisa
