overview: The Parthian Fortresses of Nisa consist of two tells of Old and New Nisa, indicating the site of one of the earliest and most important cities of the Parthian Empire.
history_significance: Illustrates the interaction of Hellenistic and Central Asian cultural elements and served as a royal residence and barrier against Roman expansion.
highlights: Old Nisa (Mithradatkert) and the Round Hall.

name: Old Walled City of Shibam
overview: Surrounded by a fortified wall, the 16th-century city of Shibam is one of the oldest and best examples of urban planning based on the principle of vertical construction.
history_significance: Known as the 'Manhattan of the Desert', its impressive tower-like structures rise out of the cliff and are built entirely of mud brick.
highlights: The mud-brick skyscrapers (up to 7 stories high) and the city walls.

name: Old City of Sana'a
overview: Situated in a mountain valley at an altitude of 2,200 m, Sana'a has been inhabited for more than 2,500 years. In the 7th and 8th centuries the city became a major centre for the propagation of Islam.
history_significance: Famous for its unique multi-story rammed earth and burnt brick tower houses decorated with geometric patterns.
highlights: The Great Mosque of Sana'a, the gingerbread-style houses, and the Souq.

name: Historic Town of Zabid
overview: Zabid's domestic and military architecture and its urban plan make it an outstanding archaeological and historical site. It was the capital of Yemen from the 13th to the 15th century.
history_significance: Played a crucial role in the spread of Islam due to its Islamic university (Madrasa of Zabid).
highlights: The Great Mosque of Zabid and the labyrinthine streets.

name: Socotra Archipelago
overview: Socotra Archipelago, in the northwest Indian Ocean, is known for its high biodiversity and distinct flora and fauna. 37% of Socotra's plant species are endemic.
history_significance: Often described as the most alien-looking place on Earth due to its unique Dragon's Blood Trees and other endemic life forms.
highlights: Dragon's Blood Trees (Diksam Plateau), Detwah Lagoon, and Homhil Protected Area.

name: Landmarks of the Ancient Kingdom of Saba, Marib
overview: This serial property comprises seven archaeological sites that bear witness to the rich Kingdom of Saba and its architectural, aesthetic and technological achievements from the 1st millennium BCE to the arrival of Islam.
history_significance: The center of the Sabaean kingdom, famous for the Marib Dam which supported a complex hydrological system.
highlights: The Great Marib Dam (Old), Temple of Awwam (Mahram Bilqis), and Temple of Barran.

name: Mana Pools National Park, Sapi and Chewore Safari Areas
overview: On the banks of the Zambezi, great cliffs overhang the river and the floodplains. The area is home to a remarkable concentration of wild animals, including elephants, buffaloes, leopards, and cheetahs.
history_significance: A pristine wilderness area known for its large concentration of Nile crocodiles and significant dry-season mammal populations. It is a serial World Heritage site consisting of Mana Pools National Park and the Sapi and Chewore Safari Areas.
highlights: Walking safaris, canoe trails on the Zambezi, and Chitake Spring.

name: Great Zimbabwe National Monument
overview: The ruins of Great Zimbabwe – the capital of the Queen of Sheba, according to an age-old legend – are a unique testimony to the Bantu civilization of the Shona between the 11th and 15th centuries.
history_significance: The largest ancient stone construction in sub-Saharan Africa, built without mortar.
highlights: The Great Enclosure, the Hill Complex, and the Conical Tower.

name: Khami Ruins National Monument
overview: Khami, which developed after the capital of Great Zimbabwe had been abandoned in the mid-16th century, is of great archaeological interest. The discovery of objects from Europe and China shows that Khami was a major center for trade.
history_significance: The capital of the Torwa dynasty, known for its intricate dry-stone walls and terraced platforms.
highlights: The Hill Complex and the Cross Ruin.

name: Matobo Hills
overview: The area exhibits a profusion of distinctive rock landforms rising above the granite shield that covers much of Zimbabwe. It features one of the highest concentrations of rock art in Southern Africa.
history_significance: A sacred place for the Shona community (Mwari religion) and the burial place of Cecil John Rhodes.
highlights: World's View (Rhodes' Grave), Nswatugi Cave rock art, and the balancing rocks.

name: Mosi-oa-Tunya / Victoria Falls
overview: These falls are among the most spectacular waterfalls in the world. The Zambezi River, which is more than 2 km wide at this point, plunges noisily down a series of basalt gorges and raises an iridescent mist that can be seen more than 20 km away.
history_significance: The largest curtain of falling water in the world, creating a rainforest ecosystem from its spray. It is a shared heritage site protecting the unique geological formation and diverse wildlife of the Zambezi basin.
highlights: The Devil's Cataract (Zimbabwe), Main Falls (Zimbabwe), Knife Edge Bridge (Zambia), and the Devil's Pool (Zambia).

name: Butrint
overview: Inhabited since prehistoric times, Butrint has been the site of a Greek colony, a Roman city and a bishopric. Following a period of prosperity under Byzantine administration, then a brief occupation by the Venetians, the city was abandoned in the late Middle Ages after marshes formed in the area.
history_significance: A microcosm of Mediterranean history, containing ruins that represent each period of the city's development from the Hellenistic to the Ottoman periods.
highlights: The Greek Theatre, the Baptistery with its mosaics, the Venetian Tower, and the Lion Gate.

name: Historic Centres of Berat and Gjirokastra
overview: These two fortified historic centers bear witness to the wealth and diversity of the urban and architectural heritage of the Ottoman period in the Balkans.
history_significance: Gjirokastra features two-story stone houses (kullë) built in the 17th century. Berat, known as the 'City of a Thousand Windows', features a castle and vernacular architecture.
highlights: Gjirokastra Castle, Berat Castle (Kala), the Ethnographic Museum, and the Onufri Museum.

name: Natural and Cultural Heritage of the Ohrid region
overview: This transboundary property encompasses Lake Ohrid and its surroundings. It combines the superlative natural phenomenon of the ancient lake with the rich cultural heritage of the region, including early Christian architecture and Byzantine art.
history_significance: Lake Ohrid is one of the oldest lakes in Europe, dating back to the pre-glacial era, and hosts a unique aquatic ecosystem with over 200 endemic species. Culturally, the region boasts one of the oldest human settlements in Europe and a significant collection of Byzantine icons.
highlights: The Church of St. John at Kaneo, Samuel's Fortress, and Saint Naum Monastery in North Macedonia; the Lin Peninsula Basilica and Drilon Springs in Albania.

locations.name: Church of St. John at Kaneo (MKD)

name: Ruins of Loropéni
overview: The 11,130 m2 property, the first to be inscribed in the country, with its imposing stone walls is the best preserved of the ten fortresses in the Lobi area and is part of a larger group of 100 stone enclosures.
history_significance: Testifies to the power of the trans-Saharan gold trade. It was occupied by the Lohron or Koulango peoples and flourished between the 14th and 17th centuries.
highlights: The imposing laterite stone walls (up to 6m high) and the ancient settlement layout.

name: Ancient Ferrous Metallurgy Sites of Burkina Faso
overview: This property is composed of five elements located in different provinces. It includes about fifteen standing, natural-draught furnaces, several other furnace structures, mines, and traces of dwellings.
history_significance: Douroula (8th century BCE) is the oldest evidence of iron production in Burkina Faso. The sites illustrate the evolution of iron smelting technologies over millennia.
highlights: The ancient furnaces of Douroula, Tiwêga, and the smithing traditions.

name: Royal Court of Tiébélé
overview: The Royal Court is an earthen architectural complex established in the 16th century. It consists of buildings arranged in distinct concessions, separated by walls and passageways.
history_significance: A testimony to the social organization and cultural values of the Kasena people. It is famous for its traditional Gurunsi architecture decorated with geometric patterns painted by women.
highlights: The decorated earthen houses (sukhala), the Sacred Hill, and the Royal Cemetery.

name: W-Arly-Pendjari Complex
overview: This vast transboundary property covers a major expanse of intact Sudano-Sahelian savannah. It includes the shared W Regional Park (named after the W-shaped meander of the Niger River) and the contiguous Arly and Pendjari National Parks.
history_significance: It represents the largest continuum of terrestrial, semi-aquatic, and aquatic ecosystems in the West African savannah belt. The site is a critical refuge for fauna, hosting the largest population of West African elephants and the region's most significant population of lions.
highlights: The W-shaped meander of the Niger River, the cliffs and ponds of Pendjari, and the rich wildlife including elephants, cheetahs, and leopards.

name: Mir Castle Complex
overview: The Mir Castle Complex is an outstanding example of 16th-century fortification art. It blends Gothic, Baroque, and Renaissance cultural elements, reflecting the long history of political and cultural clashes and coalescence in the region.
history_significance: Built by the Ilyinich family and later owned by the Radziwills, it is a symbol of the region's turbulent history.
highlights: The five towers, the inner courtyard, and the English landscape park.

name: Architectural, Residential and Cultural Complex of the Radziwill Family at Nesvizh
overview: The Architectural, Residential and Cultural Complex of the Radziwill Family at Nesvizh is located in central Belarus. The dynasty exerted great influence in the sciences, arts, crafts and architecture.
history_significance: The Corpus Christi Church (1587-1593) is the first Baroque dome in Eastern Europe.
highlights: The Residential Castle, Corpus Christi Church, and the landscaped gardens.

name: Cocos Island National Park
overview: Located 550 km off the Pacific coast of Costa Rica, Cocos Island is the only island in the tropical eastern Pacific with a tropical rainforest. Its position as the first point of contact with the northern equatorial counter-current makes it an ideal laboratory for the study of biological processes.
history_significance: Known as a top diving destination for viewing large pelagic species such as sharks, rays, tuna, and dolphins.
highlights: Chatham Bay, Wafer Bay, and the abundance of hammerhead sharks.

name: Area de Conservación Guanacaste
overview: This area contains important natural habitats for the conservation of biological diversity, including the best dry forest habitats in Central America and key habitats for endangered species.
history_significance: A pioneering site for ecological restoration and the study of tropical dry forests.
highlights: Santa Rosa National Park, Rincón de la Vieja Volcano, and Junquillal Bay.

locations.name: Rincón de la Vieja National Park

name: Precolumbian Chiefdom Settlements with Stone Spheres of the Diquís
overview: The site includes four archaeological locations in the Diquís Delta. It is considered a unique example of the complex social, economic, and political systems of the Diquís period (AD 500–1500).
history_significance: Famous for the perfectly spherical stone balls (petrospheres) whose production technique and meaning remain largely a mystery.
highlights: Finca 6 Archaeological Site, Batambal, and El Silencio.

name: Historical Monuments of Mtskheta
overview: The historic churches of Mtskheta, former capital of the Kingdom of Georgia, are outstanding examples of medieval religious architecture in the Caucasus. They show the high artistic and cultural level attained by this ancient kingdom.
history_significance: Mtskheta was the site where Christianity was proclaimed as the official religion of Georgia in 337 AD.
highlights: Svetitskhoveli Cathedral, Jvari Monastery, and Samtavro Monastery.

name: Gelati Monastery
overview: Founded in 1106 by King David IV of Georgia, Gelati Monastery is a masterpiece of the Golden Age of medieval Georgia, a period of political strength and economic growth between the 11th and 13th centuries.
history_significance: It was one of the main cultural and intellectual centers in ancient Georgia, housing an academy.
highlights: The main Church of the Virgin, the Academy building, and the mosaic of the Virgin and Child.

name: Upper Svaneti
overview: Preserved by its long isolation, the Upper Svaneti region of the Caucasus is an exceptional example of mountain scenery with medieval-type villages and tower-houses.
history_significance: The village of Chazhashi still has more than 200 of these very unusual medieval houses, which were used both as dwellings and as defence posts against invaders.
highlights: Chazhashi village (Ushguli community) and the Svan towers.

name: Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands
overview: This serial property comprises seven component parts. It contains ancient rainforests and wetlands that are home to a high diversity of flora and fauna, including threatened species.
history_significance: The first natural World Heritage site in Georgia, protecting unique Colchic ecosystems that survived the glacial cycles.
highlights: Mtirala National Park, Kolkheti National Park, and the percolation bogs.

name: Tikal National Park
overview: Tikal is one of the major sites of Mayan civilization. In the heart of the jungle, surrounded by lush vegetation, lie five major temples built in the 7th and 8th centuries AD.
history_significance: It was one of the most powerful kingdoms of the ancient Maya, dominating much of the Maya region.
highlights: Temple I (Temple of the Great Jaguar), Temple IV (the tallest pyramid), and the Great Plaza.

name: Antigua Guatemala
overview: Built 1,500 m above sea-level, in an earthquake-prone region, Antigua Guatemala was the capital of the Captaincy-General of Guatemala. It preserves its colonial grid and baroque architecture.
history_significance: Founded in the early 16th century, it contains fine examples of Spanish Baroque architecture adapted to seismic conditions.
highlights: Santa Catalina Arch, La Merced Church, and the Plaza Mayor.

name: Archaeological Park and Ruins of Quiriguá
overview: Quiriguá contains some of the finest 8th-century monuments known. The site is famous for its tall stone stelae and zoomorphic altars carved from sandstone without the use of metal tools.
history_significance: Home to the tallest stone stelae ever erected in the New World (Stela E, 10.6 meters high).
highlights: Stela E, Zoomorph P (The Great Turtle), and the Acropolis.

name: National Archaeological Park Tak'alik Ab'aj
overview: Tak'alik Ab'aj is a unique archaeological site that links the Olmec and Maya civilizations. It was a cosmopolitan center of trade and culture for over 1,700 years.
history_significance: Witnessed the transition from Olmec to Maya culture and contains some of the earliest Mayan inscriptions.
highlights: The Observatory (Structure 7), Stela 5, and the Olmec-style sculptures.

name: Old Towns of Djenné
overview: Inhabited since 250 B.C., Djenné became a market centre and an important link in the trans-Saharan gold trade. It is famous for its vernacular architecture.
history_significance: Home to the Great Mosque of Djenné, the largest mud-brick building in the world.
highlights: Great Mosque of Djenné, the Toguna, and the ancient tell sites (Djenné-Djeno).

name: Timbuktu
overview: Home of the prestigious Koranic Sankore University and other madrasas, Timbuktu was an intellectual and spiritual capital and a centre for the propagation of Islam throughout Africa in the 15th and 16th centuries.
history_significance: Features the three great mosques (Djingareyber, Sankore, and Sidi Yahia) and essential ancient manuscripts.
highlights: Djingareyber Mosque, Sankore Mosque, and the Ahmed Baba Institute.

name: Cliff of Bandiagara (Land of the Dogons)
overview: The Bandiagara site is an outstanding landscape of cliffs and sandy plateaux with some beautiful architecture (houses, granaries, altars, sanctuaries and Togu Na, or communal meeting-places).
history_significance: Home to the Dogon people, known for their complex mythology, masked dances, and unique village structures built into the cliffs.
highlights: The Bandiagara Escarpment, Songho village (circumcision cave), and Sangha.

name: Tomb of Askia
overview: The 17-meter pyramidal structure of the Tomb of Askia was built by Askia Mohamed, the Emperor of Songhai, in 1495. It bears testimony to the power and riches of the empire that flourished in the 15th and 16th centuries.
history_significance: A fine example of the monumental mud-building tradition of the West African Sahel.
highlights: The pyramidal tomb, the two flat-roofed mosque buildings, and the cemetery.

name: Natural and Culturo-Historical Region of Kotor
overview: Located in a deep inlet of the Adriatic coast, this region includes the medieval towns of Kotor, Risan, and Perast. It represents a unique fusion of Middle Ages architecture and the natural beauty of the Gulf of Kotor.
history_significance: An important artistic and commercial centre in the Middle Ages with its own famous schools of masonry and iconography.
highlights: The Cathedral of Saint Tryphon, the City Walls, and the islets of Our Lady of the Rocks and St. George.

name: Durmitor National Park
overview: This breathtaking national park was formed by glaciers and is traversed by rivers and underground streams. It features the Tara River Canyon, the deepest gorge in Europe.
history_significance: A pristine natural environment containing dense pine forests, clear lakes, and significant endemic flora.
highlights: Tara River Canyon, Black Lake (Crno Jezero), and Bobotov Kuk peak.

name: Sulaiman-Too Sacred Mountain
overview: Sulaiman-Too represents the most complete example of a sacred mountain in Central Asia, worshipped for several millennia. Located in the Fergana Valley, it features a network of caves with petroglyphs and sites of worship.
history_significance: For centuries it was a beacon for travellers on the Silk Road and a revered pilgrimage site, blending pre-Islamic and Islamic beliefs.
highlights: The mountain silhouette, numerous ancient caves, the 16th-century mosque, and the museum carved into the mountain.

name: Kathmandu Valley
overview: The Kathmandu Valley illustrates the historic, religious, and political achievements of the people of Nepal. It comprises seven groups of monuments and buildings that display the full range of historic and artistic achievements.
history_significance: A crossroads of Asian civilizations, fostering the coexistence of Hinduism and Buddhism with a unique architectural style.
highlights: The three Durbar Squares, the Buddhist Stupas of Swayambhu and Bauddhanath, and the Hindu temples of Pashupati and Changu Narayan.

name: Sagarmatha National Park
overview: Sagarmatha is an exceptional area with dramatic mountains, glaciers, and deep valleys, dominated by Mount Everest, the highest peak in the world.
history_significance: Home to the Sherpa people and their unique culture, as well as rare species like the snow leopard and the lesser panda.
highlights: Mount Everest, Tengboche Monastery, and the Ngozumpa Glacier.

name: Chitwan National Park
overview: Situated at the foot of the Himalayas, Chitwan is one of the few remaining undisturbed vestiges of the Terai region.
history_significance: A refuge for the one-horned rhinoceros and the Royal Bengal tiger, preserving the last examples of the Terai ecosystem.
highlights: Elephant safaris, canoe rides on the Rapti River, and rhinoceros spotting.

name: Lumbini, the Birthplace of the Lord Buddha
overview: Siddhartha Gautama, the Lord Buddha, was born in 623 BC in the famous gardens of Lumbini, which soon became a place of pilgrimage.
history_significance: One of the holiest Buddhist pilgrimage places, containing archaeological remains related to the birth of Buddha.
highlights: The Maya Devi Temple, the Ashoka Pillar, and the Sacred Pond (Puskarni).

name: Dja Faunal Reserve
overview: One of the largest and best-protected rainforests in Africa, with 90% of its area left undisturbed. The Dja River forms a natural boundary, protecting its rich biodiversity.
history_significance: It is home to a vast array of primates and represents an exceptional example of the biological evolution in the tropical rainforests of the Congo Basin.
highlights: Western lowland gorillas, chimpanzees, forest elephants, and the winding Dja River.

name: Diy-Gid-Biy Cultural Landscape of the Mandara Mountains
overview: Located in the Mandara Mountains of northern Cameroon, the Diy-Gid-Biy Cultural Landscape represents a unique agro-pastoral and ritual landscape shaped by centuries of human adaptation to a rugged mountain environment.
history_significance: The landscape is closely linked to the cultural traditions of the Mafa people and other Mandara communities. It features terraced fields, fortified settlements, ritual sites, and stone architecture that demonstrate a long-standing socio-ecological system.
highlights: Terraced agriculture, dry-stone fortified compounds, sacred ritual sites, and the Mandara Mountains' dramatic topography.

name: Air and Ténéré Natural Reserves
overview: This is the largest protected area in Africa, covering some 7.7 million hectares. It encompasses the volcanic rock mass of the Aïr Mountains and the surrounding Saharan desert of Ténéré.
history_significance: The reserves boast outstanding landscapes and plant and animal life, including populations of addax and gazelles, in a region of extreme climate.
highlights: The marble Blue Mountains, shifting sand dunes of Ténéré, and ancient rock engravings.

name: Historic Centre of Agadez
overview: Known as the gateway to the desert, Agadez dates back to the 15th century. It is a major caravan trade hub featuring distinct mud-brick architecture.
history_significance: It was the capital of the Sultanate of Aïr and a center of Islamic learning, preserving its layout and architectural traditions for centuries.
highlights: The 27-meter high Grand Mosque minaret (tallest mud-brick structure), the Sultan's Palace, and the old irregular street pattern.

name: Central Suriname Nature Reserve
overview: The reserve comprises 1.6 million ha of primary tropical forest of west-central Suriname. It protects the upper watershed of the Coppename River and contains a high diversity of plant and animal life.
history_significance: A pristine ecosystem containing geological features like the Julianatop and the Voltzberg dome.
highlights: Raleighvallen waterfalls, Voltzberg, and the pristine rainforest.

name: Historic Inner City of Paramaribo
overview: Paramaribo is a former Dutch colonial town from the 17th and 18th centuries planted on the northern coast of South America. Its plan is based on a grid system.
history_significance: A unique example of the fusion of European architecture and construction techniques with indigenous South American materials.
highlights: Fort Zeelandia, the Presidential Palace, and the wooden St. Peter and Paul Cathedral.

name: Jodensavanne Archaeological Site: Jodensavanne Settlement and Cassipora Creek Cemetery
overview: Located on the banks of the Suriname River, Jodensavanne was an early Jewish settlement in the Americas. It includes the ruins of the Beracha VeShalom Synagogue and cemeteries.
history_significance: Illustrates the early Jewish colonization in the Atlantic World and their interaction with enslaved people and indigenous populations.
highlights: Ruins of the Synagogue and the Cassipora Creek cemetery.

name: Historic Quarter of the City of Colonia del Sacramento
overview: Founded by the Portuguese in 1680 on the Río de la Plata, the city was disputed by the Spanish and Portuguese for a century. It has a well-preserved urban landscape.
history_significance: A unique fusion of Portuguese and Spanish colonial styles.
highlights: The City Gate (Portón de Campo), the Lighthouse, and the Street of Sighs (Calle de los Suspiros).

name: Fray Bentos Industrial Landscape
overview: Located on the Uruguay River, this massive industrial complex was built for processing meat. It includes the factory, docks, and workers' housing.
history_significance: Known as the 'Kitchen of the World', it exported Corned Beef and meat extract globally from 1859 to 1979.
highlights: The Anglo Meat Packing Plant museum and the Manager's House.

name: The Work of Engineer Eladio Dieste: Church of Atlántida
overview: The Church of Atlántida, with its belfry and underground baptistery, is the first realization of the reinforced ceramic brick system developed by Eladio Dieste.
history_significance: A masterpiece of modern architecture using Gaussian vaults and reinforced brick (cerámica armada).
highlights: The undulating brick walls and the wavy roof structure.

name: Coro and its Port
overview: Founded in 1527, Coro is one of the oldest colonial towns in the Americas. It preserves a unique example of the fusion of local and European architectural styles.
history_significance: The only surviving example of a rich fusion of local traditions with Spanish Mudejar and Dutch architectural techniques.
highlights: The cobblestone streets, colonial earthen architecture, and the nearby Medanos de Coro (dunes).

name: Canaima National Park
overview: Spread over 3 million ha in south-eastern Venezuela, the park is dominated by table-top mountains (tepuis). It includes Angel Falls, the world's highest waterfall.
history_significance: Geologically unique, the tepuis are millions of years old and host endemic flora and fauna.
highlights: Angel Falls (Salto Ángel), Mount Roraima, and the Auyán-tepui.

name: Ciudad Universitaria de Caracas
overview: Built between 1940 and 1960 by architect Carlos Raúl Villanueva, the university campus is an outstanding example of the Modern Movement in architecture.
history_significance: A masterpiece of modern planning integrating art and architecture, featuring works by Calder, Léger, and Arp.
highlights: The Aula Magna with Alexander Calder's 'Clouds' and the covered plazas.

name: Monasteries of Haghpat and Sanahin
overview: These two Byzantine monasteries in the Tumanian region date from the period of prosperity during the Kiurikian dynasty (10th to 13th century).
history_significance: They represent the highest flowering of Armenian religious architecture, blending Byzantine ecclesiastical architecture with the vernacular architecture of the Caucasian region.
highlights: Haghpat Monastery's bell tower and Sanahin's library.

name: Cathedral and Churches of Echmiatsin and the Archaeological Site of Zvartnots
overview: The cathedral and churches of Echmiatsin and the archaeological remains at Zvartnots graphically illustrate the evolution and development of the Armenian central-domed cross-hall type of church.
history_significance: The spiritual center of the Armenian Apostolic Church and the site of the oldest state-built cathedral in the world.
highlights: Mother Cathedral of Holy Etchmiadzin, St. Gayane Church, and Zvartnots Cathedral ruins.

name: Monastery of Geghard and the Upper Azat Valley
overview: The monastery of Geghard contains a number of churches and tombs, most of them cut into the rock, which illustrate the very peak of Armenian medieval architecture.
history_significance: Founded in the 4th century by Gregory the Illuminator at the site of a sacred spring inside a cave.
highlights: The rock-cut churches (Avazan), the intricate carvings, and the acoustic hall.

name: Paphos
overview: Paphos has been inhabited since the Neolithic period. It was a centre of the cult of Aphrodite and of pre-Hellenic fertility deities. The site includes the Tombs of the Kings and famous mosaics.
history_significance: Legendary birthplace of Aphrodite and former capital of Cyprus.
highlights: The Mosaics of Nea Paphos (House of Dionysos), Tombs of the Kings, and the Temple of Aphrodite.

name: Painted Churches in the Troodos Region
overview: This region is characterized by one of the largest groups of churches and monasteries of the former Byzantine Empire. The complex includes ten rural churches richly decorated with murals.
history_significance: Provides a complete overview of Byzantine and post-Byzantine painting in Cyprus.
highlights: Agios Nikolaos tis Stegis, Panagia tou Arakos, and Panagia Asinou.

name: Choirokoitia
overview: The Neolithic settlement of Choirokoitia, occupied from the 7th to the 4th millennium B.C., is one of the most important prehistoric sites in the eastern Mediterranean.
history_significance: Remarkably well-preserved remains illustrating the evolution of human society in the region.
highlights: The cylindrical stone and mud dwellings and the reconstructed village section.

name: Þingvellir National Park
overview: Þingvellir (Thingvellir) is the site where the Althing, an open-air assembly representing the whole of Iceland, was established in 930 and continued to meet until 1798.
history_significance: A rift valley marking the boundary between the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates, and a symbol of Icelandic national identity.
highlights: Almannagjá fissure, Öxarárfoss waterfall, and the Law Rock (Lögberg).

name: Surtsey
overview: A volcanic island formed by eruptions from 1963 to 1967. It is protected from human interference to allow the study of ecological succession.
history_significance: A unique natural laboratory illustrating the colonization of land by plants and animals.
highlights: The pristine volcanic landscape and bird colonies.

name: Vatnajökull National Park - Dynamic Nature of Fire and Ice
overview: This iconic volcanic region covers 14% of Iceland. It comprises the Vatnajökull ice cap and extensive lava fields, interacting with volcanoes under the ice.
history_significance: Shows the interaction between rifting, volcanism, and glaciation (jökulhlaups).
highlights: Jökulsárlón glacier lagoon, Askja caldera, and Dettifoss waterfall.

name: City of Valletta
overview: The capital of Malta is inextricably linked to the history of the military and charitable Order of St John of Jerusalem. It contains 320 monuments in a concentrated area.
history_significance: One of the most concentrated historic areas in the world, a masterpiece of Baroque urban planning.
highlights: St John's Co-Cathedral, Grandmaster's Palace, and the Upper Barrakka Gardens.

name: Megalithic Temples of Malta
overview: Seven megalithic temples are included in this property. The two temples of Ggantija on the island of Gozo are notable for their gigantic Bronze Age structures.
history_significance: These temples are some of the oldest free-standing stone structures in the world (3600-2500 BC).
highlights: Ġgantija (Gozo), Ħaġar Qim, and Mnajdra.

name: Hal Saflieni Hypogeum
overview: A rock-cut underground complex used as a sanctuary and for burial. It was discovered in 1902 and dates from 4000 B.C. to 2500 B.C.
history_significance: The only known prehistoric underground temple in the world.
highlights: The Oracle Room, the Red Ochre paintings, and the 'Sleeping Lady' figurine (replica).

name: Historic Mosque City of Bagerhat
overview: Situated in the suburbs of Bagerhat, near the meeting point of the Ganges and Brahmaputra rivers, this ancient city was founded by the Turkish general Ulugh Khan Jahan in the 15th century.
history_significance: Features an exceptional concentration of mosques and early Islamic monuments in brick.
highlights: The Sixty Dome Mosque (Shait Gumbad Mosque) and the Tomb of Khan Jahan.

name: Ruins of the Buddhist Vihara at Paharpur
overview: Somapura Mahavihara is one of the most famous Buddhist viharas (monasteries) in the Indian Subcontinent. It dates from the late 8th century AD.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Pala Dynasty architecture and a major intellectual centre of Buddhism.
highlights: The central shrine (stupa) and the terracotta plaques.

name: The Sundarbans
overview: The Sundarbans mangrove forest, one of the largest such forests in the world (140,000 ha), lies on the delta of the Ganges, Brahmaputra and Meghna rivers on the Bay of Bengal.
history_significance: A complex network of tidal waterways and home to the Royal Bengal Tiger.
highlights: Mangrove forests, spotted deer, and river cruises.

name: Town of Luang Prabang
overview: Luang Prabang is an outstanding example of the fusion of traditional architecture and Lao urban structures with those built by the European colonial authorities in the 19th and 20th centuries.
history_significance: The former royal capital and a center of Buddhism in Laos.
highlights: Wat Xieng Thong, Mount Phousi, and the Royal Palace Museum.

name: Vat Phou and Associated Ancient Settlements within the Champasak Cultural Landscape
overview: The Champasak cultural landscape, including the Vat Phou Temple complex, is a remarkably well-preserved planned landscape more than 1,000 years old.
history_significance: A major Khmer religious complex dedicated to Shiva, pre-dating Angkor Wat.
highlights: The main Vat Phou temple, the ancient road to Angkor, and the Hong Nang Sida.

name: Megalithic Jar Sites in Xiengkhuang – Plain of Jars
overview: More than 2,100 tubular-shaped megalithic stone jars used for funerary practices in the Iron Age are found across the Plain of Jars.
history_significance: Testimony to an Iron Age civilization (c. 500 BCE to 500 CE) with unique burial customs.
highlights: Site 1 (Thong Hai Hin), Site 2 (Hai Hin Phu Salato), and the large 'King's Jar'.

name: Te Wahipounamu – South West New Zealand
overview: A vast landscape in the south-west of New Zealand shaped by successive glaciations into fjords, rocky coasts, towering cliffs, lakes and waterfalls.
history_significance: The best modern representation of the ancient flora and fauna of Gondwana.
highlights: Milford Sound, Franz Josef Glacier, and Mount Cook.

locations.name: Franz Josef Glacier (Westland Tai Poutini NP)

name: Tongariro National Park
overview: Tongariro was the first national park in New Zealand and the fourth in the world. It features three active volcanoes: Tongariro, Ngauruhoe, and Ruapehu.
history_significance: A cultural landscape sacred to the Maori people and a spectacular volcanic area.
highlights: The Tongariro Alpine Crossing, Emerald Lakes, and Mount Ngauruhoe (Mount Doom).

name: New Zealand Sub-Antarctic Islands
overview: The site consists of five island groups (Snares, Bounty, Antipodes, Auckland, and Campbell) in the Southern Ocean.
history_significance: Critical breeding grounds for seabirds and penguins, with high biodiversity and endemism.
highlights: Enderby Island (Auckland Islands) and the Campbell Island flora.

name: Complex of Koguryo Tombs
overview: The property includes several groups and individual tombs – totalling about 30 – from the later period of the Koguryo Kingdom.
history_significance: Famous for their vibrant wall paintings depicting daily life, mythology, and the four guardian deities.
highlights: Tomb of King Dongmyeong and the Anak Tomb No. 3.

locations.name: Tomb of the Two Columns (Ho'ryŏng Tombs)

name: Mount Kumgang and the Historical Relics in and around the Mountain
overview: Known as the Diamond Mountain, Mount Kumgang is renowned for its scenic beauty, featuring twelve thousand granite peaks, deep ravines, waterfalls, and lagoons.
history_significance: A major center of Buddhism in Korea for centuries and a favorite subject of Korean landscape painting. It houses important historical relics including Pyohon Temple.
highlights: Kuryong Falls, Manmulsang (Rocks of Ten Thousand Shapes), and Lake Samilpo.

name: Historic Monuments and Sites in Kaesong
overview: Kaesong was the capital of the Koryo Dynasty (918–1392). The site consists of 12 separate components, including the Manwoldae Palace and the Namdaemun Gate.
history_significance: Testifies to the transition from Buddhism to Neo-Confucianism in Korea.
highlights: Manwoldae Palace ruins, Koryo Songgyungwan (Confucian Academy), and the Tomb of King Wang Kon.

name: Qal'at al-Bahrain – Ancient Harbour and Capital of Dilmun
overview: Qal'at al-Bahrain is an artificial mound created by many successive layers of human occupation. It was the capital of the Dilmun civilization.
history_significance: A strategic trading hub connecting Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley. Topped by a 16th-century Portuguese fort.
highlights: The Portuguese Fort and the excavated residential and commercial areas.

name: Pearling, Testimony of an Island Economy
overview: The site consists of 17 buildings in Muharraq City, three offshore oyster beds, a segment of the coast, and the Qal'at Bu Mahir fortress.
history_significance: Testifies to the pearling economy that sustained the region for millennia until the 1930s.
highlights: Pearl Merchant Houses, Bu Mahir Fort, and the Pearling Path.

locations.name: Bu Maher Coastal Strip and Sealine

name: Dilmun Burial Mounds
overview: The site comprises 21 archaeological sites with over 11,000 burial mounds dating from 2050 to 1750 BCE.
history_significance: Provides evidence of the early Dilmun civilization's burial practices and social structure.
highlights: Royal Mounds of A'ali.

name: Gebel Barkal and the Sites of the Napatan Region
overview: These five archaeological sites reveal the Napatan (900-270 BC) and Meroitic (270 BC-350 AD) cultures of the second kingdom of Kush.
history_significance: Gebel Barkal was a sacred mountain. The sites include pyramids, tombs, and temples dedicated to Amun.
highlights: The Temple of Amun at Gebel Barkal and the Pyramids of Nuri.

name: Archaeological Sites of the Island of Meroe
overview: Meroe was the capital of the Kingdom of Kush from the 3rd century BC to the 4th century AD. The site features extensive fields of steep pyramids.
history_significance: The heart of the Kushite kingdom, known for its unique steep pyramids and iron production.
highlights: The Pyramids of Meroe (North Cemetery) and the Royal Baths.

name: Sanganeb Marine National Park and Dungonab Bay – Mukkawar Island Marine National Park
overview: The property consists of two separate areas: Sanganeb is an isolated, coral reef structure and Dungonab Bay/Mukkawar Island is a diverse coastal system.
history_significance: Hosts major populations of dugongs, seabirds, and marine invertebrates.
highlights: Sanganeb Lighthouse and reef diving.

name: Tsingy de Bemaraha Strict Nature Reserve
overview: The reserve comprises karstic landscapes and limestone uplands cut into impressive 'tsingy' peaks and a forest of limestone needles.
history_significance: A labyrinth of limestone needles hosting unique endemic species like lemurs and birds.
highlights: The Grand Tsingy, the suspension bridge, and the Manambolo River gorge.

name: Royal Hill of Ambohimanga
overview: The Royal Hill of Ambohimanga consists of a royal city and burial site, and an ensemble of sacred places. It is the cradle of the Merina dynasty.
history_significance: A symbol of Malagasy cultural identity and a sacred pilgrimage site.
highlights: The Royal Enclosure (Rova), the sacred spring, and the giant sacrificial stone.

name: Rainforests of the Atsinanana
overview: The Rainforests of the Atsinanana comprise six national parks distributed along the eastern part of the island. These relict forests are critical for maintaining ongoing ecological processes.
history_significance: Vital for the survival of Madagascar's unique biodiversity, especially its primates and lemurs.
highlights: Marojejy National Park and Masoala National Park.

name: Mount Mulanje Cultural Landscape
overview: Mount Mulanje is a huge granite massif in southern Malawi, rising sharply from the surrounding plains. It is an 'island in the sky' supporting unique high-altitude ecosystems.
history_significance: It is home to the endemic and endangered Mulanje Cedar tree. The mountain is culturally significant to the local people, believed to be the dwelling place of ancestral spirits.
highlights: Sapitwa Peak (highest in south-central Africa), Mulanje Cedar forests, and the surrounding tea plantations.

name: Lake Malawi National Park
overview: Located at the southern end of the great expanse of Lake Malawi, with its deep, clear waters and mountain backdrop, the national park is home to many hundreds of fish species, nearly all endemic.
history_significance: Provides an outstanding example of biological evolution, particularly the adaptive radiation of cichlid fish.
highlights: The Cichlid fish diversity and Otter Point.

name: Chongoni Rock-Art Area
overview: Situated within a cluster of granite hills high in the plateau of central Malawi, the 127 sites of this area feature the richest concentration of rock art in Central Africa.
history_significance: Reflects the scarce tradition of farmer rock art, as well as paintings by BaTwa hunter-gatherers and Chewa agriculturalists.
highlights: Chentcherere rock shelters and the stylized paintings.

name: Bwindi Impenetrable National Park
overview: Located in south-western Uganda, at the junction of the plain and mountain forests, Bwindi Park is known for its exceptional biodiversity.
history_significance: Home to almost half of the world's population of endangered Mountain Gorillas.
highlights: Mountain Gorilla trekking and the dense rainforest.

name: Rwenzori Mountains National Park
overview: The Rwenzori Mountains National Park covers the main part of the Rwenzori mountain chain, which includes Africa's third highest peak (Mount Margherita: 5,109 m).
history_significance: Known as the 'Mountains of the Moon', featuring glaciers, waterfalls, and lakes in an alpine setting.
highlights: Margherita Peak and the unique giant lobelias.

name: Tombs of Buganda Kings at Kasubi
overview: The Tombs of Buganda Kings at Kasubi constitute a site embracing almost 30 ha of hillside within Kampala district.
history_significance: A major spiritual centre for the Baganda people, containing the royal palace (Muzibu-Azaala-Mpanga) which houses the tombs of four Kabakas.
highlights: The Muzibu-Azaala-Mpanga (main thatched building).

name: Minaret and Archaeological Remains of Jam
overview: The 65m-tall Minaret of Jam is a graceful, soaring structure, dating back to the 12th century. Covered in elaborate brickwork with a blue tile inscription at the top, it is noteworthy for the quality of its architecture and decoration.
history_significance: A masterpiece of Islamic architecture and the only surviving monument of the Ghurid Empire's summer capital.
highlights: The intricate brickwork, Kufic inscriptions, and the surrounding river canyon.

name: Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley
overview: The cultural landscape and archaeological remains of the Bamiyan Valley represent the artistic and religious developments which from the 1st to the 13th centuries characterized ancient Bakhtria, integrating various cultural influences into the Gandhara school of Buddhist art.
history_significance: Site of the famous giant Buddha statues (destroyed in 2001) and a major center of Buddhism on the Silk Road.
highlights: The Buddha niches, the caves with mural paintings, and the Shahr-i-Gholghola fortress.

name: Tsodilo
overview: With one of the highest concentrations of rock art in the world, Tsodilo has been called the 'Louvre of the Desert'. Over 4,500 paintings are preserved in an area of only 10 km2 of the Kalahari Desert.
history_significance: A sacred place for the San people, documenting human activities and environmental changes over at least 100,000 years.
highlights: The Rhino Cave, the Male and Female Hills, and the vibrant rock paintings.

name: Okavango Delta
overview: This delta in north-west Botswana comprises permanent marshlands and seasonally flooded plains. It is one of the very few major interior delta systems that do not flow into a sea or ocean.
history_significance: An exceptional example of the interaction between climatic, hydrological and biological processes, supporting large populations of mammals.
highlights: The seasonal flooding, the wildlife (elephants, lions, hippos), and the mokoro canoe trips.

name: Lakes of Ounianga
overview: The site includes eighteen interconnected lakes in the hyper-arid Ennedi region of the Sahara desert covering an area of 62,808 ha. It constitutes an exceptional natural landscape of great beauty with striking colors and shapes.
history_significance: A unique hydrological system in a desert setting, sustaining a diverse aquatic fauna.
highlights: Lake Yoa (largest), Lake Katam, and the contrasting colors of the lakes.

name: Ennedi Massif: Natural and Cultural Landscape
overview: Located in the northeast of the country, the Ennedi Massif is sculpted by water and wind erosion into a plateau featuring spectacular canyons and valleys. It is also home to thousands of images painted and carved into the rock surface.
history_significance: Features one of the largest ensembles of rock art in the Sahara and relict biodiversity like the desert crocodile.
highlights: The Aloba Arch, Guelta d'Archei, and the extensive rock art galleries.

name: Ecosystem and Relict Cultural Landscape of Lopé-Okanda
overview: This site demonstrates an unusual interface between dense and well-conserved tropical rainforest and relict savannah environments with a great diversity of species.
history_significance: Contains evidence of successive passages of different peoples over the last 400,000 years, including rock art and Iron Age sites.
highlights: The forest-savannah mosaic, Mandrills, and the rock carvings of Elarmekora.

name: Ivindo National Park
overview: Situated on the equator in northern Gabon, the largely pristine site encompasses an area of almost 300,000 ha crossed by a network of picturesque blackwater rivers.
history_significance: Features the spectacular Kongou and Mingouli waterfalls and is a critical habitat for forest elephants and gorillas.
highlights: Kongou Falls, Langoué Bai (forest clearing), and the rich biodiversity.

name: Forts and Castles, Volta, Greater Accra, Central and Western Regions
overview: The remains of fortified trading-posts, erected between 1482 and 1786, can be seen along the coast of Ghana between Keta and Beyin. They were links in the trade routes established by the Portuguese, Dutch, English, French and Prussians.
history_significance: Testifies to the history of European-African trade, particularly the gold trade and the transatlantic slave trade.
highlights: Cape Coast Castle, St. George's Castle (Elmina), and Fort Christiansborg.

name: Asante Traditional Buildings
overview: To the north-east of Kumasi, these remain the last material remains of the great Asante civilization, which reached its peak in the 18th century. The buildings are made of earth, wood and straw.
history_significance: Rare surviving examples of traditional Asante architecture with unique bas-relief decorations.
highlights: The intricate relief decorations on the walls and the steep thatched roofs.

name: Maya Site of Copan
overview: Discovered in 1570 by Diego García de Palacio, the Maya site of Copan is one of the most important sites of the Mayan civilization. The ruined citadel and imposing public squares reveal the three main stages of development before the city was abandoned in the early 10th century.
history_significance: Famous for its intricate stelae and the Hieroglyphic Stairway, which contains the longest known Mayan text.
highlights: The Hieroglyphic Stairway, Altar Q, and the Ball Court.

name: Río Plátano Biosphere Reserve
overview: Located on the watershed of the Río Plátano, the reserve is one of the few remains of a tropical rainforest in Central America. It has an abundant and varied plant and wildlife.
history_significance: Home to over 2,000 indigenous people who have preserved their traditional way of life.
highlights: The primary rainforest, diverse wildlife (jaguars, macaws), and the indigenous communities.

name: Brú na Bóinne - Archaeological Ensemble of the Bend of the Boyne
overview: The three main prehistoric sites of the Brú na Bóinne Complex, Newgrange, Knowth and Dowth, are situated on the north bank of the River Boyne. It is Europe's largest and most important concentration of prehistoric megalithic art.
history_significance: These passage graves dating from c. 3200 BC are older than Stonehenge and the Pyramids of Giza.
highlights: Newgrange passage tomb (Winter Solstice alignment), Knowth, and the megalithic art.

name: Sceilg Mhichíl
overview: This monastic complex, perched on the steep slopes of the rocky island of Skellig Michael, illustrates the very Spartan existence of the first Irish Christians. Since the extreme remoteness of Skellig Michael has until recently discouraged visitors, the site is exceptionally well preserved.
history_significance: A unique example of an early Christian monastic settlement founded in the 7th century.
highlights: The beehive huts, the stone stairs, and the puffin colony.

name: Blue and John Crow Mountains
overview: The site encompasses a rugged and extensively forested mountainous region in the south-east of Jamaica, which provided refuge for the indigenous Tainos and later for Maroons (escaped enslaved Africans).
history_significance: A symbol of resistance against the colonial system and a biodiversity hotspot with high endemism.
highlights: Blue Mountain Peak, the Maroon heritage trails, and the endemic flora.

name: The Archaeological Ensemble of 17th Century Port Royal
overview: Once known as the 'wickedest city on earth', Port Royal was a major center of shipping and commerce in the Caribbean until a massive earthquake and tsunami in 1692 sank much of the city into the sea.
history_significance: An outstanding example of a 17th-century English colonial town preserved in situ underwater, serving as a unique time capsule of maritime history and pirate culture.
highlights: The Sunken City (underwater archaeological site), Fort Charles, and the Giddy House.

name: Banc d'Arguin National Park
overview: Fringing the Atlantic coast, the park comprises sand dunes, coastal swamps, small islands and shallow coastal waters. The austerity of the desert and the biodiversity of the marine zone result in a land- and seascape of exceptional contrasting natural value.
history_significance: A major breeding site for migratory birds and a vital nursery for fish.
highlights: The vast flocks of migratory birds and the traditional Imraguen fishermen.

name: Ancient Ksour of Ouadane, Chinguetti, Tichitt and Oualata
overview: Founded in the 11th and 12th centuries to serve the caravans crossing the Sahara, these trading and religious centers became focal points of Islamic culture.
history_significance: Outstanding examples of traditional Saharan settlements with stone architecture.
highlights: The Friday Mosque of Chinguetti, the libraries of desert manuscripts, and the decorative stone architecture.

name: Aapravasi Ghat
overview: In the district of Port Louis lies the 1,640 m2 site where the modern indentured labour diaspora began. Between 1834 and 1920, almost half a million indentured labourers arrived from India to work in the sugar plantations.
history_significance: The site stands as a major historic testimony to the 19th-century indenture system ('Great Experiment').
highlights: The Immigration Depot steps and the interpretation centre.

name: Le Morne Cultural Landscape
overview: Le Morne Brabant is a rugged mountain that juts into the Indian Ocean in the southwest of Mauritius. It was used as a shelter by runaway slaves, the maroons, through the 18th and early 19th centuries.
history_significance: A symbol of the slaves' fight for freedom, their suffering, and their sacrifice.
highlights: Le Morne Brabant peak, the 'Underwater Waterfall' illusion (nearby), and the Maroon heritage sites.

name: Pyu Ancient Cities
overview: The site includes the remains of three brick, walled and moated cities – Halin, Beikthano and Sri Ksetra – located in the vast irrigated landscapes of the dry zone of the Ayeyarwady (Irrawaddy) River basin.
history_significance: Testifies to the Pyu Kingdoms that flourished for over 1,000 years between 200 BC and AD 900.
highlights: Bawbawgyi Stupa (Sri Ksetra), the city walls, and the burial urns.

name: Bagan
overview: Bagan is a sacred landscape featuring an exceptional range of Buddhist art and architecture. It contains more than 3,500 surviving stupas, temples, monasteries, and other structures.
history_significance: The capital of the Pagan Kingdom (11th-13th centuries), representing the peak of Buddhist temple construction in Southeast Asia.
highlights: Ananda Temple, Dhammayangyi Temple, and the sunset views over the plains.

name: Twyfelfontein or /Ui-//aes
overview: Twyfelfontein has one of the largest concentrations of rock engravings in Africa. Most of these well-preserved engravings represent rhinoceroses, elephants, ostriches, and giraffes, as well as footprints of men and animals.
history_significance: An extensive record of ritual practices relating to hunter-gatherer communities in this part of southern Africa over at least 2,000 years.
highlights: The 'Lion Man' engraving, the Dancing Kudu, and the stunning red sandstone landscape.

name: Namib Sand Sea
overview: The Namib Sand Sea is the only coastal desert in the world that includes extensive dune fields influenced by fog. It features two dune systems, an ancient semi-consolidated one overlaid by a younger active one.
history_significance: An outstanding example of the scenic, geomorphological, ecological and evolutionary consequences of wind-driven processes.
highlights: Sossusvlei, Deadvlei (with dead camel thorn trees), and Dune 45.

name: Ruins of León Viejo
overview: León Viejo is one of the oldest Spanish colonial settlements in the Americas. It was founded in 1524 but abandoned in 1610 following an eruption of the Momotombo volcano.
history_significance: Preserves the original layout of an early Spanish colonial city without later alterations.
highlights: The ruins of the Cathedral, the La Merced convent, and the view of Momotombo Volcano.

name: León Cathedral
overview: Built between 1747 and the early 19th century, the Cathedral of León represents the transition from Baroque to Neoclassical architecture.
history_significance: The largest cathedral in Central America, known for its robust construction designed to withstand earthquakes.
highlights: The roof promenade, the 'Atlantes' statues, and the tomb of Rubén Darío.

name: Sukur Cultural Landscape
overview: The Sukur Cultural Landscape, with the Palace of the Hidi (Chief) on a hill dominating the villages below, the terraced fields and their sacred symbols, and the extensive remains of a former flourishing iron industry.
history_significance: An exceptional testimony to a strong and continuing spiritual and cultural tradition that has endured for many centuries.
highlights: The Hidi's Palace, the terraced fields, and the paved walkways.

name: Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove
overview: The dense forest of the Osun Sacred Grove, on the outskirts of the city of Osogbo, is one of the last remnants of primary high forest in southern Nigeria. It contains sculptures and sanctuaries dedicated to the river goddess Osun.
history_significance: A symbol of identity for the Yoruba people and probably the last sacred grove in Yoruba culture.
highlights: The river Osun, the shrines, and the sculptures by Suzanne Wenger.

name: Nyungwe National Park
overview: This serial property represents an important area for rainforest conservation in Central Africa. It contains significant habitats for a number of species, including chimpanzees and the owl-faced monkey.
history_significance: One of the oldest rainforests in Africa, vital for its biodiversity and as a water catchment area.
highlights: The Canopy Walkway, Chimpanzee tracking, and the waterfalls.

name: Memorial sites of the Genocide: Nyamata, Murambi, Gisozi and Bisesero
overview: This serial property comprises four sites that commemorate the 1994 Genocide against the Tutsi. They are places of remembrance and learning.
history_significance: Testifies to the calculated mass killings and the resilience of the survivors.
highlights: Kigali Genocide Memorial (Gisozi) and the Nyamata Church.

name: Aldabra Atoll
overview: The atoll is comprised of four large coral islands which enclose a shallow lagoon. It is home to the world's largest population of giant tortoises.
history_significance: One of the largest raised coral atolls in the world, virtually untouched by humans.
highlights: The Aldabra Giant Tortoise population (over 100,000) and the pristine marine environment.

name: Vallée de Mai Nature Reserve
overview: Located on the island of Praslin, this reserve has a natural palm forest preserved in almost its original state. The famous Coco de Mer, the largest seed in the plant kingdom, grows here.
history_significance: A remnant of the ancient palm forests of Gondwana, once believed to be the Garden of Eden.
highlights: The Coco de Mer palms and the rare Seychelles Black Parrot.

name: Cultural Sites of Al Ain (Hafit, Hili, Bidaa Bint Saud and Oases Areas)
overview: The property provides testimony to the sedentary human occupation of a desert region since the Neolithic period with vestiges of many prehistoric cultures.
history_significance: Illustrates the transition from hunting and gathering to sedentary agriculture and the development of the 'falaj' irrigation system.
highlights: The Al Ain Oasis, Jebel Hafit Tombs, and Hili Archaeological Park.

name: Faya Palaeolandscape
overview: Located in the central region of the Emirate of Sharjah, Faya Palaeolandscape serves as an exceptional archive of human history. The site preserves a continuous record of human occupation from the Middle Palaeolithic (210,000 years ago) through the Neolithic and Bronze Ages.
history_significance: It provides critical evidence of early human migration out of Africa and their adaptation to changing climatic conditions in the Arabian Peninsula. The site contains the oldest evidence of human presence in the region.
highlights: Jebel Faya rock shelter (FAY-NE1), Neolithic tombs, and ancient stone tool assemblages.

name: Manovo-Gounda St Floris National Park
overview: The importance of this park derives from its wealth of flora and fauna. Its vast savannahs are home to a wide variety of species: black rhinoceroses, elephants, cheetahs, leopards, wild dogs, red-fronted gazelles and buffaloes.
history_significance: The largest park in the Central African savannas, representing a transition zone between the Sahel and the Sudan-Guinea zone.
highlights: The biodiversity of the savannahs and the floodplains of the Gounda and Koumbala rivers.

name: Historic Centre (Old Town) of Tallinn
overview: The origins of Tallinn date back to the 13th century, when a castle was built there by the crusading knights of the Teutonic Order. It developed as a major centre of the Hanseatic League.
history_significance: An exceptionally complete and well-preserved medieval northern European trading city.
highlights: Toompea Castle, the Alexander Nevsky Cathedral, and the medieval city walls.

name: Kunta Kinteh Island and Related Sites
overview: Kunta Kinteh Island (formerly James Island) and related sites along the Gambia River present a testimony to the main periods and facets of the encounter between Africa and Europe.
history_significance: A key site of the slave trade and the abolition movement, illustrating the history of Afro-European relations from the 15th to the 20th century.
highlights: The ruins of Fort James on Kunta Kinteh Island, Fort Bullen at the river mouth, and associated structures in Albreda and Juffureh.

locations.name: Kunta Kinteh Island (James Island and Fort James)

name: Island of Mozambique
overview: The fortified city of Mozambique is located on a small coral island. It was a major Arab trading post before becoming a Portuguese colony and capital of East Africa.
history_significance: Remarkable architectural unity due to the consistent use of local building techniques and materials since the 16th century.
highlights: Fort of São Sebastião, Chapel of Nossa Senhora de Baluarte, and the Palace of St Paul.

name: Forest Massif of Odzala-Kokoua
overview: This property is an excellent example, at an exceptionally large scale, of the post-glacial recolonization of savanna ecosystems by forest. It represents a wide range of biological diversity in the Congo Basin.
history_significance: Contains one of the most important populations of forest elephants and western lowland gorillas in Central Africa.
highlights: The vast rainforests, saline clearings (bais), and the rich primate assemblages.

name: Vatican City
overview: The Vatican City, one of the most sacred places in Christendom, attests to a great history and a formidable spiritual venture. A unique collection of artistic and architectural masterpieces lies within the boundaries of this small state.
history_significance: The centre of Christianity since the foundation of Saint Peter's Basilica by Constantine in the 4th century and later the permanent seat of the Popes.
highlights: St. Peter's Basilica, the Sistine Chapel, and the Vatican Museums.

name: Madriu-Perafita-Claror Valley
overview: The Madriu-Perafita-Claror Valley is a glacial cultural landscape preserving traditional mountain pastoral systems and human adaptation over 700 years.
history_significance: A serial high-mountain pastoral landscape illustrating seasonal transhumance, land-use continuity, and the interaction between humans and nature in the Pyrenees.
highlights: Stone shepherds' huts, ancient terraces, pastoral paths, and glacial valley ecosystems.

name: Mbanza Kongo, Vestiges of the Capital of the former Kingdom of Kongo
overview: The former capital of the Kingdom of Kongo, one of the largest states in Southern Africa between the 14th and 19th centuries.
history_significance: Illustrates the encounter of African and European cultures following the arrival of the Portuguese and the introduction of Christianity.
highlights: Kulumbimbi Cathedral ruins, the Sacred Royal Tree, and royal archaeological vestiges.

name: Antigua Naval Dockyard and Related Archaeological Sites
overview: A group of Georgian naval structures forming a major British naval base in the Caribbean during the age of sail.
history_significance: Constructed largely by enslaved Africans, the dockyard protected British sugar interests in the region.
highlights: Nelson's Dockyard, Clarence House, and Shirley Heights.

name: Historic Bridgetown and its Garrison
overview: An outstanding example of British colonial architecture with a unique serpentine street layout.
history_significance: A key commercial and military hub of the British Empire in the Caribbean.
highlights: Parliament Buildings, St. Ann's Garrison, and the Careenage.

name: Belize Barrier Reef Reserve System
overview: The largest barrier reef in the Northern Hemisphere, containing seven protected areas illustrating reef evolution and biodiversity.
history_significance: Home to threatened species such as marine turtles, manatees, and the American marine crocodile.
highlights: Great Blue Hole, Half Moon Caye, Glover's Reef, and diverse coral habitats.

name: Cidade Velha, Historic Centre of Ribeira Grande
overview: The first European colonial outpost in the tropics and a major hub of the transatlantic slave trade.
history_significance: Birthplace of Creole culture, illustrating Africa–Europe contact.
highlights: Pelourinho, São Filipe Fortress, and cathedral ruins.

name: Morne Trois Pitons National Park
overview: A volcanic national park containing tropical forests, fumaroles, hot springs, and five volcanoes.
history_significance: Includes Boiling Lake, the second-largest hot lake in the world, and exceptional biodiversity.
highlights: Boiling Lake, Emerald Pool, Valley of Desolation.

name: Colonial City of Santo Domingo
overview: Founded in 1498, the first permanent European settlement in the Americas, laid out on a grid plan that influenced New World urbanism.
history_significance: Site of the first cathedral, hospital, university, and other major institutions in the Americas.
highlights: Cathedral of Santa María la Menor, Alcázar de Colón, Ozama Fortress.

name: Joya de Cerén Archaeological Site
overview: A pre-Hispanic Mayan farming village preserved under volcanic ash, known as the 'Pompeii of the Americas'.
history_significance: Provides rare insight into daily life of non-elite Mayan people.
highlights: Adobe dwellings, household artifacts, agricultural fields.

name: Asmara: A Modernist African City
overview: An exceptional early modernist city developed by Italian planners in the 1930s.
history_significance: Contains one of the world's most complete collections of modernist, Futurist, and Rationalist architecture.
highlights: Fiat Tagliero, Cinema Impero, Opera House.

name: Levuka Historical Port Town
overview: The first colonial capital of Fiji, combining indigenous construction traditions with late 19th-century Pacific colonial architecture.
history_significance: Shows the transition of Fiji into the global economy under British rule.
highlights: Royal Hotel, Sacred Heart Church, colonial waterfront.

name: National History Park – Citadel, Sans Souci, Ramiers
overview: Monuments built after Haitian independence, including the massive Citadelle fortress.
history_significance: Symbolizes liberty as the first monuments constructed by formerly enslaved people after gaining freedom.
highlights: Citadelle Laferrière, Sans-Souci Palace, Ramiers archaeological site.

name: Phoenix Islands Protected Area
overview: The Phoenix Islands Protected Area (PIPA) is a 408,250 sq.km expanse of marine and terrestrial habitats in the Southern Pacific Ocean. It is one of the largest marine protected areas in the world.
history_significance: A pristine environment conserving one of the world's last intact oceanic coral archipelago ecosystems.
highlights: Kanton Island, the pristine coral reefs, and the abundance of pelagic fish.

name: City of Luxembourg: its Old Quarters and Fortifications
overview: Because of its strategic position, Luxembourg was one of Europe's greatest fortified sites from the 16th century until 1867. Its fortifications were dismantled, but many fine examples of military architecture remain.
history_significance: Known as the 'Gibraltar of the North', illustrating centuries of military architecture by Prussians, Austrians, Spaniards, and French.
highlights: The Bock Casemates, the Corniche (Europe's most beautiful balcony), and the Grand Ducal Palace.

name: Bikini Atoll Nuclear Test Site
overview: Between 1946 and 1958, 23 nuclear devices were detonated at Bikini Atoll by the United States. The site bears witness to the dawn of the nuclear age.
history_significance: Symbolizes the Cold War era and the immense power of nuclear weapons, including the Castle Bravo test.
highlights: The Bravo Crater and the sunken fleet of ships (including the USS Saratoga).

name: Nan Madol: Ceremonial Centre of Eastern Micronesia
overview: Nan Madol is a series of 99 artificial islets constructed of basalt and coral boulders. These islets house the remains of stone palaces, temples, tombs, and residential domains.
history_significance: The ceremonial centre of the Saudeleur Dynasty, representing a unique example of monumental megalithic architecture in the Pacific.
highlights: Nandauwas (Royal Tomb), the massive basalt log structures, and the network of canals.

name: Rock Islands Southern Lagoon
overview: This site covers 100,200 ha and includes 445 uninhabited limestone islands of volcanic origin. It features a complex reef system and marine lakes.
history_significance: Contains the highest concentration of marine lakes in the world and remains of small stone villages from 950 to 500 BP.
highlights: Jellyfish Lake, the limestone islands, and the Milky Way.

name: Kuk Early Agricultural Site
overview: The Kuk Swamp has been excavated to reveal evidence of water management and agriculture dating back 9,000 years.
history_significance: One of the few places in the world where people independently developed agriculture, transforming plant exploitation into agriculture.
highlights: Ancient drainage ditches and archaeological excavations showing the history of agriculture.

name: Jesuit Missions of La Santísima Trinidad de Paraná and Jesús de Tavarangue
overview: These two missions are reminders of the Jesuit colonization of the Rio de la Plata basin in the 17th and 18th centuries. They represent a unique urban scheme.
history_significance: Outstanding examples of the 'Guaraní Baroque' style, combining European architecture with indigenous traditions.
highlights: The Main Church of Trinidad (friezes and pulpit) and the unfinished church of Jesús de Tavarangue.

name: Al Zubarah Archaeological Site
overview: Al Zubarah is an outstanding example of a historic pearling and trading town in the Persian Gulf. Founded in the 18th century, it was destroyed in 1811 and abandoned.
history_significance: Provides evidence of the urban planning and socio-economic history of the Persian Gulf before the discovery of oil.
highlights: The Fort of Al Zubarah, the city walls, and the ancient harbor.

name: Brimstone Hill Fortress National Park
overview: Brimstone Hill is an outstanding, well-preserved example of 17th- and 18th-century military architecture in a Caribbean context. It was designed by the British and built by African slave labour.
history_significance: Known as the 'Gibraltar of the West Indies', it testifies to the European colonial expansion and the slave trade.
highlights: The Citadel (Fort George) and the panoramic views of the Caribbean Sea.

name: Pitons Management Area
overview: The 2,909-ha site near the town of Soufrière includes two dramatic volcanic spires, the Gros Piton (770 m) and Petit Piton (743 m), rising side by side from the sea.
history_significance: A spectacular geothermal field with fumaroles and hot springs, illustrating the volcanic history of the Lesser Antilles.
highlights: Gros Piton, Petit Piton, and the Sulphur Springs.

name: San Marino Historic Centre and Mount Titano
overview: San Marino is one of the world's oldest republics and the only surviving Italian city-state. The site includes the fortifications, towers, and the historic center on Mount Titano.
history_significance: Represents the continuity of a free republic since the Middle Ages.
highlights: The Three Towers (Guaita, Cesta, Montale), Palazzo Pubblico, and the Basilica of San Marino.

name: Singapore Botanic Gardens
overview: Established in 1859, this tropical garden demonstrates the evolution of a British colonial botanical garden into a modern scientific institution.
history_significance: Played a key role in the rubber trade boom in Southeast Asia by pioneering rubber cultivation techniques.
highlights: The National Orchid Garden, Swan Lake, and Burkill Hall.

name: East Rennell
overview: East Rennell forms the southern third of Rennell Island, the southernmost island in the Solomon Islands group. It is the largest raised coral atoll in the world.
history_significance: Includes Lake Tegano, the largest lake in the insular Pacific, with high endemism.
highlights: Lake Tegano and raised coral forests.

name: Chief Roi Mata's Domain
overview: Three archaeological sites associated with the life and death of Chief Roi Mata, spread across Efate, Lelepa, and Artok Islands.
history_significance: Reflects oral traditions and social reforms introduced by Chief Roi Mata.
highlights: Mangaas (residence), Fels Cave (death site), Artok Island (burial site).
